
### 4.2 Block Diagram — Feedback Loop in a Sensor-Controlled Drip Irrigation Valve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ref["Setpoint theta v 0.25"] --> sum((+))
    fb["Measured theta v sensor"] --> sum
    sum --> ctrl["PID Controller MCU"]
    ctrl --> pwm["PWM Signal 0 to 100 percent"]
    pwm --> drv["Motor Driver BTS7960"]
    drv --> valve["Solenoid Valve 12V"]
    valve --> crop["Soil and Root Zone"]
    crop --> fb
    style ref fill:#FFF8DC
    style ctrl fill:#E0FFE0
    style crop fill:#F0E68C
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Variable Rate Technology (VRT) Prescription Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    sat["Satellite Sentinel 2 Image"] --> proc1["Atmospheric Correction"]
    drone["Drone Multispectral DJI P4M"] --> proc1
    proc1 --> ndvi["NDVI Map Generation"]
    ndvi --> zone["Management Zone Delineation K Means"]
    zone --> rx["Prescription Map shapefile"]
    rx --> tractor["Tractor Terminal Trimble GFX 750"]
    tractor --> ctrl["Implement Controller Rate"]
    ctrl --> spr["Variable Rate Sprayer Spinner"]
    ctrl --> liq["Variable Rate Liquid Injector"]
    style ndvi fill:#E6FFE6
    style zone fill:#FFF0E6
    style ctrl fill:#E6F0FF
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU 2024:** The diagrams above directly map to Module 1's expected outcomes — *“describe the role of sensors, microcontrollers, communication systems, and actuators in PA”*. Memorize the **five functional blocks (SENSE, CONDITION, DECIDE, COMMUNICATE, ACTUATE)** — they earn easy 7-mark sub-parts.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. Define precision agriculture. List any four electronic components used in precision agriculture.**
`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1]`

**Model Answer:**
Precision agriculture (PA) is an information-based farm management approach that uses electronic sensing, communication, and control technologies to observe and respond to spatial and temporal variability in crops, with the goal of optimizing input use, maximizing yield, and minimizing environmental impact.

The four major electronic components used in PA are:

1. **Sensors** — soil moisture, temperature, NPK, EC, pH, weather, NDVI.
2. **Microcontrollers / Embedded Processors** — ESP32, Arduino, STM32, PLC.
3. **Communication Modules** — LoRa, Zigbee, GSM, Wi-Fi, NB-IoT.
4. **Actuators** — solenoid valves, VFD-driven pumps, dosing pumps, vent motors, foggers.

*(A clean diagram of the Sense–Decide–Actuate loop can be drawn for full 3 marks.)*

---

#### **Q2. What is NDVI? How is it used in precision agriculture?**
`[KTU University Exam – Sample, Module 1]`

**Model Answer:**
NDVI (Normalized Difference Vegetation Index) is a dimensionless spectral index computed from the red and near-infrared reflectance of vegetation:

$$
\mathrm{NDVI} = \dfrac{\mathrm{NIR} - \mathrm{Red}}{\mathrm{NIR} + \mathrm{Red}}
$$

NDVI values range from $-1$ to $+1$. Healthy green vegetation has high NDVI (0.6 to 0.9), while bare soil and water give low or negative values.

**Uses in PA:**
- Mapping within-field variability in crop vigour.
- Generating prescription maps for variable-rate fertilization.
- Monitoring crop stress (drought, disease, nitrogen deficiency) before visible symptoms appear.
- Estimating leaf area index and biomass using empirical regressions.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1, 14 Marks]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of an electronic precision-agriculture system. Label the sensing, communication, decision, and actuation layers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the working of a capacitive soil-moisture sensor. Derive the Topp equation and explain how the MCU converts the sensor output to a volumetric water content reading for automated irrigation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Functional architecture (7 marks)**

The PA system is built in **five layers** (SENSE → CONDITION → DECIDE → COMMUNICATE → ACTUATE):

1. **Sensing layer:** soil moisture (FDR), soil NPK (ion-selective), soil temp (PT1000), air T/RH (DHT22), solar radiation (pyranometer), rain (tipping bucket), leaf wetness, CO₂ (NDIR).
2. **Signal conditioning layer:** instrumentation amplifier, 24-bit ADC (ADS1256), 5 V → 3.3 V level shifter.
3. **Decision layer:** ESP32 MCU running PID/fuzzy-logic algorithm, SD-card data logger.
4. **Communication layer:** LoRa 433 MHz for field WSN, Wi-Fi LAN for greenhouse, GSM/4G/NB-IoT for cloud uplink.
5. **Actuation layer:** solenoid valve, VFD pump, dosing pump, vent stepper motor, exhaust fan, fogger.

The **closed-loop flow** is: sensor → ADC → MCU → actuator → plant/soil → sensor. (Draw the block diagram from Section 4.1.)

*(Awarding 7 marks: 2 for the block diagram, 3 for layer-wise explanation, 2 for the closed-loop narrative.)*

**(b) Capacitive soil-moisture sensor and Topp equation (7 marks)**

A capacitive soil-moisture sensor (e.g., EC-5, VH400) measures the **dielectric permittivity (ε)** of the surrounding soil. The probe forms a capacitor whose fringing electric field penetrates the soil; ε is dominated by water (ε ≈ 80) versus dry soil (ε ≈ 3–5). The capacitance change is converted to a voltage by an internal oscillator.

**Step 1 — Sensor output voltage** is read by the MCU ADC:

$$
V_{\text{out}} = f(\varepsilon)
$$

**Step 2 — Convert ε to volumetric water content using Topp's cubic:**

$$
\theta_v = -5.3 \times 10^{-2} + 2.92 \times 10^{-2}\,\varepsilon - 5.5 \times 10^{-4}\,\varepsilon^2 + 4.3 \times 10^{-6}\,\varepsilon^3
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the linear engineering approximation for the field range $\varepsilon \in [3, 25]$:**

$$
\theta_v = \dfrac{\varepsilon - 3.07}{7.46}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply temperature compensation:**

$$
\theta_{v,\text{corr}} = \theta_{v,\text{raw}} + 0.0011 \,(T_{\text{soil}} - 25)
$$

**Step 5 — Run the irrigation decision rule:**

$$
\text{Irrigate} \iff \theta_{v,\text{corr}} < \mathrm{PWP} + 0.5 \cdot \mathrm{RAW}
$$

**Numerical example:** Suppose the sensor reports ε = 18, T_soil = 28 °C, FC = 0.32, PWP = 0.12.
- $\theta_v = (18 - 3.07)/7.46 = 0.200$ m³/m³
- $\theta_{v,\text{corr}} = 0.200 + 0.0011 \cdot (28-25) = 0.203$ m³/m³
- $\mathrm{RAW} = 0.32 - 0.12 = 0.20$
- Threshold = 0.12 + 0.5·0.20 = 0.22
- 0.203 < 0.22 → **Irrigate ON** for 12 minutes.

*(Awarding 7 marks: 2 for working principle, 2 for Topp derivation, 2 for numerical example, 1 for decision rule.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

`[KTU University Exam – Supplementary 2023 Pattern, Module 1, 14 Marks]`

**(a)** Explain the role of GPS/GNSS in precision agriculture. How is it used in yield mapping and variable-rate application? Describe any one GNSS-based PA application with a block diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the role of wireless sensor networks (WSN) in protected cultivation. Compare LoRa, Zigbee, and Wi-Fi for greenhouse applications, with emphasis on range, power, and data rate. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Role of GPS/GNSS in PA (7 marks)**

**GNSS** (Global Navigation Satellite System) provides real-time geolocation (latitude, longitude, altitude) with accuracies of:

- **Standard GPS** — ± 3–5 m
- **DGPS (Differential GPS)** — ± 0.5–1 m
- **RTK-GPS** — ± 1–2 cm (used in auto-steer tractors)

**Role in yield mapping:**
A combine harvester fitted with a **yield monitor** (impact-plate or optical sensor) records crop mass flow (kg/s) and combines it with **GPS position** at 1 Hz. The data is interpolated into a **georeferenced yield map** (t/ha). Low-yield zones (CV > 30%) trigger soil sampling and prescription-map generation.

**Role in variable-rate application (VRT):**
A prescription map (.shp) containing the desired input rate (e.g., kg N/ha) per grid cell is loaded into the tractor terminal. As the tractor moves, the **GPS** tells the controller where it is, and the controller modulates a **PWM-driven hydraulic valve** on the implement to deliver the correct dose. The control loop is:

$$
\text{Rate}_{\text{actual}} = K_p \cdot (\text{Rate}_{\text{prescribed}} - \text{Rate}_{\text{feedback}}) + K_i \int (\cdots)\,dt
$$

**Block diagram (for full marks):**
GNSS receiver → Tractor terminal → Prescription map lookup → PWM controller → Hydraulic valve → Sprayer/Planter → Yield/Soil sensor → Feedback to terminal. (Refer to Section 4.3.)

**(b) WSN in protected cultivation (7 marks)**

A **protected-cultivation system** (polyhouse, greenhouse) requires continuous monitoring of microclimate: temperature, humidity, light, CO₂, soil moisture. Manual monitoring is impractical at the spatial density required (one node per 50–100 m²). Hence a **WSN** is deployed.

**Comparison of wireless protocols:**

| Parameter | LoRa (433/868 MHz) | Zigbee (2.4 GHz) | Wi-Fi (2.4/5 GHz) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Range (outdoor) | 2–10 km | 30–100 m | 50–150 m |
| Data rate | 0.3–50 kbps | 250 kbps | 11–300 Mbps |
| Power consumption | Very low (battery: years) | Low (months) | High (hours–days) |
| Node cost | ₹600–₹1,500 | ₹400–₹1,000 | ₹500–₹1,200 |
| Best suited for | Long-range, sparse sensing | Dense mesh inside greenhouse | High-bandwidth video/ML |

**Recommendation for a typical 1000 m² polyhouse:** A **hybrid LoRa + Zigbee** topology is optimal — Zigbee forms an intra-greenhouse mesh for sensor density; LoRa uplinks aggregated data to the cloud every 15 min.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Precision Farming" with "Protected Cultivation"** — they are *not synonyms*. Protected cultivation is one *application* of PA; PA is the broader umbrella. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if the boundary is blurred.
> 2. **Forgetting units in numerical answers** — a calculated θᵥ without "m³/m³" loses ½ mark.
> 3. **Writing "sensors do automation"** — vague. Always specify *which* sensor, *what* transducer principle, *which* MCU, and *which* actuator.
> 4. **Skipping the block diagram in 7-mark sub-parts** — KTU valuators allot at least 2 marks for the diagram alone.
> 5. **Mixing up NDVI and EVI** — NDVI saturates at high biomass; EVI does not. Know the difference if asked.
> 6. **No mention of "real-time" or "closed-loop"** — these are *high-frequency keywords* in the KTU marking scheme; including them boosts scores.
> 7. **No numerical example** in 14-mark questions — at least one solved example with realistic Kerala-region values (e.g., 28°C, ε = 18) is expected.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Role of Electronics in Precision Agriculture**
>
> - **Definition:** PA = sense, decide, actuate — *spatially and temporally* explicit farm management.
> - **Five functional layers:** SENSING → CONDITIONING → DECISION → COMMUNICATION → ACTUATION.
> - **Core sensors to memorize:** soil moisture (FDR/capacitive), soil NPK (ion-selective), DHT22 (T/RH), pyranometer, tipping-bucket rain gauge, NDVI multispectral camera.
> - **Key MCU platforms for PA:** Arduino Uno, ESP32, STM32, Raspberry Pi (for ML/edge), industrial PLC.
> - **Communication protocols:** RS-485, CAN (intra-machine), Zigbee, BLE, LoRa (field), Wi-Fi, GSM/4G/5G, NB-IoT (cloud).
> - **NDVI formula (must-know):**
>   $$\mathrm{NDVI} = \dfrac{\mathrm{NIR} - \mathrm{Red}}{\mathrm{NIR} + \mathrm{Red}}$$
> - **Topp equation (must-know):**
>   $$\theta_v = -5.3 \times 10^{-2} + 2.92 \times 10^{-2}\,\varepsilon - 5.5 \times 10^{-4}\,\varepsilon^2 + 4.3 \times 10^{-6}\,\varepsilon^3$$
> - **GNSS hierarchy:** GPS (±3 m) → DGPS (±1 m) → RTK-GPS (±2 cm).
> - **VRT** uses a prescription map + GPS + PWM-controlled actuator.
> - **WSN protocol choice:** LoRa for range, Zigbee for mesh, Wi-Fi for bandwidth.
> - **Irrigation rule:** Irrigate ⟺ θ_v < PWP + 0.5·RAW AND Rain = FALSE AND T_air < 35 °C.
> - **Penman-Monteith ET** drives sensor-based scheduling; **field capacity** & **permanent wilting point** define RAW.
> - **Kerala-specific note:** high humidity → emphasize *disease-warning electronics* (leaf wetness + RH > 90% = spray alert).
> - **Cost benchmark:** basic WSN node ≈ ₹1,500–₹4,000; full polyhouse automation ₹50,000–₹2,00,000 (2024 prices).
> - **Buzzwords examiners love:** *real-time, closed-loop, georeferenced, site-specific, variable-rate, edge-AI, decision support system, prescription map, sensor fusion.*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basics of Precision Agriculture and Tools for Implementation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT751 - PRECISION FARMING TECHNIQUES FOR PROTECTED CULTIVATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basics of Precision Agriculture and Tools for Implementation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Precision Agriculture (PA)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Precision Agriculture (PA)**, also termed **Site-Specific Crop Management (SSCM)** or **Satellite Farming**, is an integrated, information-based farm management system designed to identify, analyse, and manage the **spatial and temporal variability** within agricultural fields for the purpose of optimizing profitability, sustainability, and protection of the environment. (KTU 2024 – PEAGT751 Module 1 Reference)

In more granular KTU textbook language:

$$PA = f(\text{Sensing}, \text{Data}, \text{Decision}, \text{Action})$$

Precision Agriculture is built on the philosophy of **"Doing the Right Thing, in the Right Place, at the Right Time"** — the celebrated **5R Principle** (Right Input, Right Rate, Right Time, Right Place, Right Method).

> [!NOTE]
> **Spatial Variability** refers to differences in soil properties, crop yield, moisture, and nutrient status **across a field** (location-based), while **Temporal Variability** refers to changes that occur **over time** (seasonal/daily variations).

### 1.2 The Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Think of a hospital treating **100 patients with the same fever**:

- **Traditional Farming** = Giving the *same dose* of paracetamol to *everyone* regardless of body weight, age, or allergies. Result? Some get cured, some are over-dosed, some are under-dosed.
- **Precision Farming** = First measuring each patient's weight, blood test, and temperature. Then giving a *personalized dose* of medicine based on individual diagnostics.

Applied to agriculture:
- The **"patient"** = a small grid cell of land (e.g., 1 m × 1 m, 5 m × 5 m, or even 10 m × 10 m).
- The **"diagnostic test"** = sensors, satellite imagery, soil sampling, drones.
- The **"personalized dose"** = variable-rate application of water, fertilizer, pesticide.
- The **"doctor"** = Decision Support System (DSS) backed by GIS software.

### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (KTU Standard Definitions)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Coefficient of Variation (CV)** = Statistical measure of variability in soil/crop properties. **Standard threshold for "uniform" field: CV < 15%**. Above this, PA techniques are economically justified.
> - **Management Zone (MZ)** = A sub-region of a field with relatively homogeneous characteristics (soil, yield, slope) where a single management strategy is applied.
> - **Grid Cell / Pixel Resolution** = The smallest spatial unit at which decisions are made (commonly **1 m to 30 m** depending on technology).
> - **GreenSeeker / NDVI Index** = A vegetation vigour index ranging from **-1 to +1**; healthy vegetation typically **> 0.6**.

### 1.4 Objectives of Precision Agriculture (as per KTU syllabus)

1. **Optimize input use efficiency** (fertilizer, water, pesticide).
2. **Increase crop yield and quality** through site-specific management.
3. **Reduce environmental footprint** (nitrogen leaching, GHG emissions).
4. **Improve economic profitability** of the farming enterprise.
5. **Enable traceability and sustainability certification** for high-value export crops.

### 1.5 Historical Evolution (KTU Quick-Recap Timeline)

| Decade | Milestone |
|---|---|
| 1980s | Concept originated in USA; introduction of **GPS** for agriculture |
| 1990s | First commercial **yield monitors** and **VRT** equipment |
| 2000s | **GIS** integration; remote sensing adoption |
| 2010s | **UAVs (drones)**, IoT, cloud computing |
| 2020s | **AI/ML analytics**, robotics, digital twins, 5G-enabled smart farms |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Four Pillars (Operational Building Blocks) of Precision Agriculture

Precision Agriculture is built on **four sequential pillars**. Each pillar is a complete sub-system, and the failure of any one collapses the entire PA framework.

**Pillar 1 — Data Acquisition (Sensing Layer)**
- Captures variability using sensors on the ground, in the air, or from space.
- Examples: Soil electrical conductivity (EC) sensors, RTK-GPS, multispectral cameras, drone LiDAR.

**Pillar 2 — Data Processing & Analysis (Information Layer)**
- Raw data are geo-referenced, cleaned, and converted into spatial maps (thematic maps, kriged maps).
- Statistical tools: descriptive statistics, geostatistics (semivariogram, kriging), regression.

**Pillar 3 — Decision Making (Management Layer)**
- Uses **Decision Support Systems (DSS)** to determine "what action where".
- Inputs: prescription maps, agronomic models, economic thresholds.

**Pillar 4 — Variable Rate Application (Action Layer)**
- Implements site-specific input application using **Variable Rate Technology (VRT)**.
- VRT has two architectures:
  - **Map-Based VRT** — uses a pre-loaded prescription map.
  - **Sensor-Based VRT** — uses real-time sensor feedback (closed-loop control).

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Pillar

- **Why Sensing?** → You cannot manage what you cannot measure.
- **Why Geostatistics?** → Spatial autocorrelation in soil properties means neighbouring points are more similar than distant ones; kriging exploits this.
- **Why Prescription Maps?** → A human cannot compute fertilizer rates for thousands of grid cells per second; algorithms do it.
- **Why VRT?** → Conventional uniform application wastes **20–40%** of inputs and contaminates groundwater.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Coefficient of Variation** | $CV = \dfrac{\sigma}{\mu} \times 100$ | $\sigma$ = std. deviation, $\mu$ = mean; **dimensionless (%)** |
| 2 | **NDVI** (Vegetation Index) | $NDVI = \dfrac{NIR - Red}{NIR + Red}$ | Range $\left[-1, +1\right]$; healthy crop $> 0.6$ |
| 3 | **GNDVI** (Green NDVI) | $GNDVI = \dfrac{NIR - Green}{NIR + Green}$ | More sensitive to chlorophyll concentration |
| 4 | **EVI** (Enhanced Vegetation Index) | $EVI = G \times \dfrac{NIR - Red}{NIR + C_1 \cdot Red - C_2 \cdot Blue + L}$ | Coefficients: $C_1=6, C_2=7.5, L=1, G=2.5$ |
| 5 | **Semivariogram** | $\gamma(h) = \dfrac{1}{2N(h)} \sum_{i=1}^{N(h)} \left[Z(x_i) - Z(x_i + h)\right]^2$ | $h$ = lag distance, $Z$ = property value |
| 6 | **Spatial Resolution & Pixel Area** | $A_{pixel} = (\Delta x) \times (\Delta y)$ | $\Delta x, \Delta y$ in meters |
| 7 | **VRT Application Rate** | $R_{applied} = R_{target} \times CF$ | $CF$ = correction factor from prescription map |
| 8 | **Yield Map Equation** | $Y(x,y) = \dfrac{M_{grain}(x,y)}{A_{cell}(x,y)}$ | Yield in **t/ha** |
| 9 | **GPS Dilution of Precision (GDOP)** | $GDOP = \sqrt{PDOP^2 + TDOP^2}$ | $PDOP$ = position, $TDOP$ = time DOP |
| 10 | **Spatial Autocorrelation (Moran's I)** | $I = \dfrac{N}{W} \cdot \dfrac{\sum_i \sum_j w_{ij}(x_i - \bar{x})(x_j - \bar{x})}{\sum_i (x_i - \bar{x})^2}$ | $w_{ij}$ = spatial weight; $I \in [-1,1]$ |
| 11 | **Soil Sampling Density** | $n = \left(\dfrac{t \cdot s}{E \cdot \bar{x}}\right)^2$ | Cochran's formula for required samples |
| 12 | **Chlorophyll Content (SPAD)** | $SPAD = k \cdot \log\left(\dfrac{I_{940}}{I_{650}}\right)$ | $I$ = transmitted light intensity |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions for KTU Numericals:**
> - NDVI range: $-1 \leq NDVI \leq +1$
> - CV for uniform field: $CV < 15\%$
> - GPS accuracy: Sub-meter for basic GPS, **< 2.5 cm** for RTK-GPS
> - Prescription map grid: typically **5 m × 5 m to 30 m × 30 m**

### 2.4 Tools for Implementation of Precision Agriculture (KTU 2024 Module 1 Coverage)

The KTU 2024 syllabus groups PA tools into **five technological pillars**. The classification is essential for descriptive exam answers.

**Tool Cluster 1 — Positioning & Navigation Systems**
- **GPS (Global Positioning System)** — provides absolute coordinates.
- **DGPS (Differential GPS)** — improves accuracy from ±15 m to ±3–5 m.
- **RTK-GPS (Real-Time Kinematic GPS)** — accuracy of **±1–2 cm** using base station corrections.
- **GNSS (Global Navigation Satellite System)** — generic term for GPS/GLONASS/Galileo/BeiDou.

**Tool Cluster 2 — Geospatial Information Systems (GIS)**
- Software for capturing, storing, analysing, managing, and visualizing spatial data.
- Common platforms: **ArcGIS, QGIS, ERDAS Imagine**.
- Key GIS functions used in PA: overlay analysis, buffer, interpolation (kriging, IDW), spatial querying.

**Tool Cluster 3 — Remote Sensing (RS)**
- Acquisition of information about an object without physical contact.
- Platforms: **Satellites (Sentinel-2, Landsat-8, MODIS)** and **Aircraft / UAVs / Drones**.
- Sensor types: RGB, Multispectral, Hyperspectral, Thermal, LiDAR, SAR (Radar).

**Tool Cluster 4 — Variable Rate Technology (VRT)**
- Hardware + software for differential application of inputs.
- Components: **Controller, GPS receiver, speed sensor, actuator (servo valve), prescription map loader**.
- Used in: fertilizer spreaders, pesticide sprayers, planters, irrigation systems.

**Tool Cluster 5 — Proximal Sensing & IoT**
- Sensors in direct contact or near the crop/soil.
- Examples: soil moisture probes, EC sensors, canopy reflectance sensors (GreenSeeker, Crop Circle), chlorophyll meters (SPAD), weather stations.
- Connected via **LoRaWAN, NB-IoT, 4G/5G** for real-time data streaming.

**Tool Cluster 6 — Decision Support Systems (DSS) & Data Analytics**
- Software that integrates multi-source data and recommends actions.
- Modern PA DSS uses **AI/ML models** (Random Forest, XGBoost, CNN for image classification).
- Examples: **AquaCrop, DSSAT, APSIM, Agrio, Climate FieldView, John Deere Operations Center**.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Water-stressed grape vineyards in Maharashtra & Kerala** → NDVI drone maps guide drip-irrigation scheduling, saving up to **30% water**.
- **Pineapple plantations** → VRT fertilization based on grid-sampled soil EC reduces N-leaching by **40%**.
- **Polyhouse vegetable cultivation** → IoT sensors + automated climate control maintain ideal microclimate (25 °C, 70% RH), boosting yield by **2–3×** compared to open field.
- **Tea estates** → GIS-based soil pH maps identify patches for targeted lime application, optimizing cup-quality.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Coefficient of Variation (CV) for Field Variability Assessment

The CV is the **first statistical test** applied to grid-sampled soil data. It quantifies whether a field is uniform (justifying uniform management) or variable (justifying PA).

**Step 1: Define the property to be analysed**
Let $X = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n\}$ be $n$ samples of a soil property (e.g., soil nitrogen in kg/ha) collected from grid points.

**Step 2: Compute the arithmetic mean**

$$
\mu = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i
$$

This represents the central tendency — the average value across the field.

**Step 3: Compute the standard deviation**

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n-1} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( x_i - \mu \right)^2}
$$

**Reason for $n-1$ in denominator:** This is Bessel's correction. We lose 1 degree of freedom because the mean $\mu$ itself is estimated from the same data; $n-1$ gives an **unbiased estimator** of population variance.

**Step 4: Compute the Coefficient of Variation**

$$
CV = \frac{\sigma}{\mu} \times 100 \quad (\%)
$$

**Step 5: Decision Rule (KTU threshold)**
- If $CV < 15\%$ → Field is uniform; uniform management is justified.
- If $15\% \leq CV \leq 35\%$ → Moderate variability; PA is partially justified.
- If $CV > 35\%$ → High variability; PA is strongly recommended.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI)

NDVI is the most widely used remote-sensing index in PA for assessing crop vigour.

**Step 1: Define the spectral bands**
- $NIR$ = reflectance in Near-Infrared band (typically 760–900 nm).
- $Red$ = reflectance in Red band (typically 630–690 nm).

**Step 2: Concept of contrast**
Healthy vegetation strongly reflects NIR (mesophyll scattering) and strongly absorbs Red (chlorophyll absorption). Bare soil reflects both approximately equally.

**Step 3: Form the numerator (contrast) and denominator (normalization)**

$$
NDVI = \frac{NIR - Red}{NIR + Red}
$$

**Step 4: Mathematical bounds**
- Bare soil: $NIR \approx Red \Rightarrow NDVI \approx 0$
- Dense green vegetation: $NIR \gg Red \Rightarrow NDVI \rightarrow +1$
- Water bodies: $NIR < Red \Rightarrow NDVI \rightarrow -1$

Hence $\left[-1, +1\right]$ range is proven.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Semivariogram and Range (Spatial Autocorrelation)

The semivariogram models how soil property similarity decreases with distance.

**Step 1: Lag definition**
For any two sample points separated by lag distance $h$, define the squared difference:

$$
\left[Z(x_i) - Z(x_i + h)\right]^2
$$

**Step 2: Average over all $N(h)$ point pairs separated by lag $h$**

$$
\gamma(h) = \frac{1}{2N(h)} \sum_{i=1}^{N(h)} \left[ Z(x_i) - Z(x_i + h) \right]^2
$$

**Step 3: Identify three key parameters from the variogram plot**

- **Nugget ($C_0$)** = $\gamma(h)$ at $h \to 0$; represents micro-scale variability and measurement error.
- **Sill ($C_0 + C$)** = total variance at which variogram plateaus.
- **Range ($a$)** = lag distance at which sill is reached; beyond this, samples are spatially independent.

**Step 4: Interpretation for PA**
- If $a < $ grid spacing → grid sampling is inadequate; reduce spacing.
- If $a \gg $ grid spacing → use **kriging** to interpolate values between samples.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Variable Rate Application (VRT) Prescription

**Step 1: Define management zones**
Divide field into $k$ zones based on similarity (using NDVI or yield history). For zone $j$, the target input rate is $R_j$ (kg/ha).

**Step 2: Apply the rate equation**

$$
R_{applied}(x,y) = R_{target}(x,y) \times CF(x,y)
$$

where $CF(x,y)$ is the correction factor from the prescription map (typically 0.5–1.5).

**Step 3: Compute total input for a zone**

$$
T_j = R_j \times A_j
$$

where $A_j$ = area of zone $j$ in hectares.

**Step 4: Total input for the entire field**

$$
T_{total} = \sum_{j=1}^{k} R_j \cdot A_j
$$

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Yield Map Calculation

A yield monitor records **grain mass flow** $M(t)$ at time $t$ and **GPS position** $(x(t), y(t))$.

**Step 1: Compute instantaneous yield at each geo-referenced point**

$$
Y(x, y) = \frac{M_{grain}(x,y)}{A_{cell}(x,y)} \quad \left[ \text{t/ha} \right]
$$

**Step 2: Cell area from instantaneous speed and header width**

$$
A_{cell} = w \cdot v \cdot \Delta t
$$

where $w$ = harvester header width (m), $v$ = forward speed (m/s), $\Delta t$ = logging interval (s).

**Step 3: Total field yield**

$$
Y_{field} = \frac{1}{A_{field}} \sum_{i=1}^{N} Y_i \cdot A_{cell,i}
$$

---

### 3.6 Numerical Worked Example (KTU 3-mark style)

**Problem:** A 4-hectare paddy field is divided into 20 grid cells. Soil nitrogen values (kg/ha) are measured and given below. Compute the mean, standard deviation, and CV. Decide whether PA is justified.

Data: $\{120, 135, 150, 165, 180, 200, 220, 240, 255, 270, 290, 310, 330, 355, 380, 400, 425, 450, 480, 510\}$ kg/ha

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Sum of all values:

$$
\sum x_i = 120+135+\cdots+510 = 5865
$$

Step 2 — Mean:

$$
\mu = \frac{5865}{20} = 293.25 \text{ kg/ha}
$$

Step 3 — Sum of squared deviations:

$$
\sum (x_i - \mu)^2 = (120-293.25)^2 + \cdots + (510-293.25)^2
$$

Let us compute term by term. After full expansion, $\sum (x_i - \mu)^2 = 312,687.5$

Step 4 — Variance:

$$
\sigma^2 = \frac{312687.5}{20-1} = \frac{312687.5}{19} \approx 16,457.24
$$

Step 5 — Standard deviation:

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{16457.24} \approx 128.29 \text{ kg/ha}
$$

Step 6 — Coefficient of Variation:

$$
CV = \frac{128.29}{293.25} \times 100 \approx 43.75\%
$$

**Conclusion:** Since $CV \approx 43.75\% \gg 35\%$, **PA is strongly recommended** for this field.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Figure 1: Architecture of Precision Agriculture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Field Variability Source] --> B[Data Acquisition Layer]
    B --> C1[GPS / GNSS Sensors]
    B --> C2[Soil Sensors EC pH Moisture]
    B --> C3[Drone UAV Multispectral Camera]
    B --> C4[Satellite Remote Sensing]
    B --> C5[Yield Monitor on Harvester]
    C1 --> D[Data Storage Cloud Database]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
    D --> E[Geospatial Analysis GIS]
    E --> F1[Interpolation Kriging]
    E --> F2[NDVI EVI Map Generation]
    E --> F3[Management Zone Delineation]
    F1 --> G[Decision Support System DSS]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[Prescription Map Generation]
    H --> I[Variable Rate Technology VRT]
    I --> J1[Variable Rate Fertilizer Spreader]
    I --> J2[Variable Rate Pesticide Sprayer]
    I --> J3[Variable Rate Irrigation System]
    J1 --> K[Site Specific Crop Management]
    J2 --> K
    J3 --> K
    K --> L[Outcome Increased Yield Reduced Cost Sustainability]
```

### 4.2 Figure 2: VRT Closed-Loop Control Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Prescription Map Loaded] --> B[VRT Controller Microprocessor]
    B --> C[GPS Position Fix]
    C --> D[Compute Current Cell Coordinate]
    D --> E[Lookup Target Rate from Map]
    E --> F[Compare Actual vs Target Rate]
    F --> G{PID Controller}
    G --> H[Servo Valve Actuator]
    H --> I[Flow Meter Feedback]
    I --> F
    H --> J[Output Variable Rate Application]
```

### 4.3 Figure 3: Workflow of NDVI-Based Crop Health Assessment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Drone Flight Mission Planning] --> B[Multispectral Image Capture NIR Red Green]
    B --> C[Image Orthomosaic Stitching]
    C --> D[Radiometric Calibration]
    D --> E[Compute NDVI per Pixel]
    E --> F[Generate NDVI Thematic Map]
    F --> G{Vigour Threshold}
    G -->|NDVI greater than 0.6| H[Healthy No Action]
    G -->|NDVI 0.3 to 0.6| I[Moderate Apply Targeted Fertilizer]
    G -->|NDVI less than 0.3| J[Stressed Investigate Pest Disease Water]
    I --> K[Update Prescription Map]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Re Flight Next Cycle]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB

        A1[Yield Map Layer]
    end
```
