```mermaid
    q3 -->|VEGETABLES OPEN MARKET| r5["LOW OR MID-TECH POLYHOUSE"]
    q3 -->|EXPORT FLOWERS| r6["MID-TECH FAN PAD"]
    q3 -->|HYDROPONIC EXPORT| r7["HIGH-TECH VENLO"]
    q3 -->|NURSERY SEEDLINGS| r8["SHADE NET HOUSE"]
```

### 4.4 Schematic — Typical Even-Span Greenhouse Cross-Section (Functional Map)

```mermaid
graph TD
    roof["TRANSPARENT ROOF CLADDING GLASS OR PE"]
    ridge["RIDGE VENT TOP"]
    sidevent["SIDE ROLL-UP VENT"]
    gutter["EAVE GUTTER"]
    sidewall["TRANSPARENT SIDE WALL"]
    pad["EVAPORATIVE COOLING PAD"]
    fan["EXHAUST FAN OPPOSITE END"]
    floor["CONCRETE OR GRAVEL FLOOR"]
    truss["STRUCTURAL TRUSS FRAME"]
    crop["CROP ROWS ON BENCHES OR BEDS"]
    drip["DRIP IRRIGATION LATERALS"]

    roof --> truss
    ridge --> roof
    sidevent --> sidewall
    gutter --> roof
    pad --> sidewall
    fan --> sidewall
    floor --> crop
    truss --> roof
    drip --> crop
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied:**
> - All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., `node1` → `B1`, `act1`, `sensor1`).
> - No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) have been used as standalone node names.
> - All double-quoted node labels contain **raw uppercase alphanumeric text** only — no markdown bold/italic, no HTML tables.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define a greenhouse. List any four types of greenhouses based on shape.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **greenhouse** is a framed structure covered with a transparent or translucent material in which crops can be grown under **controlled or partially controlled environmental conditions** to achieve optimum growth, productivity, and off-season availability.

Four types of greenhouses based on shape:
1. **Lean-to** greenhouse
2. **Even-span** (A-frame) greenhouse
3. **Uneven-span** greenhouse
4. **Ridge-and-furrow** (or Sawtooth / Venlo / Quonset — any one)

*(1 mark for correct definition; 0.5 mark each for the four types.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Differentiate between a low-tech polyhouse and a high-tech greenhouse in terms of cladding, control systems, and investment.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Low-Tech Polyhouse | High-Tech Greenhouse |
|---|---|---|
| **Cladding** | UV-stabilized polyethylene film (100–200 µm) | Tempered glass / twin-wall polycarbonate |
| **Control System** | Manual / natural ventilation; no automation | Computerized control of temperature, RH, CO₂, light |
| **Investment (₹/m²)** | 350 – 600 | 3 000 – 6 000+ |
| **Yield vs. open field** | 1.5× – 2× | 5× – 10× |

*(0.5 mark per correct row; 0.5 mark for any additional valid distinguishing point.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **ESE Pattern Note (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks, subdivided into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Candidates must answer **either Option A or Option B** in full.

---

#### **Option A — Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**
**(a)** Classify greenhouses in detail based on **(i) shape** and **(ii) cladding material**, giving at least four sub-types under each category with their relative merits.
**(b)** Explain the classification of greenhouses based on **environmental control level**. Compare a low-tech polyhouse, a medium-tech greenhouse, and a high-tech phytotron in a tabular format covering cladding, ventilation, irrigation, cost, and typical crops.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification by Shape and Cladding (7 marks)**

*By Shape (3.5 marks):*
1. **Lean-to** — single roof slope, attached to building wall. *Merit:* low cost, uses existing wall. *Demerit:* limited size.
2. **Even-span** — symmetric A-frame. *Merit:* best light, easiest ventilation.
3. **Uneven-span** — asymmetric roof; *Merit:* adapts to sloped terrain (Kerala hills).
4. **Ridge-and-furrow** — multiple spans joined. *Merit:* large area, low wall/floor ratio.
5. **Venlo** — Dutch modular design, 4 m/8 m modules. *Merit:* >90 % light transmission.
6. **Quonset** — semicircular arch. *Merit:* cheapest, easy erection.

*By Cladding (3.5 marks):*
1. **Glass** — life 25–30 years, transmission 88–90 %, very high cost.
2. **Polyethylene film** — life 3–5 years, transmission 70–85 %, lowest cost.
3. **Polycarbonate** — life 10–15 years, transmission 75–85 %, high cost.
4. **Acrylic (PMMA)** — life 15–20 years, transmission 85–92 %, very high cost.
5. **Shade net** — life 5–8 years, transmission 25–50 %, very low cost.
6. **Insect net** — life 5–7 years, transmission 60–70 %, low cost, ideal for pest exclusion in Keralan vegetable fields.

**(b) Classification by Control Level (7 marks)**

Three classes are recognized by the KTU 2024 syllabus:

1. **Low-tech / Uncontrolled polyhouse** — passive climate modification, ₹ 350–600/m², suitable for **season extension and rain shelter**; crops: tomato, chilli, cabbage seedlings.
2. **Medium-tech / Partially controlled** — equipped with **fan-and-pad cooling, drip, foggers, motorized vents**; ₹ 900–1500/m²; crops: capsicum, gerbera, rose, cucumber.
3. **High-tech / Fully controlled phytotron** — **computerized control** of temperature (±1 °C), humidity, CO₂ (800–1200 ppm), PAR, EC, pH; ₹ 3000–6000+/m²; crops: export-grade exotic vegetables, hybrid seed production, tissue-cultured plantlets.

A **comparison table** (3 marks of the 7) covering cladding, ventilation, irrigation, cost, crop, and yield factor is expected — the table is provided in **Section 3.4** of these notes and may be reproduced verbatim in the answer script.

---

#### **Option B — Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**
**(a)** With the help of a **labelled block diagram**, describe the **functional architecture** of a fully-controlled greenhouse showing sensors, controller, and actuators.
**(b)** Discuss in detail the **uses of greenhouses** in modern agriculture. Highlight their role in **precision farming, off-season cultivation, and hybrid seed production.**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Functional Architecture (7 marks)**

A modern fully-controlled greenhouse is a **closed-loop cyber-physical system** comprising:
- **Sensor sub-system (2 marks):** Air and soil temperature sensors, RH sensor, CO₂ infrared gas analyser, PAR quantum sensor, soil-moisture TDR probes, EC and pH probes. These continuously digitize the microclimate.
- **Controller (2 marks):** A **PLC / industrial PC / microcontroller (e.g., Arduino / Raspberry Pi)** running **PID control algorithms** with set-points programmed per crop phenological stage.
- **Actuator sub-system (2 marks):** Exhaust fans, motorized roof vents, evaporative cooling pads, shade curtains, foggers, drip-fertigation pumps, CO₂ burners, supplemental LED grow lights.
- **Closed-loop feedback (1 mark):** Sensor data is fed back to the controller, which drives the actuators to maintain set-point conditions, thereby realizing **precision climate management**.

A **labelled block diagram** (refer Section 4.2) must be drawn showing **Sensors → Controller → Actuators → Crop → Sensors** in a closed loop.

**(b) Uses of Greenhouses (7 marks)**

Greenhouse technology underpins **Protected Cultivation** and offers the following high-impact uses:

1. **Off-season cultivation** (2 marks): Growing high-value vegetables (capsicum, cucumber, tomato) and flowers (gerbera, rose, carnation) **outside their normal season**, capturing **3×–5× market price** during scarcity.
2. **Precision farming integration** (2 marks): Real-time control of **water (drip), nutrients (fertigation), and climate** enables **resource-use efficiency** of >80 % water saving and >40 % fertilizer saving compared with open-field cultivation.
3. **Hybrid seed production and breeding** (1 mark): Pollen-proof isolation, insect-pest exclusion, and controlled photoperiod enable **high-purity hybrid seed** of tomato, chilli, cucurbits, and flowers.
4. **Nursery raising** (0.5 mark): Year-round, disease-free vegetable and forest-tree seedling production.
5. **Plant disease and pest management** (0.5 mark): Insect-proof net houses break the life-cycle of **whitefly, thrips, and aphids**, drastically reducing pesticide use.
6. **Demonstration and capacity building** (0.5 mark): KVKs, agricultural universities, and State Horticulture Missions use greenhouses for **farmer training and technology transfer**.
7. **Hydroponics and vertical farming** (0.5 mark): Greenhouses form the **envelope** for soil-less cultivation systems such as **NFT, DFT, and substrate culture** for strawberry, lettuce, and exotic herbs.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT** write the definition in vague terms like "a house made for plants" — KTU awards marks only for the precise **"transparent/translucent enclosed structure providing controlled or partially controlled environment"** phrasing.
> 2. **Do NOT** confuse **cladding** (covering material) with **framing** (load-bearing structure). They are separate classification axes.
> 3. **Do NOT** omit the **cost band (₹/m²)** when classifying by control level — examiners expect quantitative differentiation.
> 4. **Do NOT** forget to draw the **labelled block diagram** in 14-mark answers on functional architecture. A missing diagram typically costs **2–3 marks**.
> 5. **Do NOT** write "greenhouse effect = global warming" — the **greenhouse effect in horticulture** is a beneficial, controlled phenomenon, distinct from the climate-change context.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for End-Semester Preparation:**

- **Definition:** A greenhouse is a transparent/translucent enclosed structure providing **controlled or partially controlled** environmental conditions for crop growth.
- **5 Classification Axes:** Shape, Cladding, Frame, Control Level, Scale/Cost.
- **Six Shapes:** Lean-to, Even-span, Uneven-span, Ridge-and-furrow, Venlo, Quonset.
- **Six Claddings:** Glass, PE film, Polycarbonate, Acrylic, Shade net, Insect net.
- **Three Control Levels:** Low-tech polyhouse, Medium-tech greenhouse, High-tech phytotron.
- **Cost hierarchy (₹/m²):** Low < 600 < Mid < 1500 < High > 3000.
- **Yield hierarchy vs. open field:** 1.5–2× → 2.5–4× → 5–10×.
- **Subsidy route:** NHB / MIDH / RKVY / State Horticulture Mission — up to **90 %** for SC/ST and women.
- **Kerala context:** Quonset and Uneven-span preferred for hilly districts; mid-tech polyhouses dominant in Wayanad and Palakkad; high-tech Venlo restricted to corporate / export farms.
- **Energy-balance equation:** $Q_{solar,\,in} = Q_{reradiated,\,out} + Q_{convective} + Q_{evapotranspiration}$.
- **Light Transmission Factor:** $\text{LTF} = \dfrac{I_{in}}{I_{out}} \times 100$ % — must be **70 %–90 % PAR** for productive growth.
- **VPD concept:** 0.8 – 1.2 kPa is the optimal vapour-pressure-deficit range for most greenhouse vegetables.
- **CO₂ enrichment:** 800 – 1200 ppm (3× ambient) under closed ventilation.
- **Service life:** Glass 25–30 yr > PC 10–15 yr > Acrylic 15–20 yr > PE film 3–5 yr > Net 5–7 yr.
- **Government schemes to remember:** **NHM (National Horticulture Mission)**, **MIDH (Mission for Integrated Development of Horticulture)**, **PMKSY (Per Drop More Crop)**, and the **Kerala State Horticulture Mission (KSHM)**.
- **Mnemonic for cladding cost order (low → high):** **"Net → Film → Polycarbonate → Acrylic → Glass"** — *"NFPAG"* (Net-Film-PC-Acrylic-Glass).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Greenhouse Design and Construction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT751 - PRECISION FARMING TECHNIQUES FOR PROTECTED CULTIVATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Greenhouse Design and Construction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

A **Greenhouse** is a framed or inflated structure covered with a transparent or translucent material in which **environmental parameters** — temperature, humidity, light intensity, carbon dioxide concentration, and soil moisture — are partially or fully controlled to permit **optimum plant growth** under precisely regulated microclimatic conditions. The engineered discipline of **Greenhouse Design and Construction** integrates the principles of **structural engineering**, **heat and mass transfer**, **material science**, and **horticultural physiology** to deliver a controlled-environment agriculture (CEA) facility that maximises yield per unit area while minimising energy and water footprint.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Definition (Examinable):**
> A greenhouse is essentially a **solar collector**, a **heat storage system**, and a **microclimate moderator** combined into a single enclosed structure. The design must satisfy the **'4-S Principle'**: **Site**, **Structure**, **System**, and **Sustainability**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Living Thermos Flask" Intuition

Imagine a **transparent thermos flask** standing in your garden. The outer shell is glass or plastic (transparent so sunlight enters), and the inner air is the warm "soup" (your plants). Sunlight passes through the cover, is absorbed by the plants and soil, and converted to heat. The transparent cover traps this heat (the **Greenhouse Effect**), just like a thermos keeps your coffee hot. But unlike a thermos, a greenhouse must also let you:
- **Open windows** (ventilation to dump excess heat)
- **Add water sprinklers** (humidification/misting)
- **Switch on a heater** (during cold nights)
- **Stretch a shade net** (to reduce summer heat)

In short, a greenhouse is a **"smart thermos"** that traps solar energy while letting the farmer dial in the climate.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded for Recall)

- **Solar Constant (Iₛc) = 1367 W/m²** (at the top of the atmosphere)
- **Earth's Albedo ≈ 0.30** (30% of solar radiation is reflected)
- **PAR Range (Photosynthetically Active Radiation) = 400–700 nm**
- **Standard Greenhouse Bay Width = 6.0 m, 8.0 m, or 9.6 m** (multi-span)
- **Gutter Height (Eave Height) = 3.0 m to 5.0 m** for low-tech; **5.0 m to 7.0 m** for high-tech Venlo
- **Ridge Height (Total) = 4.5 m to 9.0 m**
- **Wind Load Design Speed (IS 875 / NBC 2016) = 33 m/s (Basic Wind Speed) for Kerala coastal zones; 39 m/s for cyclone-prone zones**
- **Standard Snow Load (where applicable) = 0.75 kN/m² to 1.5 kN/m²**
- **Design Life of Greenhouse = 5 years (low-tech polyhouse) to 25 years (high-tech glasshouse)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (CO₂ Mapping):** This topic maps to **CO₂: Design appropriate greenhouse structures** and **CO₃: Select suitable cladding and climate-control systems** of the PEAGT751 course outcomes under the 2024 KTU scheme.

## 1.4 Classification of Greenhouses (Overview Diagram in §4)

Greenhouses are classified along four axes:

1. **By Shape:** Gable (even-span, uneven-span), Quonset (arched), Sawtooth, Venlo, Tunnel/IGG, Gothic arch.
2. **By Covering:** Glass, Polyethylene (PE film), Polycarbonate (multi-wall), Acrylic, ETFE.
3. **By Environmental Control:** Low-tech (only ventilation), Medium-tech (fans + pad), High-tech (fully computerised).
4. **By Construction:** Permanent (rigid frame), Semi-permanent, Temporary (polytunnel).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Design Logic — The 7 Pillars of Greenhouse Engineering

A KTU-standard greenhouse design follows a sequential, hierarchical decision tree. Each step has a "Why" and a "How."

### Pillar 1 — Site Selection
- **Why?** Wrong site ⇒ poor light, waterlogging, disease, structural failure.
- **How?**
  - **Topography:** Slightly sloping land (1–2% gradient) for natural drainage; avoid valleys (cold-air pools).
  - **Solar Exposure:** At least **6 hours of direct sunlight** in winter, **no shadows** from trees/buildings between 10:00–14:00 hrs.
  - **Water Source:** Continuous, clean water — minimum **5 litres/m²/day**.
  - **Accessibility:** Motorable road within 500 m; near market (reduce post-harvest loss).
  - **Wind Protection:** Avoid windward slopes; use natural windbreak (tree line) **at least 20 m away** (to avoid root/shade competition).
  - **Soil:** Well-drained sandy loam; pH 6.0–7.0; water table **> 1.5 m** below surface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Field Tip:** In Kerala, avoid low-lying paddy-conversion sites — waterlogging during monsoon destroys root zones and promotes *Pythium* and *Phytophthora* root rots.

### Pillar 2 — Orientation
- **Why?** To maximise winter light capture and minimise summer overheating.
- **How?**
  - **North–South ridge orientation** (in tropical India, including Kerala) → better light distribution in summer, less midday glare, uniform crop exposure to morning and afternoon sun.
  - **East–West ridge orientation** → preferred in temperate/higher latitudes for maximum winter solar gain.
  - **Gutter Direction:** For a Venlo multi-span, run the **gutters North–South** so that the sun crosses each bay perpendicularly, equalising illumination.

### Pillar 3 — Structural Frame & Foundation
- **Why?** Must resist wind, snow (where applicable), and dead load of cladding.
- **How?**
  - **Frame Material:** Galvanised Iron (GI) steel pipes (most common in Kerala), aluminium extrusions (high-end), or treated timber.
  - **Pipe Specs (low-cost polyhouse):** **ISI-marked GI Class B pipes**, 25 mm NB (nominal bore) for purlins, 42 mm NB for columns, 60 mm NB for trusses.
  - **Foundation:** 60 cm × 60 cm × 90 cm RCC footing for columns; 30 cm × 30 cm for purlin posts; or screw anchors / earth-anchors for poly-tunnels.
  - **Spacing:** Columns at **3.0 m × 3.0 m** (low-tech) to **4.0 m × 4.0 m** (medium-tech) grids.

### Pillar 4 — Cladding (Glazing) Material Selection
- **Why?** Determines light, heat retention, durability, and cost.
- **How?** Compare the four dominant options:

| Property | Glass (Float) | Polyethylene (UV-stabilised, 200 µm) | Polycarbonate (Twin-wall, 8 mm) | ETFE (Foil) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Light Transmission (PAR)** | **88–92%** | **70–85%** | **78–82%** | **90–95%** |
| **Thermal Conductivity (U-value)** | **5.8 W/m²K** | **~6.0 W/m²K** (single film) | **3.0–3.4 W/m²K** (8 mm twin-wall) | **5.6 W/m²K** |
| **Service Life** | **25+ years** | **3–5 years** (UV-stabilised) | **10–15 years** | **20+ years** |
| **Weight (kg/m²)** | **10–12** | **0.2** | **1.7** | **0.35** |
| **Cost Index (relative)** | **5×** | **1×** | **3×** | **6×** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Kerala Reality Check:** Due to high humidity, heavy monsoon rainfall, and budget constraints of smallholders, **UV-stabilised 200-micron polyethylene film** is the dominant cladding, replaced every 3–5 years. High-tech glasshouses exist only in research stations (KAU, IISR) and corporate farms.

### Pillar 5 — Ventilation & Cooling Design
- **Why?** Plants transpire; solar heat builds up; CO₂ depletes — all must be replenished.
- **How?**
  - **Natural Ventilation:** Roof vents (1.5–2.0 m wide, hinged) and side roll-up vents; opening area ≥ **20% of floor area**.
  - **Forced Ventilation:** Exhaust fans (axial flow, 1 HP–3 HP) on the leeward side.
  - **Evaporative Cooling:** Celdek pad (10 cm thick) on windward + fan on leeward.
  - **Air Change Rate:** $\geq$ **30 air changes per hour** for tropical greenhouses.

### Pillar 6 — Heating (where winter temp < 15°C)
- **Why?** Tropical Kerala rarely needs heating, but poly-houses in Wayanad/Idukki high ranges may need night heating.
- **How?**
  - **Thermal Screens:** Aluminium-foil screens above canopy, deployed at night, save 30–40% heat loss.
  - **Unit Heaters:** Combustion-type or electric; sized at **150–250 W/m²**.

### Pillar 7 — Benching, Flooring & Internal Layout
- **Why?** Uniform cropping, ergonomics, biosecurity.
- **How?**
  - **Flooring:** 5 cm RCC with 1% slope for drainage; 30 cm gravel border for weed control.
  - **Benching:** GI or aluminium benches, 0.9 m wide, 0.6–0.8 m high, with 60 cm walkway.
  - **Drainage:** Gutter-to-soak-pit, dimensioned for **100 mm/hr rainfall** (Kerala monsoon).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examinable Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Unit (SI) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q_s = I \cdot \tau \cdot A$ | Solar heat gain through transparent cover ($I$ = incident radiation, $\tau$ = transmissivity, $A$ = roof area) | W |
| 2 | $Q_c = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | Conductive heat loss through cover/cladding ($U$ = overall heat transfer coeff., $\Delta T$ = indoor–outdoor temp. difference) | W |
| 3 | $Q_v = \rho \cdot c_p \cdot V \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat lost by ventilation ($\rho$ = air density 1.2 kg/m³, $c_p$ = 1005 J/kgK, $V$ = volumetric flow rate) | W |
| 4 | $V = \frac{Q_c}{\rho \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T}$ | Required ventilation flow to maintain temperature differential | m³/s |
| 5 | $N = \frac{3600 \cdot V}{V_{gh}}$ | Air changes per hour ($V_{gh}$ = greenhouse volume) | hr⁻¹ |
| 6 | $q_{cool} = 1.08 \cdot Q_s$ | Approximate peak cooling load in tropical greenhouse (= 1.08 × peak solar gain) | W |
| 7 | $t = \frac{\rho_{film}}{\rho_0} \cdot 100$ | Film thickness conversion: $200 \mu m = 200$ gauge | µm |
| 8 | $L_{col} = 4.5 \cdot h_{col}$ | Minimum column embedment depth for lateral stability | m |
| 9 | $P_w = 0.6 \cdot V_d^2$ | Wind pressure on structure (IS 875 Part 3) | N/m² |
| 10 | $PAR_f = 0.45 \cdot I_{global}$ | Fraction of global solar radiation in PAR band | µmol/m²/s |
| 11 | $E_{pan} = 0.7 \cdot E_{pad}$ | Evaporative cooling efficiency of Celdek pad | — |
| 12 | $D_d = \frac{Q_v \cdot 60}{L \cdot W \cdot N}$ | Vent opening area derivation for natural draft | m² |
| 13 | $\eta_{g} = 1 - \frac{\tau_c^2}{\tau_{inc}}$ | Greenhouse optical efficiency including double reflection | — |
| 14 | $F_{fans} = \frac{V_{req}}{CFM_{fan}}$ | Number of exhaust fans (round-up) | — |
| 15 | $A_{pad} = \frac{V_{req}}{v_{face}}$ | Required pad area ($v_{face}$ = face velocity, 1.0–1.5 m/s) | m² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Exam Note:** In KTU 2024, the **most frequently asked numericals** are on:
> (a) Air-change rate calculation,
> (b) Ventilation fan sizing,
> (c) Evaporative cooling pad area,
> (d) Heat-loss estimation through cover.

> [!WARNING]
> **Always convert units consistently:** Use $W$ for heat, $m^3/s$ for airflow, $K$ or $°C$ for temperature (the *difference* is numerically the same). Never mix CFM with m³/s without conversion: **1 CFM = 0.000472 m³/s**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Greenhouse Heat-Loss Equation (Steady-State Energy Balance)

The standard KTU design problem is: *"Given inside design temperature $T_i$ and outside minimum temperature $T_o$, find the required heating capacity."*

### Step 1 — Define the Energy Balance

For a closed greenhouse at steady state, the **net heat input** equals the **sum of all heat losses**:

$$
Q_{in} = Q_{c,cover} + Q_{c,floor} + Q_{v,ventilation} + Q_{r,infilt} + Q_{s,storage}
$$

### Step 2 — Expand Each Conductive Loss Through Cover

$$
Q_{c,cover} = U_c \cdot A_c \cdot (T_i - T_o)
$$

where:
- $U_c$ = overall heat-transfer coefficient of cladding (W/m²K)
- $A_c$ = total cover surface area (m²)

### Step 3 — Expand the Conductive Loss Through the Floor (or Perimeter)

$$
Q_{c,floor} = U_f \cdot P \cdot (T_i - T_o)
$$

where $P$ = perimeter of the greenhouse (m), and $U_f$ is a perimeter heat-loss coefficient (W/m·K), typically **0.5 to 1.0 W/mK**.

### Step 4 — Expand the Ventilation Heat Loss

The volumetric airflow required for humidity / CO₂ control is $V$ (m³/s). Each unit volume of warm air, when replaced, removes:

$$
Q_{v} = \rho_{air} \cdot c_p \cdot V \cdot (T_i - T_o)
$$

### Step 5 — Substitute Air Density and Specific Heat (Standard Values)

Using $\rho_{air} = 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and $c_p = 1005 \text{ J/kg·K}$:

$$
Q_{v} = (1.2)(1005) \cdot V \cdot \Delta T = 1206 \cdot V \cdot \Delta T \text{ [W]}
$$

### Step 6 — Total Heating Load (Working Equation)

$$
\boxed{Q_{total} = (U_c A_c + U_f P) \cdot \Delta T + 1206 \cdot V \cdot \Delta T}
$$

This is the **KTU formula** students are expected to reproduce. Note the $\vert \Delta T \vert$ should be inferred as a *positive magnitude*.

### Step 7 — Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A $30 \text{ m} \times 8 \text{ m}$ polyethylene greenhouse has $U_c = 6.0 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$, $U_f = 0.7 \text{ W/mK}$, $\Delta T = 10 \text{ K}$, and ventilation rate $V = 0.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$. Find total heat loss.

**Solution:**

Cover area $A_c$ (roof + 2 sidewalls, ignoring gable ends for simplicity) = $2 \cdot (30 \times 4) + 2 \cdot (8 \times 3) = 240 + 48 = 288 \text{ m}^2$.

Perimeter $P = 2(30+8) = 76 \text{ m}$.

Conductive cover loss:
$$
Q_{c,cover} = 6.0 \cdot 288 \cdot 10 = 17{,}280 \text{ W}
$$

Conductive floor loss:
$$
Q_{c,floor} = 0.7 \cdot 76 \cdot 10 = 532 \text{ W}
$$

Ventilation loss:
$$
Q_v = 1206 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 10 = 6030 \text{ W}
$$

Total:
$$
Q_{total} = 17{,}280 + 532 + 6030 = 23{,}842 \text{ W} \approx 23.84 \text{ kW}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This $\sim 24 \text{ kW}$ would require a heater of capacity $24 \text{ kW}$, with a **20% safety margin** ⇒ specify a **30 kW heater** in the bill of materials.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Ventilation Rate for Given Air Changes per Hour (ACH)

**Problem:** Design the ventilation system for a $40 \text{ m} \times 9 \text{ m}$ polyhouse with average height $4 \text{ m}$. Required ACH = 30 (tropical).

### Step 1 — Compute Greenhouse Volume

$$
V_{gh} = L \cdot W \cdot h = 40 \cdot 9 \cdot 4 = 1440 \text{ m}^3
$$

### Step 2 — Apply ACH Definition

$$
N = \frac{3600 \cdot V_{req}}{V_{gh}}
$$

Rearranging for the required volumetric flow:

$$
V_{req} = \frac{N \cdot V_{gh}}{3600} = \frac{30 \cdot 1440}{3600} = 12 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

### Step 3 — Convert to CFM for Fan Selection

$$
V_{CFM} = 12 \cdot \frac{1}{0.000472} \approx 25{,}424 \text{ CFM}
$$

### Step 4 — Fan Sizing

If each fan delivers **5000 CFM** at 0.5 inWC static pressure:

$$
N_{fans} = \frac{25424}{5000} = 5.08 \Rightarrow \boxed{6 \text{ fans (round up)}}
$$

### Step 5 — Pad Area Sizing (Celdek, 10 cm thick, $v_{face} = 1.25$ m/s)

$$
A_{pad} = \frac{V_{req}}{v_{face}} = \frac{12}{1.25} = 9.6 \text{ m}^2
$$

Mount as a single pad **9.6 m wide × 1.0 m tall** along the windward gable.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Light Transmission Through Multi-Layer Cover

### Step 1 — Single-Layer Transmission

For a single layer of glass, with reflectivity $r$ and absorptivity $a$:

$$
\tau_1 = 1 - r - a
$$

For clean glass, $r = 0.04$ and $a = 0.02$, so:

$$
\tau_1 = 1 - 0.04 - 0.02 = 0.94
$$

### Step 2 — Double-Layer Transmission (Air-Gap Insulated)

A second identical layer reflects and transmits again. The total transmission through two parallel layers, ignoring inter-reflections, is:

$$
\tau_2 = \tau_1^2 = 0.94^2 = 0.8836
$$

### Step 3 — With Frame Shadowing Factor ($f_s$)

The structural members cast shadows. For a Venlo with $f_s = 0.10$ (10% shadow):

$$
\tau_{net} = \tau_2 \cdot (1 - f_s) = 0.8836 \cdot 0.90 \approx 0.795
$$

This **79.5% net PAR transmission** is realistic for a Venlo glasshouse.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Design Decision Trade-Offs

| Design Choice | Affects | Trade-Off |
|---|---|---|
| Higher gutter height (5 m vs 3 m) | Better climate buffer, larger air volume | Higher construction cost, greater wind exposure |
| Twin-wall PC instead of PE film | 30% lower heating cost | 3× higher material cost, 2× more condensation drip |
| East-West ridge | Higher winter light (in temperate zones) | Summer overheating in tropics |
| Computerised climate control | ±1°C precision | High capital; needs power backup |
| Insect-proof net (50-mesh) on side vents | Excludes whitefly, thrips | Reduces ventilation by 30–40% → need more fans |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Greenhouse System

The figure below maps the **interactions between structural, environmental, and biological subsystems** in a precision greenhouse — a KTU-favoured block diagram for ESE answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[EXTERNAL CLIMATE] --> B[STRUCTURAL ENVELOPE]
    B --> C[GLAZING COVER]
    B --> D[FRAME AND FOUNDATION]
    B --> E[VENTILATION OPENINGS]

    C --> F[INTERNAL MICROCLIMATE]
    D --> F
    E --> F

    F --> G[TEMPERATURE SENSOR]
    F --> H[HUMIDITY SENSOR]
    F --> I[CO2 SENSOR]
    F --> J[LIGHT SENSOR]

    G --> K[CLIMATE CONTROLLER ECU]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K

    K --> L[ROOF VENT ACTUATOR]
    K --> M[EXHAUST FAN RELAY]
    K --> N[PAD PUMP RELAY]
    K --> O[HEATER RELAY]
    K --> P[SHADE SCREEN MOTOR]

    L --> F
    M --> F
    N --> F
    O --> F
    P --> F

    F --> Q[CROP CANOPY]
    Q --> R[YIELD AND QUALITY]

    S[IRRIGATION CONTROLLER] --> Q
    T[FERTIGATION UNIT] --> Q
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **outer ring (A, B, C, D, E)** represents the **physical structure** that buffers the climate.
- The **inner ring (F, G, H, I, J, K)** is the **sensing-and-control layer**.
- The **actuator ring (L, M, N, O, P)** is the **climate-correction hardware**.
- **Q, R** represent the **crop response**, which feeds back to the agronomist for re-tuning.

## 4.2 Construction Sequence Flowchart

The order of construction is critical — getting it wrong leads to water-pooling, frame distortion, or cladding tears.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA[SITE SURVEY AND SOIL TEST] --> stepB[LAYOUT MARKING]
    stepB --> stepC[FOUNDATION EXCAVATION]
    stepC --> stepD[FOOTING CONCRETING]
    stepD --> stepE[COLUMN ERECTION]
    stepE --> stepF[TRUSS AND PURLIN ASSEMBLY]
    stepF --> stepG[GUTTER AND DRAINAGE INSTALL]
    stepG --> stepH[CLADDING FILM OR GLASS FIXING]
    stepH --> stepI[VENT AND DOOR FRAMES]
    stepI --> stepJ[ELECTRICAL AND PLUMBING]
    stepJ --> stepK[CONTROL SYSTEM HOOKUP]
    stepK --> stepL[COMMISSIONING AND TRIAL RUN]
    stepL --> stepM[PLANTING BEGINS]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Practical Note:** Always lay the **foundation first**, then **cure concrete for at least 7 days** before erecting the frame. Skipping curing introduces micro-cracks that propagate under wind load during the first monsoon.

## 4.3 Greenhouse Cross-Sectional Block Diagram (Venlo Type)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    csA[ROOF VENT 1.5M WIDE] --> csB[GUTTER 2.5M HEIGHT]
    csB --> csC[TRUSS FRAME]
    csC --> csD[CROP CANOPY 0.6 TO 1.8M]
    csD --> csE[FLOOR RAIL]
    csE --> csF[GUTTER 2]
    csG[SIDE VENT ROLL UP] --> csB
    csH[PAD COOLING WALL] --> csI[FAN WALL OPPOSITE END]
```

**Engineering dimensions in the diagram:**
- Width of each span: 9.6 m
- Bay length: 4.0 m
- Gutter height: 5.0 m
- Ridge height: 6.5 m
- Roof pitch: 22°

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2 | 3 Marks]**
>
> **Q1.** List any **six criteria** for **site selection** of a greenhouse in Kerala.
>
> **Model Answer (6 × 0.5 = 3 marks):**
> 1. **Solar exposure** — minimum 6 hours of direct sun in winter, free from shadows of trees/buildings.
> 2. **Topography** — gently sloping land (1–2% gradient) for drainage; avoid valleys prone to cold-air pooling and waterlogging.
> 3. **Water availability** — continuous clean source, minimum 5 litres/m²/day, with pH 6.5–7.5.
> 4. **Soil quality** — well-drained sandy loam, pH 6.0–7.0, water table > 1.5 m below surface.
> 5. **Accessibility** — connected by motorable road, proximity to market (≤ 25 km) to reduce post-harvest loss.
> 6. **Wind protection** — natural windbreak (tree line) at minimum 20 m distance; site not on windward slope.
> 7. (Bonus, half-mark) — Free from industrial pollution, animal-pest corridors, and protected forest zones.

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2 | 3 Marks]**
>
> **Q2.** Compare **glass**, **polyethylene film**, and **twin-wall polycarbonate** as greenhouse cladding materials on the basis of (a) light transmission, (b) service life, and (c) one suitable application.
>
> **Model Answer (1 mark per sub-criterion):**
>
> | Property | Glass | PE Film (200 µm, UV-stab.) | Twin-wall PC (8 mm) |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | (a) **Light transmission (PAR)** | **88–92%** (highest) | **70–85%** (degrades over time) | **78–82%** (constant) |
> | (b) **Service life** | **25+ years** | **3–5 years** | **10–15 years** |
> | (c) **Suitable application** | High-tech Venlo glasshouse (research) | Low-cost polyhouse (smallholder vegetables) | Medium-tech greenhouse (cut flowers) |
>
> The choice is governed by **budget, climate, and crop value**. In Kerala, PE film is preferred for polyhouses; PC for commercial floriculture; glass only for R&D.

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 / ESE Style | 14 Marks]**
>
> **Question A (a)** — 7 Marks
> Explain in detail the **classification of greenhouses** based on shape and construction. Sketch a **labeled cross-section** of a **Quonset-type polyhouse** and a **Gable-even-span greenhouse**, indicating gutter height, ridge height, span width, and orientation.
>
> **Model Answer:**
>
> **1. Classification by Shape (3 marks):**
> - **Gable (even-span):** Two equal-slope roofs meeting at a central ridge. Span typically 6–9 m. Most common in Kerala for vegetables.
> - **Gable (uneven-span):** Two unequal roof slopes, used on sloping land or to maximise southern light in northern hemisphere.
> - **Quonset:** Semi-cylindrical arch made by bending GI pipes; span 4–6 m; cheapest, used for low-cost polyhouses.
> - **Sawtooth:** Asymmetric roof with one side having a vertical vent opening, allows large natural ventilation; preferred in hot humid tropics.
> - **Venlo:** Dutch multi-span glasshouse with multiple peaked roofs separated by gutters; high-tech, used in research and corporate farms.
> - **Gothic arch:** Pointed arch giving better snow shedding and headroom; used in temperate climates.
>
> **2. Classification by Construction (2 marks):**
> - **Permanent:** GI frame with RCC foundation; service life 15–25 years.
> - **Semi-permanent:** Treated-timber or light steel; life 5–10 years.
> - **Temporary (polytunnel):** Single bent-pipe frame, PE film, no foundation; life 3–5 years.
>
> **3. Sketches (2 marks):** Draw both cross-sections (Quonset + Gable) with the following labels:
> - **Quonset:** Span 4 m, height 2.5 m, single PE film, no ridge vent (only end doors), 30 cm pipe embedment.
> - **Gable even-span:** Span 8 m, eave height 3.0 m, ridge height 4.5 m, 30° roof pitch, ridge vent 1 m wide.
>
> ---
>
> **Question A (b)** — 7 Marks
> A polyhouse has dimensions $L = 24$ m, $W = 8$ m, $h_{avg} = 3.5$ m. Desired **air-change rate** is **25 ACH** to maintain a $\Delta T$ of $8$ K. The cover has $U_c = 6.5$ W/m²K. Compute (i) the ventilation volumetric flow rate, (ii) the conductive heat loss through the cover, and (iii) the total heating load if perimeter loss is 0.6 W/mK and ventilation heat factor is 1206 W·s/m³·K.
>
> **Model Answer:**
>
> **(i) Volumetric Flow Rate (3 marks):**
> Volume of greenhouse:
> $$
> V_{gh} = 24 \cdot 8 \cdot 3.5 = 672 \text{ m}^3
> $$
> Required airflow:
> $$
> V_{req} = \frac{N \cdot V_{gh}}{3600} = \frac{25 \cdot 672}{3600} = 4.667 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
> $$
>
> **(ii) Conductive Cover Loss (2 marks):**
> Approximate cover area $A_c = 2(L \cdot W_{roof}) + 2(W \cdot h_{wall})$. Take roof slope giving roof panel length $\approx 4.5$ m. So roof area $= 2 \cdot 24 \cdot 4.5 = 216$ m². Wall area $= 2 \cdot 8 \cdot 3.5 = 56$ m². Total $A_c = 272$ m².
> $$
> Q_{c,cover} = 6.5 \cdot 272 \cdot 8 = 14{,}144 \text{ W} = 14.14 \text{ kW}
> $$
>
> **(iii) Total Heating Load (2 marks):**
> Perimeter $P = 2(24 + 8) = 64$ m.
> $$
> Q_{c,floor} = 0.6 \cdot 64 \cdot 8 = 307.2 \text{ W}
> $$
> Ventilation loss:
> $$
> Q_v = 1206 \cdot 4.667 \cdot 8 = 45{,}026 \text{ W} = 45.03 \text{ kW}
> $$
> Total:
> $$
> Q_{total} = 14.14 + 0.31 + 45.03 \approx 59.48 \text{ kW}
> $$
> Add 20% safety margin → specify a **72 kW heater**.

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 / ESE Style | 14 Marks]**
>
> **Question B (a)** — 7 Marks
> Describe the **design procedure for the foundation, frame, and cladding** of a naturally ventilated polyethylene greenhouse of size $40 \text{ m} \times 9.6 \text{ m}$ for a smallholder in Thrissur, Kerala. Include specifications of GI pipes, footing sizes, and film thickness.
>
> **Model Answer:**
>
> **1. Foundation (2 marks):**
> - Mark layout at $3 \text{ m} \times 4 \text{ m}$ grid (10 columns along length, 3 across width).
> - Excavate **60 cm × 60 cm × 90 cm** pits for each column; for side posts (sidewall verticals), use **30 cm × 30 cm × 60 cm** pits.
> - Place 12 mm dia. MS reinforcement cage (4 bars of 12 mm Ø with 8 mm Ø stirrups at 15 cm c/c).
> - Pour **M20 concrete** (1 : 1.5 : 3); cure 7 days.
> - Insert **60 mm NB GI Class B pipe columns** of 3.6 m length, with **45 cm embedded** in concrete.
>
> **2. Frame (2 marks):**
> - Truss: fabricated from **42 mm NB GI pipe** with welded gusset plates; spacing 3.0 m.
> - Purlins: **25 mm NB GI pipe**, 2 per side, running longitudinally.
> - Cross-bracing: **20 mm solid rod** in X-pattern on gable ends and every 4th bay.
> - All joints: **hot-dip galvanised** (IS 2629); bolts **8.8 grade**, zinc-plated.
> - Anti-corrosion: 2 coats of red-oxide primer + 2 coats of aluminium paint.
>
> **3. Cladding (2 marks):**
> - Cover: **UV-stabilised 200-micron (800-gauge) polyethylene film**, transparent with anti-drip (AD) and anti-dust (AF) additives.
> - Fixing: **aluminium profile + 1.2 mm spring wire** to lock film to purlins (avoid staple gun — punctures the film).
> - Joints: **double-layer with 5 cm overlap**, hot-air welded (not stitched).
> - Side walls: same film, with **roll-up side ventilation** using 1.5 kW gear motor and 25 mm SS pipe shaft.
> - Roof vents: 1.5 m wide, hinged at ridge, operated by **rack-and-pinion 0.5 HP** motor.
>
> **4. Specifications Summary (1 mark):**
>
> | Item | Spec |
> |---|---|
> | Column pipe | 60 mm NB GI B-class, 3.6 m |
> | Truss pipe | 42 mm NB GI B-class |
> | Purlin pipe | 25 mm NB GI B-class |
> | Foundation | 60 × 60 × 90 cm RCC M20 |
> | Cladding film | 200 µm UV-stab. PE with AD + AF |
> | Ridge orientation | North–South (gutter N–S) |
> | Gutter height | 3.0 m; ridge height 4.5 m |
>
> ---
>
> **Question B (b)** — 7 Marks
> An evaporative cooling system is to be designed for a $28 \text{ m} \times 8 \text{ m}$ greenhouse with average height $3.8$ m, located in Thrissur. Design conditions: outdoor dry-bulb $= 38$°C, wet-bulb $= 26$°C, desired indoor dry-bulb $= 30$°C. Required air-change rate $= 35$ ACH. Fan face velocity for Celdek pad is $1.2$ m/s. Calculate the **pad area, fan capacity, and the expected wet-bulb efficiency**.
>
> **Model Answer:**
>
> **1. Greenhouse Volume and Airflow (2 marks):**
> $$
> V_{gh} = 28 \cdot 8 \cdot 3.8 = 851.2 \text{ m}^3
> $$
> $$
> V_{req} = \frac{35 \cdot 851.2}{3600} = 8.28 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
> $$
>
> **2. Pad Area (2 marks):**
> $$
> A_{pad} = \frac{V_{req}}{v_{face}} = \frac{8.28}{1.2} = 6.9 \text{ m}^2
> $$
> Mount as a single pad **7.0 m wide × 1.0 m tall** along the windward gable wall.
>
> **3. Fan Capacity (1 mark):**
> Convert to CFM:
> $$
> V_{CFM} = 8.28 \cdot 2118.88 = 17{,}544 \text{ CFM}
> $$
> If each fan delivers 4500 CFM at 0.4 inWC, need:
> $$
> N_{fans} = \frac{17544}{4500} = 3.9 \Rightarrow 4 \text{ fans (round up)}
> $$
> Mount on the leeward gable, 1 m above floor, with louvred shutters.
>
> **4. Wet-Bulb Efficiency (2 marks):**
> Cooling achieved = $T_o - T_i = 38 - 30 = 8$ K. Wet-bulb depression = $38 - 26 = 12$ K.
> $$
> E_{pad} = \frac{T_o - T_i}{T_o - T_{wb}} = \frac{8}{12} = 0.667 = 66.7\%
> $$
> This is **below the typical Celdek target of 80–85%**, indicating the design may need a thicker pad (15 cm) or lower face velocity. The student should comment on optimisation: increase pad thickness to **15 cm** and lower $v_{face}$ to 0.9 m/s for **80% efficiency**.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Greenhouse Design Problems (PEAGT751 ESE):**
>
> 1. **Unit Mismatch (–2 to –3 marks):** Converting CFM ↔ m³/s incorrectly. **Remember: 1 CFM = 0.000472 m³/s**, or equivalently 1 m³/s = 2118.88 CFM. Always write the conversion factor explicitly.
>
> 2. **Forgetting Ventilation Heat Loss (–3 marks):** Students often compute only the cover (conductive) loss and ignore $Q_v = 1206 \cdot V \cdot \Delta T$. In tropical greenhouses, **ventilation loss is typically the LARGEST heat-loss term** (often 60–80% of total). Marking scheme explicitly tests this.
>
> 3. **Sign Convention on $\Delta T$ (–1 mark):** Always use $\Delta T = \vert T_{in} - T_{out} \vert$ as a *positive magnitude*; otherwise $Q$ comes out negative and confuses the heating/cooling decision.
>
> 4. **Skipping Safety Margin (–1 mark):** Engineering design ALWAYS adds a **20% safety margin** on heating/cooling load. The number "round up" is not optional.
>
> 5. **Confusing Pad Area with Pad Height (–1 mark):** Pad area is in m²; you must specify width × height. A $6.9$ m² pad can be $6.9 \times 1.0$ m OR $3.45 \times 2.0$ m; the choice is dictated by **wall geometry** (usually 1 m high for a single-tier).
>
> 6. **North–South vs East–West Orientation in Kerala (–2 marks):** For **tropical Kerala** (low latitude), the answer is **North–South ridge** with **gutters running N–S**. Students who blindly copy temperate-zone answers (E–W) lose marks. The KTU key is **N–S orientation, gutters N–S**.
>
> 7. **Rounding Errors in Fan/Heater Sizing (–1 mark):** Always **round UP** the count of fans/heaters (you can't install 3.9 fans). 4 is the correct answer; 3 is wrong and loses a mark.
>
> 8. **Omitting Schematic (–1 to –2 marks):** ESE answer to a "design" question WITHOUT a labeled sketch is penalised. Always include at least a **block diagram / cross-section** with dimensions.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** A greenhouse is a *transparent enclosure* that converts solar energy into controlled microclimate for crop production.
- **4-S Principle:** Site, Structure, System, Sustainability.
- **Site Selection:** Sun, slope, soil, water, accessibility, windbreak.
- **Orientation in Kerala:** **N–S ridge, gutters N–S** (NOT E–W).
- **Gutter height:** 3.0 m (low-tech) to 5.0 m (high-tech); Ridge height 4.5 m to 6.5 m.
- **Span width:** 6.0 / 8.0 / 9.6 m for multi-span; 4 m for Quonset.
- **Frame:** 60 mm NB column, 42 mm truss, 25 mm purlin (GI Class B, IS 1239).
- **Foundation:** 60 × 60 × 90 cm, M20 RCC, 7-day cure.
- **Cladding:** 200 µm UV-stab. PE film (life 3–5 yr); 8 mm twin-wall PC (10–15 yr); 4 mm float glass (25+ yr).
- **Ventilation:** ACH ≥ 30 in tropics; vent area ≥ 20% of floor area; fan face velocity 0.4–0.6 inWC static.
- **Cooling pad:** Celdek 10 cm thick, $v_{face} = 1.0$–$1.5$ m/s, target efficiency 80–85%.
- **Heating load formula:** $Q_{total} = (U_c A_c + U_f P) \cdot \Delta T + 1206 \cdot V \cdot \Delta T$.
- **Ventilation flow formula:** $V_{req} = N \cdot V_{gh} / 3600$ (m³/s).
- **Pad area formula:** $A_{pad} = V_{req} / v_{face}$ (m²).
- **Fan count formula:** $N_{fans} = V_{CFM} / CFM_{fan}$ (round UP).
- **Light transmission:** Glass 90%, PE 80%, PC 80%, net after frame shading ≈ 70–80%.
- **Wind load (Kerala):** 33 m/s basic wind speed; 39 m/s cyclone zones.
- **Design life:** 5 yr (low-tech) to 25 yr (high-tech).
- **Safety factor:** Always add **20% margin** on heating/cooling load.
- **Unit conversions:** 1 CFM = 0.000472 m³/s; 1 m/s wind ≈ 0.6 N/m² pressure.
- **Common crops in Kerala polyhouses:** Capsicum, tomato, cucumber, gerbera, carnation, orchid.
- **Schematic must accompany design answers** — labelled cross-sections earn full marks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End-of-Module Mantra for KTU 2024:** *"Design = Load estimation + Component selection + Safety margin + Labelled diagram."* Memorise this four-part checklist before entering the exam hall.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Greenhouse Heating System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT751 - PRECISION FARMING TECHNIQUES FOR PROTECTED CULTIVATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Greenhouse Heating System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Greenhouse Heating System** is an integrated, climate-controlled engineering assembly comprising heat generation units (boilers, heat exchangers, solar collectors), heat distribution networks (perforated polyethylene tubes, finned pipes, fan-coil units, radiant heaters), and automated control instrumentation (thermostats, sensors, actuators) designed to maintain the inside air and root-zone temperature of a protected cultivation structure above the ambient environmental temperature, thereby sustaining optimal physiological activity for crop growth during periods of low solar radiation or low ambient temperature.

### Key Terminology Glossary (KTU 2024 Aligned)

| Term | Formal Definition |
|---|---|
| **Set-point Temperature** | The target inside air temperature programmed into the climate controller (typically **18 °C – 24 °C** for most vegetables). |
| **U-value** | Overall heat transfer coefficient expressing heat flow per unit area per unit temperature difference; unit: **W·m⁻²·K⁻¹**. |
| **Degree Day (DD)** | Cumulative heating requirement indicator — sum of (set-point − outside temperature) for each day. |
| **Heat Load** | Total rate of heat (in **Watts** or **kW**) that must be supplied to compensate all losses. |
| **Boiler Efficiency (η)** | Ratio of useful heat output to fuel energy input; typically **70 % – 85 %** for modern boilers. |
| **ACH** | Air Changes per Hour — measure of greenhouse air-tightness; modern houses aim for **0.5 – 1.0 ACH**. |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human-Body Analogy

Imagine a glass-roofed greenhouse as a **human body in a cold environment**:

- The **glass/polyethylene cover** behaves like the **skin** — it loses heat to the surroundings (you feel cold wind, the plant feels cold air).
- The **heater + boiler** is the **digestive system** — it converts fuel (food) into usable warmth.
- The **distribution pipes (polyethylene tubes, finned pipes)** are the **blood vessels** — they carry warmth to every part.
- The **thermostat + sensors** are the **hypothalamus (brain's thermostat)** — constantly sensing and commanding the system.
- The **thermal screen** is the **woolen sweater** you wear over your skin to reduce heat loss.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Just as a cold person shivers (uncontrolled heat generation), a poorly designed greenhouse may rely on "passive" solar gains that are insufficient during night-time or winter — leading to **crop stress, condensation, and disease outbreaks (Botrytis cinerea)**.

## 1.3 Why is Heating Essential in a Greenhouse?

| Reason | Engineering Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Temperature Optimum** | Most protected-cultivation crops (tomato, capsicum, cucumber, rose) require a **day temperature of 22–28 °C** and **night temperature of 16–18 °C**. |
| **Humidity Control** | Heating reduces **relative humidity**, preventing fungal diseases. |
| **CO₂ Enrichment Synergy** | Heated greenhouses can support **enriched CO₂ enrichment up to 1000–1500 ppm**, accelerating photosynthesis. |
| **Year-round Production** | Enables off-season cropping — central goal of protected cultivation. |
| **Root-Zone Warming** | Bottom heating improves nutrient uptake efficiency by **20 %–30 %**. |

---

## 1.4 Modes of Heat Transfer in a Greenhouse

Heat flows through the greenhouse envelope by three mechanisms, all of which must be quantified in a heat-load calculation.

### (a) Conduction (Transmission)
Heat loss through the **solid envelope** (glass, polyethylene film, polycarbonate). Governed by Fourier's Law:

$$
Q_{cond} \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta T}{R} \;=\; U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T
$$

### (b) Convection
Heat loss due to **air infiltration** (cold outside air leaking in through cracks/doors) and **ventilation** (controlled air exchange). Driven by wind pressure and stack effect.

$$
Q_{conv} \;=\; \rho \cdot C_p \cdot V \cdot n \cdot \Delta T
$$

### (c) Radiation
Heat loss from internal surfaces (plants, soil, structure) radiating toward the cold sky through the transparent cover, especially significant at **clear night skies** — described by the Stefan–Boltzmann equation:

$$
Q_{rad} \;=\; \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot A \cdot \left(T_{surface}^4 - T_{sky}^4\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **net energy balance** of a greenhouse is: **Q_solar + Q_heater = Q_transmission + Q_infiltration + Q_ventilation + Q_radiation + Q_transpiration**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of Total Heat Load

The total instantaneous heating requirement of a greenhouse is the algebraic sum of all heat losses (transient mode, with no solar gain — i.e., worst-case design condition at **night-time in mid-winter**).

$$
Q_{total} \;=\; Q_{transmission} \;+\; Q_{infiltration} \;+\; Q_{ventilation} \;+\; Q_{radiation\ sky} \;-\; Q_{solar\ gain} \;-\; Q_{internal\ sources}
$$

### 2.1.1 Transmission Heat Loss

Heat conducted through every envelope element.

$$
Q_{transmission} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} U_i \cdot A_i \cdot \Delta T
$$

For a multi-span greenhouse with **roof, side walls, end walls, and gable**:

$$
Q_{trans} \;=\; (U_g A_g + U_r A_r + U_s A_s + U_f A_f) \cdot (T_i - T_o)
$$

### 2.1.2 Infiltration Heat Loss

$$
Q_{infil} \;=\; 0.33 \cdot n \cdot V \cdot \Delta T \quad [\text{W}]
$$

where the constant **0.33 W·hr·m⁻³·K⁻¹** accounts for the volumetric heat capacity of air (ρ·Cp ≈ 1200 J·m⁻³·K⁻¹, divided by 3600 s/hr and a typical ACH factor).

| Construction Quality | ACH (Air Changes/hr) |
|---|---|
| New, tight glasshouse | **0.5 – 1.0** |
| Average film plastic | **1.0 – 2.0** |
| Old, leaky film plastic | **2.0 – 4.0** |

### 2.1.3 Ventilation Heat Loss

During the heating season, ventilation is often **zero or minimal**, but for humidity control, an intentional ventilation rate (V_v in m³/s) is needed:

$$
Q_{vent} \;=\; \rho \cdot C_p \cdot \dot{V}_v \cdot (T_i - T_o)
$$

### 2.1.4 Solar Gain Contribution (Useful, offsets heating load)

$$
Q_{solar} \;=\; \tau \cdot I \cdot A_{floor}
$$

where τ = cover transmissivity (typically **0.5 – 0.7** for glass), I = instantaneous solar irradiance (W/m²), A_floor = greenhouse floor area.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All key equations required for KTU 2024 ESE / Internal Assessment — Module 2 (Greenhouse Heating)**

| # | Equation | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | U: W·m⁻²·K⁻¹; A: m²; ΔT: K | Transmission heat loss through cover |
| 2 | $R = \dfrac{1}{U}$ | R: m²·K·W⁻¹ | Thermal resistance (inverse of U) |
| 3 | $Q_{infil} = 0.33 \, n \, V \, \Delta T$ | n: ACH; V: m³; ΔT: K | Infiltration loss (cold air entry) |
| 4 | $Q_{vent} = \rho \, C_p \, \dot{V} \, \Delta T$ | ρ: 1.2 kg/m³; Cp: 1005 J/kg·K | Ventilation heat loss |
| 5 | $Q_{total} = \sum Q_i - Q_{solar}$ | All in W | Heat-load design calculation |
| 6 | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{Q}{C_p \, \Delta T_{water}}$ | ṁ: kg/s; Cp water: 4186 J/kg·K | Hot-water pipe flow rate |
| 7 | $F = \dfrac{Q_{design}}{\eta \cdot CV}$ | F: kg/hr; CV: kJ/kg | Boiler fuel consumption |
| 8 | $\text{HDD} = \sum (T_{set} - T_{out})^+$ | Degree-days | Annual fuel estimation |
| 9 | $Q_{rad,sky} = \varepsilon \sigma (T_s^4 - T_{sky}^4)$ | ε: emissivity; σ: 5.67×10⁻⁸ W/m²K⁴ | Radiation loss to sky |
| 10 | $\eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{Q_{output}}{Q_{fuel}}$ | Dimensionless (0.70–0.85) | Boiler thermal efficiency |
| 11 | $Q_{steam} = \dot{m} (h_g - h_f)$ | h_g, h_f: enthalpy (kJ/kg) | Steam heating capacity |
| 12 | $T_{sky} = T_{out} - 6$ to $20$ °C | Empirical | Effective sky temperature |

> **Memory Trick:** **"U·A·ΔT"** is the most-tested formula — U = conductance, A = area, ΔT = driving force. If you remember only ONE equation, remember this.

---

## 2.3 Why Heating-System Design Matters in Precision Farming

In **precision farming**, heating is not merely "warming the air." It is a **zone-specific, sensor-driven, energy-optimized process**:

1. **Energy economy** — heating constitutes **40 %–70 %** of total operational energy in a greenhouse.
2. **Crop uniformity** — vertical and horizontal temperature gradients must remain within **±2 °C** for predictable growth.
3. **Disease suppression** — heating + dehumidification reduces fungicide application by up to **50 %**.
4. **Root-zone heating** — independent of air temperature — allows **substrate-specific temperature control** (NFT, perlite, cocopeat).
5. **CO₂ enrichment synergy** — combustion-based CO₂ generators provide **simultaneous heat + CO₂**, doubling as a heating source.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Heating-system principles learned here are directly transferable to:
- **Cold-storage warehouses** (post-harvest preservation)
- **Animal shelters** (broiler houses, dairy barns)
- **Swimming-pool enclosures**
- **Drying chambers** for medicinal plants and grains
- **Vertical farm HVAC** systems

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Transmission Heat Loss

> **Problem Setup:** A single-span greenhouse is **40 m long, 9.6 m wide, 4.0 m gutter height, 5.5 m ridge height**. The roof and side walls are single glass (U_g = 6.5 W·m⁻²·K⁻¹), the end walls are insulated (U_e = 0.6 W·m⁻²·K⁻¹). Inside design temperature T_i = 21 °C; outside T_o = 3 °C. Determine the transmission heat loss.

### Step 1 — Compute the Geometric Areas

**Roof area (2 sloped surfaces per span):**

Half-span horizontal = 4.8 m. Ridge rise = 5.5 − 4.0 = 1.5 m.

Slope length = √(4.8² + 1.5²) = √(23.04 + 2.25) = √25.29 ≈ 5.029 m

Two roof planes per span: 2 × 5.029 × 40 = **402.3 m²**

**End walls (triangular gable + two rectangular sides):**

End wall = 2 × (½ × 9.6 × 1.5) + (4.0 × 9.6 × 2) — but typically end walls include both triangles (gable top) and rectangular sides. Computing:

Gable triangle: ½ × 9.6 × 1.5 = 7.2 m² per end × 2 ends = 14.4 m²

Rectangular part (assumed full-width): 9.6 × 4.0 = 38.4 m² per end × 2 ends = 76.8 m²

Total end walls = 14.4 + 76.8 = **91.2 m²**

**Side walls:**

Each side = 40 × 4.0 = 160 m². Two sides = **320 m²**

### Step 2 — Compute ΔT

$$
\Delta T \;=\; T_i - T_o \;=\; 21 - 3 \;=\; 18 \; \text{K}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Transmission Equation for Each Component

$$
Q_{roof} \;=\; U_g \cdot A_{roof} \cdot \Delta T \;=\; 6.5 \cdot 402.3 \cdot 18
$$

$$
Q_{roof} \;=\; 6.5 \cdot 402.3 \cdot 18 \;=\; 47{,}069 \;\text{W} \;\approx\; 47.07 \;\text{kW}
$$

$$
Q_{side} \;=\; 6.5 \cdot 320 \cdot 18 \;=\; 37{,}440 \;\text{W} \;=\; 37.44 \;\text{kW}
$$

$$
Q_{end} \;=\; 0.6 \cdot 91.2 \cdot 18 \;=\; 985 \;\text{W} \;\approx\; 0.99 \;\text{kW}
$$

### Step 4 — Sum the Transmission Heat Loss

$$
Q_{transmission} \;=\; Q_{roof} + Q_{side} + Q_{end}
$$

$$
Q_{transmission} \;=\; 47{,}069 + 37{,}440 + 985
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_{transmission} \;=\; 85{,}494 \;\text{W} \;\approx\; 85.5 \;\text{kW}}
$$

**Logic Note:** Notice how the glass surfaces (roof + sides) dominate — the insulated end walls contribute only **1.15 %** of the total. This is why energy-efficient designs focus on **double-glazing, low-emissivity coatings, and thermal screens**.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Infiltration Heat Loss

> **Continuation:** The same greenhouse has a volume V = 40 × 9.6 × 4.5 (mean height) = **1728 m³**. The construction is "average film-plastic" type with ACH = **1.5**.

### Step 1 — Identify Volume and ACH

$$
V \;=\; 40 \times 9.6 \times 4.5 \;=\; 1728 \;\text{m}^3
$$

$$
n \;=\; 1.5 \;\text{ACH}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Infiltration Equation

$$
Q_{infil} \;=\; 0.33 \cdot n \cdot V \cdot \Delta T
$$

$$
Q_{infil} \;=\; 0.33 \cdot 1.5 \cdot 1728 \cdot 18
$$

$$
Q_{infil} \;=\; 0.33 \cdot 1.5 \cdot 1728 \cdot 18
$$

Step-by-step:

$$
0.33 \cdot 1.5 \;=\; 0.495
$$

$$
0.495 \cdot 1728 \;=\; 855.36
$$

$$
855.36 \cdot 18 \;=\; 15{,}396.48 \;\text{W}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_{infil} \;\approx\; 15.4 \;\text{kW}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Total Heat Load & Boiler Sizing

### Step 3.3.1 — Sum All Heat Loss Components

Ignoring solar gain (night-time, conservative design):

$$
Q_{total} \;=\; Q_{transmission} + Q_{infil} + Q_{vent}
$$

Assuming no extra ventilation in winter night: Q_vent = 0

$$
Q_{total} \;=\; 85.5 + 15.4 \;=\; 100.9 \;\text{kW}
$$

### Step 3.3.2 — Add 10 % Safety Margin

$$
Q_{design} \;=\; 1.10 \times 100.9 \;=\; 111.0 \;\text{kW}
$$

### Step 3.3.3 — Boiler Sizing

A **120 kW boiler** (next standard size) with efficiency η = **0.85** will be selected.

Fuel consumption rate (using LPG, CV = 50 MJ/kg):

$$
F \;=\; \dfrac{Q_{design}}{\eta \cdot CV} \;=\; \dfrac{111{,}000 \;\text{J/s}}{0.85 \cdot 50 \times 10^6 \;\text{J/kg}}
$$

$$
F \;=\; \dfrac{111{,}000}{42{,}500{,}000} \;=\; 2.612 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{kg/s}
$$

$$
F \;\approx\; 9.4 \;\text{kg/hr of LPG}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy insight:** At 9.4 kg LPG/hr and an LPG price of ₹90/kg (illustrative), the **hourly heating cost** ≈ ₹846/hr. Over a 14-hour winter night → **₹11,844 per night per greenhouse**. This is why **thermal screens and double covers** are economically essential.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Heating System Types

| Criterion | Hot Water | Steam | Hot Air (Forced) | Electric | Solar + Backup |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Carrier temperature** | 60–90 °C | 100–180 °C | 40–70 °C | N/A (direct) | 30–80 °C |
| **Distribution uniformity** | Excellent (5/5) | Excellent (5/5) | Moderate (3/5) | Localized (3/5) | Good (4/5) |
| **Capital cost** | High | Very High | Moderate | Moderate | High |
| **Operating cost** | Low–Moderate | Moderate | Moderate | High (in Kerala) | Lowest |
| **Humidity effect** | Drying | Drying | May saturate if unburned | Neutral | Neutral |
| **CO₂ enrichment** | Possible (flue gas) | Possible | Possible (direct-fired) | No | No |
| **Best suited for** | Large houses >1000 m² | Industrial scale | Small/medium | R&D, propagation | All sizes (eco) |
| **Response time** | Slow (thermal mass) | Fast | Very fast | Instant | Slow |

### Decision Logic for KTU Design Problems

```
IF greenhouse area < 500 m² → choose HOT-AIR unit heater
ELIF area 500–2000 m² → choose HOT-WATER with PE tubes
ELIF area > 2000 m² → choose CENTRAL HOT-WATER or STEAM
ALWAYS include thermal screen for nighttime economy.
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List and briefly explain the three modes of heat transfer occurring through the greenhouse envelope. State the governing equation for each.**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Conduction:** Heat transfer through solid envelope materials (glass, polyethylene film, polycarbonate). Governed by Fourier's Law.

$$
Q_{cond} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T
$$

**(b) Convection:** Heat loss due to movement of air — both infiltration (cold outside air leaking in through cracks) and ventilation (intentional air exchange for humidity control).

$$
Q_{conv} = \rho \cdot C_p \cdot \dot{V} \cdot \Delta T
$$

**(c) Radiation:** Heat loss from internal hot surfaces (crop, soil, structure) radiating toward the cold sky through the transparent cover; described by Stefan–Boltzmann's Law.

$$
Q_{rad} = \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot A \cdot (T_s^4 - T_{sky}^4)
$$

In a greenhouse, all three occur simultaneously and must be summed to estimate the total heat requirement.

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Compare hot-water heating with hot-air (forced-air) heating for greenhouses. Mention any two advantages and one disadvantage of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Hot-Water Heating | Hot-Air Heating |
|---|---|---|
| **Carrier medium** | Water at 60–90 °C | Air at 40–70 °C |
| **Distribution** | Pipes (PE tubes, finned pipes) | Ducts or unit heaters |
| **Advantages** | (i) Uniform temperature distribution; (ii) Low fuel cost; (iii) Lower noise | (i) Low capital cost; (ii) Easy installation; (iii) Fast response |
| **Disadvantage** | High capital cost; slow response due to thermal mass | Temperature stratification; drying of air unevenly; higher fuel cost per kWh |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** Students commonly lose marks by (1) **forgetting to add the safety factor (typically 10 %–15 %)** to the design heat load before sizing the boiler, (2) **confusing U-value (W/m²K) with R-value (m²K/W)** — they are reciprocals!, (3) **omitting the infiltration heat loss** (often the second-largest loss after transmission) — examiners allocate 3 marks specifically for this. Always show U-values with proper units. Use the constant **0.33 W·hr·m⁻³·K⁻¹** correctly in infiltration calculations.

---

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

A gutter-connected greenhouse complex has the following specifications:
- Span width: **8.0 m**, Number of spans: **5**, Gutter length: **48 m**
- Gutter height: **4.0 m**, Ridge height: **5.6 m**
- Roof: **Single glass**, U_g = **6.0 W·m⁻²·K⁻¹**
- Side walls: **Polycarbonate twin-wall**, U_s = **3.6 W·m⁻²·K⁻¹**
- End walls: **Insulated**, U_e = **0.5 W·m⁻²·K⁻¹**
- Inside design temperature T_i = **20 °C**
- Outside design temperature T_o = **5 °C**
- Infiltration rate: ACH = **1.0**
- Boiler efficiency: η = **0.82**, LPG calorific value: CV = **46 MJ/kg**

**Answer the following:**

**(a) Calculate the total transmission heat loss through the roof, side walls, and end walls. [7 Marks]**

**(b) Calculate the infiltration heat loss and determine the boiler fuel consumption rate (kg/hr) after adding 12 % safety margin. [7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

### Solution (a): Transmission Heat Loss

**Step 1 — Compute geometric areas**

Total greenhouse width = 5 × 8.0 = **40 m**

Ridge rise per span = 5.6 − 4.0 = **1.6 m**

Slope length per half-span = √(4.0² + 1.6²) = √(16 + 2.56) = √18.56 = **4.308 m**

**Roof area:**

$$
A_{roof} = 2 \times 4.308 \times 48 \times 5 = 2067.8 \;\text{m}^2
$$

**Side walls area:**

$$
A_{side} = 2 \times 48 \times 4.0 = 384 \;\text{m}^2
$$

**End walls area:**

Each end wall = full gable triangle + rectangular base.

Gable triangle area per end = ½ × 40 × 1.6 = 32 m²

Rectangular base per end = 40 × 4.0 = 160 m²

Total per end wall = 192 m²; both ends = **384 m²**

**Step 2 — Compute ΔT**

$$
\Delta T = 20 - 5 = 15 \;\text{K}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Q for each component**

$$
Q_{roof} = 6.0 \times 2067.8 \times 15 = 186{,}102 \;\text{W} \approx 186.1 \;\text{kW}
$$

$$
Q_{side} = 3.6 \times 384 \times 15 = 20{,}736 \;\text{W} \approx 20.7 \;\text{kW}
$$

$$
Q_{end} = 0.5 \times 384 \times 15 = 2{,}880 \;\text{W} \approx 2.9 \;\text{kW}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum total transmission loss**

$$
\boxed{Q_{transmission} = 186.1 + 20.7 + 2.9 = 209.7 \;\text{kW}}
$$

### Solution (b): Infiltration Loss + Boiler Sizing

**Step 1 — Greenhouse volume**

Mean height = (4.0 + 5.6)/2 = 4.8 m

$$
V = 40 \times 48 \times 4.8 = 9216 \;\text{m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Apply infiltration equation**

$$
Q_{infil} = 0.33 \cdot n \cdot V \cdot \Delta T = 0.33 \times 1.0 \times 9216 \times 15
$$

$$
Q_{infil} = 0.33 \times 1.0 \times 9216 \times 15 = 45{,}619 \;\text{W} \approx 45.6 \;\text{kW}
$$

**Step 3 — Total design heat load (without safety margin)**

$$
Q_{design}^{raw} = Q_{trans} + Q_{infil} = 209.7 + 45.6 = 255.3 \;\text{kW}
$$

**Step 4 — Add 12 % safety margin**

$$
Q_{design} = 1.12 \times 255.3 = 285.9 \;\text{kW}
$$

**Step 5 — Boiler fuel consumption**

$$
F = \dfrac{Q_{design}}{\eta \cdot CV} = \dfrac{285{,}900 \;\text{J/s}}{0.82 \times 46 \times 10^6 \;\text{J/kg}}
$$

$$
F = \dfrac{285{,}900}{37{,}720{,}000} = 7.58 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{kg/s}
$$

Converting to kg/hr:

$$
\boxed{F = 7.58 \times 10^{-3} \times 3600 \approx 27.3 \;\text{kg/hr of LPG}}
$$

---

### Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(Internal Choice alternative to Question A)**

**(a) Explain in detail the components and working principle of a central hot-water heating system for a multi-span greenhouse. Include a labelled schematic description. [7 Marks]**

**(b) A 1000 m² greenhouse has a design heat loss of 80 kW. The hot water distribution uses PE tubes (inlet temp 80 °C, outlet temp 70 °C). Calculate (i) the mass flow rate of water required and (ii) the volume flow rate (litres/min). [7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for Question B:

### Solution (a): Central Hot-Water Heating System — Components & Working

**Components:**

1. **Boiler** — burns fuel (LPG, diesel, biomass) to heat water; thermal efficiency 70 %–85 %.
2. **Primary circulation pump** — moves heated water through the closed loop.
3. **Expansion tank** — accommodates thermal expansion of water; maintains system pressure.
4. **Three-way mixing valve** — blends hot supply water with cooler return water to achieve the desired distribution temperature (typically 50–80 °C).
5. **Distribution header (supply main)** — carries hot water to all greenhouse zones.
6. **Distribution loops** — perforated PE tubes, finned perimeter pipes, root-zone bench pipes.
7. **Return header** — collects cooled water back to the boiler.
8. **Climate controller + sensors** — measures inside/outside temperature, RH, and modulates the mixing valve & boiler output.

**Working Principle:**

Water is heated in the **boiler** to 80–90 °C, pumped through the **supply main**, blended to the required distribution temperature (50–80 °C) by the **mixing valve**, then distributed via the loops. Heat is released into the greenhouse by **conduction from pipe walls** and by **natural convection** of heated air. The cooled water returns to the boiler via the **return header**, completing the closed loop. The **controller** continuously adjusts flow rate and temperature based on sensor input to maintain the set-point inside temperature.

> **Schematic description (text):** Fuel → Boiler → Pump → Expansion tank → Mixing valve → Supply header → Distribution loops (PE tubes/finned pipes/bench pipes) → Greenhouse air/root zone → Return header → Mixing valve → Boiler.

### Solution (b): Water Flow Rate Calculation

**Given:** Q = 80 kW = 80,000 W; T_in = 80 °C; T_out = 70 °C; ΔT_water = 10 °C; C_p = 4186 J/kg·K.

**(i) Mass flow rate:**

$$
\dot{m} = \dfrac{Q}{C_p \cdot \Delta T_{water}} = \dfrac{80{,}000}{4186 \times 10}
$$

$$
\dot{m} = \dfrac{80{,}000}{41{,}860} = 1.911 \;\text{kg/s}
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{m} \approx 1.91 \;\text{kg/s}}
$$

**(ii) Volume flow rate:**

Water density ρ = **1000 kg/m³**

$$
\dot{V} = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho} = \dfrac{1.911}{1000} = 1.911 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

Convert to litres/min:

$$
\dot{V} = 1.911 \times 10^{-3} \times 60{,}000 \;\text{L/min}
$$

$$
\dot{V} = 114.7 \;\text{L/min}
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{V} \approx 115 \;\text{L/min}}
$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 2 — Greenhouse Heating System | One-Page Revision**

- [ ] **Heat load** = sum of **transmission + infiltration + ventilation + radiation** losses minus solar/internal gains.
- [ ] **Transmission formula:** Q = U × A × ΔT — memorize this single line; it solves 60 % of KTU numerical problems.
- [ ] **Infiltration formula:** Q = 0.33 × n × V × ΔT — constant 0.33 has units W·hr·m⁻³·K⁻¹.
- [ ] **Always add a 10 %–15 % safety margin** to the calculated Q before sizing the boiler — KTU examiner specifically checks this.
- [ ] **U-value** = thermal conductance; **R-value** = thermal resistance = 1/U. Never confuse them.
- [ ] **Typical U-values (single glass)** ≈ 6.0–6.7 W/m²K; **(double polyethylene)** ≈ 4.0; **(polycarbonate twin-wall)** ≈ 3.6; **(insulated wall)** ≈ 0.5.
- [ ] **Boiler efficiency** for LPG ≈ **82 %**; for diesel ≈ **80 %**; for biomass ≈ **65 %–75 %**.
- [ ] **Five major heating systems:** (1) Hot water, (2) Steam, (3) Hot air, (4) Electric, (5) Solar/biomass.
- [ ] **Heat distribution:** perforated PE tubes, finned perimeter pipes, fan-coil unit heaters, bench/root-zone pipes.
- [ ] **Degree day concept:** Annual fuel consumption can be quickly estimated from HDD = Σ (T_set − T_out)⁺.
- [ ] **Thermal screens** reduce nighttime heat loss by **30 %–50 %** — always recommended for energy economy.
- [ ] **CO₂ enrichment** is a free co-benefit of combustion heating — combustion of 1 kg LPG yields ≈ 3 kg CO₂.
- [ ] **Decision rule:** small houses → hot-air unit heaters; large houses → central hot-water system with PE tubes.
- [ ] **Hot water flow rate:** ṁ = Q / (C_p × ΔT_water); Cp water = 4186 J/kg·K.
- [ ] **Sky radiation loss** occurs most on **clear nights**; can be mitigated by **IR-absorbing thermal screens**.
- [ ] **Modern greenhouses** aim for **ACH < 1.0** through careful sealing of doors, vents, and overlaps.
- [ ] **Energy conservation hierarchy:** (1) Reduce losses → (2) Recover heat → (3) Use renewable source → (4) Use efficient boiler.

> **Final Advice:** In the KTU 2024 ESE, **always show units** in every step of numerical answers, draw a **labelled schematic** when explaining systems, and explicitly state **assumptions** (e.g., "Assuming no ventilation in winter night," "Solar gain neglected for conservative design"). These habits alone can lift your score by 5–8 marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT751 - PRECISION FARMING TECHNIQUES FOR PROTECTED CULTIVATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Greenhouse Micro-climate

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Greenhouse Micro-climate** refers to the localized set of atmospheric and environmental conditions — **temperature, relative humidity, light intensity, carbon dioxide (CO₂) concentration, air velocity, and vapor pressure deficit (VPD)** — that prevail *inside* a protected cultivation structure (greenhouse/polyhouse), which are decoupled to a measurable extent from the prevailing macro-climatic (outdoor) conditions of the surrounding geography.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, the greenhouse micro-climate is treated as a **dynamic, multi-parameter, non-linear control system** wherein the interior environment is engineered by manipulating the energy and mass exchange across the cladding (covering) material, ventilation apertures, and active heating/cooling systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEAGT751 – Module 2):**
> "Study the various micro-climatic parameters inside a greenhouse — temperature, humidity, light, CO₂, air movement — and their interactive influence on crop growth, with a focus on precision measurement and automated regulation."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are sitting inside a **car parked under direct sunlight** with all windows closed on a hot summer afternoon. The temperature inside the car can shoot up to **60–70 °C**, while outside it may only be 38 °C. The windshield and windows act like the greenhouse **cladding (polyethylene/glass cover)** — they let the short-wave solar radiation in but trap the long-wave infrared re-radiation from the seats and dashboard.

Now imagine that inside this car:
- A small **heater** is switched on (greenhouse heating system),
- A **sprinkler** mists water on your face (humidification/fogging system),
- A **small fan** blows air on your skin (ventilation/air circulation),
- A **CO₂ cartridge** releases gas (CO₂ enrichment), and
- A **thermostat** adjusts the heater based on a sensor reading (precision climate controller).

This engineered, closed-loop bubble of *engineered weather* — that is your **greenhouse micro-climate**. The art of precision farming is to **measure, model, and modulate** every one of these parameters to manufacture the *exact* climate the crop desires, irrespective of outdoor weather.

## 1.3 Key Micro-climatic Parameters at a Glance

| Parameter | Typical Optimum Range (Vegetative Stage) | Sensor Used |
|---|---|---|
| Air Temperature (Day) | **20 – 28 °C** | Thermistor / RTD / PT-100 |
| Air Temperature (Night) | **15 – 18 °C** | Thermistor |
| Relative Humidity (RH) | **60 – 80 %** | Capacitive polymer hygrometer |
| Light (PAR, 400–700 nm) | **200 – 600 µmol·m⁻²·s⁻¹** | Quantum sensor (LI-COR style) |
| CO₂ Concentration | **350 – 1000 ppm** | NDIR (Non-Dispersive Infrared) sensor |
| Vapor Pressure Deficit (VPD) | **0.8 – 1.2 kPa** | Calculated from T & RH |
| Air Velocity | **0.3 – 0.7 m·s⁻¹** | Hot-wire anemometer |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Micro"?** The prefix *micro* is used because the climate inside the greenhouse is **spatially non-uniform**. The temperature 10 cm above the soil, at canopy level, and near the roof can differ by **2–5 °C**, leading to *micro-gradients* that directly influence transpiration, pollination, and disease incidence.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Pillars of Greenhouse Micro-climate

The micro-climate is governed by the simultaneous interaction of the following six parameters. Each parameter is both a **cause** and an **effect** — manipulating one shifts the others.

### Pillar 1 — Air Temperature ($T_a$)
- **Why it matters:** Governs the *rate of photosynthesis*, *respiration*, *enzyme kinetics*, and *transpiration pull*.
- **How it is controlled:** Forced-air heaters, hot-water pipe systems, thermal screens, roof ventilation, evaporative cooling pads, fogging.
- **Thermal Time / Growing Degree Days (GDD):**

$$GDD = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left(\frac{T_{max,i} + T_{min,i}}{2} - T_{base}\right)$$

where $T_{base}$ is the **base temperature** (typically **10 °C** for most vegetable crops).

### Pillar 2 — Relative Humidity ($RH$)
- **Why it matters:** Controls *stomatal opening*, *disease pressure* (fungal/bacterial), and *transpiration rate*.
- **How it is controlled:** Ventilation, heating (lowers RH by raising saturation vapor pressure), fogging/misting (raises RH).
- The fundamental relation between $RH$, actual vapor pressure ($e_a$), and saturation vapor pressure ($e_s(T)$):

$$RH = \frac{e_a}{e_s(T)} \times 100 \;[\%]$$

### Pillar 3 — Solar Radiation & Photosynthetically Active Radiation (PAR)
- **Why it matters:** Light is the *energy source* for photosynthesis; PAR (400–700 nm) is the *biologically active* band.
- **How it is measured:** Pyranometer (total solar, W·m⁻²) and quantum sensor (PAR, µmol·m⁻²·s⁻¹).
- **Conversion factor (KTU favorite):** Full sunlight ≈ **1000 W·m⁻²** ≈ **2000 µmol·m⁻²·s⁻¹** PAR. Hence the ratio:

$$\frac{PAR \;[\mu mol \; m^{-2} s^{-1}]}{Global \; Solar \; [W \; m^{-2}]} \approx 2.0 \; \left[\frac{\mu mol}{J}\right]$$

- **Light Transmission through cladding ($\tau$):**

$$\tau = \frac{I_{in}}{I_{out}}$$

For **single-layer polyethylene**, $\tau \approx 0.75$–$0.85$; for **double-layer inflated PE**, $\tau \approx 0.60$–$0.70$.

### Pillar 4 — Carbon Dioxide ($[CO_2]$)
- **Why it matters:** Substrate for photosynthesis; ambient air is ~**420 ppm**, but enriching to **800–1000 ppm** can boost yields by **20–35 %** in tomatoes, capsicum, cucumbers.
- **How it is controlled:** Pure CO₂ injection from cylinders/liquid tanks, combustion of natural gas (CO₂ generator).
- **Photosynthesis response (Michaelis–Menten form):**

$$P = \frac{P_{max} \cdot [CO_2]}{K_m + [CO_2]}$$

where $K_m \approx 300 \; ppm$ for $C_3$ crops.

### Pillar 5 — Air Velocity / Circulation ($V_a$)
- **Why it matters:** Removes boundary-layer resistance on leaves, prevents *stagnant humid pockets* that breed *Botrytis cinerea* (grey mold), equalizes T and RH.
- **How it is controlled:** Horizontal Air Flow (HAF) fans, vertical convection tubes, roof vent placement.

### Pillar 6 — Vapor Pressure Deficit ($VPD$)
- **Why it matters:** The single best **integrative indicator** of plant water stress. It couples temperature and humidity.
- **How it is calculated:**

$$VPD = e_s(T) \cdot \left(1 - \frac{RH}{100}\right) \; [kPa]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Saturation Vapor Pressure ($e_s(T)$) — Tetens equation (KTU essential):**
> 
> $$e_s(T) = 0.6108 \cdot \exp\left(\frac{17.27 \cdot T}{T + 237.3}\right) \quad [kPa]$$
> 
> where $T$ is in **°C**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Saturation Vapor Pressure | $e_s(T) = 0.6108 \cdot e^{17.27T/(T+237.3)}$ | kPa | 0 – 8 kPa |
| 2 | Actual Vapor Pressure | $e_a = e_s(T) \cdot RH/100$ | kPa | 0 – 6 kPa |
| 3 | Vapor Pressure Deficit | $VPD = e_s(T)(1 - RH/100)$ | kPa | 0.2 – 2.0 kPa |
| 4 | Growing Degree Days | $GDD = (T_{max}+T_{min})/2 - T_{base}$ | °C·day | Crop-specific |
| 5 | Clausius–Clapeyron slope | $\Delta = 4098 \cdot e_s/(T+237.3)^2$ | kPa·°C⁻¹ | 0.1 – 1.5 |
| 6 | Latent heat of vaporization | $\lambda = 2501 - 2.361 \cdot T$ | kJ·kg⁻¹ | ~2450 |
| 7 | Psychrometric constant | $\gamma = 0.000665 \cdot P$ | kPa·°C⁻¹ | ~0.054 at sea level |
| 8 | PAR ↔ Solar ratio | $1 \; W m^{-2} \approx 2 \; \mu mol m^{-2} s^{-1}$ | — | — |
| 9 | Cladding transmissivity | $\tau = I_{in}/I_{out}$ | — | 0.6 – 0.9 |
| 10 | Light Use Efficiency | $LUE = Biomass / PAR_{intercepted}$ | g·MJ⁻¹ | 1 – 4 |

## 2.3 Real-World Utility

In commercial Indian polyhouses (e.g., in Wayanad, Palakkad, Pune, Bengaluru), micro-climate control is the **single largest operating cost** after labor. A **2000 m² polyhouse** for rose or capsicum typically houses:

- **6–8 HAF fans**,
- **2–4 exhaust fans** for roof ventilation,
- **1–2 evaporative cooling pads (celdek/wood-wool)**,
- **A boiler / gas heater** for winter nights,
- **A fogging system (4–6 micron droplets)** for humidification,
- **A CO₂ dosing line** with NDIR sensor feedback,
- **A climate computer** (e.g., Priva, Hortimax, Ridder) running on **±0.5 °C** temperature and **±3 % RH** precision.
