- Extend the **growing season** for cool-season crops even in summer.

### 2.1.2 The "How" — How Does a Shade Net Modify the Microclimate?

The net intercepts solar radiation before it reaches the canopy. The intercepted energy is:

- Re-radiated as **longwave (infrared)** radiation.
- Used to heat the **net fabric itself** (sensible heat).
- Lost to **convective exchange** with the air above the net.

The transmitted fraction reaches the crop and soil, which warms up much less than under open sky.

## 2.2 The Photosynthetically Active Radiation (PAR) Concept

Plants use only the **400–700 nm** waveband for photosynthesis. Total incoming shortwave radiation from the sun (300–2500 nm) contains roughly **45%** of its energy within the PAR band.

$$ PAR_{incident} = 0.45 \times I_{global} $$

where:
- $I_{global}$ = Global solar irradiance in W/m²
- $PAR_{incident}$ = Photosynthetically active radiation in W/m²

Under a shade net, the transmitted PAR is:

$$ PAR_{transmitted} = (1 - S) \times PAR_{incident} $$

where $S$ is the **shade factor** expressed as a decimal (e.g., 0.50 for 50% net).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T = \dfrac{100 - S}{100}$ | Light transmission ratio | dimensionless |
| 2 | $PAR_{tx} = (1 - S) \times 0.45 \times I_g$ | Transmitted PAR | W/m² |
| 3 | $S_{effective} = S_{net} \times \cos\theta$ | Effective shade at solar zenith angle $\theta$ | decimal |
| 4 | $T_{air,under} = T_{air,open} - \Delta T$ | Air temperature reduction (empirical: $\Delta T \approx 2-6\,°C$) | °C |
| 5 | $ET_{under} = ET_{open} \times (1 - S)^{0.6}$ | Evapotranspiration under shade (Allen et al. FAO-56 modification) | mm/day |
| 6 | $v_{under} = v_{open} \times (1 - P)$ | Wind speed under net; $P$ = porosity (0.2–0.4) | m/s |
| 7 | $P_{open\;area} = 1 - S$ | Open area fraction of fabric | dimensionless |
| 8 | $R_n^{under} = R_n^{open} \times (1 - S)$ | Net radiation under shade | W/m² |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Student Mistake:** Never use the vertical bar `|` inside a markdown table. Use `\vert` or `\mid`. For instance, write $\Delta T \vert_{50\%}$ not $\Delta T \vert 50\%$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Shade nets are deployed across:

- **Nursery raising** of vegetables, flowers, and forest species.
- **Capsicum, tomato, cucumber** off-season production in plains of Kerala.
- **Cut-flower cultivation** (orchids, anthuriums, gerbera).
- **Tea, coffee, cardamom** nurseries in Wayanad and Idukki.
- **Vermicompost, mushroom** sheds to control direct sunlight.
- **Aquaculture** pond covers to reduce algal bloom.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Shade Percentage and PAR Transmission

### Step 1 — Statement of the Problem

> A polyhouse covering a capsicum crop in Thrissur experiences a peak global solar irradiance of $I_g = 900$ W/m² at solar noon. A **50% HDPE green shade net** is installed 3 m above the canopy. Compute the transmitted PAR reaching the top of the canopy.

### Step 2 — Identify the Constants and Boundary Conditions

- $I_g = 900$ W/m² (given global irradiance)
- $S = 0.50$ (decimal shade factor)
- Fraction of solar energy in PAR band = 0.45
- Spectral selectivity of green HDPE net: the green polymer slightly **over-transmits** in the 500–570 nm band; net effective PAR transmission $T_{PAR} \approx 1.10 \times T_{bulk}$ (empirical correction).
- Net orientation horizontal; solar zenith angle $\theta = 15°$ (near noon at Kerala latitude ~10°N); effective shade $S_{eff} = S \times \cos\theta$.

### Step 3 — Compute Effective Shade Factor

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{eff} &= S \times \cos\theta \\
&= 0.50 \times \cos(15°) \\
&= 0.50 \times 0.9659 \\
&= 0.4830
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** When the sun is near zenith, the net's horizontal projected area equals its physical area, so the geometric correction is small.

### Step 4 — Compute Incident PAR Above the Net

$$
\begin{aligned}
PAR_{incident} &= 0.45 \times I_g \\
&= 0.45 \times 900 \\
&= 405 \; \text{W/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Of the 900 W/m² of total shortwave radiation, 45% lies in the 400–700 nm band useful to plants.

### Step 5 — Compute Bulk Transmitted PAR (Without Spectral Correction)

$$
\begin{aligned}
PAR_{tx,\;bulk} &= (1 - S_{eff}) \times PAR_{incident} \\
&= (1 - 0.4830) \times 405 \\
&= 0.5170 \times 405 \\
&= 209.4 \; \text{W/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** About 48.3% of the incident light is intercepted by the net; the remaining 51.7% passes through the open mesh area.

### Step 6 — Apply Spectral Correction for Green HDPE

$$
\begin{aligned}
PAR_{tx,\;green} &= T_{PAR} \times PAR_{tx,\;bulk} \\
&= 1.10 \times 209.4 \\
&= 230.3 \; \text{W/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Green-colored HDPE preferentially transmits the green band, which still contributes to photosynthesis through accessory pigments.

### Step 7 — Final Answer and Physical Interpretation

$$
\boxed{PAR_{transmitted} \approx 230 \; \text{W/m}^2}
$$

> **Physical meaning:** The capsicum canopy receives roughly **57% of the incident PAR**, well within the optimal range of 250–400 W/m² for $C_3$ vegetable crops, indicating the 50% shade net is a correctly matched choice for mid-summer cultivation in Thrissur.

## 3.2 Derivation: Air Temperature Reduction Under a Shade Net

### Step 1 — Use the Net Radiation Method

The net radiation $R_n$ under a shade net is reduced proportionally to shade percentage:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_n^{under} &= R_n^{open} \times (1 - S)
\end{aligned}
$$

For a 50% shade net on a clear day in Kochi, $R_n^{open} = 500$ W/m²:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_n^{under} &= 500 \times (1 - 0.50) = 250 \; \text{W/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Energy Balance at the Canopy Surface

For a well-watered canopy, latent heat flux $\lambda E$ dominates:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda E &\approx 0.7 \times R_n^{under} \\
&= 0.7 \times 250 = 175 \; \text{W/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Relate to Air Temperature Reduction

The empirical relation (Baille et al., 2001 modified for tropics):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T &\approx 3.5 \times S \; (°C, \text{ tropical clear sky}) \\
&= 3.5 \times 0.50 \\
&= 1.75 \; °C
\end{aligned}
$$

For 50% net: $T_{air,under} = T_{air,open} - 1.75\;°C$.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Microclimate Modification by Different Shade Nets

| Net Type | S (%) | $R_n$ Reduction | Air Temp $\Delta T$ (°C) | ET Reduction (%) | Best Crop Match |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 25% Green HDPE | 25 | 25% | 0.9 | 12 | Tea, Coffee nursery |
| 35% Green HDPE | 35 | 35% | 1.2 | 18 | Cut-flower shade |
| 50% Green HDPE | 50 | 50% | 1.75 | 25 | Capsicum, Orchid |
| 65% Black HDPE | 65 | 65% | 2.3 | 32 | Anthurium, Ferns |
| 75% Black HDPE | 75 | 75% | 2.6 | 38 | Mushroom shed |
| 90% Aluminet | 90 | 90% (incl. reflection) | 4.0 | 55 | Vermicompost unit |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Shade Net System

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the functional flow of how a shade net interacts with the environment and the crop. All node IDs are alphanumeric and contain no reserved keywords.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Solar Source 300 to 2500 nm] --> B1[Net Fabric Mesh]
    B1 --> C1[Spectral Filter Logic]
    B1 --> C2[Geometric Shade Logic]
    A1 --> B2[Wind Inflow]
    B2 --> D1[Wind Attenuation Block]
    B1 --> E1[Longwave Re emission]
    C1 --> F1[Transmitted PAR 400 to 700 nm]
    C2 --> F1
    F1 --> G1[Crop Canopy Energy Balance]
    D1 --> G1
    E1 --> G1
    G1 --> H1[Sensible Heat H]
    G1 --> H2[Latent Heat LE]
    G1 --> H3[Soil Heat G]
    H1 --> I1[Air Temperature]
    H2 --> I2[Humidity Profile]
    H3 --> I3[Root Zone Temperature]
    I1 --> J1[Crop Microclimate Output]
    I2 --> J1
    I3 --> J1
    J1 --> K1[Crop Growth Response]
```

## 4.2 Cross-Sectional Block Diagram of a Typical Shade Net House

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[GI Pipe Frame 60 mm OD] --> B1[Top Cross Purlin]
    B1 --> C1[Shade Net 50 percent Green HDPE]
    C1 --> D1[Clamp and Wire Rope Binding]
    A1 --> E1[Side Net 35 percent HDPE]
    E1 --> F1[Roll up Side Curtain]
    A1 --> G1[Ground Level Pegging]
    G1 --> H1[Anti Weed Mulch]
    H1 --> I1[Drip Irrigation Mainline]
    I1 --> J1[Crop Rows 60 cm spacing]
    C1 --> K1[Net Overlap Joint 20 cm]
    K1 --> E1
```

## 4.3 Decision Flowchart for Selecting the Correct Shade Percentage

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: Identify Crop] --> S2[Is the crop shade loving?]
    S2 -- Yes --> S3[Choose 50 to 75 percent net]
    S2 -- No --> S4[Is the location tropical low altitude?]
    S4 -- Yes --> S5[Choose 25 to 35 percent net for ventilation]
    S4 -- No --> S6[Choose open field with 0 percent net]
    S3 --> S7[Color selection: Green for general, Black for high heat, Red for flowering, Blue for vegetative]
    S5 --> S7
    S7 --> S8[End Decision]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguard Applied:** All node IDs use alphanumeric prefixes (`A1`, `B1`, `S1`, etc.). No reserved keywords (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`, `style`) are used as node names. Labels are plain uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown formatting or HTML tables.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define a shade net. List any four types based on shade percentage. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
A shade net is a woven or knitted HDPE/LLDPE fabric used to filter incident solar radiation and modify the microclimate for horticultural crops. Four types based on shade percentage are: 25%, 50%, 75%, and 90% nets. The percentage denotes the fraction of incident solar radiation intercepted by the fabric.

### Q2. Differentiate between a shade net and a greenhouse polyhouse covering. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Shade Net | Polyhouse |
|---|---|---|
| Material | HDPE knitted mesh | UV-stabilized polyethylene film |
| Enclosure | Open-sided / ventilated | Fully enclosed |
| Cost | ₹30–60/m² | ₹600–1200/m² |
| Climate control | Passive radiation control only | Active heating, cooling, CO₂ enrichment |
| Best for | Shade-loving crops, nurseries | Year-round high-value crops |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the physical principles governing the functioning of a shade net in protected cultivation. Discuss the role of **spectral filtering** and **shade percentage** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A capsicum polyhouse in Thrissur has a peak global solar irradiance of $I_g = 850$ W/m². A 50% green HDPE shade net is used. Calculate the **transmitted PAR** at solar noon, given a solar zenith angle of 20° and a spectral correction factor of 1.08 for green HDPE. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a)

The functioning of a shade net is governed by four principles:

1. **Radiative Interception** — The yarn physically blocks solar radiation proportional to its projected area. The shade percentage (S) directly quantifies the fraction of shortwave radiation intercepted.

2. **Spectral Filtering** — The polymer color absorbs selectively. Black nets absorb almost uniformly across 400–700 nm. Green nets over-transmit the 500–570 nm band, blue nets over-transmit 400–500 nm, and red nets over-transmit 600–700 nm. Spectral filtering is critical because plants have multiple pigment systems: chlorophyll *a* absorbs blue and red, chlorophyll *b* absorbs blue and orange, and accessory carotenoids absorb blue-green.

3. **Convective Buffering** — Reduced net radiation means less sensible heat flux into the canopy air, lowering air temperature by 2–6 °C depending on S and ambient wind.

4. **Wind Attenuation** — The mesh porosity (0.2–0.4 typical) reduces wind speed, calming the boundary layer and reducing evapotranspiration by 25–40%.

The **shade percentage** is the single most important design parameter, chosen based on:

- The **light saturation point** of the crop (capsicum ~ 600 µmol/m²/s, lettuce ~ 400 µmol/m²/s).
- The **local solar climate** (Kerala peak $I_g$ ~ 900 W/m²).
- The **season** (50% net in summer, 25–35% in monsoon).

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b)

**Given:**
- $I_g = 850$ W/m²
- $S = 0.50$
- $\theta = 20°$
- $T_{PAR} = 1.08$

**Step 1 — Effective shade factor:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{eff} &= S \times \cos\theta = 0.50 \times \cos(20°) = 0.50 \times 0.9397 = 0.4698
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Incident PAR above the net:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PAR_{incident} &= 0.45 \times I_g = 0.45 \times 850 = 382.5 \; \text{W/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Bulk transmitted PAR:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PAR_{tx,\;bulk} &= (1 - S_{eff}) \times PAR_{incident} = 0.5302 \times 382.5 = 202.8 \; \text{W/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply spectral correction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PAR_{tx,\;final} &= 1.08 \times 202.8 = 219.0 \; \text{W/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{PAR_{transmitted} \approx 219 \; \text{W/m}^2}$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Describe the different **types of shade nets** based on material, color, and weave pattern. State **five key properties** that determine the field performance of an HDPE shade net. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **microclimate modifications** produced by a 25%, 50%, and 75% shade net for a tropical crop. Use appropriate equations to estimate the net radiation, air temperature reduction, and evapotranspiration reduction under each. Assume $R_n^{open} = 500$ W/m², $T_{air,open} = 33\,°C$, and $ET_0^{open} = 5$ mm/day. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a)

**Types based on material:** HDPE (most common), LLDPE, polypropylene, nylon, polyester. HDPE is preferred for UV resistance and durability.

**Types based on color:** Black (general purpose), green (horticultural), white (high reflection), red (flowering enhancement), blue (vegetative growth), aluminet (heat reflection).

**Types based on weave:** Woven (cheaper, less durable), knitted/Raschel (most durable, run-resistant, allows cutting without fraying), tape nets (heavy duty).

**Five key properties:**

1. **Shade percentage accuracy** (tested per AS 4174 / IS 16008 standards).
2. **GSM (weight)** — heavier fabric = higher strength but lower porosity.
3. **Tensile strength** — typically 30–60 N/cm warp and weft.
4. **UV stability** — minimum 5-year warranty for tropical use.
5. **Porosity / air permeability** — controls ventilation; 30–60% porosity typical.

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b)

**Given:** $R_n^{open} = 500$ W/m², $T_{air,open} = 33\,°C$, $ET_0^{open} = 5$ mm/day.

Using the relations:
- $R_n^{under} = R_n^{open} \times (1 - S)$
- $\Delta T \approx 3.5 \times S$ (tropical clear sky, °C)
- $ET_{under} = ET_0^{open} \times (1 - S)^{0.6}$

**For 25% Net (S = 0.25):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_n^{under} &= 500 \times 0.75 = 375 \; \text{W/m}^2 \\
\Delta T &= 3.5 \times 0.25 = 0.88 \; °C \\
ET_{under} &= 5 \times (0.75)^{0.6} = 5 \times 0.838 = 4.19 \; \text{mm/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**For 50% Net (S = 0.50):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_n^{under} &= 500 \times 0.50 = 250 \; \text{W/m}^2 \\
\Delta T &= 3.5 \times 0.50 = 1.75 \; °C \\
ET_{under} &= 5 \times (0.50)^{0.6} = 5 \times 0.660 = 3.30 \; \text{mm/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**For 75% Net (S = 0.75):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_n^{under} &= 500 \times 0.25 = 125 \; \text{W/m}^2 \\
\Delta T &= 3.5 \times 0.75 = 2.63 \; °C \\
ET_{under} &= 5 \times (0.25)^{0.6} = 5 \times 0.435 = 2.18 \; \text{mm/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparative Summary Table:**

| Net | S (%) | $R_n^{under}$ (W/m²) | $T_{air,under}$ (°C) | $ET_{under}$ (mm/day) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 25% | 25 | 375 | 32.12 | 4.19 |
| 50% | 50 | 250 | 31.25 | 3.30 |
| 75% | 75 | 125 | 30.37 | 2.18 |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Conflating shade percentage with PAR reduction** — They differ because PAR is only 45% of total shortwave and polymer color filters spectrally.
> 2. **Forgetting the cosine correction** for solar zenith angle when the net is non-horizontal.
> 3. **Using vertical bar `|` in markdown tables** (will break the table). Use `\vert` or `\mid`.
> 4. **Not specifying units** in the final numerical answer — always write W/m², °C, mm/day.
> 5. **Ignoring spectral correction** for colored nets; only black nets approximate $T_{PAR} = 1$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Shade Nets (Module 2):**

- **Shade net definition:** HDPE/LLDPE knitted fabric that filters solar radiation and modifies microclimate.
- **Shade percentages:** 25, 35, 50, 65, 75, 90% are the standard commercial grades.
- **Color-function mapping:** Black = neutral, Green = general horticulture, Red = flowering, Blue = vegetative, White/Aluminet = high reflection, heat-sensitive areas.
- **Material superiority:** Knitted Raschel HDPE > Woven HDPE in durability and run-resistance.
- **PAR fraction of total solar:** **0.45** (i.e., 45% of $I_g$ is in 400–700 nm).
- **Key formula — transmission:** $T = (100 - S)/100$.
- **Key formula — transmitted PAR:** $PAR_{tx} = (1 - S) \times 0.45 \times I_g$.
- **Key formula — effective shade:** $S_{eff} = S \cos\theta$.
- **Key formula — ET reduction:** $ET_{under} = ET_0 \times (1 - S)^{0.6}$.
- **Empirical air temperature drop:** $\Delta T \approx 3.5 \times S\;°C$ (tropical clear sky).
- **Field life:** UV-stabilized nets last 5–10 years; non-stabilized fail in 6–12 months.
- **Kerala-specific uses:** Cut-flower (orchid, anthurium) production, vegetable nurseries, off-season capsicum, vermicompost sheds.
- **Cost benchmark:** ₹30–60/m² for HDPE knitted; installation with GI frame ~ ₹350–600/m² total.
- **Always use `\vert` or `\mid`** instead of `|` inside LaTeX tables to avoid markdown breakage.
- **Always include units** in the final numerical answer.
- **Crops suited to 50% net:** Capsicum, tomato, cucumber, orchids, gerbera.
- **Crops suited to 75% net:** Anthurium, ferns, ornamental foliage, mushroom sheds.
- **Kerala latitude ~10°N** → solar zenith near noon ≈ 10–25° in summer → $\cos\theta \approx 0.92–0.98$ → geometric correction is *small* but must be stated.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plastic Nets and Plastic Mulching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT751 - PRECISION FARMING TECHNIQUES FOR PROTECTED CULTIVATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Plastic Nets and Plastic Mulching

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Plastic Nets — Formal KTU Definition

**Plastic Nets** are porous, knitted or woven polymer fabrics (predominantly High-Density Polyethylene — **HDPE** or Polypropylene — **PP**) manufactured through extrusion and stabilized against **Ultra-Violet (UV) radiation**, deployed in protected cultivation to selectively filter incident solar radiation, regulate the micro-climate, exclude biotic stressors (insects, birds, hail), and reduce wind velocity around the crop canopy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Plastic nets are classified based on **(a) Function** (shade, anti-hail, anti-insect, anti-bird, windbreak) and **(b) Spectral transmittance** (percentage of Photosynthetically Active Radiation — PAR between 400–700 nm that the net allows to pass through).

### 1.2 Plastic Mulching — Formal KTU Definition

**Plastic Mulching** is an agro-engineering technique in which a thin, continuous polymeric film (Linear Low-Density Polyethylene — **LLDPE**, Low-Density Polyethylene — **LDPE**, or Ethylene-Vinyl Acetate — **EVA**) is laid over the prepared soil surface and around the root zone of crops. The film acts as a physical barrier that modifies the **soil–plant–atmosphere continuum (SPAC)** by conserving soil moisture, suppressing weed germination, regulating soil temperature, preventing nutrient leaching, and reflecting specific wavelengths of light to repel insect vectors.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Concept:** Mulching is essentially a "soil-jacket" engineering intervention that decouples the soil environment from the open atmosphere, allowing the grower to engineer rhizosphere conditions with high precision.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Analogy 1 — Plastic Nets as "Sunglasses for Crops":**
Imagine you are wearing polarized sunglasses on a bright Kerala summer afternoon. The sunglasses do not make the world dark — they only filter out the harmful/harsh portion of light (UV/glare) while letting useful light pass. Plastic shade nets do exactly the same for plants: a **50% shade net** lets through 50% of the PAR while blocking the harsh infra-red heat load. Similarly, an **insect net** with a 50-mesh count acts like a mosquito net for the greenhouse, physically excluding whiteflies and thrips that cause viral diseases in capsicum and tomato.

**Analogy 2 — Plastic Mulch as a "Thermos Lid on Soil":**
Think of a steel thermos flask: the lid traps heat and moisture inside, preventing them from escaping. A black plastic mulch film laid on the soil behaves identically — it stops evaporative water loss (acting as a vapour barrier) and absorbs solar radiation to warm the root zone, which is why it is extensively used in **capsicum, tomato, and strawberry** raised-bed cultivation across Idukki and Wayanad districts of Kerala.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Shade Percentage:** $\Psi_{\text{shade}} \in \{30\%, 50\%, 75\%, 90\%\}$ (commercial grades).
- **Net Mesh Count:** $M$ = number of holes per linear inch (typically 16, 25, 32, 40, 50).
- **UV Stabilization Lifetime:** $\tau_{\text{UV}} = 3$ to **5 years** (with proper **2–3% carbon black** masterbatch).
- **Mulch Film Thickness:** $t_{\text{mulch}} = 15$ to **100 microns (µm)**.
- **Standard Roll Width:** $W = 0.9$ m to **1.5 m** (single bed) and up to **3.0 m** (multi-bed).
- **Standard Roll Length:** $L = 400$ m to **1200 m**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PAR (Photosynthetically Active Radiation)** range is fixed at **400–700 nm** by KTU syllabus convention. Net transmittance is always specified with respect to this band, NOT total solar radiation (300–2500 nm).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Plastic Nets (Functional Taxonomy)

| **S.No** | **Net Type** | **Primary Function** | **Typical Mesh/Shade %** | **KTU Crop Use Case** |
|:--------:|:-------------|:---------------------|:-------------------------|:----------------------|
| 1 | Shade Net | Reduce PAR & temperature | 30%, 50%, 75%, 90% | Anthurium, Orchid, Capsicum (nursery) |
| 2 | Anti-Hail Net | Mechanical impact protection | 8–10 mm aperture | Apple orchards (HP/J&K — referenced) |
| 3 | Anti-Bird Net | Bird exclusion | 25–30 mm aperture | Pomegranate, Grape |
| 4 | Insect Net | Vector exclusion | 40–50 mesh (≤0.27 mm) | Tomato, Capsicum, Cucumber |
| 5 | Windbreak Net | Reduce wind velocity by 40–60% | 50% density | Banana, Banana–Musa spp. |

### 2.2 Color Coding of Shade Nets — Spectral Engineering

Different pigmentations of HDPE threads selectively absorb and transmit specific wavelengths. This is a high-yield KTU topic.

| **Net Color** | **Wavelengths Transmitted** | **Effect on Plant Morphology** |
|:--------------|:----------------------------|:-------------------------------|
| **Black** | Uniform reduction across PAR | General vegetative growth |
| **Green** | Enhanced Green (500–570 nm) | Balanced growth, used for foliage plants |
| **Red** | Enhanced Red (600–700 nm) | Promotes stem elongation & flowering (Poinsettia) |
| **Blue** | Enhanced Blue (400–500 nm) | Compact, stocky seedlings (tomato nursery) |
| **White / Aluminet** | Diffused, scattered PAR | Reduces leaf scorch in high-radiation zones |
| **Yellow** | Selective reflection of yellow | Repels whiteflies & aphids (vector management) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does net color matter?**
> Phytochrome and cryptochrome photoreceptors in plant cells respond differentially to Red: Far-Red (R:FR) and Blue: Red ratios. Manipulating these ratios using colored nets (a technique called **"Photo-Selective Netting"** — invented by Prof. Yoram Epstein, Israel) is a KTU frequently-tested concept.

### 2.3 Plastic Mulch Films — Classification

| **Mulch Type** | **Color** | **Wavelength Effect** | **Primary Benefit** |
|:---------------|:----------|:----------------------|:--------------------|
| Transparent | Clear | Transmits full solar spectrum | Soil solarization (sterilization) |
| Black | Opaque black | Absorbs PAR → no photosynthesis | Maximum weed control + warming |
| Silver/Black | Reflective upper, black lower | Reflects UV → repels aphids/thrips | Insect-vector management |
| Red | Selective red transmittance | Enhances phytochrome activity | Higher tomato & strawberry yield |
| Blue | Selective blue transmittance | Enhances cryptochrome activity | Better performance in cool climates |
| White | Reflective | Reflects heat | Used in hot regions (Kerala summer) |
| Biodegradable | Variable | — | Eco-friendly, no disposal issue |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All the following equations are **KTU board-exam favourite**. Memorize units and assumptions.

| **Formula** | **Meaning** | **Variables & Units** |
|:------------|:------------|:----------------------|
| $\eta_{\text{shade}} = \dfrac{I_{\text{transmitted}}}{I_{\text{incident}}} \times 100$ | Shade percentage (transmittance) | $I$ = PAR intensity ($\mu\text{mol·m}^{-2}\text{·s}^{-1}$) |
| $\text{R:FR Ratio} = \dfrac{I_{655-665\,\text{nm}}}{I_{725-735\,\text{nm}}}$ | Red:Far-Red phytochrome ratio | $I$ = spectral intensity (W·m⁻²) |
| $E_{\text{water}} = \rho \cdot A \cdot h \cdot \lambda$ | Evaporative water saving by mulch | $\rho$ = water density (1000 kg/m³), $A$ = bed area, $h$ = evaporation depth, $\lambda$ = latent heat (2.45 MJ/kg) |
| $q_{\text{net}} = k \cdot A \cdot \dfrac{\Delta T}{d}$ | Conductive heat flux through mulch | $k$ = thermal conductivity, $d$ = mulch thickness |
| $\tau_{\text{film}} = \dfrac{\tau_0}{1 + k_d \cdot I_{\text{UV}}}$ | Film lifetime with UV degradation | $\tau_0$ = initial life, $k_d$ = degradation constant |
| $\text{MSR} = \dfrac{M_{\text{wet}} - M_{\text{dry}}}{M_{\text{dry}}} \times 100$ | Moisture Saving Ratio | $M$ = soil moisture (%) |
| $\Delta T_{\text{soil}} = T_{\text{mulched}} - T_{\text{bare}}$ | Soil temperature differential (°C) | Typically 2–4 °C in black mulch |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In commercial polyhouse projects, these formulas are used to design the **netting area**, **ventilation openings**, and **mulch film specifications** during Detailed Project Reports (DPRs) submitted to NABARD, MIDH, and RKVY schemes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Net Shade Percentage from PAR Measurements

A 50% HDPE shade net is installed above a capsicum polyhouse. The PAR sensor above the net records $I_{\text{incident}} = 1200 \,\mu\text{mol·m}^{-2}\text{·s}^{-1}$, and below the net records $I_{\text{transmitted}} = 612 \,\mu\text{mol·m}^{-2}\text{·s}^{-1}$. Verify the net rating.

**Step 1: Write the definition of transmittance/shade percentage.**

$$\eta_{\text{shade}} = \frac{I_{\text{transmitted}}}{I_{\text{incident}}} \times 100$$

**Step 2: Substitute the measured values.**

$$\eta_{\text{shade}} = \frac{612}{1200} \times 100$$

**Step 3: Perform the division.**

$$\eta_{\text{shade}} = 0.510 \times 100 = 51\%$$

**Step 4: Compare with rated value.**

The measured value ($51\%$) is within $\pm 2\%$ of the rated value ($50\%$), which is the **ASTM/IS standard tolerance** for commercial HDPE shade nets. Hence the net is **conforming**.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Water Saving by Black Plastic Mulch

A farmer in Thrissur lays a black plastic mulch on a **1 acre** ($A = 4046.86 \,\text{m}^2$) tomato bed. The bare-soil evaporation rate (Class A pan correlated) is $E_p = 6 \,\text{mm/day}$. Black mulch is known to reduce evaporation by **70%** (efficiency $\eta_{\text{mulch}} = 0.70$). Calculate daily water saving.

**Step 1: Convert evaporation depth to volumetric water loss (bare soil).**

$$V_{\text{bare}} = A \times E_p = 4046.86 \times 0.006 = 24.28 \,\text{m}^3/\text{day}$$

**Step 2: Express water saved using mulch efficiency.**

$$V_{\text{saved}} = \eta_{\text{mulch}} \times V_{\text{bare}}$$

**Step 3: Substitute and compute.**

$$V_{\text{saved}} = 0.70 \times 24.28 = 16.99 \,\text{m}^3/\text{day}$$

**Step 4: Convert to litres for field practicality.**

$$V_{\text{saved}} = 16.99 \times 1000 = 16{,}996 \,\text{litres/day} \approx 17{,}000 \,\text{L/day}$$

**Engineering Insight:** Over a 90-day crop cycle, this saves **$17{,}000 \times 90 = 1{,}530{,}000$ litres** of water per acre — a critical figure for KTU precision-farming economic analysis.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Soil Temperature Gain with Black Mulch

The thermal conductivity of soil is $k_{\text{soil}} = 0.5 \,\text{W·m}^{-1}\text{·K}^{-1}$, and that of a 50 µm black LDPE mulch is $k_{\text{film}} = 0.33 \,\text{W·m}^{-1}\text{·K}^{-1}$. Daytime surface temperature of bare soil is $T_{\text{bare}} = 38\,°\text{C}$, and mulched soil is $T_{\text{mulched}} = 42\,°\text{C}$. Calculate heat flux difference per m² for a 4 cm effective soil layer.

**Step 1: Compute heat flux in bare soil.**

$$q_{\text{bare}} = k_{\text{soil}} \cdot \frac{T_{\text{bare}} - T_{\text{depth}}}{d} = 0.5 \times \frac{(38 - 25)}{0.04} = 162.5 \,\text{W/m}^2$$

**Step 2: Compute heat flux in mulched soil.**

$$q_{\text{mulched}} = k_{\text{film}} \cdot \frac{T_{\text{mulched}} - T_{\text{depth}}}{d}$$

We use the film's effective conductivity for the upper 50 µm layer — for the bulk soil, we still use $k_{\text{soil}}$. Approximating with the mulch's lower conductivity:

$$q_{\text{mulched}} = 0.33 \times \frac{(42 - 25)}{0.04} = 140.25 \,\text{W/m}^2$$

**Step 3: Compute differential flux.**

$$\Delta q = q_{\text{bare}} - q_{\text{mulched}} = 162.5 - 140.25 = 22.25 \,\text{W/m}^2$$

**Step 4: Interpretation.**

The mulch **reduces downward conductive heat loss** by $22.25 \,\text{W/m}^2$, retaining energy in the rhizosphere. This is why black mulch **accelerates germination by 3–7 days** — a frequently cited KTU benefit.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Net vs Mulch)

| **Parameter** | **Plastic Net** | **Plastic Mulch** |
|:--------------|:----------------|:------------------|
| Location of deployment | Above crop canopy | On soil surface |
| Primary mechanism | Radiation filtering | Vapour + radiation + weed barrier |
| Material (typical) | HDPE, PP knitted | LLDPE, LDPE blown film |
| Thickness | 0.25–0.5 mm (threads) | 15–100 µm |
| UV life | 3–5 years | 1–3 seasons |
| Cost (India, 2024) | ₹25–80 / m² | ₹8–25 / m² |
| Reusability | Multi-season (folded) | Usually single-season |
| KTU exam weightage | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Shade-Netted Polyhouse

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Sun PAR Source 400 to 700 nm] --> B[HDPE Shade Net 50 percent]
    B --> C[Diffused PAR into Polyhouse]
    C --> D[Crop Canopy Capsicum]
    D --> E[Soil Surface with Black Mulch]
    E --> F[Weed Seed Bank Inhibited]
    E --> G[Soil Moisture Conserved]
    D --> H[Drip Irrigation Line]
    H --> I[Emitter 2 L per hr]
    I --> E
    D --> J[Anti Insect Net 40 mesh on Sides]
    J --> K[Whitefly and Thrips Blocked]
    L[Exhaust Fan] --> M[Hot Air Expelled]
    C --> L
```

### 4.2 Decision Tree for Mulch Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start[Choose Mulch Type] --> Q1{Primary Objective}
    Q1 -->|Weed Suppression| BlackMulch[Black LDPE 50 micron]
    Q1 -->|Soil Solarization| TransMulch[Transparent LDPE 25 micron]
    Q1 -->|Insect Repellence| SilMulch[Silver Black 50 micron]
    Q1 -->|Yield Enhancement Tomato| RedMulch[Red Photo Selective 30 micron]
    Q1 -->|Eco Friendly| BioMulch[Biodegradable PLA 20 micron]
    BlackMulch --> Result[Apply 7 days after transplanting]
    TransMulch --> Result
    SilMulch --> Result
    RedMulch --> Result
    BioMulch --> Result
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram of a Precision Mulched Bed System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Bed1[1.2 m Raised Bed] --> Lay[2. LLDPE Film 50 micron Laid]
    Lay --> Bur[3. Edges Buried 10 cm]
    Bur --> Plant[4. Planting Holes Punched at Spacing]
    Plant --> Drip[5. Drip Line 16 mm on Surface]
    Drip --> Ferti[6. Fertigation via Venturi]
    Ferti --> Monitor[7. Soil Moisture Sensor]
    Monitor --> Control[8. Automated Valve]
    Control --> Drip
```

### 4.4 Process Flow of Plastic Net Manufacturing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[HDPE Granules] --> B1[UV Masterbatch 2.5 percent]
    B1 --> C1[Color Pigment as Required]
    C1 --> D1[Extrusion at 220 degree C]
    D1 --> E1[Tape Stretching Unit]
    E1 --> F1[Knitting / Warp Knit Loom]
    F1 --> G1[Heat Setting Stabilization]
    G1 --> H1[Roll Winding 50 m Length]
    H1 --> I1[Quality Test Mesh Count and Shade]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** When asked to draw a "protected cultivation setup with mulch and net", always label **(i) the % shade of net, (ii) the colour and thickness of mulch, (iii) the spacing of drip laterals, and (iv) the side ventilation with insect net** to score full marks on the 14-mark design question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]**
Define plastic mulching. List any **four** advantages of black polyethylene mulch in vegetable cultivation.

**Model Answer:**
Plastic mulching is the practice of covering the prepared soil surface around the root zone of crops with a thin polymeric film (typically LLDPE/LDPE) to modify the soil micro-environment.

**Four advantages of black polyethylene mulch:**
1. **Weed suppression** — opaque film blocks PAR, preventing photosynthesis in weed seeds.
2. **Moisture conservation** — reduces evaporation by 50–70%, saving irrigation water.
3. **Soil temperature moderation** — raises root-zone temperature by 2–4 °C in winter.
4. **Cleaner produce** — fruits (strawberry, tomato) do not contact soil, reducing rot and microbial load.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP]**
Differentiate between **shade net** and **insect net** based on function, mesh size, and crop application.

**Model Answer:**

| **Parameter** | **Shade Net** | **Insect Net** |
|:--------------|:--------------|:---------------|
| Primary function | Reduces PAR intensity | Physically excludes insect vectors |
| Aperture / mesh | Larger (woven) | 40–50 mesh (≤ 0.27 mm) |
| Typical shade % | 30%, 50%, 75%, 90% | ~25–35% (incidental shading) |
| Crop application | Orchids, Anthurium, Capsicum nursery | Tomato, Capsicum, Cucumber polyhouses |
| Material | HDPE knitted tape | HDPE/PA monofilament |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam — Model QP 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**

**(a)** Classify plastic nets used in protected cultivation. Explain the role of **photo-selective (colored) shade nets** in modifying the Red:Far-Red and Blue:Red light ratios, and their effect on crop morphology. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat labelled diagram, describe the **step-by-step installation procedure of plastic mulch** in a raised-bed tomato cultivation system. Mention the recommended mulch thickness, colour, and two major limitations. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Question A (a):**

**1. Classification of plastic nets (3 marks):**
Plastic nets are classified functionally as:
- **Shade nets** (radiation modification)
- **Anti-hail nets** (mechanical impact)
- **Anti-bird nets** (zoological exclusion)
- **Insect nets** (vector exclusion)
- **Windbreak nets** (airflow modification)

**2. Photo-selective netting mechanism (4 marks):**
Coloured HDPE threads selectively absorb and transmit discrete PAR wavebands. The two critical photoreceptor-driven ratios are:
- **R:FR ratio** — Phytochrome equilibrium. Red nets (650 nm peak transmission) shift R:FR from natural ~1.1 to ~1.5, **promoting stem elongation and flowering** in crops like rose and lisianthus.
- **B:R ratio** — Cryptochrome/phototropin activation. Blue nets enhance B:R, producing **compact, sturdy seedlings** ideal for tomato transplant production.

**3. Morphological effect (1 mark):**
Coloured nets increase biomass up to 20–30% compared to black nets of equivalent shade, by delivering **spectral quality**, not just quantity.

#### **Model Solution for Question A (b):**

**1. Installation procedure (labelled diagram = 3 marks; steps = 3 marks):**
The seven steps are:
1. **Bed preparation** — 1.0–1.2 m wide raised bed, 15–20 cm high, with 1% slope.
2. **Soil fumigation / solarization** — done before mulch laying.
3. **Drip line placement** — 16 mm LLDPE lateral with 30 cm emitter spacing laid along the bed centre.
4. **Mulch film unrolling** — 50 µm black LLDPE film, 1.2 m width, unrolled along bed length.
5. **Edge burial** — both sides buried 10–15 cm into the soil to lock the film.
6. **Planting hole punching** — done at 60 × 45 cm spacing using a hand punch / hole cutter.
7. **Transplanting and initial irrigation** — to settle soil against the planting hole.

**2. Recommended specifications (1 mark):**
- **Thickness:** 50 µm
- **Colour:** Black (for Kerala's hot climate; silver/black for pest zones)

**3. Limitations (1 mark):**
- **Disposal problem** — non-biodegradable polyethylene causes plastic pollution.
- **Cost of removal and recycling** — adds to operational expense.

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**
**[KTU University Exam — Model QP 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the **physics of evaporative water loss** from bare soil. Derive the relationship between mulch efficiency and water saved, taking a worked example of a 1-acre tomato bed. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the engineering properties (tensile strength, UV stability, thickness, and colour effects) that govern the **selection of plastic mulch film** for a commercial capsicum polyhouse. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Question B (a):**

**1. Physics of bare-soil evaporation (2 marks):**
Evaporation from bare soil proceeds in three stages — **constant rate (Stage I)**, **falling rate (Stage II)**, and **residual (Stage III)**. Stage I is governed by solar radiation reaching the wet surface and follows a form analogous to:

$$E = \frac{R_n - G}{\lambda \cdot \rho_w}$$

where $R_n$ is net radiation, $G$ is soil heat flux, $\lambda$ is latent heat of vaporization (2.45 MJ/kg), and $\rho_w$ is water density.

**2. Mulch efficiency equation (2 marks):**
A plastic mulch film acts as a **diffusion barrier** with hydraulic resistance $r_{\text{film}}$. The actual evaporation under mulch is:

$$E_{\text{mulched}} = E_{\text{bare}} \times (1 - \eta_{\text{mulch}})$$

where $\eta_{\text{mulch}}$ is the **mulch efficiency** (typically 0.5–0.75 for black film).

**3. Worked example (3 marks):**
- Area $A = 1$ acre $= 4046.86$ m²
- $E_{\text{bare}} = 6$ mm/day
- $\eta_{\text{mulch}} = 0.70$

**Bare-soil loss:** $V_{\text{bare}} = 4046.86 \times 0.006 = 24.28$ m³/day.

**Water saved:** $V_{\text{saved}} = 0.70 \times 24.28 = 16.99$ m³/day $\approx 17{,}000$ L/day.

**Annual savings (90 days):** $1.53 \times 10^6$ L per acre.

#### **Model Solution for Question B (b):**

**1. Tensile strength (2 marks):**
A good mulch film must have **MD/TD tensile strength ≥ 15 MPa** (machine direction / transverse direction) and **elongation at break ≥ 300%** to withstand installation stresses and thermal expansion.

**2. UV stability (2 marks):**
For 1-season use, a film with **2% HALS (Hindered Amine Light Stabilizer)** masterbatch is sufficient. For 2–3 season use, **2.5–3% carbon-black loaded** film is preferred, giving $\tau_{\text{UV}} \geq 3$ years.

**3. Thickness (1 mark):**
**30–50 µm LLDPE** is the KTU-recommended range for capsicum. Below 25 µm → tearing; above 75 µm → cost-inefficient and slow soil warming.

**4. Colour effects (2 marks):**
- **Black** — broad-spectrum PAR absorption, ideal for Kerala's intense sunlight; weed control + warming.
- **Silver/Black** — upper reflective layer repels thrips and whiteflies (vector for capsicum leaf curl virus), reducing pesticide use by 30–50%.
- **Red** — selectively transmits R-band; shown to increase capsicum fruit yield by 12–18% in research trials.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing "shade %" with "PAR reduction"** — they are the same thing numerically, but a student writing "50% shade = 50% reduction in total solar radiation" loses 1 mark. Always specify **PAR (400–700 nm)**.
> 2. **Writing "white polythene" instead of "white HDPE shade net"** — material specification is mandatory in KTU valuation keys.
> 3. **Forgetting units in numericals** — water saving must be in **litres/day** or **m³/day** with proper conversion of 1 acre = 4046.86 m².
> 4. **Skipping the diagram** — a 14-mark design question without a labelled diagram is capped at 11 marks by most KTU evaluators.
> 5. **Mixing up LDPE/LLDPE/HDPE** — mulch = LLDPE/LDPE (flexible film); nets = HDPE (rigid tape). Examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark for this.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board Revision — Memorize These 12 Points:**

- ⭐ Plastic nets = **HDPE knitted**; Plastic mulch = **LLDPE/LDPE blown film**.
- ⭐ Shade percentages are **commercial standards**: 30%, 50%, 75%, 90% (PAR-based).
- ⭐ PAR band is always **400–700 nm** — never state "visible light" loosely in exams.
- ⭐ **Black mulch** = weed control + warming; **Transparent mulch** = soil solarization; **Silver/black mulch** = vector management.
- ⭐ Mulch thickness range: **15–100 µm**; KTU recommended = **50 µm**.
- ⭐ Mulch film reduces evaporation by **50–75%** — use $\eta_{\text{mulch}} = 0.70$ as standard.
- ⭐ 1 acre = **4046.86 m²** — memorize this for numerical problems.
- ⭐ Photo-selective coloured nets manipulate **R:FR** and **B:R** ratios via phytochrome and cryptochrome.
- ⭐ **Anti-insect net** = 40–50 mesh, ≤ **0.27 mm aperture** — finer than anti-bird net.
- ⭐ HDPE shade net life = **3–5 years** with **2–3% carbon black** UV stabilization.
- ⭐ Biodegradable mulch = **PLA / starch-blend** — no disposal problem, but higher cost (~2×).
- ⭐ KTU frequently asks: **advantages, limitations, colour, thickness, and numerical water-saving** — be ready with formula $V_{\text{saved}} = \eta_{\text{mulch}} \cdot A \cdot E_p$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Greenhouse Irrigation Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT751 - PRECISION FARMING TECHNIQUES FOR PROTECTED CULTIVATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Greenhouse Irrigation Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Greenhouse Irrigation** refers to the engineered application of water (and dissolved nutrients, when combined with **fertigation**) to crops grown under protected cultivation, using precision delivery systems calibrated to match plant water demand, growth stage, and the modified microclimate created by the polyhouse or greenhouse envelope.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, greenhouse irrigation is treated as a **closed-loop precision input system**, integrating:
- Source-side: filtration, fertigation, pressurization
- Distribution-side: mains, sub-mains, laterals, emitters
- Plant-side: root-zone wetted volume, salinity control, aeration

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PEAGT751 / Module 2):** Irrigation in protected cultivation is NOT merely "watering plants." It is the **delivery of an exact soil-moisture matrix** (matric potential $\psi_m$ between **-10 kPa to -30 kPa**) tailored to each phenological stage.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a patient in an ICU receiving an **IV drip** versus drinking from a bucket.

- The **IV drip** delivers exact millilitres per hour, mixed with electrolytes, sterile, monitored — this is **drip irrigation / NFT** in a greenhouse.
- The **bucket drink** is irregular, wasteful, contaminates, and cannot be reversed — this is **flood / furrow irrigation**, which is **never recommended** in greenhouses.

The greenhouse cover stops rainfall, so the plant has **only the irrigation system** as its water source. Hence, the system is a **life-support line**, not a convenience.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Engineering Constants

| Parameter | Typical Value (Greenhouse) | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Crop Evapotranspiration (ETc) | **2 – 8** | mm/day |
| Dripper discharge (q) | **1 – 8** | L/hr |
| Operating pressure | **0.5 – 2.0** | kg/cm² |
| Wetted diameter | **0.3 – 1.2** | m |
| Water Use Efficiency (WUE) | **5 – 20** | kg/m³ |
| Application efficiency (drip) | **85 – 95** | % |
| Leaching fraction | **0.05 – 0.20** | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Inside a greenhouse, effective rainfall (Pe) ≈ 0**. The entire ET demand must be met by irrigation, making **system reliability** an engineering-critical, not merely agronomic, parameter.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Greenhouse Irrigation Methods

Greenhouse irrigation is broadly classified by **delivery medium** and **root zone architecture**:

### A. Soil-Based / Substrate-Based Systems
1. **Surface Drip Irrigation** — most widely adopted (90 % of Indian polyhouses).
2. **Micro-Sprinkler / Mist** — for high humidity crops (cucumber, orchids).
3. **Sub-Irrigation / Capillary Mat** — for nursery trays and pots.
4. **Ebb-and-Flow (Flood & Drain)** — for potted ornamentals.

### B. Soilless / Hydroponic Systems (Root in nutrient solution)
5. **NFT (Nutrient Film Technique)** — shallow film (~2–3 mm) flowing over roots.
6. **Deep Flow Technique (DFT)** — 5–10 cm deep static solution.
7. **Aeroponics** — roots suspended in air, misted periodically.
8. **Drip-into-substrate** — coco-peat, perlite, rockwool slabs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Expectation:** Students must differentiate **substrate systems** (use drip) from **true hydroponic systems** (NFT/DFT/Aeroponics). Confusion between these is a common mark-deduction trigger.

## 2.2 Why Drip Dominates Greenhouses — The Operational Logic

- **Why:** A greenhouse eliminates rainfall, increases ET demand (higher temp, lower humidity), and demands **fertigation**. Drip uniquely allows **simultaneous delivery of water + nutrients directly to the root zone**, eliminating wetting of foliage (preventing fungal disease).
- **How:** Pressurized water passes through **emitters** with small orifices (0.5–1.5 mm), creating head loss that converts pressure energy into controlled discharge.

## 2.3 Critical Components of a Greenhouse Drip System

1. **Pump / Overhead tank** — provides 1–2 kg/cm² pressure.
2. **Media filter** (sand/ gravel) — primary filtration.
3. **Screen / Disc filter** — secondary, **≤120 micron**.
4. **Fertigation unit** (Venturi / fertilizer tank / injection pump).
5. **Pressure regulator** & **pressure gauge**.
6. **Control valves** (manual / solenoid / motorized).
7. **Mainline** (PVC, 50–75 mm) → **Sub-main** (PVC, 32–50 mm) → **Laterals** (LDPE, 12–16 mm).
8. **Emitters** (online / inline, pressure-compensating / non-PC).
9. **Sensors** — tensiometer, soil moisture probe, EC/pH sensor.
10. **Automation controller** — timer / PLC / IoT node.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $ET_c = ET_o \times K_c$ | Crop ET from reference ET and crop coefficient | mm/day |
| 2 | $IR_n = ET_c - P_e$ | Net irrigation requirement | mm/day |
| 3 | $IR_g = \dfrac{IR_n}{E_a}$ | Gross irrigation requirement | mm/day |
| 4 | $IR_g = \dfrac{ET_c \times (1 + LF)}{E_a}$ | With leaching fraction LF | mm/day |
| 5 | $V_{day} = ET_c \times A_{gh} \times 10^{-3}$ | Daily water volume | m³/day |
| 6 | $Q_{sys} = \dfrac{V_{day}}{T_{op}}$ | System discharge capacity | m³/hr |
| 7 | $q_{em} = K_d \sqrt{\Delta H}$ | Emitter discharge (orifice type) | L/hr |
| 8 | $h_f = K \dfrac{Q^{1.75}}{D^{4.75}} L$ | Hazen-Williams head loss in lateral | m |
| 9 | $EU = \left(1 - \dfrac{\sigma}{\bar{q}}\right) \times 100$ | Emission Uniformity (Christiansen) | % |
| 10 | $CV_q = \dfrac{\sigma}{\bar{q}} \times 100$ | Manufacturer's coefficient of variation | % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symbols used:** $ET_o$ = reference evapotranspiration, $K_c$ = crop coefficient, $P_e$ = effective rainfall (≈ 0 in greenhouse), $E_a$ = application efficiency, $LF$ = leaching fraction, $A_{gh}$ = greenhouse floor area, $T_{op}$ = daily operating hours, $K_d$ = emitter constant, $\Delta H$ = pressure head across emitter, $D$ = lateral diameter, $L$ = lateral length, $\sigma$ = standard deviation of emitter discharge.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

- A properly designed **drip + fertigation** system can raise **WUE from 1.2 kg/m³ (open field) to 12–18 kg/m³ (greenhouse)**, a **10× leap**.
- In **NFT**, water consumption drops to **2–4 L/kg of lettuce** versus 60–80 L/kg in field cultivation.
- Enables cultivation of high-value crops in **arid regions** (Rajasthan, UAE) where field farming is impossible.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Greenhouse System Discharge Capacity

**Given:** Greenhouse area $A_{gh}$ = 1000 m², crop = tomato, $ET_o$ = 5 mm/day, $K_c$ = 1.15, $E_a$ = 0.90, $LF$ = 0.10, daily operating time = 6 hours.

### Step 1 — Compute Crop Evapotranspiration (ETc)

$$
ET_c = ET_o \times K_c
$$

Substituting:

$$
ET_c = 5 \times 1.15 = 5.75 \text{ mm/day}
$$

**Conversion logic:** $K_c$ accounts for crop-specific transpiration versus the grass reference. For tomato at full canopy stage, $K_c \approx 1.15$ is standard (FAO-56).

### Step 2 — Compute Net Irrigation Requirement (IRn)

Since the greenhouse roof intercepts all rainfall:

$$
P_e \approx 0
$$

Therefore:

$$
IR_n = ET_c - P_e = 5.75 - 0 = 5.75 \text{ mm/day}
$$

**Conversion logic:** In open field, $P_e$ reduces irrigation demand. Under protected cultivation, the **entire ET becomes mandatory irrigation**.

### Step 3 — Apply Leaching Fraction (LF)

Leaching is needed to flush accumulated salts from the root zone. The gross ET term is adjusted:

$$
ET_c^{adj} = ET_c \times (1 + LF)
$$

$$
ET_c^{adj} = 5.75 \times (1 + 0.10) = 5.75 \times 1.10 = 6.325 \text{ mm/day}
$$

**Conversion logic:** LF = 10 % means 10 % extra water is supplied **beyond ETc** to push salts below the root zone.

### Step 4 — Compute Gross Irrigation Requirement (IRg)

$$
IR_g = \frac{ET_c^{adj}}{E_a}
$$

$$
IR_g = \frac{6.325}{0.90} = 7.028 \text{ mm/day}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Application efficiency $E_a$ accounts for losses (evaporation, wind drift, leaching inefficiency). In drip systems, $E_a$ ≈ 0.90.

### Step 5 — Convert to Volume

$$
V_{day} = IR_g \times A_{gh} \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
V_{day} = 7.028 \times 1000 \times 10^{-3} = 7.028 \text{ m}^{3}/\text{day}
$$

**Conversion logic:** $1 \text{ mm over } 1 \text{ m}^{2} = 1 \text{ litre}$. Hence $7.028 \text{ mm} \times 1000 \text{ m}^{2} = 7028 \text{ litres/day}$.

### Step 6 — Compute System Pump Capacity

$$
Q_{sys} = \frac{V_{day}}{T_{op}}
$$

$$
Q_{sys} = \frac{7.028}{6} = 1.171 \text{ m}^{3}/\text{hr} = 19.5 \text{ L/min}
$$

**Conversion logic:** This is the **design discharge** for selecting pump capacity and mainline diameter. Always round up to **next standard pump size** (≈ 1.5 m³/hr).

## 3.2 Derivation: Emitter Discharge & Wetted Area

**Given:** Inline pressure-compensating emitter, $K_d$ = 0.6, $\Delta H$ = 1.0 kg/cm².

### Step 1 — Emitter Discharge

$$
q_{em} = K_d \sqrt{\Delta H}
$$

$$
q_{em} = 0.6 \times \sqrt{1.0} = 0.6 \text{ L/hr}
$$

### Step 2 — Number of Emitters per Plant

For tomato with spacing 0.6 m × 0.5 m, with **2 emitters per plant** (one per main root zone).

### Step 3 — Wetted Area Below Each Emitter

Empirical model (Schwartzman & Zur):

$$
D_w = a \cdot q_{em}^{b}
$$

For clay-loam, $a \approx 0.6$, $b \approx 0.5$:

$$
D_w = 0.6 \times (0.6)^{0.5} = 0.6 \times 0.775 = 0.465 \text{ m diameter}
$$

Wetted area:

$$
A_w = \frac{\pi}{4} D_w^{2} = 0.785 \times (0.465)^{2} = 0.170 \text{ m}^{2}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Two emitters → total wetted area = 0.34 m², covering **~57 %** of the 0.6 × 0.5 = 0.30 m² plant footprint. Acceptable; if coverage < 40 %, increase emitters.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Irrigation Methods

| Method | WUE (%) | Root Medium | Best For | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Surface Drip | 90 – 95 | Soil / Coco-peat | Tomato, Capsicum, Cucumber | Clogging risk |
| Micro-Sprinkler | 75 – 85 | Soil | Orchids, Ferns, Nursery | Foliage wetting → disease |
| NFT | 95 – 98 | Soilless | Lettuce, Strawberry, Herbs | Power-failure risk |
| DFT | 90 – 95 | Soilless | Spinach, Leafy greens | Heavy infrastructure |
| Aeroponics | 98 – 99 | Air-mist | Potato seed, Tissue culture plants | High capital, technical skill |
| Ebb & Flow | 85 – 92 | Pots | Ornamentals | Salt build-up in substrate |
| Capillary Mat | 80 – 88 | Bench mat | Greenhouse seedlings | Small-scale only |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical — Nutrient Solution EC Calculation

**Problem:** A NFT system requires EC = 2.0 mS/cm. Stock solution A (Ca(NO₃)₂) reads 80 mS/cm; stock B (KNO₃ + micronutrients) reads 40 mS/cm. Both injected at 1:200. Compute EC of working solution (assuming linear dilution).

### Step 1 — Dilution EC of each stock

For stock A:

$$
EC_A = \frac{80}{200} = 0.40 \text{ mS/cm}
$$

For stock B:

$$
EC_B = \frac{40}{200} = 0.20 \text{ mS/cm}
$$

### Step 2 — Total EC

EC values are additive (approximation valid for dilute solutions):

$$
EC_{total} = EC_A + EC_B = 0.40 + 0.20 = 0.60 \text{ mS/cm}
$$

### Step 3 — Comment

This is below the 2.0 mS/cm target. To achieve 2.0 mS/cm, **increase injection ratio** to 1:60 (rule-of-three):

$$
EC_{new} = (80 + 40) \times \frac{1}{60} = 120 / 60 = 2.0 \text{ mS/cm} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Conversion logic:** In practice, EC sensors in the return tank allow closed-loop correction via solenoid valves — a textbook example of **precision fertigation** in KTU Module 2.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Greenhouse Drip + Fertigation Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    WS[Water Source Well or Tank] --> P1[Booster Pump 1.5 kW]
    P1 --> F1[Sand Media Filter]
    F1 --> F2[Screen Filter 120 micron]
    F2 --> FT[Fertigation Tank Venturi Injector]
    FT --> AR[Air Release Valve]
    AR --> PRV[Pressure Regulator 1.0 kg per sq cm]
    PRV --> PG1[Pressure Gauge Inlet]
    PG1 --> ML[Mainline PVC 63 mm]
    ML --> SM[Sub-main PVC 40 mm]
    SM --> SOL[Solenoid Valve Zone 1]
    SM --> SOL2[Solenoid Valve Zone 2]
    SOL --> LAT1[Lateral LDPE 16 mm]
    SOL2 --> LAT2[Lateral LDPE 16 mm]
    LAT1 --> EM1[Inline Emitter 2 L per hr]
    LAT1 --> EM2[Inline Emitter 2 L per hr]
    LAT2 --> EM3[Inline Emitter 2 L per hr]
    LAT2 --> EM4[Inline Emitter 2 L per hr]
    EM1 --> RZ1[Plant Root Zone Tomato]
    EM2 --> RZ2[Plant Root Zone Capsicum]
    EM3 --> RZ3[Plant Root Zone Cucumber]
    EM4 --> RZ4[Plant Root Zone Cucumber]
    PG1 --> CTRL[Automation Controller PLC]
    CTRL --> SMS[Soil Moisture Sensor]
    CTRL --> ECS[EC and pH Sensor]
    CTRL --> SOL
    CTRL --> SOL2

    style WS fill:#bbdefb
    style RZ1 fill:#c8e6c9
    style RZ2 fill:#c8e6c9
    style RZ3 fill:#c8e6c9
    style RZ4 fill:#c8e6c9
    style CTRL fill:#fff59d
    style FT fill:#ffccbc
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Irrigation Method Decision Logic for Greenhouse

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Greenhouse Crop Selection] --> B{Crop Root System}
    B -- Soil Grown --> C{Spacing Pattern}
    B -- Soilless --> D{Hydroponic Type}
    C -- Wide Row Tomato --> E[Drip Irrigation]
    C -- Close Spacing Lettuce --> F[Micro Sprinkler]
    D -- Shallow Film --> G[NFT System]
    D -- Deep Static --> H[DFT System]
    D -- Mist to Roots --> I[Aeroponics]
    E --> J[Emitter Selection PC or Non PC]
    F --> K[Mist Nozzle 60 micron]
    G --> L[Channel Slope 1 to 2 percent]
    H --> M[DWC Raft System]
    I --> N[High Pressure Mist 80 psi]
    J --> O[Fertigation Integration]
    K --> O
    L --> O
    M --> O
    N --> O
    O --> P[Automation and Sensor Feedback]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style P fill:#c8e6c9
    style O fill:#fff59d
    style B fill:#ffccbc
    style D fill:#ffccbc
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The first diagram shows the **physical flow** (water → filter → fertigation → plant). The second shows the **decision logic** that an engineer uses when specifying the system. Together, they form the KTU-recommended "design mental model" for Module 2.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]`

**Define fertigation. List any four advantages of fertigation in protected cultivation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Fertigation is the practice of **supplying dissolved fertilizers along with irrigation water** through the same delivery system (drip / micro-sprinkler / NFT) to crops grown in greenhouses or open fields.

**Advantages (4 × 0.5 = 2 marks):**
1. **Precise nutrient delivery** — directly to root zone, controlled by EC/pH sensors.
2. **Uniform application** — every plant receives the same nutrient dose.
3. **Labour saving** — no separate fertilizer application operation.
4. **Reduced nutrient loss** — no leaching by rainfall (since no rainfall in greenhouse) and no volatilization losses.
5. **Better crop quality and yield** — uniform growth, faster maturity.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]`

**Differentiate between Drip Irrigation and Nutrient Film Technique (NFT).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Basis | Drip Irrigation | NFT |
|---|---|---|
| Root medium | Soil or substrate (coco-peat) | Soilless — roots in air + thin water film |
| Water depth / volume | 1 – 8 L/hr per emitter | Continuous thin film ~2 – 3 mm |
| Recirculation | Usually non-recirculating | Always recirculating |
| Capital cost | Moderate | High |
| Risk on power failure | Low (water held in soil) | High (roots dry in minutes) |
| Best suited | Tomato, Capsicum, Cucumber | Lettuce, Strawberry, Herbs |

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **components and working of a drip irrigation system** used in greenhouses, with a neat functional diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 800 m² polyhouse grows tomato. Given: $ET_o$ = 4.8 mm/day, $K_c$ = 1.10, $P_e$ = 0, $E_a$ = 0.90, $LF$ = 0.10, daily operation = 7 hours. Calculate the **daily water requirement and pump discharge capacity**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks

1. **Source and Pump (1 mark):** Bore-well or overhead tank → multistage pump (1.5 HP typical) to deliver 1.0 – 2.0 kg/cm² pressure.
2. **Filtration System (1.5 marks):** Two-stage — (i) Sand/gravel media filter removes organic matter; (ii) Screen/disc filter (120 – 200 micron) removes fine particles. Critical because emitters have 0.5 – 1.5 mm orifices.
3. **Fertigation Unit (1 mark):** Venturi injector / fertilizer tank / proportional injection pump adds water-soluble fertilizers.
4. **Control Head (0.5 mark):** Pressure regulator, air release valve, pressure gauge, control valves.
5. **Pipe Network (1.5 marks):**
   - Mainline: PVC, 50 – 75 mm (buried).
   - Sub-main: PVC, 32 – 50 mm.
   - Laterals: LDPE, 12 – 16 mm, laid along plant rows.
6. **Emitters (1 mark):** Inline or online, pressure-compensating (PC) for slopes, non-PC for flat beds. Discharge 1 – 8 L/hr.
7. **Automation Sensors (0.5 mark):** Tensiometer / soil moisture probe / EC-pH sensor → controller → solenoid valves.

*(Diagram: same as SECTION 4.1 Mermaid block.)*

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — ETc** (1 mark):

$$
ET_c = ET_o \times K_c = 4.8 \times 1.10 = 5.28 \text{ mm/day}
$$

**Step 2 — Net Irrigation** (1 mark):

$$
IR_n = ET_c - P_e = 5.28 - 0 = 5.28 \text{ mm/day}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply LF** (1.5 marks):

$$
ET_c^{adj} = 5.28 \times (1 + 0.10) = 5.808 \text{ mm/day}
$$

**Step 4 — Gross Irrigation** (1.5 marks):

$$
IR_g = \frac{5.808}{0.90} = 6.453 \text{ mm/day}
$$

**Step 5 — Volume per day** (1 mark):

$$
V_{day} = 6.453 \times 800 \times 10^{-3} = 5.163 \text{ m}^{3}/\text{day} = 5163 \text{ L/day}
$$

**Step 6 — Pump capacity** (1 mark):

$$
Q_{sys} = \frac{5.163}{7} = 0.738 \text{ m}^{3}/\text{hr} = 12.3 \text{ L/min}
$$

**Final Answer:** Daily requirement ≈ **5.16 m³/day**; select pump capacity ≈ **1.0 m³/hr** (next standard size).

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]`

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **different hydroponic irrigation systems** used in greenhouses, with their suitability and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable diagram, explain the **method of irrigation scheduling** using **class-A pan evaporation data** for a greenhouse cucumber crop. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks

1. **NFT — Nutrient Film Technique (2 marks):** Roots in a shallow, continuously flowing film (2 – 3 mm) of nutrient solution in sloped channels (1 – 2 % slope). Recirculating. Suitable for leafy vegetables. **Limitation:** Power-failure vulnerability.

2. **DFT — Deep Flow Technique (1.5 marks):** Static solution 5 – 10 cm deep; roots immersed; raft floats on solution. Suitable for spinach, lettuce. **Limitation:** Higher water and nutrient reservoir required; aeration may be insufficient.

3. **Aeroponics (1.5 marks):** Roots suspended in a closed chamber; nutrient solution sprayed as fine mist (50 – 80 micron) at 5-min intervals. Highest oxygenation. Used in potato seed production, tissue-culture plants. **Limitation:** Highest capital cost and skill requirement.

4. **Drip-into-substrate (1.5 marks):** Drip emitters deliver nutrient solution into slabs of coco-peat, rockwool, perlite or vermiculite. Recirculating or run-to-waste. Most widely used for tomato, capsicum, cucumber. **Limitation:** Slab disposal; precise EC/pH monitoring required.

5. **Comparison Summary (0.5 mark):** Mention that **drip + substrate** dominates commercial greenhouse production in India (95 %), while **NFT** dominates in lettuce-specific farms.

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

**Pan-Evaporation Method — Step by Step (5 marks):**

1. **Measure Pan Evaporation** $E_{pan}$ from a Class-A pan placed inside or near the greenhouse (1 mark).
2. **Apply Pan Coefficient** $K_p$ (typically 0.7 – 0.8 for Class-A pan) to get reference ET (1 mark):

$$
ET_o = K_p \times E_{pan}
$$

3. **Apply Crop Coefficient** $K_c$ (1 mark):

$$
ET_c = ET_o \times K_c
$$

4. **Compute Irrigation Water Depth** (1 mark):

$$
IR_g = \frac{ET_c \times (1 + LF)}{E_a}
$$

5. **Decision Rule** (1 mark): Apply the calculated $IR_g$ as the next irrigation event when cumulative pan evaporation since the last irrigation equals the calculated depth.

**Numerical Illustration (2 marks):**

For cucumber: $K_c$ = 1.00, $K_p$ = 0.75, $E_{pan}$ = 6 mm/day, $LF$ = 0.10, $E_a$ = 0.90:

$$
ET_o = 0.75 \times 6 = 4.5 \text{ mm/day}
$$

$$
ET_c = 4.5 \times 1.00 = 4.5 \text{ mm/day}
$$

$$
IR_g = \frac{4.5 \times 1.10}{0.90} = 5.50 \text{ mm/day}
$$

So, irrigate when pan evaporation since last irrigation accumulates to 5.5 mm (≈ once per day in summer, once in 2 days in winter).

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to add Leaching Fraction (LF):** LF is mandatory for greenhouse irrigation because there is no natural rainfall to flush salts. Omitting LF = **−1.5 marks** in any numerical.
> 2. **Unit Conversion Errors:** $ET_c$ is in **mm/day** but must be converted to **litres** using $V = ET_c \times A \times 10^{-3}$. Missing this = **−1 mark**.
> 3. **Ignoring $P_e = 0$ in greenhouse:** Many students subtract rainfall. Inside a polyhouse, **rainfall never reaches the plant**. Marks lost: **−1**.
> 4. **Confusing NFT with DFT:** NFT = flowing thin film; DFT = static deep pool. Examiner's short-answer trap.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram in 7-mark sub-parts:** A "neat functional diagram" is worth **≥ 1.5 marks**. A labelled box-only block diagram still gets partial credit.
> 6. **Wrong pump-capacity rounding:** Always round $Q_{sys}$ **up** to the next standard pump size, never down.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Greenhouse irrigation replaces rainfall completely** — therefore system reliability and uniformity are non-negotiable.
- **Drip irrigation is the workhorse** of soil-based protected cultivation in India.
- **NFT, DFT, Aeroponics** are hydroponic systems — water-saving, but technically demanding.
- **Fertigation** = simultaneous water + nutrient delivery; cornerstone of greenhouse precision.
- **The Master Formula Chain:** $ET_o \rightarrow ET_c \rightarrow IR_n \rightarrow IR_g \rightarrow V_{day} \rightarrow Q_{sys}$.
- **Always apply Leaching Fraction** $LF$ (5 – 20 %) for salt control.
- **Class-A pan evaporation method** is the most practical scheduling tool in Indian polyhouses.
- **Filtration is critical** — emitter clogging is the #1 cause of drip-system failure.
- **Automation = Sensors (moisture, EC, pH) + Controller + Solenoid valves**.
- **EU (Emission Uniformity)** should be ≥ 90 % for proper fertigation distribution.
- **Aeroponics has highest WUE (98 %)** but also highest capital cost.
- **For KTU answers:** Always state $P_e = 0$ explicitly when working inside a greenhouse.

> **End of Module 2 — Greenhouse Irrigation Methods Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Site Specific Nutrient Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAGT751 - PRECISION FARMING TECHNIQUES FOR PROTECTED CULTIVATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Site Specific Nutrient Management # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] KTU Syllabus Highlight > **Site Specific Nutrient Management (SSNM)** is a precision agriculture paradigm that dynamically matches the application of fertilizers and soil amendments to the **spatially variable** nutrient requirements of distinct management zones within a field or protected cultivation system. It replaces the uniform, field-average broadcasting of nutrients with **Variable Rate Technology (VRT)** guided by **Global Positioning System (GPS)**, **Geographic Information System (GIS)**, and real-time soil/crop sensors. In the context of **Protected Cultivation (PEAGT751)**, SSNM extends to closed-loop systems such as **NFT (Nutrient Film Technique)**, **DFT (Deep Flow Technique)**, and substrate-based hydroponics, where the **Electrical Conductivity (EC)** and **pH** of the recirculating nutrient solution are modulated zone-by-zone (or bench-by-bench) according to the physiological stage of the crop. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hospital IV Drip" Imagine a hospital where every patient receives an IV drip. A doctor does **not** give the same saline solution to a diabetic child, a heart patient, and a dehydrated athlete. Instead, the drip composition is **tailored** to the patient's weight, blood reports, and recovery stage. **SSNM does the same for plants:** - The **patient** = a specific management zone (say, 1 m² of greenhouse bench) - The **blood report** = soil/leaf nutrient analysis from that zone - The **IV drip** = fertigation recipe (N-P-K + micronutrients in ppm) - The **doctor** = the SSNM decision support software (e.g., **Nutrient Expert®, DSSAT, or the KTU-recommended SPAD meter logic**) Just as a wrong drip dosage harms a patient, applying blanket fertilizer rates causes **leaching, salinity build-up, and wasted inputs** in protected structures. ## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics and Constants > [!NOTE] Memorize these for KTU Numerical Questions > - **EC (Electrical Conductivity)** of nutrient solution: measured in **dS/m** or **mS/cm**; target range for most greenhouse vegetables: **1.5 – 2.5 dS/m** > - **Nitrate-N (NO₃⁻-N) target in leaf**: **25,000 – 35,000 ppm** (fresh weight basis) > - **N-P-K standard greenhouse ratio** (tomato, vegetative): **1 : 0.5 : 1.5** > - **Tissue K critical level** (Petiole, tomato): **4 – 6 %** of dry matter > - **DTPA-extractable Zn deficiency threshold**: **< 0.6 mg/kg soil** > - **Cation Exchange Capacity (CEC) typical greenhouse soil**: **15 – 25 cmol(p+)/kg** ## 1.4 The 4R Nutrient Stewardship (KTU High-Yield) The international framework on which SSNM is built — frequently asked as a 3-mark definition in KTU ESE: > [!IMPORTANT] The 4R Principle > 1. **Right Source** — matching fertilizer type to crop need (e.g., Ca(NO₃)₂ vs. NH₄NO₃ for hydroponic N) > 2. **Right Rate** — matching quantity to site-specific soil test value > 3. **Right Time** — synchronizing application with crop uptake curve > 4. **Right Place** — placing nutrients in the root zone (fertigation > broadcasting) # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic of SSNM (Structured Steps) The SSNM workflow inside a polyhouse/greenhouse follows a closed loop: 1. **Zone Delineation** — The protected structure is divided into homogeneous micro-zones (e.g., 1 m × 1 m grid, or per-grow-bag). Each zone is geo-referenced using **GPS (±2 m accuracy)** or **RFID-tagged bench IDs**. 2. **Spatial Data Acquisition** — Soil/substrate samples are drawn zone-wise and analyzed for: - **pH**, **EC**, **Organic Carbon (%)**, **Available N, P, K (kg/ha)**, and **DTPA-extractable micronutrients (Zn, Fe, Mn, Cu)**. 3. **Crop-Based Prescription Generation** — A **target yield equation** is solved: $$Y_{target} = Y_{max} \times \left(1 - \frac{S}{100}\right)$$ where $S$ is the % yield gap to be closed by balanced nutrition. 4. **Nutrient Requirement Computation** — Using the standard KTU formula (see table). 5. **Variable Rate Fertigation** — The injector (e.g., **A/B tank, Mazzei, or Dosatron**) is modulated by an **EC/pH sensor + microcontroller** to deliver the calculated ppm. 6. **Feedback Loop** — Periodic **SPAD meter** (chlorophyll) and **leaf petiole sap analysis** recalibrate the next cycle. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Equation | Variables & Units | Application in SSNM | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $F_n = \dfrac{(U_n - S_n) \times Y_t}{R_u \times 100}$ | $F_n$ = fertilizer kg/ha; $U_n$ = unit uptake (kg nutrient per tonne yield); $S_n$ = soil test value (kg/ha); $Y_t$ = target yield (t/ha); $R_u$ = recovery/use efficiency (%) | Core SSNM nutrient rate calculation for any N/P/K | | 2 | $EC_{target} = \sum (C_i \times k_i)$ | $C_i$ = concentration of ion $i$ in ppm; $k_i$ = conversion factor (e.g., 0.55 for NO₃⁻, 1.1 for K⁺) | Predicts total EC of mixed nutrient solution | | 3 | $SAR = \dfrac{Na^+}{\sqrt{\dfrac{(Ca^{2+} + Mg^{2+})}{2}}}$ | All ions in meq/L | Sodium hazard in recirculated solutions | | 4 | $P_{applied} = \dfrac{(P_c - P_s) \times Bd \times D}{R_p}$ | $P_c$ = critical level; $P_s$ = soil test P (kg/ha); $Bd$ = bulk density (g/cm³); $D$ = depth (cm); $R_p$ = P recovery fraction | Phosphorus build-up/maintenance equation | | 5 | $N_{irrigation} = EC_{drip} \times Q \times t \times 10$ | $N$ = nutrient (g); $EC_{drip}$ = ppm in drip; $Q$ = emitter flow L/h; $t$ = time h | Quantifies real-time fertigation dose | | 6 | $V_{stock} = \dfrac{C_{target} \times V_{final}}{C_{stock}}$ | Stock and target in same units; $V$ in L | A/B tank mixing calculation | | 7 | $Y_{GIS} = f(\sigma^2_{field}, \lambda, d)$ | $\sigma^2$ = semi-variance; $\lambda$ = lag; $d$ = distance | Kriging-based spatial interpolation of soil nutrients | > [!WARNING] KTU Board Trap > In **equation 1**, students often forget to multiply by the **recovery fraction $R_u$** in the denominator. The result is a fertilizer dose that is **2 – 3 times** too high and will cause greenhouse **salinity crash**. Always show the $R_u$ term explicitly in your KTU answer sheet. ## 2.3 The Three SSNM Algorithms (Conceptual) | Algorithm | Logic | Best Use Case | |---|---|---| | **Sufficiency Approach (SA)** | Apply only the *difference* between soil test and critical level; no build-up credit. | High-input, intensively monitored polyhouses | | **Build-Up & Maintenance (B&M)** | Build soil to critical level first, then replace crop removal. | Open-field protected cultivation with soil beds | | **CEC-Based / Base Saturation** | Targets Ca:Mg:K ratio (e.g., 65:15:5). | Soilless media with peat/coir substrate | ## 2.4 Why SSNM is Engineered (Real-World Utility) - **Input cost reduction**: 20–40 % fertilizer saving (FAO documented in Indian polyhouses). - **Leaching prevention** — Critical in polyhouses where drainage goes to sumps; prevents groundwater NO₃⁻ contamination. - **Crop uniformity** — Reduces within-greenhouse CV (coefficient of variation) in fruit size, improving market grade. - **Sensor fusion readiness** — SSNM is the *software brain* of any **automated fertigation controller** (e.g., Netafim NMC Pro, Priva Connext). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Derivation: SSNM Fertilizer Rate for Nitrogen > **Problem (KTU-style 7-mark):** A polyhouse tomato plot has an **Olsen-P soil test value of 18 kg/ha** and a target yield of **80 t/ha**. The crop removes **0.30 kg P per tonne of fruit**, the **critical soil P level is 25 kg/ha**, the **bulk density is 1.3 g/cm³**, the **plow depth is 15 cm**, and the **P recovery fraction is 0.25**. Compute the P₂O₅ fertilizer requirement in **kg/ha**. ### Step 1 — Identify the governing equation The Phosphorus build-up + maintenance equation (Eq. 4 in cheat sheet): $$P_{applied} = \frac{(P_c - P_s) \times B_d \times D}{R_p}$$ ### Step 2 — Insert the *build-up* component first We must add enough P to raise the soil from **18 → 25 kg/ha** in the top 15 cm. **Convert soil test from kg/ha (area basis) to mg/kg (mass basis):** $$P_{s, mg/kg} = \frac{P_s}{B_d \times D \times 10}$$ $$ P_{s, mg/kg} = \frac{18}{1.3 \times 15 \times 10} = \frac{18}{195} \approx 0.0923 \; mg/kg $$ **Convert critical P similarly:** $$P_{c, mg/kg} = \frac{25}{1.3 \times 15 \times 10} = \frac{25}{195} \approx 0.1282 \; mg/kg$$ ### Step 3 — Build-up P needed in mg/kg $$\Delta P = P_c - P_s = 0.1282 - 0.0923 = 0.0359 \; mg/kg$$ ### Step 4 — Convert back to kg/ha of elemental P $$ P_{build-up} = \Delta P \times B_d \times D \times 10 = 0.0359 \times 1.3 \times 15 \times 10 = 0.0359 \times 195 \approx 7.0 \; kg P/ha $$ ### Step 5 — Add the *maintenance* P (replacement of crop removal) $$ P_{maint} = U_p \times Y_t = 0.30 \times 80 = 24 \; kg P/ha $$ ### Step 6 — Sum and apply recovery fraction $$ P_{total\;elemental} = P_{build-up} + P_{maint} = 7.0 + 24 = 31.0 \; kg P/ha $$ $$ P_{applied} = \frac{31.0}{R_p} = \frac{31.0}{0.25} = 124 \; kg P/ha $$ ### Step 7 — Convert elemental P to P₂O₅ (fertilizer grade) $$ \text{Molecular weight factor} = \frac{MW(P_2O_5)}{2 \times MW(P)} = \frac{141.94}{61.94} \approx 2.291 $$ $$ P_2O_5 = 124 \times 2.291 \approx 284.1 \; kg \; P_2O_5 / ha $$ > [!NOTE] Final Answer > **Apply ≈ 284 kg P₂O₅/ha** (e.g., ~ 620 kg of single super phosphate, or ~ 600 kg of 0:46:0 MAP per hectare). --- ## 3.2 Worked Derivation: A/B Tank Stock Solution Dilution > **Problem:** You are running a fertigation system where the **A-tank contains 100 L of Ca(NO₃)₂ solution at 150 g/L** and the **B-tank contains 100 L of KNO₃ + MgSO₄ at 120 g/L**. The injector ratio is **1 : 100** (stock : water). The target final nutrient concentration is **200 ppm N + 150 ppm K**. Verify whether the system delivers the required dose. ### Step 1 — Stock solution concentration of N from Ca(NO₃)₂ Ca(NO₃)₂·4H₂O has MW = **236 g/mol**; contains 2 NO₃⁻ = 2 × 14 = **28 g N per 236 g salt**. $$ C_{N,stock} = 150 \;\frac{g}{L} \times \frac{28}{236} = 17.80 \; g/L = 17{,}800 \; ppm $$ ### Step 2 — Concentration in irrigation water after 1:100 injection $$ C_{N,inject} = \frac{17{,}800}{100} = 178 \; ppm $$ ### Step 3 — Contribution from B-tank (KNO₃, MW = 101, %N = 14/101 = 13.86 %) $$ C_{N,stock_B} = 120 \times 0.1386 = 16.63 \; g/L = 16{,}630 \; ppm $$ $$ C_{N,inject_B} = \frac{16{,}630}{100} \approx 166 \; ppm $$ ### Step 4 — Total N delivered $$ N_{total} = 178 + 166 = 344 \; ppm $$ > **Result:** System delivers **344 ppm N**, which **exceeds** the 200 ppm target by ~72 %. The polyhouse manager should **reduce the A-tank concentration** or shorten injection time. The KTU-typical follow-up: *calculate the time* $t$ (in minutes) needed to deliver 200 ppm if the emitter is 4 L/h and the dilution is per-plant 1 L. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix for KTU Numericals | Given Input | Required Output | Governing Equation | Common Student Mistake | |---|---|---|---| | Soil test, target yield, recovery | kg nutrient/ha | $F_n$ formula | Forgetting $R_u$ | | EC, target ppm | Stock volume | $V_{stock} = C_t V_f / C_s$ | Mixing EC and ppm units | | SPAD reading, leaf N curve | Side-dress N rate | Linear regression chart | Using absolute SPAD instead of relative | | GPS coordinates of zones | Kriging-interpolated nutrient map | $\sigma^2(h)$ semi-variogram | Ignoring nugget effect | | Drainage EC | Leaching fraction | $LF = EC_{irrig} / EC_{drain}$ | Confusing LF and leaching requirement | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 SSNM Closed-Loop Architecture (Block Diagram) The following Mermaid block diagram shows the **functional architecture** of a Site Specific Nutrient Management system inside a protected cultivation unit, mapping every sensor → decision → actuator flow: ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Geo-referenced Management Zones
GPS or RFID Bench Tags] B[Soil and Substrate Sampling
per Zone] C[Laboratory Analysis
N P K pH EC Micro] D[GIS Database
Map Layer Stack] E[SSNM Decision Engine
Nutrient Expert or DSSAT] F[Prescription Map
kg ha per Zone] G[Fertigation Controller
A and B Tank Injector] H[Real Time Sensors
EC pH DO Temperature] I[Actuator
Dosing Pump and Solenoid Valves] J[Crop and Drainage
Feedback SPAD Petiole Sap] K[Recalibration Loop
