Update Prescription Map] A --> B B --> C C --> D D --> E E --> F F --> G G --> I H --> E I --> J J --> K K --> E ``` ## 4.2 SSNM Decision Flow (Sequential) ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Start Cycle] --> S2{Soil Test Below Critical Level?} S2 -- Yes --> S3[Compute Build Up Dose] S2 -- No --> S4[Compute Maintenance Dose Only] S3 --> S5[Add Crop Removal Component] S4 --> S5 S5 --> S6[Divide by Recovery Fraction] S6 --> S7[Convert to Fertilizer Grade kg] S7 --> S8[Schedule Fertigation Pulse] S8 --> S9[Verify with EC and pH Sensor] S9 --> S10[Log to GIS and Reset] ``` ## 4.3 Spatial Variability Heatmap — Conceptual ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Greenhouse_Area Z1[Zone A1
Low N 12 ppm]:::low Z2[Zone A2
High N 45 ppm]:::high Z3[Zone B1
Medium N 28 ppm]:::med Z4[Zone B2
Low N 15 ppm]:::low Z5[Zone C1
High N 42 ppm]:::high end classDef low fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000 classDef med fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000 classDef high fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#006600,color:#000 ``` > **Reading the diagram:** In a typical polyhouse, Zone A1 and B2 show low N because of the shadow side and high leaching under heavy emitters, while A2 and C1 (centre, southern aspect) show N accumulation. A *single uniform dose* would over-fertilize A2/C1 and under-fertilize A1/B2 — this is the exact inefficiency SSNM corrects. ## 4.4 Variable Rate Technology (VRT) Mapping Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TB P1[Raw Soil Sample Points] --> P2[Spatial Interpolation
Kriging or IDW] P2 --> P3[Nutrient Surface Map
Grid 1 m Resolution] P3 --> P4[Overlay with Yield Goal Map] P4 --> P5[Differential Fertilizer Map] P5 --> P6[Prescription File
Shapefile or ISO XML] P6 --> P7[Controller Card to Injector] P7 --> P8[Variable Rate Fertigation
per Zone] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define Site Specific Nutrient Management (SSNM). State the 4R Nutrient Stewardship framework.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1.5 marks):** SSNM is the *spatially variable* application of nutrients — water-soluble fertilizers, soil amendments, or foliar sprays — based on the *real-time* and *site-specific* nutrient status of each management zone, as determined by **soil testing, plant tissue analysis, and GPS-based prescription maps** (1 mark). It is a core precision-farming technique that replaces field-average fertilization (0.5 mark). - **4R framework (1.5 marks):** - **Right Source** — correct fertilizer form (e.g., nitrate-N for hydroponics) - **Right Rate** — quantity matches site-specific soil test - **Right Time** — synchronized with crop phenology - **Right Place** — within root zone via fertigation (0.5 × 3 = 1.5) --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **What is the Sufficiency Approach of SSNM? How does it differ from the Build-Up and Maintenance approach?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Sufficiency Approach (1.5 marks):** Apply fertilizer *only* for the amount of the nutrient the current crop will remove, *without* building soil reserves. Logic: if soil test is below critical, add a "build-up" dose; if above, *no* fertilizer is recommended. Economical for high-value, intensively tested polyhouse crops (1 mark). Uses critical-level concept (0.5 mark). - **Build-Up & Maintenance (1.5 marks):** Two-stage logic — first *build* soil nutrient to the critical level over seasons, then *maintain* by replacing crop removal each cycle (1 mark). Preferred for open-field soil-based protected cultivation where residual fertility matters (0.5 mark). --- ## Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU ESE – Module 3 Pattern, Modified]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **functional architecture of an SSNM-enabled fertigation system** in a polyhouse. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A capsicum polyhouse has been divided into **3 management zones (A, B, C)**. The soil-test values, target yields and critical levels are tabulated below. Using the SSNM equation $F_n = \dfrac{(U_n - S_n) \times Y_t}{R_u \times 100}$ (rearranged to *build-up* form), calculate the **per-zone N, P₂O₅ and K₂O requirement** in **kg/ha**. Recovery fractions: **N = 50 %, P = 25 %, K = 70 %**. **(7 marks)** | Zone | Soil N (kg/ha) | Soil P (kg/ha) | Soil K (kg/ha) | Target Yield (t/ha) | |------|----------------|----------------|----------------|---------------------| | A | 180 | 15 | 200 | 35 | | B | 240 | 22 | 280 | 40 | | C | 200 | 18 | 230 | 38 | Unit uptakes for capsicum (kg per tonne yield): **N = 2.5, P = 0.35, K = 3.0**. Critical levels (kg/ha): **N = 250, P = 25, K = 280**. #### **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — 7 marks:** A complete block diagram (already shown in Section 4.1) earns 4 marks. Add a brief explanation of each block (3 marks): - **Geo-referenced zone identification** — GPS/RFID (0.5) - **Sampling → lab analysis** for pH, EC, NPK, micronutrients (0.5) - **GIS map layer** — spatial nutrient map (0.5) - **SSNM engine** — applies 4R algorithm to generate prescription (0.5) - **Fertigation controller** — actuates injector (0.5) - **Sensor feedback** — EC, pH sensors close the loop (0.5) **Part (b) — 7 marks:** **Step 1 — Identify the equation form (1 mark):** Build-up form requires $(S_n - C_n)$ to be positive; if soil > critical, the term is *zero* (no build-up, only maintenance). **Step 2 — Compute per-zone N (2 marks):** Using maintenance equation $F_n = \dfrac{U_n \times Y_t}{R_u \times 100}$ for soil already at/above critical, and build-up form $F_n = \dfrac{(U_n \times Y_t) + (C_n - S_n)}{R_u \times 100}$ for soil below critical. **Zone A:** Soil N 180 < 250 → build-up $$ F_N = \frac{(2.5 \times 35) + (250 - 180)}{50 \times 100} \times 100 = \frac{87.5 + 70}{50} = \frac{157.5}{50} = 3.15 \; \text{? wait, unit correction} $$ > Re-derive: $F_n$ in **kg/ha** = $\dfrac{U_n Y_t + (C_n - S_n)}{R_u}$ where $U_n Y_t$ is in kg/ha of elemental nutrient (uptake per tonne × tonnes/ha), and $C_n - S_n$ is in kg/ha. $$ F_N^{A} = \frac{(2.5 \times 35) + (250 - 180)}{0.50} = \frac{87.5 + 70}{0.50} = \frac{157.5}{0.50} = 315 \; kg N/ha $$ **Zone B:** Soil N 240 < 250 → mild build-up $$ F_N^{B} = \frac{(2.5 \times 40) + (250 - 240)}{0.50} = \frac{100 + 10}{0.50} = 220 \; kg N/ha $$ **Zone C:** Soil N 200 < 250 → build-up $$ F_N^{C} = \frac{(2.5 \times 38) + (250 - 200)}{0.50} = \frac{95 + 50}{0.50} = 290 \; kg N/ha $$ **Step 3 — Compute per-zone P₂O₅ (2 marks):** Conversion factor elemental P → P₂O₅ = **2.291**. **Zone A:** Soil P 15 < 25 $$ F_P^{A} = \frac{(0.35 \times 35) + (25 - 15)}{0.25} = \frac{12.25 + 10}{0.25} = \frac{22.25}{0.25} = 89 \; kg P/ha = 204 \; kg P_2O_5/ha $$ **Zone B:** Soil P 22 < 25 $$ F_P^{B} = \frac{(0.35 \times 40) + (25 - 22)}{0.25} = \frac{14 + 3}{0.25} = 68 \; kg P/ha = 156 \; kg P_2O_5/ha $$ **Zone C:** Soil P 18 < 25 $$ F_P^{C} = \frac{(0.35 \times 38) + (25 - 18)}{0.25} = \frac{13.3 + 7}{0.25} = 81.2 \; kg P/ha = 186 \; kg P_2O_5/ha $$ **Step 4 — Compute per-zone K₂O (2 marks):** Conversion factor elemental K → K₂O = **1.205**. **Zone A:** Soil K 200 < 280 $$ F_K^{A} = \frac{(3.0 \times 35) + (280 - 200)}{0.70} = \frac{105 + 80}{0.70} = \frac{185}{0.70} = 264.3 \; kg K/ha = 318.5 \; kg K_2O/ha $$ **Zone B:** Soil K 280 ≥ 280 → maintenance only $$ F_K^{B} = \frac{3.0 \times 40}{0.70} = \frac{120}{0.70} = 171.4 \; kg K/ha = 206.6 \; kg K_2O/ha $$ **Zone C:** Soil K 230 < 280 $$ F_K^{C} = \frac{(3.0 \times 38) + (280 - 230)}{0.70} = \frac{114 + 50}{0.70} = \frac{164}{0.70} = 234.3 \; kg K/ha = 282.3 \; kg K_2O/ha $$ **Final Prescription Map (1 mark):** | Zone | N (kg/ha) | P₂O₅ (kg/ha) | K₂O (kg/ha) | |------|-----------|--------------|-------------| | A | 315 | 204 | 318.5 | | B | 220 | 156 | 206.6 | | C | 290 | 186 | 282.3 | > [!WARNING] KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout > - **Common mistake 1:** Forgetting to convert elemental nutrient → fertilizer oxide (P → P₂O₅, K → K₂O). Examiner deducts 1–2 marks. > - **Common mistake 2:** Using the *same* soil test value for all zones. The whole point of SSNM is *zone-wise* variation. > - **Common mistake 3:** Forgetting the build-up term $(C_n - S_n)$. Without it, you are *not* doing SSNM, you are doing maintenance fertilization. > - **Common mistake 4:** Reporting $F_n$ in kg nutrient/tonne instead of kg/ha. Always check units. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **(a)** Explain the **role of GPS, GIS, and VRT** in implementing SSNM. Describe any **two** algorithms used to convert soil-test data into a variable-rate prescription map. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A protected cultivation farm uses an **NFT system** with a recirculating nutrient solution. The current solution has the following composition (in ppm): **NO₃⁻-N = 180, K⁺ = 210, Ca²⁺ = 160, Mg²⁺ = 50, SO₄²⁻ = 80**. The crop (lettuce) requires an **EC of 1.6 dS/m** and the **target N concentration is 150 ppm**. Calculate: (i) the **current EC** of the solution, and (ii) the **volume of deionized water** that must be added per 1000 L of stock to bring N down to target. Use $EC \approx 0.55 \times C_{NO_3-N}$ (ppm) for approximation. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — 7 marks:** - **GPS (1.5 marks):** Provides ±2 m positional accuracy for sample points, machinery, and prescription files. Lets the controller "know" which zone it is in. - **GIS (1.5 marks):** Stores layered maps (pH, EC, NPK, organic carbon, yield history) and allows *spatial queries*. - **VRT (1.5 marks):** Variable Rate Technology — uses the prescription map to modulate fertilizer/pesticide injectors on-the-fly. - **Algorithm 1 — Sufficiency Index Mapping (1.25 marks):** Each grid cell assigned a *relative index* = (soil test / critical level); a *prescription factor* = 1 − SI is generated and multiplied by base dose. - **Algorithm 2 — Kriging with External Drift (1.25 marks):** Geostatistical interpolation that uses yield maps as a drift variable; produces continuous nutrient surfaces for VRT. **Part (b) — 7 marks:** **(i) Current EC calculation (3.5 marks):** Given approximation $EC \approx 0.55 \times C_{NO_3-N}$ (using nitrate as proxy for the ionic-strength contribution). $$ EC_{current} \approx 0.55 \times 180 = 99 \; \text{? – correct unit check} $$ > Re-evaluate: $0.55 \times 180 = 99$. However, EC in dS/m is not numerically 99; the proxy formula is **EC (dS/m) ≈ C_N (meq/L) / 10**, and 1 ppm NO₃⁻-N ≈ 0.0714 meq/L. **Refined solution using standard conversion:** 1 ppm NO₃⁻-N = 1 / 14 meq/L ≈ 0.0714 meq/L $$ C_{NO_3-N,meq} = 180 \times 0.0714 = 12.86 \; meq/L $$ **Total meq/L of cations ≈ K + Ca + Mg:** $$ K^+: 210 / 39.1 = 5.37; \quad Ca^{2+}: 160 / 20 = 8.0; \quad Mg^{2+}: 50 / 12.15 = 4.12 $$ $$ \sum cations = 5.37 + 8.0 + 4.12 = 17.49 \; meq/L $$ $$ \sum anions \approx NO_3^- + SO_4^{2-} = 12.86 + 80/48 = 12.86 + 1.67 = 14.53 \; meq/L $$ **Average ionic strength (TDS estimate):** $$ TDS \approx 0.64 \times EC \times 1000 \; ppm $$ Iteratively, EC such that ionic sum matches: $$ EC \approx 2.4 \; dS/m $$ (Using standard $EC \approx \sum c_i \cdot k_i$ with $k_{NO_3}=0.55, k_K=1.1, k_{Ca}=1.9, k_{Mg}=2.1, k_{SO_4}=0.7$ in mS/cm per 1000 ppm of salt equivalent): $$ EC \approx \frac{180}{1000}(0.55) + \frac{210}{1000}(1.1) + \frac{160}{1000}(1.9) + \frac{50}{1000}(2.1) + \frac{80}{1000}(0.7) \approx 1.65 \; dS/m $$ > **Answer (i):** **EC ≈ 1.65 dS/m**, very close to the 1.6 dS/m target. (3.5 marks — show ionic contributions and unit conversions.) **(ii) Dilution water needed (3.5 marks):** Target N = 150 ppm. Current N = 180 ppm. Use dilution law: $$ C_1 V_1 = C_2 (V_1 + V_w) \Rightarrow V_w = V_1 \left(\frac{C_1 - C_2}{C_2}\right) $$ $$ V_w = 1000 \times \left(\frac{180 - 150}{150}\right) = 1000 \times 0.20 = 200 \; L $$ > **Answer (ii):** Add **200 L of deionized water per 1000 L of stock solution** to lower NO₃⁻-N from 180 → 150 ppm. (3.5 marks) --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] High-Density Revision Block > - **SSNM** = spatially variable nutrient application, replacing field-average doses with **zone-specific prescriptions**. > - The **4R Nutrient Stewardship**: **Right Source, Right Rate, Right Time, Right Place.** > - The governing equation: $F_n = \dfrac{U_n Y_t + (C_n - S_n)}{R_u}$ for **build-up + maintenance**; $F_n = \dfrac{U_n Y_t}{R_u}$ for **maintenance only** when soil test ≥ critical. > - **Conversion factors:** P → P₂O₅ = **2.291**; K → K₂O = **1.205**; K₂O → K = **0.830**; P₂O₅ → P = **0.436**. > - **Recovery fractions** (commonly used in KTU problems): N = **0.50**, P = **0.25**, K = **0.70**, S = **0.30**, Zn = **0.05**. > - **Algorithms:** Sufficiency, Build-up & Maintenance, CEC/Base Saturation. > - **VRT pipeline:** Sampling → Lab → GIS Map → Decision Engine → Prescription File → Injector. > - **Sensors in closed loop:** EC, pH, DO, temperature; **calibrators:** SPAD meter, petiole sap test. > - **NFT/DFT formulas:** $V_{stock} = C_t V_f / C_s$; dilution $V_w = V_1(C_1 - C_2)/C_2$. > - **EC ↔ ppm:** $EC (dS/m) \approx TDS(ppm) / 640$; $EC (mS/cm) = 1 dS/m$ (units identical). > - **Kriging** is the gold-standard geostatistical interpolator; requires ≥ 30 sample points for a reliable variogram. > - **Always** state the units (kg/ha, ppm, dS/m) explicitly in the final answer. > - For **greenhouse vegetables**: target EC 1.5 – 2.5 dS/m; pH 5.5 – 6.5 in recirculating solution. > - **KTU hot topics to memorize:** 4R, three SSNM algorithms, VRT pipeline, conversion factors, sufficiency vs. build-up equation forms.

topics.title: Weeds Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT751 - PRECISION FARMING TECHNIQUES FOR PROTECTED CULTIVATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Weeds Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Weeds

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight**
> A **weed** is any plant growing *out of place*, *out of time*, or *in competition* with the cultivated crop for resources such as water, nutrients, light, and space. In the context of **Protected Cultivation** (polyhouses, greenhouses, net houses, and shade net structures), weeds are unwanted vegetation that invades the controlled environment, compromising the precision inputs applied to the main crop.

The most widely accepted academic definition, as adopted by the **Indian Council of Agricultural Research (ICAR)** and the **Indian Society of Weed Science (ISWS)**, is:

> *"A weed is a plant that is considered undesirable in a particular situation, or 'a plant out of place'. Weeds are plants whose virtues have not yet been discovered."* — Adapted from Ralph Waldo Emerson

### 1.1.1 Formal KTU Definition
$$W = \{P_i \mid P_i \notin C_{target} \;\wedge\; P_i \in \Omega_{protected}\}$$

Where:
- $W$ = Set of weed species
- $P_i$ = Individual plant of species $i$
- $C_{target}$ = Set of target cultivated crop species
- $\Omega_{protected}$ = Spatial domain of the protected structure

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy**
> Imagine a **luxury hotel room** (your polyhouse) where you have invited a specific set of **VIP guests** (your tomato/capsicum/cucumber plants). You have arranged the lighting, temperature, and food (precision inputs) to perfectly suit these guests. Now, imagine a group of **uninvited gate-crashers** (weeds) slipping in through the door. They eat from the VIP buffet, occupy the best seats under the grow lights, and spread out into the lobby. The hotel manager (the grower) must now spend extra energy and resources to evict them.
>
> In the same way, weeds in a protected structure **steal** the carefully calibrated fertigation water, nutrients, dripper outputs, and PAR (Photosynthetically Active Radiation) light, thereby reducing the **input use efficiency** of the entire precision system.

## 1.3 Why Weed Management is Critical in Protected Cultivation

In open-field agriculture, the *crop-weed competition threshold* is typically expressed as a **Critical Period of Weed Competition (CPWC)** of 15–45 days after sowing. However, inside a polyhouse, this threshold is **drastically compressed** for the following reasons:

1. **High-value crops**: Crops like **capsicum**, **gerbera**, and **cherry tomato** have a farm gate value of ₹150–₹400/kg. Every kilogram lost to weed competition represents significant revenue erosion.
2. **Closed environment**: The warm, humid microclimate of a greenhouse (T = 25–32°C, RH = 65–85%) is the **ideal incubator** for weed seed germination.
3. **Soil solarization synergy**: The same heat trapped under black polythene (used for *solarization* to kill *Fusarium* and nematodes) also pre-germinates weed seeds — but they may be missed if not monitored.
4. **Precision input diversion**: A single *Cyperus rotundus* (nutgrass) plant can divert up to **30%** of drip irrigation water meant for the main crop.

## 1.4 Classification of Weeds (KTU-Level Classification)

### 1.4.1 Based on Life Cycle
| Class | Example | Common Name | Longevity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Annual** | *Amaranthus viridis* | Pigweed | 1 season |
| **Biennial** | *Daucus carota* (wild) | Wild carrot | 2 seasons |
| **Perennial** | *Cyperus rotundus* | Nutgrass / Purple nutsedge | > 2 years (indefinite) |

### 1.4.2 Based on Botanical Family (Monocot vs. Dicot)
- **Monocot weeds**: Grasses (e.g., *Echinochloa*, *Cynodon dactylon* — Bermuda grass) and Sedges (e.g., *Cyperus rotundus*).
- **Dicot weeds**: Broadleaf weeds (e.g., *Amaranthus*, *Chenopodium*, *Portulaca oleracea*).

### 1.4.3 Based on Habitat
- **Field crops weeds** (open field)
- **Aesthetic weeds** (ornamental plantings)
- **Greenhouse weeds** — *specific to protected structures* (e.g., *Oxalis corniculata*, *Stellaria media*, *Poa annua*)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip**
> Always remember the **3 C's of Weeds** for the 3-mark definitions:
> 1. **C**ompetitors (for resources)
> 2. **C**arriers (of pests and diseases)
> 3. **C**ontaminators (of produce and equipment)

## 1.5 Characteristics of "Successful" Greenhouse Weeds

The polyhouse environment exerts a strong **selection pressure**, favouring weeds with:
- **High seed output** (e.g., *Portulaca* can produce > 240,000 seeds/plant).
- **Dormancy mechanisms** (seeds survive soil solarization at 50°C).
- **C4 photosynthesis pathway** (e.g., *Amaranthus*) — efficient at high light and CO₂.
- **Rhizomatous/stoloniferous growth** (e.g., *Cynodon dactylon*) — regrows from fragments left during intercultural operations.
- **Resistance to common greenhouse herbicides** — e.g., trifluralin-resistant *Lolium* biotypes.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Principles of Weed Management in Protected Structures

Weed management follows a **decision hierarchy** of five control categories. In KTU 2024 Scheme, students must master the *integration* of all five into a single **IWM (Integrated Weed Management)** module.

### 2.1.1 The 5-Tier Weed Control Pyramid

```
                    [TIER 1: PREVENTIVE]
                          ▲
                  [TIER 2: CULTURAL]
                          ▲
                [TIER 3: MECHANICAL/PHYSICAL]
                          ▲
                  [TIER 4: BIOLOGICAL]
                          ▲
                 [TIER 5: CHEMICAL]
```

Each tier reduces the weed pressure progressively. The strategy is to use **Tier 1 to Tier 3** as the foundation, **Tier 4** when feasible, and **Tier 5** only as a last resort in protected cultivation.

## 2.2 Tier 1: Preventive Weed Management

Preventive measures are *pre-emptive* actions that stop weeds from entering the polyhouse in the first place.

| Method | Description | Effectiveness |
|---|---|---|
| **Clean planting material** | Certified, weed-free seed/seedlings/transplants | High |
| **Sanitation of tools, footwear, and equipment** | Wash trays, trolleys, pruning shears with **2% NaOCl** | High |
| **Walkway matting** | Use weed-mat (polypropylene geo-textile, 100–120 GSM) on paths | Very High |
| **Filtered irrigation water** | Drip system with **120-mesh filters** to prevent seed ingress via water | High |
| **Quarantine for new media** | Sterilize coco-peat, perlite, and vermiculite before introduction | Medium-High |
| **Inspecting imported pots and trays** | Reject containers with visible weed seeds/seedlings | Medium |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight**
> The geo-textile weed mat suppresses weeds by physically blocking **PAR (400–700 nm)** from reaching the soil surface. Without light, weed seeds cannot synthesize chlorophyll or drive the **Hill reaction** of photosynthesis, and the seedling exhausts its endosperm reserves and dies.

## 2.3 Tier 2: Cultural Weed Management

Cultural practices manipulate the **cropping environment** to favour the crop over the weed.

1. **Stale seedbed technique**:
   - Prepare the bed 2–3 weeks before transplanting.
   - Irrigate lightly → weed seeds germinate.
   - Destroy the flush with a flame weeder or shallow tillage.
   - *Net effect*: Reduces the weed seed bank in the top **2–5 cm** of soil.
2. **Mulching**:
   - **Black polythene mulch (25–50 micron)** reduces weed emergence by **85–98%**.
   - **Organic mulch** (paddy straw, coconut coir) for natural systems.
3. **Crop geometry**:
   - Closer spacing (e.g., 30 × 30 cm for capsicum) shades the soil faster, suppressing late-germinating weeds.
4. **Fertigation management**:
   - Avoid broadcasting fertilizer; use **point application** through drippers. This denies weeds the "free nitrogen buffet".

## 2.4 Tier 3: Mechanical / Physical Weed Management

In protected cultivation, mechanical options are *restricted* because:
- Bed geometry is fixed (typically 90–120 cm raised beds).
- Tractor-mounted implements cannot enter.
- Labour is expensive but the *weed-to-labour ratio* justifies hand weeding in high-value crops.

| Method | Tool | Cost Index | Labour (man-hr/ha) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hand weeding | Khurpi, hand hoe | Low | 400–600 |
| Wheel hoe | Long-handled wheel hoe | Low | 100–150 |
| Power weeder (petrol) | Rotary blade tiller | Medium | 30–50 |
| Thermal/flame weeder | LP gas torch | Medium-High | 20–40 |
| Steaming | Steam boiler (soil sterilization) | High | 10–15 |
| Solarization | Transparent polythene (50–100 micron), 4–6 weeks | Low (one-time) | 5–10 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note**
> **Soil Solarization** is *both* a **nematode + pathogen control** method *and* a **weed management** method. The same 4–6 week summer treatment at 45–55°C that kills *Meloidogyne* juveniles also kills most annual weed seeds in the top **10 cm** of soil. The temperature must be sustained > 45°C for at least 4 hours/day for 30 days.

## 2.5 Tier 4: Biological Weed Management

Bio-control is *underutilized* in polyhouses because of the closed environment, but research breakthroughs include:

- **Releasing *Zygogramma bicolorata* beetles** for control of *Parthenium hysterophorus* (open field).
- **Fungal bioherbicides**: *Alternaria cassiae* on *Cassia obtusifolia*; *Colletotrichum gloeosporioides* on *Aeschynomene virginica*.
- **Allelopathic cover crops**: *Rye* (Secale cereale) secretes **benzoxazinoids** that suppress *Amaranthus* and *Chenopodium* germination.

> [!NOTE]
> In protected cultivation, biological control of weeds is **rarely practical** because the polyhouse is designed for *monoculture* of a single high-value crop, and the introduction of any non-target organism (insect, fungus) poses an unacceptable risk.

## 2.6 Tier 5: Chemical Weed Management (Herbicide Science)

Chemical control uses **herbicides** — chemicals that kill or suppress weeds. This is the *most technically demanding* tier and requires precision in:

### 2.6.1 Classification of Herbicides

**A) Based on Mode of Action (MoA) — HRAC Classification**

| HRAC Group | MoA | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **A** | ACCase inhibitors | Quizalofop, Clodinafop, Fluazifop |
| **B** | ALS inhibitors | Metsulfuron-methyl, Imazethapyr |
| **C1** | PSII inhibitors (atrazine site) | Atrazine, Simazine |
| **C2** | PSII inhibitors (uracil) | Bromacil, Terbacil |
| **F1** | PDS inhibitors | Diflufenican |
| **G** | EPSPS inhibitors | Glyphosate |
| **K1** | Microtubule inhibitors | Trifluralin, Pendimethalin |
| **K3** | VLCFA inhibitors | Alachlor, Metolachlor |
| **O** | Synthetic auxins | 2,4-D, MCPA |
| **Z** | Unknown / multi-site | MSMA, DSMA |

**B) Based on Time of Application**

- **Pre-plant incorporated (PPI)**: e.g., Fluchloralin mixed into soil before sowing.
- **Pre-emergence (PE)**: e.g., Pendimethalin, Atrazine applied *before* weed seed germination.
- **Post-emergence (PoE)**: e.g., Quizalofop applied *after* weeds emerge.
  - *Selective* PoE: Kills only grasses (used in dicot crops like capsicum).
  - *Non-selective* PoE: Kills all vegetation (e.g., Paraquat, Glyphosate — used only on inter-row spaces with **shields**).

### 2.6.2 Herbicide Selectivity Equation

A herbicide is "selective" if it kills the weed but not the crop. The selectivity ratio is:

$$S = \frac{LD_{50}^{weed}}{LD_{50}^{crop}}$$

Where $LD_{50}$ is the lethal dose at which 50% mortality occurs. **If S >> 1**, the herbicide is *safe* for the crop at the recommended dose.

### 2.6.3 Herbicide Dose Calculation (KTU Numerical Favourite)

The standard field dose is often expressed in **g a.i./ha** (grams of active ingredient per hectare). For a spray solution:

$$D_{formulation} = \frac{A_{field} \times D_{ai}}{C_{formulation} \times A_{covered}}$$

Where:
- $D_{formulation}$ = Quantity of commercial formulation to be mixed (mL or g)
- $A_{field}$ = Total field area (m² or ha)
- $D_{ai}$ = Recommended dose of active ingredient (g a.i./ha or kg/ha)
- $C_{formulation}$ = Concentration of a.i. in the commercial product (%)
- $A_{covered}$ = Area that one spray operation covers

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Engineering / Field Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Critical Period of Weed Competition (CPWC) | $CPWC = T_{i}^{*} - T_{f}^{*}$ | Determines when to start & stop weeding |
| 2 | Weed Control Efficiency (WCE) | $WCE = \frac{WC_{c} - WC_{t}}{WC_{c}} \times 100$ | Compares treatment effectiveness |
| 3 | Weed Index (WI) | $WI = \frac{Y_{c} - Y_{t}}{Y_{c}} \times 100$ | Crop yield loss due to weeds |
| 4 | Herbicide Selectivity Ratio | $S = LD_{50}^{weed} / LD_{50}^{crop}$ | Drug screening for crop safety |
| 5 | Herbicide Dose | $D_{formulation} = (A_{field} \cdot D_{ai}) / (C_{formulation} \cdot A_{covered})$ | Field-level calibration |
| 6 | Spray Volume Rate | $V = \frac{Q}{A \cdot S_{speed}}$ | Sprayer calibration |
| 7 | Soil Solarization Threshold | $T_{soil} \geq 45°C$ for ≥ 4 hr/day × 30 days | Kills weeds + nematodes |
| 8 | Mulch Weed Suppression | PAR transmission < 5% → > 95% weed kill | Geo-textile design criterion |
| 9 | Seed Bank Decline | $S_{n} = S_{0}(1 - g \cdot m)^{n}$ | Multi-year weed management planning |
| 10 | Herbicide Half-life (t½) | $t_{1/2} = \frac{0.693}{k}$ | Persistence in soil |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **"weed control efficiency" (WCE)** with **"weed index" (WI)**. WCE measures weed *count* reduction; WI measures *crop yield* reduction. Both are expressed in %, but their interpretation is opposite.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Herbicide Dose Calculation for a Greenhouse Bed

> **Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Pattern)**

A polyhouse has **8 raised beds**, each of dimension **30 m × 1.0 m**. You are to apply **Pendimethalin 30% EC** as a pre-emergence herbicide at the recommended dose of **1.0 kg a.i./ha**. Calculate the quantity of commercial formulation required for the entire polyhouse. Assume a spray volume of **500 L/ha**.

### Step 1: Calculate the total area
$$A_{field} = 8 \times (30 \text{ m} \times 1.0 \text{ m}) = 8 \times 30 = 240 \text{ m}^2$$

### Step 2: Convert to hectares
$$A_{field} = \frac{240}{10{,}000} = 0.024 \text{ ha}$$

### Step 3: Identify the given parameters
- $D_{ai} = 1.0 \text{ kg a.i./ha} = 1000 \text{ g a.i./ha}$
- $C_{formulation} = 30\% = 0.30$ (fraction of a.i. in EC formulation)
- $A_{covered} = 1 \text{ ha}$ (per the standard dose)

### Step 4: Apply the formulation quantity formula
$$D_{formulation} = \frac{A_{field} \times D_{ai}}{C_{formulation} \times A_{covered}}$$

$$D_{formulation} = \frac{0.024 \text{ ha} \times 1000 \text{ g a.i./ha}}{0.30 \times 1 \text{ ha}}$$

$$D_{formulation} = \frac{24}{0.30} = 80 \text{ g of commercial formulation}$$

### Step 5: Convert to volume (since EC is liquid)
Pendimethalin 30% EC has a typical density of **1.05 g/mL**.

$$V_{formulation} = \frac{80 \text{ g}}{1.05 \text{ g/mL}} \approx 76.2 \text{ mL}$$

### Step 6: Calculate the spray water volume
$$V_{water} = 0.024 \text{ ha} \times 500 \text{ L/ha} = 12 \text{ L}$$

### **Final Answer**
- **Pendimethalin 30% EC required: ≈ 76 mL**
- **Mix in: 12 L of water**
- **Concentration: ≈ 6.3 mL/L**

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Weed Control Efficiency (WCE) — Comparative Study

> **Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Pattern)**

A field experiment in a polyhouse recorded the following data on *Cyperus rotundus* (nutgrass) population:

| Treatment | Weed count (per m²) |
|---|---|
| Control (no weeding) | 250 |
| Hand weeding at 30 DAT | 80 |
| Black polythene mulch | 15 |
| Pendimethalin (PE) | 60 |

Calculate the **Weed Control Efficiency (WCE)** for each treatment.

### Step 1: Recall the formula
$$WCE = \frac{WC_{c} - WC_{t}}{WC_{c}} \times 100$$

Where $WC_{c}$ = weed count in control, $WC_{t}$ = weed count in treatment.

### Step 2: Compute for each treatment

**(a) Hand weeding at 30 DAT**:
$$WCE_{HW} = \frac{250 - 80}{250} \times 100 = \frac{170}{250} \times 100 = 68.0\%$$

**(b) Black polythene mulch**:
$$WCE_{BPM} = \frac{250 - 15}{250} \times 100 = \frac{235}{250} \times 100 = 94.0\%$$

**(c) Pendimethalin (PE)**:
$$WCE_{PEN} = \frac{250 - 60}{250} \times 100 = \frac{190}{250} \times 100 = 76.0\%$$

### Step 3: Rank the treatments (KTU Board loves this)
**Ranking (best to worst): Black polythene mulch (94%) > Pendimethalin (76%) > Hand weeding (68%)**

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Weed Index (WI) and Crop Yield Loss

> **Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Pattern)**

The fruit yield of capsicum under different weed management treatments in a polyhouse is:

| Treatment | Yield (t/ha) |
|---|---|
| Weed-free check | 40.0 |
| Hand weeding | 32.0 |
| Mulch | 38.5 |
| Herbicide (PE) | 34.0 |
| Weedy check | 18.0 |

Calculate the **Weed Index (WI)** for all treatments and interpret.

### Step 1: Recall the formula
$$WI = \frac{Y_{c} - Y_{t}}{Y_{c}} \times 100$$

Where $Y_{c}$ = yield in *weed-free* check (not weedy check), $Y_{t}$ = yield in treatment.

### Step 2: Compute for each treatment

**(a) Hand weeding**:
$$WI_{HW} = \frac{40.0 - 32.0}{40.0} \times 100 = \frac{8}{40} \times 100 = 20.0\%$$

**(b) Black polythene mulch**:
$$WI_{BPM} = \frac{40.0 - 38.5}{40.0} \times 100 = \frac{1.5}{40} \times 100 = 3.75\%$$

**(c) Herbicide (PE)**:
$$WI_{PEN} = \frac{40.0 - 34.0}{40.0} \times 100 = \frac{6}{40} \times 100 = 15.0\%$$

### Step 3: Interpretation

| Treatment | WI (%) | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| Hand weeding | 20.0 | 20% yield loss vs. weed-free check |
| Mulch | **3.75** | **Best (closest to weed-free)** |
| Herbicide (PE) | 15.0 | Moderate yield loss |
| Weedy check | 55.0 | (computed below for completeness) |
$$WI_{weedy} = \frac{40.0 - 18.0}{40.0} \times 100 = 55.0\%$$

**Inference**: Mulching is the most efficient strategy in this scenario because the WI value (3.75%) is closest to 0%, indicating maximum crop protection.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Spray Calibration for a Backpack Sprayer

> **Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Pattern)**

A grower uses a knapsack sprayer with a **nozzle discharge of 0.8 L/min** and walks at **1 m/s** along a bed that is **1.0 m wide**. Calculate the **spray volume rate (L/ha)** and the **time required to cover 0.5 ha**.

### Step 1: Compute area covered per minute
The sprayer treats a **strip of 1.0 m width** as the operator walks. Walking speed $S_{speed}$ = 1 m/s = 60 m/min.

$$A_{covered/min} = 1.0 \text{ m} \times 60 \text{ m/min} = 60 \text{ m}^2/\text{min}$$

### Step 2: Spray volume rate
$$V_{L/m^2} = \frac{Q}{A_{covered/min}} = \frac{0.8 \text{ L/min}}{60 \text{ m}^2/\text{min}} = 0.0133 \text{ L/m}^2$$

### Step 3: Convert to L/ha (1 ha = 10,000 m²)
$$V = 0.0133 \text{ L/m}^2 \times 10{,}000 \text{ m}^2/\text{ha} = 133 \text{ L/ha}$$

### Step 4: Time to cover 0.5 ha
$$T = \frac{V_{total}}{Q}$$

Total volume needed = $133 \text{ L/ha} \times 0.5 \text{ ha} = 66.5 \text{ L}$

$$T = \frac{66.5 \text{ L}}{0.8 \text{ L/min}} = 83.1 \text{ min} \approx 1 \text{ hr } 23 \text{ min}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Decision Table for Weed Management in Protected Cultivation

| Weed Type / Pressure | Recommended Tier 1+2 | Recommended Tier 3 | Recommended Tier 4 | Recommended Tier 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Annual broadleaf** (e.g., *Amaranthus*) | Stale seedbed + clean seed | Hand weeding at 15 DAT | Not applicable | Pendimethalin 1.0 kg a.i./ha (PE) |
| **Annual grass** (e.g., *Echinochloa*) | Stale seedbed | Wheel hoe between rows | Not applicable | Quizalofop 50 g a.i./ha (PoE) |
| **Perennial sedge** (*Cyperus rotundus*) | Solarization + clean seed | Repeated hand weeding (depletes tubers) | Fungal bioherbicides (research stage) | Halosulfuron-methyl 67.5 g a.i./ha (PoE) |
| **Perennial grass** (*Cynodon dactylon*) | Solarization | Deep digging to remove rhizomes | Not applicable | Glyphosate 1.0 kg a.i./ha (PoE, *inter-row shielded spray only*) |
| **Mixed flora** | Stale seedbed + mulch (black PE) | Power weeder monthly | Not applicable | Pendimethalin + directed glyphosate |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "weed" and list any four characteristics of a "successful" greenhouse weed.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A weed is a plant growing out of place, out of time, or in competition with the cultivated crop (1 mark). In the context of protected cultivation, four characteristics of a "successful" greenhouse weed are:
1. **High seed output** — e.g., *Portulaca oleracea* produces > 240,000 seeds/plant (0.5 marks).
2. **Dormancy mechanisms** — seeds remain viable for years, surviving soil solarization (0.5 marks).
3. **C4 photosynthesis** — efficient at the high light and CO₂ conditions of a polyhouse (0.5 marks).
4. **Rhizomatous/stoloniferous growth** — vegetative regrowth from fragments, e.g., *Cynodon dactylon* (0.5 marks).

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**What is the "Critical Period of Weed Competition" (CPWC)? Mention its significance in protected cultivation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Critical Period of Weed Competition (CPWC) is the **time interval** during the crop growth period in which the crop must be kept *weed-free* to prevent *irreversible* yield loss (1.5 marks).

**Significance in protected cultivation (1.5 marks):**
- In open fields, CPWC is typically 15–45 DAT. In a polyhouse, it is **drastically compressed** (often 10–30 DAT) because the warm, humid microclimate accelerates weed growth.
- The CPWC dictates the **spray/intervention schedule** of pre-emergence herbicides.
- Missing this window leads to *permanent* yield loss because protected crops are grown at narrow spacing with high input cost.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Classify herbicides based on their mode of action. List the HRAC groups A, B, C, G, and K3 with one example each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the operation of a **knapsack (backpack) sprayer** for herbicide application in a polyhouse. Derive the formula for calculating the **spray volume rate (L/ha)**. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — HRAC Classification (7 marks)

| HRAC Group | Site of Action | Mechanism | Example (1.4 marks each) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | ACCase (Acetyl-CoA Carboxylase) | Inhibits fatty acid synthesis in grasses | **Quizalofop-ethyl** (also Clodinafop) |
| **B** | ALS (Acetolactate Synthase) | Inhibits branched-chain amino acid synthesis | **Metsulfuron-methyl** (also Imazethapyr) |
| **C** | PSII (Photosystem II) — D1 protein | Blocks electron transport at QB site | **Atrazine** (also Simazine) |
| **G** | EPSPS (5-enolpyruvyl-shikimate-3-phosphate synthase) | Inhibits aromatic amino acid synthesis | **Glyphosate** (non-selective) |
| **K3** | VLCFA (Very Long Chain Fatty Acids) | Inhibits cell division in meristems | **Pendimethalin** (also Alachlor) |

**Note**: Herbicides in groups A and B are *highly selective* between grasses and dicots, which is critical in polyhouse vegetables.

#### Part (b) — Knapsack Sprayer Operation & Calibration (7 marks)

**Components of a knapsack sprayer (3.5 marks):**
1. **Tank** (15–20 L capacity, typically high-density polyethylene).
2. **Piston pump** (hand-operated, lever on the side).
3. **Pressure chamber** (maintains 1–4 bar).
4. **Lance / boom** (1–2 m length) with adjustable **nozzle tips** (e.g., flat-fan, hollow-cone, flood-jet).
5. **Pressure regulator** and **strainer** (to prevent nozzle clogging).
6. **Straps** for carrying on the back.

**Operating sequence (2 marks):**
- Fill the tank with the herbicide-water mixture (calibrated per Section 3.4).
- Pump the lever 8–10 strokes per minute to build pressure.
- Walk at a steady speed (typically 1 m/s) along the inter-row space.
- Maintain nozzle height of 45–60 cm above the weed canopy for even coverage.
- **Use shields** (plastic cones around the nozzle) in polyhouses to prevent spray drift onto the main crop.

**Derivation of Spray Volume Rate (1.5 marks):**
$$V = \frac{Q \times 10{,}000}{W \times S_{speed} \times 60}$$

Where $Q$ = nozzle discharge (L/min), $W$ = effective swath width (m), $S_{speed}$ = walking speed (m/s).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**(a)** What is **Integrated Weed Management (IWM)**? Explain its five tiers with examples specific to protected cultivation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A polyhouse capsicum trial recorded the following data. Calculate the **Weed Control Efficiency (WCE)** and **Weed Index (WI)** for each treatment and identify the best treatment. **(7 marks)**

| Treatment | Weed count (per m²) | Capsicum yield (t/ha) |
|---|---|---|
| Control (Weedy) | 240 | 19.0 |
| Hand weeding at 30 DAT | 75 | 33.0 |
| Black polythene mulch | 18 | 38.0 |
| Pendimethalin 1.0 kg a.i./ha (PE) | 60 | 35.0 |
| Weed-free check (HW at 15, 30, 45 DAT) | 0 | 40.0 |

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Integrated Weed Management (7 marks)

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
IWM is the **combination of two or more weed control methods** (preventive, cultural, mechanical, biological, chemical) in a *compatible* manner to keep weed pressure below the economic threshold level (ETL), while minimizing environmental and human health risks.

**The Five Tiers (5.5 marks — 1.1 marks each):**

| Tier | Name | Protected Cultivation Example |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Preventive** | 120-mesh drip filter, foot-bath with 2% NaOCl, certified weed-free coco-peat |
| 2 | **Cultural** | Stale seedbed, black polythene mulch (25 micron), drip-fertigation (no broadcast) |
| 3 | **Mechanical** | Hand weeding, wheel hoe, power weeder, soil solarization |
| 4 | **Biological** | *Alternaria cassiae* for *Cassia*; allelopathic rye cover (limited in polyhouse) |
| 5 | **Chemical** | Pendimethalin (PE), Quizalofop (PoE for grasses), shielded Glyphosate (inter-row) |

**Synergy in polyhouse**: Soil solarization (Tier 3) doubles up as a nematode control measure, exemplifying the *integration* concept.

#### Part (b) — WCE and WI Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1: WCE Formula and Calculation (3.5 marks)**

$$WCE = \frac{WC_{c} - WC_{t}}{WC_{c}} \times 100$$

- Hand weeding: $WCE = (240 - 75)/240 \times 100 = \mathbf{68.75\%}$
- Black polythene mulch: $WCE = (240 - 18)/240 \times 100 = \mathbf{92.50\%}$
- Pendimethalin: $WCE = (240 - 60)/240 \times 100 = \mathbf{75.00\%}$
- Weed-free check: $WCE = (240 - 0)/240 \times 100 = \mathbf{100.00\%}$

**Step 2: WI Formula and Calculation (2.5 marks)**

$$WI = \frac{Y_{weed-free} - Y_{t}}{Y_{weed-free}} \times 100$$

- Hand weeding: $WI = (40 - 33)/40 \times 100 = \mathbf{17.50\%}$
- Black polythene mulch: $WI = (40 - 38)/40 \times 100 = \mathbf{5.00\%}$
- Pendimethalin: $WI = (40 - 35)/40 \times 100 = \mathbf{12.50\%}$
- Weedy check: $WI = (40 - 19)/40 \times 100 = \mathbf{52.50\%}$

**Step 3: Best Treatment Identification (1 mark)**

**Black polythene mulch** is the best treatment because:
- Highest WCE (92.50%, second only to weed-free check).
- Lowest WI (5.00%) — closest to zero, indicating maximum crop protection.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake 1**: Students use the *Weedy Check* yield (19 t/ha) as $Y_{c}$ in the WI formula. **The correct reference is the *Weed-Free Check* yield (40 t/ha)**. Using the wrong denominator gives a meaningless WI.
> - **Common Mistake 2**: WCE and WI are often interchanged. Remember: **WCE evaluates weed population; WI evaluates crop yield loss.**
> - **Common Mistake 3**: When the weed-free check has zero weeds, WCE = 100% by definition, but the WI is also 0% — students sometimes report 100% for WI by confusion.
> - **Common Mistake 4**: Forgetting to convert units (g ↔ kg, m² ↔ ha) in herbicide dose calculations. KTU examiners specifically allot 1 mark for correct unit conversion.
> - **Common Mistake 5**: Using the wrong nozzle for the wrong herbicide (e.g., flood-jet nozzle for a contact herbicide). Always recommend **flat-fan nozzles** for systemic herbicides and **hollow-cone nozzles** for contact herbicides.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist**

### Core Definitions
- [ ] **Weed**: Plant out of place, out of time, in competition.
- [ ] **CPWC**: Critical Period of Weed Competition — the time window the crop MUST be kept weed-free.
- [ ] **IWM**: Integration of ≥ 2 control methods to keep weeds below ETL.
- [ ] **WCE (Weed Control Efficiency)**: $WCE = [(WC_c - WC_t)/WC_c] \times 100$ — population-based.
- [ ] **WI (Weed Index)**: $WI = [(Y_c - Y_t)/Y_c] \times 100$ — yield-based.
- [ ] **HRAC**: Herbicide Resistance Action Committee — groups herbicides by site of action.
- [ ] **Solarization**: Covering moist soil with transparent polythene for 4–6 weeks in summer to trap solar heat (≥ 45°C).

### Must-Remember Numbers
- [ ] **Soil solarization** requires T ≥ **45°C** for ≥ **4 hr/day** for **30 days** minimum.
- [ ] **Black polythene mulch** of **25–50 micron** suppresses **85–98%** of weeds.
- [ ] **Cyperus rotundus** diverts up to **30%** of drip irrigation water.
- [ ] **HRAC Group A** = ACCase inhibitors = grass killers (e.g., Quizalofop).
- [ ] **HRAC Group G** = EPSPS inhibitors = Glyphosate = non-selective.
- [ ] **LD₅₀ ratio** for selectivity must be **>> 1** for crop safety.
- [ ] **Stale seedbed** technique: 2–3 weeks pre-sowing weed flush before killing.
- [ ] **120-mesh drip filter** recommended for protected structures.

### Critical Pitfalls to Avoid
- [ ] Never apply **Glyphosate** in an open polyhouse without **shields** — it kills the main crop.
- [ ] **Paraquat** is banned in many countries; do not recommend in KTU answers.
- [ ] Do not use the *weedy check* as the reference for WI — use the *weed-free check*.
- [ ] 2,4-D ester formulations have **high vapour drift** — strictly avoid in polyhouses.
- [ ] Pre-emergence herbicides must be applied **before weed emergence AND within 3 days of irrigation/transplanting** for activation.

### Engineering-Specific Points
- [ ] **Geo-textile weed mat**: 100–120 GSM, blocks > 95% PAR.
- [ ] **Knapsack sprayer**: Typical tank = **15–20 L**, working pressure = **1–4 bar**.
- [ ] **Nozzle discharge** for calibration: usually **0.5–1.5 L/min**.
- [ ] **Walking speed** for herbicide application: **1 m/s** (≈ 3.6 km/hr).
- [ ] **Spray volume rate** for polyhouse: **200–500 L/ha** (lower than open field).
- [ ] **Drip irrigation** with point-source fertigation *already* suppresses inter-row weeds by **40–60%** by denying them water and nutrients.

### Top 5 HRAC Groups for KTU Answers
1. **Group A — ACCase** → Quizalofop (grasses in dicot crops).
2. **Group B — ALS** → Metsulfuron, Halosulfuron (sedges).
3. **Group C — PSII** → Atrazine (limited in polyhouse).
4. **Group G — EPSPS** → Glyphosate (shielded use only).
5. **Group K3 — VLCFA** → Pendimethalin (most popular PE in vegetables).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Agrochemicals and Fertilizer Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT751 - PRECISION FARMING TECHNIQUES FOR PROTECTED CULTIVATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Agrochemicals and Fertilizer Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Agrochemicals** are chemical agents (synthetic or biological) purposefully applied to crops, soil, or the growing environment to enhance plant nutrition, regulate physiological growth, and protect against biotic stresses (pests, pathogens, weeds). In the context of **Protected Cultivation** (greenhouses, polyhouses, shade-net houses), agrochemical use is a **precision-driven science** rather than a blanket application.

**Fertilizer Management** in protected structures refers to the **systematic, science-based planning and application of plant nutrients** through:
- **Fertigation** (delivery via the irrigation system)
- **Foliar feeding** (spray application on leaves)
- **Media/soil amendment**
- **Hydroponic nutrient solutions**

It is governed by the **4R Nutrient Stewardship Framework** adopted globally by the fertilizer industry (TFI/IFA/IPNI/IFA consortium):
1. **Right Source** – matching nutrient form to crop need
2. **Right Rate** – applying only the quantity required
3. **Right Time** – synchronizing availability with crop demand
4. **Right Place** – positioning nutrients at the root zone

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Hospital IV Drip" Concept

Imagine a patient in an ICU. Instead of giving one large meal a day (traditional open-field farming), the doctors give a **continuous, precisely dosed IV drip** (protected cultivation fertigation) tailored to the patient's vitals (soil/leaf analysis). 

- **Agrochemicals** = the medicines (antibiotics, vitamins) given at specific doses.
- **Fertilizer Management** = the dosing protocol — never too much (causes toxicity/salinization), never too little (causes deficiency).
- **Protected structure** = the controlled ICU environment where temperature, humidity, and $\text{CO}_2$ are regulated, allowing the plant to absorb nutrients with maximum efficiency.

> [!NOTE]
> In open fields, fertilizer **Use Efficiency (NUE)** is only **30–50%**. In protected cultivation with drip fertigation, NUE can exceed **80–90%** because nutrients are delivered directly to the root zone in a controlled volume.

### 1.3 Core Categories of Agrochemicals Used in Protected Cultivation

| Category | Purpose | Common Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Macronutrient Fertilizers** | Supply N, P, K, Ca, Mg, S | Urea, MAP, $\text{KNO}_3$, $\text{Ca(NO}_3\text{)}_2$ |
| **Micronutrient Fertilizers** | Supply Fe, Mn, Zn, Cu, B, Mo, Cl | Chelated Fe-EDDHA, $\text{ZnSO}_4$, Borax |
| **Pesticides (Insecticides)** | Control sucking/chewing insects | Imidacloprid, Spinosad, Neem oil |
| **Fungicides** | Control fungal pathogens | Mancozeb, Carbendazim, Copper oxychloride |
| **Bactericides** | Control bacterial diseases | Streptocycline, Copper-based bactericides |
| **Nematicides** | Control root-knot nematodes | Carbofuran, Fluensulfone |
| **Biofertilizers / Biocontrol agents** | Biological N-fixation, P-solubilization, pest control | *Trichoderma*, *Azotobacter*, PSB, *Pseudomonas* |
| **Plant Growth Regulators (PGRs)** | Modulate flowering, fruit set, ripening | NAA, $\text{GA}_3$, Ethrel, Cytokinins |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics Recalled from Module 1–2:**
> - **Electrical Conductivity (ECe)** of nutrient solution: **1.5 – 2.5 dS/m** for most vegetables.
> - **pH** of root zone: **5.5 – 6.5** (ideal for nutrient availability).
> - **N:P:K ratio** for leafy crops ≈ **3:1:2**; for fruiting crops ≈ **1:1:2**.
> - **1 ppm = 1 mg/L** of nutrient in solution.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Logic of Fertilizer Management in Protected Cultivation

The decision flow in protected cultivation is **diagnostic-first**, not calendar-based. The reasoning proceeds in 5 logical steps:

1. **Step 1 — Diagnostic Sampling**
   - Soil/media analysis: pH, EC, N, P, K, micronutrients, organic carbon.
   - Water analysis: $\text{pH}_\text{w}$, $\text{EC}_\text{w}$, residual carbonates, SAR.
   - Leaf tissue analysis for in-season corrections.

2. **Step 2 — Crop Nutrient Demand Mapping**
   - Total uptake curve ($\text{kg/ha}$) is plotted for the entire crop duration.
   - Demand is split into **vegetative phase** (high N), **flowering phase** (balanced), and **fruiting/harvest phase** (high K, Ca).

3. **Step 3 — Source Selection**
   - Water-soluble fertilizers (e.g., $\text{KNO}_3$, MAP, $\text{Ca(NO}_3\text{)}_2$) for fertigation.
   - Chelated micronutrients (Fe-EDDHA, $\text{Zn-EDTA}$) for protected conditions where pH drift is common.

4. **Step 4 — Scheduling (Time × Dose)**
   - Split doses: 60–70 % during vegetative stage, 30–40 % during reproductive stage for N.
   - Fertigation frequency: typically **2–4 cycles per day** in soilless media.

5. **Step 5 — Real-Time Monitoring & Correction**
   - Continuous EC and pH monitoring in the drain.
   - Visual deficiency/toxicity diagnosis on leaves.
   - Recalibration of the fertigation recipe weekly.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **most frequently tested formulas** for KTU ESE questions on this topic. Master these units and conversions.

| # | Formula / Conversion | Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | ppm $\leftrightarrow$ mg/L | $1 \text{ ppm} = 1 \text{ mg/L} = 1 \text{ mg kg}^{-1} \text{ (for water)}$ | Used for solution concentration |
| 2 | ppm to percent | $\% = \dfrac{\text{ppm}}{10{,}000}$ | e.g., 200 ppm = 0.02 % |
| 3 | EC to TDS | $\text{TDS (mg/L)} \approx \text{EC (dS/m)} \times 640$ | Empirical, varies 550–700 |
| 4 | Fertilizer dose from nutrient requirement | $D = \dfrac{N_\text{req} \times 100}{P_\text{fert}}$ | $D$ = dose (kg/ha), $N_\text{req}$ = nutrient needed (kg/ha), $P_\text{fert}$ = % nutrient in fertilizer |
| 5 | Stock solution concentration | $C_\text{stock} = \dfrac{C_\text{final} \times V_\text{final}}{V_\text{stock}}$ | For fertigation injectors |
| 6 | Injector ratio (e.g., 1:200) | $C_\text{irrigation} = \dfrac{C_\text{stock}}{\text{ratio}}$ | e.g., ratio 1:200 |
| 7 | Water requirement by Penman or pan evaporation | $\text{ET}_c = K_c \times \text{ET}_o$ | mm/day; $K_c$ varies with crop stage |
| 8 | Nutrient element content in fertilizer | $E\% = \dfrac{\text{Atomic mass of element}}{\text{Molar mass of compound}} \times 100$ | For chemical formula conversion |
| 9 | Leaching fraction | $LF = \dfrac{\text{Drain}}{\text{Irrigation}}$ | Recommended 10–20 % for protected cultivation |
| 10 | Volume of solution | $V = A \times d$ | $A$ = area, $d$ = depth (m), $V$ in m³ = 1000 L |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Greenhouse tomato fertigation:** A 1 ha polyhouse with drip irrigation applies nutrients at 8–12 mm/day. A miscalculation of just 50 ppm of N for 30 days can waste **₹15,000–20,000** in fertilizer and cause **blossom end rot** (Ca deficiency).
- **Hydroponic lettuce:** Nutrient solution is prepared at EC 1.2–1.8 dS/m, pH 5.8–6.2. The solution is recirculated, and EC drift > ±10% triggers a "top-up" using a calibrated stock.
- **Export-grade capsicum:** Cauliflower and capsicum growers use **$\text{Ca(NO}_3\text{)}_2$** as the primary Ca source because it is highly soluble and nitrate-based, avoiding the Ca-phosphate precipitation that occurs in concentrated stock tanks.

### 2.4 Key Principles of Agrochemical Selection in Protected Structures

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Protected" changes agrochemical choice:**
> - **Closed environment** = high humidity = greater fungal pressure → systemic fungicides preferred.
> - **No rain wash-off** → pesticides persist longer, so **lower dose** and **shorter PHI (Pre-Harvest Interval)** are critical.
> - **Beneficial pollinators (bumbles, honeybees)** often introduced → only **bee-safe** pesticides allowed.
- **Rotation of chemical groups** is mandatory to prevent pathogen/pest resistance (especially in *Botrytis cinerea*, *Sclerotinia*).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Fertilizer Dose from Nutrient Requirement

**Problem Statement (Worked Example):**
A capsicum crop in a polyhouse requires **150 kg N/ha** for the full season. Available fertilizer is **Urea** containing **46% N**. Calculate the quantity of urea required per hectare.

**Step 1: Identify the governing relationship.**
Every 100 kg of fertilizer delivers $P_\text{fert}$ kg of the desired nutrient. Therefore, the required fertilizer dose $D$ to deliver $N_\text{req}$ kg of nutrient is:

$$D = \frac{N_\text{req} \times 100}{P_\text{fert}}$$

**Step 2: Substitute the given values.**
$N_\text{req} = 150 \text{ kg/ha}$, $P_\text{fert} = 46\%$.

$$D = \frac{150 \times 100}{46}$$

**Step 3: Evaluate the arithmetic.**

$$\begin{aligned}
D &= \frac{15{,}000}{46} \\
  &= 326.08 \text{ kg/ha of Urea.}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** Approximately **326 kg of urea per hectare** will supply 150 kg of N.

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **base rate**. In practice, we also add 20–30 % extra to account for **N losses via volatilization (NH₃)** and **leaching**, so the actual field dose ≈ 390–420 kg/ha.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stock Solution Preparation for Fertigation

**Problem Statement:**
A farmer uses a **1:200 injector (venturi)** with a stock tank. The target concentration in irrigation water is **200 ppm of N** using **Urea (46% N)**. Calculate the mass of urea to dissolve in a **200 L stock tank**.

**Step 1: Required concentration in irrigation water.**
$C_\text{irrigation} = 200 \text{ ppm} = 200 \text{ mg/L of N}$.

**Step 2: Back-calculate stock concentration using injector ratio.**
The injector dilutes the stock by a factor of 200, so:

$$C_\text{stock} = C_\text{irrigation} \times \text{ratio}$$

$$C_\text{stock} = 200 \text{ mg/L} \times 200 = 40{,}000 \text{ mg/L} = 40 \text{ g/L of N.}$$

**Step 3: Convert N mass to urea mass.**
Since urea is 46 % N, the urea concentration in stock must be:

$$C_\text{urea,stock} = \frac{C_\text{stock}}{0.46} = \frac{40}{0.46} = 86.96 \text{ g/L of urea.}$$

**Step 4: Multiply by stock tank volume.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Mass of urea} &= 86.96 \text{ g/L} \times 200 \text{ L} \\
                    &= 17{,}391 \text{ g} \\
                    &\approx 17.4 \text{ kg of urea in 200 L tank.}
\end{aligned}$$

**Verification:**
Stock = 17.4 kg urea / 200 L = 87 g/L urea. After 1:200 dilution = 0.435 g/L urea = 435 mg/L urea. Multiply by 0.46 = **200 mg/L N** ✓.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Nutrient Content from Chemical Formula

**Problem Statement:**
Calculate the **% K** (potassium) in **Muriate of Potash (KCl)**.
- Atomic mass: K = **39**, Cl = **35.5**

**Step 1: Write the formula and find its molar mass.**
$\text{KCl}$ molar mass = $39 + 35.5 = 74.5 \text{ g/mol}$.

**Step 2: Apply the percent-element formula.**

$$\%K = \frac{\text{Atomic mass of K}}{\text{Molar mass of KCl}} \times 100$$

$$\%K = \frac{39}{74.5} \times 100$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\%K &= 0.5235 \times 100 \\
    &= 52.35\%.
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** MOP contains **52.35 % K** (often rounded to 60% $\text{K}_2\text{O}$ in fertilizer trade; $\text{K}_2\text{O} \times 0.83 = \text{K element}$).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Deficiency vs. Toxicity in Polyhouses

| Nutrient | Deficiency Symptom | Toxicity Symptom | Management Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| **N** | Pale yellow older leaves, stunted growth | Dark green, succulent, lush leaves (delayed flowering) | Reduce N, increase K |
| **P** | Purple/bronze older leaves, slow growth | Induced Zn & Fe deficiency | Foliar spray DAP, check root pH |
| **K** | Marginal scorch on older leaves, weak stems | Induced Mg, Ca deficiency | Foliar $\text{KNO}_3$ spray |
| **Ca** | **Blossom End Rot** in tomato/peppers | Rare in protected systems | Foliar $\text{CaCl}_2$ or $\text{Ca(NO}_3\text{)}_2$ |
| **Mg** | Interveinal chlorosis (older leaves) | Rare | Foliar $\text{MgSO}_4$ |
| **Fe** | Interveinal chlorosis (younger leaves) | Rare, but causes bronzing | Chelated Fe-EDDHA in fertigation |
| **B** | Cracking, internal cork, hollow stem | Tip burn, leaf margin yellowing | Stop B, leach media |

---

### 3.5 Derivation 4 — Leaching Requirement for Salt Build-Up

**Problem Statement:**
Greenhouse media has $\text{EC}_e = 4.5 \text{ dS/m}$ (too high for tomato, limit is 2.5). Irrigation water EC is 1.2 dS/m. Calculate the **leaching fraction (LF)** needed to bring root zone EC to 2.5.

**Step 1: Write the leaching equation.**

$$LF = \frac{EC_\text{desired} - EC_\text{irrigation}}{EC_\text{root\ zone} - EC_\text{irrigation}} \times \frac{1}{1}$$

A more standard form:

$$LF = \frac{EC_\text{water}}{EC_\text{drain}}$$

For the **replacement** requirement:

$$\text{LR} = \frac{EC_i}{EC_d} = \frac{1.2}{2.5} = 0.48 \text{ (48\%)}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Excess water to apply} &= \frac{LR}{1 - LR} \times \text{ET}_c \\
                             &= \frac{0.48}{0.52} \times 10 \text{ mm} \\
                             &= 9.23 \text{ mm extra per day.}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** Apply **~19 mm/day** total irrigation (10 mm ET + 9.2 mm leaching) to maintain root zone EC at 2.5 dS/m.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Fertilizer Management Decision Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Crop Sown in Polyhouse] --> B[Sample Soil and Water]
    B --> C[Lab Analysis pH, EC, N, P, K, Micronutrients]
    C --> D{Deficiency Detected?}
    D -- No --> E[Apply Maintenance Fertigation]
    D -- Yes --> F[Identify Limiting Nutrient]
    F --> G[Select Fertilizer Source]
    G --> H[Calculate Dose Using Formula]
    H --> I[Choose Application Method]
    I --> I1[Fertigation via Drip]
    I --> I2[Foliar Spray]
    I --> I3[Media Drench]
    I1 --> J[Monitor EC and pH of Drain]
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
    J --> K{Within Target Range?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Continue Crop Cycle]
    K -- No --> M[Adjust Recipe Reapply]
    M --> I
    L --> N[End: Harvest]
```

### 4.2 Agrochemical Classification Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AC[Agrochemicals in Protected Cultivation] --> SYN[Synthetic Chemicals]
    AC --> BIO[Bio Agents]
    SYN --> FERT[Fertilizers]
    SYN --> PES[Pesticides]
    SYN --> PGR[PGRs]
    FERT --> MAC[Macronutrients N P K Ca Mg S]
    FERT --> MIC[Micronutrients Fe Mn Zn Cu B Mo]
    PES --> INS[Insecticides]
    PES --> FUN[Fungicides]
    PES --> BAC[Bactericides]
    PES --> NEM[Nematicides]
    PGR --> GRO[Growth Promoters GA3 NAA]
    PGR --> RET[Growth Retardants CCC MH]
    BIO --> BF[Biofertilizers]
    BIO --> BC[Biocontrol Agents]
    BF --> BF1[Azotobacter]
    BF --> BF2[PSB]
    BF --> BF3[Mycorrhiza]
    BC --> BC1[Trichoderma]
    BC --> BC2[Pseudomonas]
    BC --> BC3[Beauveria]
```

### 4.3 Fertigation System Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    WT[Water Source Tank] --> FT[Filtration Unit]
    FT --> IPF[Inline Particle Filter]
    IPF --> INJ[Fertilizer Injector Venturi or Dosing Pump]
    INJ --> MIX[Mixing Chamber]
    MIX --> MS[Main Supply Line]
    MS --> SUB1[Sub-main 1]
    MS --> SUB2[Sub-main 2]
    SUB1 --> DL1[Drip Line Polyhouse Zone 1]
    SUB2 --> DL2[Drip Line Polyhouse Zone 2]
    DL1 --> RT1[Root Zone Crop A]
    DL2 --> RT2[Root Zone Crop B]
    MIX --> EC[EC pH Sensor]
    EC --> CTRL[Automation Controller]
    CTRL --> INJ
```

### 4.4 Nutrient Deficiency Symptom Decision Tree (Leaf-Based Diagnosis)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Symptom Observed on Leaf] --> AGE{Affected Leaf Age}
    AGE -- Older Leaves --> OL[Mobile Nutrient Deficiency]
    AGE -- Younger Leaves --> YL[Immobile Nutrient Deficiency]
    OL --> OL1[Check N: Uniform Yellowing]
    OL --> OL2[Check P: Purple Bronzing]
    OL --> OL3[Check K: Marginal Necrosis]
    OL --> OL4[Check Mg: Interveinal Chlorosis]
    YL --> YL1[Check Fe: Interveinal Yellowing]
    YL --> YL2[Check Ca: Tip Burn Distortion]
    YL --> YL3[Check B: Brittle Deformed Growth]
    YL --> YL4[Check S: Uniform Yellowing of Young Leaves]
    OL1 --> ACT1[Foliar Urea 1 percent]
    OL3 --> ACT2[Foliar KNO3 1 percent]
    YL2 --> ACT3[Foliar CaCl2 0.5 percent]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **Define the term "Fertigation" and list any three advantages of fertigation in protected cultivation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Fertigation is the precise application of water-soluble fertilizers through the irrigation system (drip or micro-sprinkler) directly into the root zone of crops.
- **Advantages (any 3 × 0.5 mark = 1.5 marks, and 0.5 mark for neat presentation):**
  1. **High Nutrient Use Efficiency (80–90%)** as nutrients reach the active root zone with minimal loss.
  2. **Uniform distribution** of nutrients in the wetted volume.
  3. **Labour saving** — no separate manual fertilizer application.
  4. **Flexible scheduling** — nutrients can be split into many small doses matched to crop demand.
  5. **Reduced leaching and groundwater pollution** compared to broadcast application.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
> **What are biofertilizers? Give two examples and their role in protected cultivation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Biofertilizers are **microbial inoculants** containing living or latent cells of beneficial microorganisms that, when applied to seed, soil, or roots, mobilize or increase nutrient availability to plants.
- **Examples (1 mark):**
  - *Azotobacter* / *Azospirillum* — fixes atmospheric N.
  - **Phosphate Solubilizing Bacteria (PSB)** like *Pseudomonas* — solubilizes fixed P.
  - *Trichoderma viride* — biocontrol agent against soil-borne fungi.
- **Role in protected cultivation (1 mark):** They reduce dependence on chemical fertilizers, improve soil/media biology, suppress pathogens in the root zone, and are especially valuable in soilless and organic protected cultivation systems.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

#### **Option A — 14 Marks**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **4R Nutrient Stewardship** concept in fertilizer management. Discuss how each 'R' is implemented in a polyhouse vegetable system.

**(b) [7 marks]** A farmer wants to apply **120 kg P₂O₅/ha** through **Single Super Phosphate (SSP, 16% P₂O₅)**. Calculate the SSP dose. If the same P is to be applied through **Mono-Ammonium Phosphate (MAP, 11% N, 52% P₂O₅)** via drip, calculate the MAP dose and the bonus N that enters the system.

---

#### **Model Solution — Option A**

**(a) 4R Nutrient Stewardship in Polyhouse [7 marks]**

The 4R framework, promoted by the global fertilizer industry, is a **decision-support system** for sustainable nutrient management. Each R in a polyhouse is implemented as follows:

1. **Right Source [2 marks]:**
   - Select fertilizers based on **solubility, compatibility, and crop need**.
   - For fertigation: 100 % water-soluble sources like $\text{KNO}_3$, MAP, $\text{Ca(NO}_3\text{)}_2$, chelated Fe-EDDHA.
   - Avoid $\text{Ca(NO}_3\text{)}_2$ + $\text{K}_2\text{SO}_4$ or SSP in the same tank to prevent precipitation of Ca-sulphate.
   - Match N form to crop stage: **nitrate-N** for vegetative leafy crops; **ammonium-N** for fruit-set enhancement in tomato.

2. **Right Rate [2 marks]:**
   - Rate is decided by **soil test** (macro + micro) and **crop removal pattern**.
   - Use the dose formula: $D = \dfrac{N_\text{req} \times 100}{P_\text{fert}}$
   - Example: capsicum needs 150 kg N, 80 kg P, 200 kg K per ha. Apply as fertigation split across 120 days.

3. **Right Time [1.5 marks]:**
   - Split total N into **20 equal fertigations** through the dripper, applied 2–3 times per week.
   - K application is **front-loaded in the fruiting stage** (post-anthesis).
   - Foliar Ca sprays at **fruit set** and **2 weeks before harvest** to prevent BER.

4. **Right Place [1.5 marks]:**
   - Place nutrients in the **wetted bulb** of the dripper, which is the active root zone.
   - In media-bag systems, apply **at top of the bag** so nutrients percolate down through the root mass.
   - Avoid broadcasting in polyhouses — leads to salt accumulation in non-wetted zones and wastage.

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 marks]**

**Step 1: SSP dose calculation [2 marks]**
$$D_\text{SSP} = \frac{120 \times 100}{16} = 750 \text{ kg/ha}$$

**Step 2: MAP dose for same $\text{P}_2\text{O}_5$ [2 marks]**
$$D_\text{MAP} = \frac{120 \times 100}{52} = 230.77 \text{ kg/ha}$$

**Step 3: Bonus N from MAP [2 marks]**
MAP contains 11 % N, so:
$$\text{N from MAP} = 230.77 \times 0.11 = 25.38 \text{ kg N/ha}$$

**Step 4: Adjustment [1 mark]**
The crop's N plan must subtract this bonus 25.38 kg N from the planned N supply, or risk excess N (delayed flowering, lush vegetative growth).

---

#### **Option B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**(a) [7 marks]** Differentiate between **macronutrients and micronutrients** in plant nutrition. List at least **four micronutrients** and explain the role of **chelated Fe** in protected cultivation. Why is **Fe-EDDHA** preferred over $\text{FeSO}_4$ in polyhouses?

**(b) [7 marks]** Describe the **step-by-step procedure for diagnosing nutrient deficiency** in a polyhouse crop. With the help of a table, list the **visible deficiency symptoms of N, P, K, Ca, and Mg** in tomato.

---

#### **Model Solution — Option B**

**(a) Macronutrients vs. Micronutrients + Chelated Fe [7 marks]**

| Aspect | Macronutrients | Micronutrients |
|---|---|---|
| **Required quantity** | > 100 mg/kg of plant dry matter | < 100 mg/kg |
| **Total in plant** | 0.1 – 10 % each | 0.001 – 0.1 % each |
| **Examples** | N, P, K, Ca, Mg, S | Fe, Mn, Zn, Cu, B, Mo, Cl, Ni |
| **Uptake form** | $\text{NO}_3^-$, $\text{NH}_4^+$, $\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4^-$, $\text{K}^+$, $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ | $\text{Fe}^{2+}$, $\text{Zn}^{2+}$, $\text{H}_3\text{BO}_3$, $\text{MoO}_4^{2-}$ |
| **Mobility in plant** | N, P, K, Mg = mobile; Ca, S = immobile | Fe, Mn, B, Cu = immobile; Mo, Zn = mobile |
| **Deficiency first seen on** | Older leaves (mobile) | Younger leaves (immobile) |

- **Four micronutrients [1 mark]:** Fe, Mn, Zn, B.
- **Role of chelated Fe in protected cultivation [2 marks]:**
  - Fe is essential for **chlorophyll synthesis** and electron transport (Fe-S clusters).
  - In polyhouses, **high pH drift** (often > 7) in media due to bicarbonate-rich irrigation water makes Fe unavailable.
  - **Chelation** keeps Fe in soluble form even at pH 6–8, preventing interveinal chlorosis.
- **Why Fe-EDDHA over $\text{FeSO}_4$ [3 marks]:**
  - At pH > 6.5, $\text{FeSO}_4$ rapidly oxidizes to insoluble $\text{Fe}^{3+}$ hydroxide — unavailable to roots.
  - **Fe-EDDHA** is stable up to **pH 9**, providing sustained Fe supply.
  - Chelation prevents Fe-P precipitation in concentrated stock tanks.
  - Higher **bioavailability** = lower dose needed = economical for recurring fertigation.
  - Reduced risk of Fe-staining on polyhouse cladding (aesthetic and photosynthetic benefit).

**(b) Deficiency Diagnosis Procedure + Symptoms Table [7 marks]**

**Diagnostic Procedure [3 marks]**
1. **Visual scouting** — start from older leaves (mobile) and younger leaves (immobile).
2. **Pattern recognition** — uniform yellowing (N, S), interveinal (Mg, Fe, Mn), marginal (K, Ca), distorted (B, Ca).
3. **Soil/media test** — confirm pH, EC, and available nutrient status.
4. **Plant tissue analysis** — send leaf samples to lab for confirmatory diagnosis.
5. **Drain analysis** — check EC and nutrient concentration in the leachate.
6. **Trial correction** — apply suspected nutrient as foliar spray; if symptoms reverse in 3–5 days, diagnosis confirmed.

**Deficiency Symptom Table for Tomato [4 marks]**

| Nutrient | Symptom in Tomato |
|---|---|
| **N (Nitrogen)** | Older leaves turn uniformly pale yellow; stems become thin; fruit size reduces. |
| **P (Phosphorus)** | Older leaves develop **purple/bronze tint** on undersides; delayed maturity. |
| **K (Potassium)** | **Marginal scorching** of older leaves; uneven fruit ripening (blotchy). |
| **Ca (Calcium)** | **Blossom End Rot (BER)** — black sunken spot at fruit tip; young leaves cupped. |
| **Mg (Magnesium)** | **Interveinal chlorosis** on older leaves; veins remain green. |

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common pitfalls where students lose marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Unit confusion:** Forgetting that **1 ppm = 1 mg/L** but **1 ppm ≠ 1 mg/kg** in soil (soil uses mg/kg or $\text{µg/g}$).
> 2. **Mixing elemental and oxide forms:** Question may ask for "120 kg P" — students must check if it is **elemental P** (×2.29 to get $\text{P}_2\text{O}_5$) or **$\text{P}_2\text{O}_5$** (÷2.29 to get elemental P). KTU frequently tests this.
> 3. **Ignoring losses:** Forgetting that urea loses 20–30% N via volatilization in unprotected conditions, and therefore the "calculated dose" is not the "applied dose."
> 4. **Confusing $\text{K}_2\text{O}$ with K element:** $\text{K}_2\text{O} \times 0.83 = \text{K}$ element; $\text{K} \times 1.20 = \text{K}_2\text{O}$.
> 5. **Missing the EC/pH link:** Fertigation questions often embed an EC target (e.g., 2.0 dS/m) — students must convert to TDS to relate to fertilizer quantity, which is a 2-step process.
> 6. **Ignoring compatibility:** Listing $\text{Ca(NO}_3\text{)}_2$ and $\text{K}_2\text{SO}_4$ together in a stock tank without mentioning **calcium sulphate precipitation** is a guaranteed half-mark deduction.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **Agrochemicals** = Fertilizers + Pesticides + PGRs + Biocontrol agents.
- ✅ **Fertilizer management** in polyhouses = 4R Stewardship: Right **S**ource, **R**ate, **T**ime, **P**lace.
- ✅ **Fertigation** delivers nutrients via drip; NUE rises from 30–50% (open field) to **80–90%** (protected).
- ✅ **Master formulas:**
  - $D = \dfrac{N_\text{req} \times 100}{P_\text{fert}}$ (fertilizer dose)
  - $C_\text{stock} = C_\text{irrigation} \times \text{injector ratio}$ (stock preparation)
  - $1 \text{ ppm} = 1 \text{ mg/L}$; $EC \times 640 \approx \text{TDS mg/L}$
- ✅ **Target root zone conditions:** EC 1.5–2.5 dS/m; pH 5.5–6.5.
- ✅ **Mobile nutrients (deficiency on old leaves):** N, P, K, Mg.
- ✅ **Immobile nutrients (deficiency on new leaves):** Ca, S, Fe, Mn, B, Cu, Zn.
- ✅ **Chelates preferred** for Fe, Zn in polyhouses because high media pH reduces ionic availability.
- ✅ **BER (Blossom End Rot) = Ca deficiency**, NOT a disease — fix by foliar $\text{CaCl}_2$ and uniform irrigation.
- ✅ **Stock tank rule:** Never mix **Ca sources with $\text{SO}_4$ or $\text{PO}_4$** sources in the same tank — precipitation occurs.
- ✅ **Leaching fraction 10–20%** is recommended to flush accumulated salts below the root zone.
- ✅ **PGRs commonly tested:** $\text{GA}_3$ (gibberellin — elongation), NAA (auxin — rooting/fruit set), Ethrel/Cepha (ethylene — ripening), CCC (cycocel — growth retardant).
- ✅ **Biocontrol trio:** *Trichoderma* (fungus), *Pseudomonas* (bacteria), *Beauveria bassiana* (entomo-pathogen).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Sources and Decision Making for Site Specific Management
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT751 - PRECISION FARMING TECHNIQUES FOR PROTECTED CULTIVATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Data Sources and Decision Making for Site Specific Management

# 📘 Data Sources and Decision Making for Site Specific Management

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Site-Specific Management (SSM)**, also called **Site-Specific Crop Management (SSCM)** or **Precision Crop Management (PCM)**, is a comprehensive, information-based, decision-making agricultural system designed to identify, analyze, and manage spatial and temporal variability *within* a production unit (field, greenhouse zone, or polyhouse compartment) for the purpose of optimizing profitability, sustainability, and resource-use efficiency.

In the context of **Protected Cultivation** (KTU PEAGT751), SSM extends to micro-management of:
- Greenhouse sub-sections (zones, benches, rows)
- Hydroponic/soilless culture zones
- Microclimate pockets inside polyhouses
- Individual plant or cluster-level management

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (Board Exam Ready):**
> *Site-Specific Management is the application of the right input, in the right amount, at the right place, and at the right time — using spatial data, sensing technologies, and Decision Support Systems (DSS) to match management practices with localized conditions rather than treating the field/greenhouse as a homogeneous unit.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a hospital that gives **the same dose of medicine to every patient** regardless of their weight, age, or disease severity. That would be absurd — and dangerous. 

Now think of traditional farming as that hospital: the **entire field receives the same amount of fertilizer, water, and pesticide**, even though different corners of the field have different soil, moisture, and nutrient levels.

**Site-Specific Management is like a modern hospital that customizes treatment per patient:**
- Each "patient" is a small **management zone (grid cell)**.
- Diagnostic tools (sensors, satellites, soil tests) are the **medical tests**.
- The **doctor's prescription** = the prescription/variable-rate map.
- The **nurse administering medicine** = the Variable Rate Applicator (VRA).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The unit of management shrinks from "field" → "sub-plot" → "grid cell" → in protected cultivation, even to **individual plant or sensor node**. This granularity is what defines precision.

### 1.3 Why SSM Matters in Protected Cultivation

Inside a **polyhouse or greenhouse**, microclimate gradients (light, temperature, humidity, CO₂) can vary dramatically over a few meters. A tomato plant near the ventilation fan experiences different conditions than one in the centre. SSM captures and responds to these gradients.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Pillars of SSM (Memorize for KTU):**
> 1. **Data Acquisition** (sensors, GPS, RS, scouting)
> 2. **Data Interpretation & Decision** (GIS, models, DSS, expert systems)
> 3. **Variable-Rate Implementation** (actuators, VRA, automated irrigation/fertigation)

### 1.4 Spatial vs. Temporal Variability

| Type | Description | Example in Polyhouse |
|---|---|---|
| **Spatial Variability** | Differences *across* location | North side cooler than south side |
| **Temporal Variability** | Differences *across* time | Morning humidity higher than afternoon |
| **Spatio-Temporal Variability** | Both combined | Disease spread over 7 days across rows |

> [!NOTE]
> In **protected cultivation**, temporal variability is often more dominant than in open-field farming, because the greenhouse environment is artificially controlled yet still subject to diurnal cycles, seasonal shifts, and weather influences through vents.
