# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**Define yield monitoring and yield mapping. List any four components of a yield monitoring system used in protected cultivation.**

**Model Answer:**

**Yield Monitoring** is the real-time measurement and recording of the quantity of crop produce (mass, volume, or count) during the harvesting or post-harvest handling process, using a network of sensors integrated with a positioning system. **Yield Mapping** is the spatial visualization of the georeferenced yield data as a thematic map to depict intra-greenhouse or intra-field variability of productivity.

**Four Components:**

1. **Mass/Volume Sensor** — Load cell, impact plate, or optical sensor to measure produce quantity.
2. **Positioning System** — GNSS receiver with RTK, or indoor positioning (UWB, BLE) for protected structures.
3. **Data Logger / Processor** — Microcontroller or PLC with onboard memory.
4. **Display / GIS Software** — For real-time visualization and post-processing into a yield map.

> [!NOTE]
> *Word limit for Part A in KTU ESE: 30–50 words for definitions; bullet points for components. Marks awarded for keyword presence.*

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**Differentiate between mass flow yield monitors and volumetric yield monitors. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Mass Flow Yield Monitor | Volumetric Yield Monitor |
|---|---|---|
| **Measured quantity** | Mass (kg) per unit time | Volume (m³ or L) per unit time |
| **Working principle** | Load cell / impact force / weighing | Ultrasonic level sensor, optical volume |
| **Output** | Direct mass (kg/s) | Volume → mass via density ($\rho$) |
| **Accuracy** | Higher (±1–2%) | Lower (±3–5%) due to density variability |
| **Example** | Load-cell weighbridge on conveyor | Ultrasonic sensor in collection bin |

**Example of mass flow monitor:** A piezoelectric impact-plate sensor mounted under a tomato conveyor.
**Example of volumetric monitor:** An ultrasonic sensor measuring the rise in produce level inside a collection tray.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1): **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the architecture of a yield monitoring system suitable for a polyhouse cultivating tomatoes. Describe in detail the function of each layer with suitable examples of sensors and positioning systems.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A polyhouse has the following yield data at 4 sample locations. Using the Inverse Distance Weighting (IDW) method with power parameter $p = 2$, estimate the yield at the centre of the polyhouse (2.5, 2.5).

| Point | $x$ (m) | $y$ (m) | Yield (kg/m²) |
|---|---|---|---|
| P1 | 0 | 0 | 3.5 |
| P2 | 5 | 0 | 4.0 |
| P3 | 0 | 5 | 4.5 |
| P4 | 5 | 5 | 3.0 |

---

### Question A — Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Architecture of Yield Monitoring System for Polyhouse Tomatoes

The yield monitoring system for a polyhouse cultivating tomatoes is organized in **five functional layers**:

**Layer 1 — Sensing Layer:**
- **Load cells** on harvest trays: measure cumulative mass of tomatoes in each picking crate (capacity 0–50 kg, accuracy ±0.05%).
- **Optical counters** (photoelectric break-beam sensors) on the grading conveyor: count individual fruits passing per minute.
- **RGB-D cameras** above the canopy: estimate fruit count and predicted mass via machine vision.
- **NIR spectroscopy sensor**: inline Brix and dry-matter content measurement.

**Layer 2 — Processing Layer:**
- A **Programmable Logic Controller (PLC)** or industrial microcontroller aggregates sensor data, applies a **Kalman filter** to remove vibration noise, and time-stamps each reading with a real-time clock synchronized via NTP.

**Layer 3 — Positioning Layer:**
- Since GPS signals are attenuated inside polyhouses, **Ultra-Wideband (UWB) beacons** mounted on the polyhouse pillars (at least 4 anchors) provide **decimeter-level indoor positioning** of the harvest trolley.
- The trolley carries a UWB tag that trilaterates its position with reference to the anchors.

**Layer 4 — Storage Layer:**
- All records are stored locally on an **industrial SD card** (for resilience) and pushed to a **cloud database via MQTT over Wi-Fi/LoRaWAN** for redundancy.

**Layer 5 — Visualization Layer:**
- A **GIS dashboard** (e.g., QGIS with PostGIS backend) renders the yield points as a heat map and contour map.
- Management zones (Zone A, B, C) are color-coded: **green** for high yield, **yellow** for medium, **red** for low.

#### Part (b) — IDW Solution

**Step 1 — Compute distances** from (2.5, 2.5) to each point:

$$
d_1 = \sqrt{(2.5)^2 + (2.5)^2} = \sqrt{12.5} \approx 3.536 \text{ m}
$$
$$
d_2 = \sqrt{(-2.5)^2 + (2.5)^2} = \sqrt{12.5} \approx 3.536 \text{ m}
$$
$$
d_3 = \sqrt{(2.5)^2 + (-2.5)^2} = \sqrt{12.5} \approx 3.536 \text{ m}
$$
$$
d_4 = \sqrt{(-2.5)^2 + (-2.5)^2} = \sqrt{12.5} \approx 3.536 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute weights** with $p = 2$:

$$
w_1 = w_2 = w_3 = w_4 = \dfrac{1}{12.5} = 0.0800
$$

**Step 3 — Weighted sum:**

$$
\sum w_i Z_i = 0.0800 \times (3.5 + 4.0 + 4.5 + 3.0) = 0.0800 \times 15.0 = 1.200
$$

**Step 4 — Sum of weights:**

$$
W = 4 \times 0.0800 = 0.320
$$

**Step 5 — Interpolated yield:**

$$
Z^{*}(2.5, 2.5) = \dfrac{1.200}{0.320} = 3.75 \text{ kg/m}^2
$$

**Step 6 — Symmetry check:** Mean of four values = (3.5 + 4.0 + 4.5 + 3.0)/4 = 15.0/4 = **3.75 kg/m²** ✓ (matches by symmetry).

**Conclusion:** The estimated yield at the polyhouse centre is **3.75 kg/m²**.

---

### Question B (Choice 2): **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model — Internal Choice]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is a yield map? Explain the step-by-step procedure for generating a yield map using GIS software. Discuss any three interpolation methods used in yield mapping with their relative merits and demerits.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A greenhouse cucumber yield monitor was tested with 5 reference loads. The recorded values and actual values are given below. Compute the calibration equation $M_{ref} = a \cdot M_{obs} + b$ using the least-squares method, and find the $R^2$ value.

| Trial | $M_{obs}$ (kg) | $M_{ref}$ (kg) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1.0 | 0.9 |
| 2 | 2.5 | 2.7 |
| 3 | 4.0 | 3.8 |
| 4 | 6.0 | 6.2 |
| 5 | 8.0 | 7.8 |

---

### Question B — Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Yield Map Definition and Procedure

A **yield map** is a geo-referenced thematic map that represents the spatial distribution of crop yield (kg/m² or t/ha) across a field or a protected structure. It is generated by aggregating individual georeferenced yield measurements, interpolating the sparse data into a continuous surface, and classifying the surface into management zones.

**Procedure for generating a yield map:**

1. **Data Collection:** Harvest data is collected with a yield monitor (load cell + GPS/IPS) at regular time intervals (e.g., every 1 second) or at fixed positions.
2. **Pre-processing:** Remove noise using threshold, speed, and Kalman filters. Exclude erroneous points (negative mass, zero velocity, etc.).
3. **Georeferencing:** Assign coordinates (X, Y) to every yield record.
4. **Grid Cell Aggregation:** Divide the area into a uniform grid (e.g., 1 m × 1 m) and compute the mean yield for all points falling within each cell.
5. **Spatial Interpolation:** Apply IDW, Kriging, or Spline to estimate yield at unsampled grid points.
6. **Contouring:** Generate iso-yield lines connecting points of equal yield.
7. **Zone Classification:** Classify the map into Low / Medium / High zones using standard deviation breaks or quantile classification.
8. **Visualization:** Render in GIS software (QGIS/ArcGIS) with colour ramp (red–yellow–green) and overlay with ancillary maps (soil EC, irrigation, climate).

**Three Interpolation Methods:**

**1. Inverse Distance Weighting (IDW):**
- *Merits:* Simple, deterministic, no variogram fitting required, computationally fast.
- *Demerits:* No statistical confidence intervals; produces "bull's eye" artefacts around data clusters; sensitive to outliers.

**2. Kriging:**
- *Merits:* Best linear unbiased estimator (BLUE); provides prediction variance (kriging variance); handles anisotropy.
- *Demerits:* Requires semivariogram modelling; computationally heavy; needs stationary dataset; expert knowledge required.

**3. Spline Interpolation:**
- *Merits:* Produces smooth, visually appealing surfaces; ideal for dense data.
- *Demerits:* Can overshoot/undershoot at sharp boundaries; poor at extrapolating beyond data range; no uncertainty quantification.

#### Part (b) — Calibration of Cucumber Yield Monitor

**Step 1 — Compute means:**

$$
\bar{M}_{obs} = \dfrac{1.0 + 2.5 + 4.0 + 6.0 + 8.0}{5} = \dfrac{21.5}{5} = 4.30 \text{ kg}
$$

$$
\bar{M}_{ref} = \dfrac{0.9 + 2.7 + 3.8 + 6.2 + 7.8}{5} = \dfrac{21.4}{5} = 4.28 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute slope $a$:**

**Numerator terms** $\sum (M_{obs,i} - \bar{M}_{obs})(M_{ref,i} - \bar{M}_{ref})$:

$$
(1.0 - 4.30)(0.9 - 4.28) = (-3.30)(-3.38) = 11.154
$$
$$
(2.5 - 4.30)(2.7 - 4.28) = (-1.80)(-1.58) = 2.844
$$
$$
(4.0 - 4.30)(3.8 - 4.28) = (-0.30)(-0.48) = 0.144
$$
$$
(6.0 - 4.30)(6.2 - 4.28) = (1.70)(1.92) = 3.264
$$
$$
(8.0 - 4.30)(7.8 - 4.28) = (3.70)(3.52) = 13.024
$$

**Sum of numerator** = $11.154 + 2.844 + 0.144 + 3.264 + 13.024 = 30.430$

**Denominator terms** $\sum (M_{obs,i} - \bar{M}_{obs})^2$:

$$
(-3.30)^2 = 10.890
$$
$$
(-1.80)^2 = 3.240
$$
$$
(-0.30)^2 = 0.090
$$
$$
(1.70)^2 = 2.890
$$
$$
(3.70)^2 = 13.690
$$

**Sum of denominator** = $10.890 + 3.240 + 0.090 + 2.890 + 13.690 = 30.800$

**Step 3 — Slope:**

$$
a = \dfrac{30.430}{30.800} \approx 0.9880
$$

**Step 4 — Intercept:**

$$
b = \bar{M}_{ref} - a \cdot \bar{M}_{obs} = 4.28 - 0.9880 \times 4.30 = 4.28 - 4.2484 = 0.0316
$$

**Step 5 — Calibration Equation:**

$$
\boxed{M_{ref} = 0.9880 \cdot M_{obs} + 0.0316 \text{ kg}}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute $R^2$:**

**Predicted values** $M_{pred,i} = 0.9880 \cdot M_{obs,i} + 0.0316$:

$$
M_{pred,1} = 0.9880(1.0) + 0.0316 = 1.0196
$$
$$
M_{pred,2} = 0.9880(2.5) + 0.0316 = 2.5016
$$
$$
M_{pred,3} = 0.9880(4.0) + 0.0316 = 3.9836
$$
$$
M_{pred,4} = 0.9880(6.0) + 0.0316 = 5.9596
$$
$$
M_{pred,5} = 0.9880(8.0) + 0.0316 = 7.9356
$$

**Residuals** $e_i = M_{ref,i} - M_{pred,i}$:

$$
e_1 = 0.9 - 1.0196 = -0.1196
$$
$$
e_2 = 2.7 - 2.5016 = 0.1984
$$
$$
e_3 = 3.8 - 3.9836 = -0.1836
$$
$$
e_4 = 6.2 - 5.9596 = 0.2404
$$
$$
e_5 = 7.8 - 7.9356 = -0.1356
$$

**Sum of squared residuals (SSR):**

$$
SSR = (-0.1196)^2 + (0.1984)^2 + (-0.1836)^2 + (0.2404)^2 + (-0.1356)^2
$$
$$
SSR = 0.0143 + 0.0394 + 0.0337 + 0.0578 + 0.0184 = 0.1636
$$

**Total sum of squares (SST):**

$$
SST = \sum (M_{ref,i} - \bar{M}_{ref})^2
$$
$$
SST = (0.9-4.28)^2 + (2.7-4.28)^2 + (3.8-4.28)^2 + (6.2-4.28)^2 + (7.8-4.28)^2
$$
$$
SST = 11.4244 + 2.4964 + 0.2304 + 3.6864 + 12.3904 = 30.228
$$

**Step 7 — Coefficient of determination:**

$$
R^2 = 1 - \dfrac{SSR}{SST} = 1 - \dfrac{0.1636}{30.228} = 1 - 0.00541 = 0.9946
$$

**Interpretation:** $R^2 = 0.9946$ indicates an **excellent fit**; the yield monitor requires only minor calibration. The monitor slightly **over-reads** at low masses (positive intercept $b \approx 0.03$ kg) and shows a near-unity slope — within typical KTU-acceptable accuracy of **±2%**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing yield monitor with yield map.** Yield monitor is the *hardware system*; yield map is the *GIS output*. Examiners award 0 marks if these are interchanged.
> 2. **Forgetting units.** Always write "kg/m²" or "t/ha" — a numerical answer without units loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Skipping the sym-variogram discussion for Kriging.** A common 14-mark question asks you to compare Kriging with IDW; failing to mention the *semi-variogram* $\gamma(h)$ and *nugget/sill/range* loses 3–4 marks.
> 4. **Using `|` for absolute value in tables.** Use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX tables to avoid markdown table breakage.
> 5. **Not distinguishing open-field vs protected cultivation.** PEAGT751 specifically deals with protected cultivation; an answer that ignores the polyhouse/greenhouse context loses CO-mapping marks under KTU 2024 outcome-based evaluation.
> 6. **Skipping the calibration step.** A yield monitor without calibration is a theoretical device; the linear regression calibration is a *mandatory* sub-step.
> 7. **Wrong use of IDW formula.** Many students write $w_i = d_i^p$ instead of $w_i = 1/d_i^p$. This single sign error leads to a fully inverted map.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Yield Monitoring and Mapping**

- [x] **Yield Monitoring** = real-time sensor-based mass/volume measurement; **Yield Mapping** = post-processed spatial visualization.
- [x] Four core components: **Sensor, Positioning, Processor, Visualizer**.
- [x] Sensor types: **Load cell, Impact plate, Optical, NIR, Ultrasonic, RGB-D camera**.
- [x] Positioning: **GNSS/RTK outdoor**; **UWB/BLE/Wi-Fi indoor** (protected cultivation).
- [x] Key formula: $Q(t) = \dfrac{(F(t) - F_{tare}) \cdot v(t)}{g \cdot L}$ — mass flow rate on a conveyor with weighing section.
- [x] Yield per cell: $Y_i = \dfrac{M_i}{A_i}$ in **kg/m²** or **t/ha**.
- [x] Variability indices: $\sigma^2$ (variance) and $CV = \sigma/\bar{Y} \times 100\%$ (coefficient of variation).
- [x] **IDW formula:** $Z^{*}(x_0) = \dfrac{\sum w_i Z_i}{\sum w_i}$ with $w_i = 1/d_i^p$, **$p = 2$** most common.
- [x] **Kriging** is geostatistical and provides prediction variance; requires **semivariogram** $\gamma(h)$ with nugget, sill, and range.
- [x] **Calibration equation:** $M_{ref} = a \cdot M_{obs} + b$ using least-squares; acceptable $R^2 > 0.95$.
- [x] Pipeline order: **Data → Filter → Aggregate → Interpolate → Contour → Classify → Map**.
- [x] $g = 9.81$ **m/s²** is the standard gravity constant (must be quoted in derivations).
- [x] In protected cultivation, replace "field" with **management zone / bench / bed**; use **UWB IPS** instead of GPS.
- [x] Yield maps drive **Variable Rate Technology (VRT)** for fertigation, climate, and labour in greenhouses.
- [x] **Threshold filter, Speed filter, Kalman filter** are the three standard noise-removal steps.
- [x] Always overlay yield maps with **ancillary maps** (EC, pH, climate) for diagnostic interpretation.
- [x] $HDOP < 2$ is the benchmark for acceptable GPS quality in yield monitoring.
- [x] Distinguish **mass flow** vs **volumetric** yield monitors — they have different accuracies and use-cases.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Soil Sampling and Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT751 - PRECISION FARMING TECHNIQUES FOR PROTECTED CULTIVATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Soil Sampling and Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Soil Sampling and Analysis** is the systematic, statistically-valid scientific procedure of collecting representative soil specimens from a defined land area (open field or polyhouse/protected structure) and subjecting them to physical, chemical, and biological laboratory evaluation. In the context of **Precision Farming (PF)** and **Protected Cultivation**, it forms the foundational **geo-referenced input layer** upon which variable-rate fertilizer prescription maps, drip-fertigation schedules, substrate blending recipes, and yield-zone delineations are constructed. KTU 2024 Scheme treats it as the pre-requisite decision-support input for the Site-Specific Crop Management (SSCM) loop.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEAGT751 / M3):** Soil sampling must be understood as a **geo-spatial data acquisition** process — not a random chemical test. The output is a **Spatial Fertility Map** that drives variable rate technology (VRT), not a single farm-average recommendation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **doctor diagnosing a patient**. The doctor does not test the entire body — he draws **blood samples from specific points**, runs them through tests (hemoglobin, sugar, cholesterol), and based on the *spatial distribution* of abnormalities, prescribes a *zone-specific* treatment.

Soil sampling works identically:
- The **field** = the patient's body.
- The **soil core** = the blood sample.
- The **laboratory** = the diagnostic lab.
- The **GPS coordinates** of each sample = the patient's organ-map.
- The **fertility map** = the diagnostic report.
- The **variable-rate fertilizer prescription** = the personalized medicine.

A single mixed "composite" sample tells you *"the field is sick"*. A *grid-based geo-referenced* sample tells you *"the north-east corner is potassium-deficient and the south-west is over-saline"* — that is **precision**.

### 1.3 Core Standard Metrics & Constants

- Standard sampling depth for arable crops: **0 – 15 cm** (plough layer) and **15 – 30 cm** (sub-plough).
- Standard sampling depth for deep-rooted / perennial protected crops: **0 – 30 cm** and **30 – 60 cm**.
- One composite sample must represent a **uniform area of ≤ 2 hectares** (≈ 5 acres) for conventional farming and **≤ 0.4 ha** for high-value protected cultivation.
- A composite sample = **8 to 15 sub-samples** mixed thoroughly.
- Standard soil-sieve aperture for analysis: **2 mm**.
- Standard pH (1:2 soil:water suspension) and EC (dS/m) are the **first** parameters measured.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Soil Sampling is the Heart of Precision Farming:**
> Without an accurate spatial fertility map, the entire precision farming loop (VRT → variable fertigation → variable yield → profit) collapses into guesswork. **Garbage In → Garbage Out (GIGO)** applies brutally to soil data.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Workflow — Step-by-Step Logic

The complete Soil Sampling & Analysis chain consists of **six sequential stages**:

**Stage 1 — Pre-Survey & Grid Design**
- Obtain cadastral map / Google Earth image of the protected structure or open field.
- Overlay a **geo-referenced grid**. Two dominant approaches:
  - **Grid Sampling:** Uniform square/rectangular cells (e.g., 0.25 ha, 0.5 ha, 1.0 ha grids).
  - **Zone / Management Zone Sampling:** Cells defined by prior yield maps, ECa (apparent electrical conductivity) surveys, or topography.
- Mark sampling points with **DGPS** (Differential Global Positioning System) — accuracy **± 1 m** preferred.

**Stage 2 — Sample Collection (The "How")**
- Use a clean **soil auger** (screw type for moist soils), **soil probe / tube** (for dry/hard soils), or **Dutch auger**.
- Sampling depth must be consistent — use a marked stick.
- Avoid:
  - Field bunds, channels, and shade-lines.
  - Spots near manure pits, irrigation outlets, or trees.
  - Recently fertilized zones (wait **15 – 30 days** for basal application, longer for top-dress).
- Collect **8 – 15 sub-samples per composite** in a **W-pattern or Z-pattern** within the cell.

**Stage 3 — Composite Preparation**
- Mix the 8–15 sub-samples in a clean plastic bucket → **quartering** to ~ **500 g**.
- Air-dry in shade (NOT direct sun) for **24 – 48 h**.
- Crush gently, sieve through **2 mm** mesh.
- Label bag with: **Farmer ID, Field ID, Grid ID, GPS Lat/Long, Depth, Date, Crop history**.

**Stage 4 — Laboratory Analysis**
- **Physical tests:** Texture (Hydrometer / Pipette), Bulk density (Core method), Particle density, Porosity, Water Holding Capacity (WHC), Infiltration rate.
- **Chemical tests:** pH, EC, Organic Carbon (Walkley-Black), Available N (Subbiah & Asija), Available P (Bray's / Olsen's), Available K (Flame photometer), Exchangeable Ca, Mg, S, Micronutrients (Zn, Fe, Mn, Cu — DTPA extractant), CEC, SAR.
- **Biological tests:** Microbial Biomass Carbon (MBC), Soil Respiration (CO₂ evolution).

**Stage 5 — Interpretation & Fertilizer Recommendation**
- Compare values against **STCR (Soil Test Crop Response)** or **Muhr's rating chart** (Low / Medium / High).
- Compute **Soil Test Index** for each nutrient.
- Compute **Fertilizer Dose** using:
  - **STCR equation** (Targeted yield approach)
  - **General fertilizer recommendation equation**

**Stage 6 — Map Generation & Variable-Rate Application**
- Use GIS software (QGIS, ArcGIS) to interpolate point data via **Inverse Distance Weighting (IDW)** or **Kriging**.
- Generate **thematic fertility maps** (N-map, P-map, K-map, pH-map, EC-map).
- Feed prescription to **Variable Rate Fertilizer Applicator / Fertigation Controller**.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---------|---------------------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Particle Density (PD) | $PD = \dfrac{Mass\ of\ oven-dry\ soil}{Volume\ of\ soil\ solids}$ | $\text{Mg/m}^3$ | Pycnometer method |
| 2 | Bulk Density (BD) | $BD = \dfrac{Mass\ of\ oven-dry\ soil}{Total\ volume\ of\ core}$ | $\text{Mg/m}^3$ | Core / Clod method |
| 3 | Porosity (n) | $n = \left(1 - \dfrac{BD}{PD}\right) \times 100$ | % | Pore space |
| 4 | Gravimetric Soil Moisture (θg) | $\theta_g = \dfrac{M_{wet} - M_{dry}}{M_{dry}} \times 100$ | % | Oven at **105 °C** for **24 h** |
| 5 | Volumetric Moisture (θv) | $\theta_v = \theta_g \times BD$ | % | For irrigation scheduling |
| 6 | Available Water Capacity (AWC) | $AWC = \dfrac{(FC - PWP)}{100} \times BD \times d$ | mm | d = root depth (mm) |
| 7 | Soil pH (1:2.5 suspension) | $pH = -\log_{10}[H^+]$ | — | Calomel / Glass electrode |
| 8 | Electrical Conductivity (EC) | $EC = \dfrac{Cell\ constant}{Resistance\ of\ suspension}$ | $\text{dS/m}$ | 1:2 soil:water |
| 9 | Available Nitrogen (N) — Subbiah & Asija | $Avail\ N\ (kg/ha) = \%\ OM \times 224$ | kg/ha | Where OM ≈ OC × 1.724 |
| 10 | Available Phosphorus (P) — Olsen's | $P\ (kg/ha) = R \times DF \times 2.24$ | kg/ha | Spectrophotometer |
| 11 | Available Potassium (K) | $K\ (kg/ha) = R \times DF \times 1.21$ | kg/ha | Flame photometer |
| 12 | Cation Exchange Capacity (CEC) | $CEC = \sum \dfrac{C_i \times V_i}{1000}$ | $\text{cmol}(c)/\text{kg}$ | Sum of exchangeable cations |
| 13 | Sodium Adsorption Ratio (SAR) | $SAR = \dfrac{[Na^+]}{\sqrt{\frac{[Ca^{2+}] + [Mg^{2+}]}{2}}}$ | — | meq/L basis |
| 14 | Exchangeable Sodium Percentage (ESP) | $ESP = \dfrac{\text{Exchangeable Na}}{CEC} \times 100$ | % | Alkalinity indicator |
| 15 | Fertilizer Dose (STCR) | $FD = \dfrac{(STV \times CF) - (SCS)}{UFC}$ | kg/ha | Targeted yield model |
| 16 | Composite Sample Count | $N = \dfrac{A_{field}}{A_{grid}} \times 8\ to\ 15$ | samples | 8–15 cores per composite |
| 17 | Kriging Variance | $\gamma(h) = \dfrac{1}{2N(h)} \sum (Z_i - Z_{i+h})^2$ | — | Semi-variogram |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Convention (KTU standard):** Nutrients in soil are reported in **kg/ha** for field use, **ppm** or **mg/kg** in lab reports, and **%** for organic carbon. Always verify the form — 1 ppm ≈ 2.24 kg/ha (assuming 0–15 cm depth and BD = 1.33 Mg/m³).

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Drip-Fertigation Scheduling in Polyhouses:** Real-time N-P-K maps drive NPK stock-solution injection ratios (e.g., 1:1:2 for tomato flowering stage).
- **Variable Rate Lime Application:** Grid pH map → lime prescription to neutralize acidity zone-specifically.
- **Substrate Blending for Soilless Protected Cultivation:** Soil analysis guides the base-substrate selection (coco-peat, perlite, vermiculite ratios) when transitioning from soil to hydroponic production.
- **Carbon Sequestration Audits:** Organic-C maps feed MRV (Measurement, Reporting, Verification) for climate-smart agriculture.
- **Salinity Management in Coastal Protected Structures:** EC + SAR maps drive leaching fraction design.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Bulk Density, Particle Density, and Porosity Relationship

**Given:** A soil core of total volume $V_t = 100\ cm^3$ (taken with a standard core sampler).
- Oven-dry mass of soil in the core: $M_s = 133\ g$.
- Pycnometer-measured volume of soil solids only: $V_s = 50\ cm^3$.
- Volume of water to fill pore space: $V_w = 47\ cm^3$ (saturated).

**Find:** Bulk Density, Particle Density, and Porosity.

**Step 1 — Compute Bulk Density (BD).**
Bulk density is the ratio of oven-dry mass to the *total (bulk)* volume of the undisturbed core.

$$
BD = \frac{M_s}{V_t}
$$

Substitute the values:

$$
BD = \frac{133\ g}{100\ cm^3} = 1.33\ g/cm^3
$$

Convert to standard SI unit (Mg/m³):

$$
1\ g/cm^3 = 1\ Mg/m^3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad BD = 1.33\ Mg/m^3
$$

> **Logic:** A typical mineral soil lies between 1.10 – 1.60 Mg/m³; values > 1.6 indicate compaction (a problem in heavy machinery traffic zones of protected structure floors).

---

**Step 2 — Compute Particle Density (PD).**
Particle density is the ratio of oven-dry mass to the *solid-only* volume (measured by pycnometer — water displacement).

$$
PD = \frac{M_s}{V_s}
$$

Substitute:

$$
PD = \frac{133\ g}{50\ cm^3} = 2.66\ g/cm^3
$$

> **Logic:** Pure mineral soils average 2.65 Mg/m³ (quartz density); peat/organic soils fall to 1.0 – 1.4 Mg/m³. This value is a quality check for organic-matter contamination.

---

**Step 3 — Compute Porosity (n).**
Porosity is the fraction of total volume occupied by pores (water + air).

$$
n = \left(1 - \frac{BD}{PD}\right) \times 100
$$

Substitute:

$$
n = \left(1 - \frac{1.33}{2.66}\right) \times 100
$$

Simplify the fraction:

$$
n = (1 - 0.5) \times 100 = 50\%
$$

> **Logic:** Ideal agricultural soil porosity = **45 – 55%**. Macropores (aeration) should be ≈ 1/3, micropores (water storage) ≈ 2/3. Protected-cultivation floors often show reduced macro-porosity due to foot-traffic.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Grid Sample Number for a Protected Structure

**Given:** A polyhouse (protected structure) of dimensions **50 m × 30 m = 1500 m²** (0.15 ha).
- Grid resolution chosen: **10 m × 10 m = 100 m²** (0.01 ha) per cell.
- Sub-samples per composite: **10 cores**.
- Sampling depth: **0 – 15 cm and 15 – 30 cm** (two depth composites per cell).

**Find:** Total number of composite samples, total number of sub-samples, and total weight of soil handled.

**Step 1 — Number of grid cells (composites).**

$$
N_{cells} = \frac{A_{field}}{A_{cell}} = \frac{50 \times 30}{10 \times 10} = \frac{1500}{100} = 15\ cells
$$

**Step 2 — Number of depth composites** (two depths per cell).

$$
N_{depth\ composites} = 15 \times 2 = 30
$$

**Step 3 — Total sub-samples (cores).**

$$
N_{cores} = 30 \times 10 = 300\ cores
$$

**Step 4 — Total soil mass (assuming 250 g per core).**

$$
M_{total} = 300 \times 250\ g = 75{,}000\ g = 75\ kg
$$

**Step 5 — Mass sent to lab after quartering (≈ 500 g per composite).**

$$
M_{lab} = 30 \times 500\ g = 15{,}000\ g = 15\ kg
$$

> **Logic:** 75 kg of field soil is collected, but only 15 kg reaches the laboratory. The quartering process is critical — improper quartering destroys representativeness. This is a **common KTU exam trap** — students often compute only one depth.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — STCR-Based Fertilizer Recommendation

**Given:** A polyhouse tomato target yield $Y_T = 100\ t/ha$.
- Soil Test Value (STV) for available N: $280\ kg/ha$ (medium range).
- STCR calibration equation for N (tomato, Kerala conditions):

$$
F_N = 6.5\,Y_T - 0.45\,STV
$$

Where $F_N$ = fertilizer N dose (kg/ha), $Y_T$ = target yield (t/ha), STV = soil test value (kg/ha).

**Find:** Recommended N dose.

**Step 1 — Substitute target yield.**

$$
F_N = 6.5 \times 100 - 0.45 \times 280
$$

**Step 2 — Multiply term by term.**

$$
F_N = 650 - 126
$$

**Step 3 — Subtract.**

$$
F_N = 524\ kg\ N/ha
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to urea (46 % N).**

$$
Urea = \frac{F_N}{0.46} = \frac{524}{0.46} \approx 1139\ kg\ urea/ha
$$

> **Logic:** STCR (Soil Test Crop Response) equations are **site-and-crop-specific** and integrate (a) the indigenous nutrient supply, (b) the crop's uptake requirement, and (c) the fertilizer use efficiency. This is the **gold standard** of site-specific nutrient management under KTU's 2024 precision-farming module.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sampling Method Selection

| Field Condition | Recommended Method | Grid Size | Sub-samples | Rationale |
|----------------|-------------------|-----------|-------------|-----------|
| Uniform open field, low variability | Composite (bulk) | 1 composite per 2 ha | 10 – 15 | Cost-effective baseline |
| Protected structure (polyhouse) | Grid | 100 – 400 m² per cell | 8 – 10 | High-value crop, intensive |
| Saline-prone coastal field | Grid + ECa survey | 2500 m² per cell | 10 – 12 | Salinity is highly variable |
| Sloping / hilly terrain | Contour-zone | 1 zone per slope class | 8 – 12 | Erosion gradient |
| Long-term experiment plots | Grid (uniform) | 100 m² per cell | 5 – 8 | Statistical power |
| Organic farm | Zone-based on management | Variable | 10 | Different amendment zones |
| Subsurface drip fertigation trial | Grid + multi-depth | 100 m² per cell | 10, two depths | Nutrient stratification |

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Soil Test Rating (Muhr's Chart Simplified)

| Nutrient | Low | Medium | High | Unit |
|----------|-----|--------|------|------|
| Available N (Subbiah) | < 280 | 280 – 560 | > 560 | kg/ha |
| Available P (Olsen) | < 11 | 11 – 22 | > 22 | kg/ha |
| Available K | < 140 | 140 – 280 | > 280 | kg/ha |
| Organic Carbon | < 0.5 | 0.5 – 0.75 | > 0.75 | % |
| pH (1:2.5) | < 6.0 (acidic) | 6.0 – 7.5 | > 7.5 (alkaline) | — |
| EC (1:2) | < 1.0 | 1.0 – 2.0 | > 2.0 (saline) | dS/m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked *"Classify the soil"*, you must state **pH, EC, OC, N, P, K** values with their *rating category* in a single tabular answer. Examiners deduct marks for incomplete classification.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme]**
*Define soil sampling. List any two standard soil sampling tools used in protected cultivation.*

**Model Answer:**
Soil sampling is the systematic process of collecting representative soil specimens from a defined field or protected-structure zone for laboratory analysis of physical, chemical, and biological properties.
**Two standard tools:**
1. **Soil Auger (Screw type)** — for moist, soft soils.
2. **Soil Probe / Tube Auger** — for dry, hard soils.
*(Acceptable: Dutch auger, Piston sampler, Post-hole auger.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme]**
*State the importance of geo-referencing in soil sampling for precision farming.*

**Model Answer:**
Geo-referencing (DGPS-based latitude-longitude tagging) of every soil sample converts point data into a **spatial database**, enabling:
1. **Interpolation** of point data into continuous fertility maps (N, P, K, pH, EC).
2. **Zone-specific fertilizer prescription** via Variable Rate Technology (VRT).
3. **Temporal comparison** across seasons for yield-zone stability analysis.
Without geo-referencing, a soil analysis is a number — not a map. The field cannot be precision-managed.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** Describe the step-by-step procedure for **grid-based geo-referenced soil sampling** in a 0.5 ha polyhouse. Discuss the rationale for choosing an appropriate grid size and sub-sample count. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the laboratory analysis protocol for the **physical properties** (texture, bulk density, porosity) and **chemical properties** (pH, EC, OC, available N, P, K) of a composite soil sample, including the principle behind each test. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

1. **Step 1 — Reconnaissance & Map Acquisition:** Obtain a scaled layout of the polyhouse, identify crop rows, irrigation lines, and shade patterns. **(1 mark)**
2. **Step 2 — Grid Design:** For a 0.5 ha polyhouse, a **10 m × 10 m grid (100 m²)** is recommended — giving **50 cells**. This balances spatial resolution with analytical cost. **(1 mark)**
3. **Step 3 — DGPS Marking:** Each cell centroid is marked using a DGPS receiver (accuracy ± 1 m); coordinates logged in a GIS database. **(1 mark)**
4. **Step 4 — Tool Selection:** Soil probe for the upper 0–15 cm and 15–30 cm layers; use marked sticks for consistent depth. **(1 mark)**
5. **Step 5 — W-Pattern Collection:** Within each cell, walk a W-pattern collecting **10 sub-samples** at 2 m intervals, avoiding irrigation outlets and bunds. **(1 mark)**
6. **Step 6 — Composite Preparation:** Mix 10 sub-samples in a clean bucket → quarter to 500 g → air-dry in shade (24–48 h) → sieve through 2 mm mesh. **(1 mark)**
7. **Step 7 — Labelling & Dispatch:** Label each bag with (Farm ID, Grid ID, GPS lat/long, depth, date, crop). Send to lab. **(0.5 mark)**
8. **Rationale:** Grid size determines *spatial resolution vs cost*. Smaller grids (5 m × 5 m) reveal micro-variability but escalate lab costs exponentially; 10 m × 10 m is the KTU-recommended optimum for high-value protected crops. **(0.5 mark)**

**Model Solution (b):**

| Test | Method | Principle | Unit | Marks |
|------|--------|-----------|------|-------|
| **Texture** | Hydrometer (Bouyoucos) | Stokes' law of settling | % sand/silt/clay | 1.5 |
| **Bulk Density** | Core method | Mass of oven-dry soil per unit total volume | Mg/m³ | 1.0 |
| **Porosity** | Calculated | $n = \left(1 - \dfrac{BD}{PD}\right) \times 100$ | % | 0.5 |
| **pH** | Glass electrode (1:2.5) | H⁺ ion activity | — | 0.5 |
| **EC** | Conductivity cell (1:2) | Salt concentration proxy | dS/m | 0.5 |
| **OC** | Walkley-Black | Chromic acid wet oxidation | % | 1.0 |
| **Available N** | Subbiah & Asija | Alkaline KMnO₄ distillation | kg/ha | 0.7 |
| **Available P** | Olsen's | NaHCO₃ extractant, spectrophotometry | kg/ha | 0.7 |
| **Available K** | Flame photometry | NH₄OAc extractant, atomic emission | kg/ha | 0.6 |

**(Total: 7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Soil Test Crop Response (STCR)** based fertilizer recommendation. Derive the fertilizer dose for a polyhouse tomato crop given:
- Target yield = 100 t/ha
- Soil test value of available N = 280 kg/ha
- STCR equation: $F_N = 6.5\,Y_T - 0.45\,STV$
- Also compute the urea requirement (46 % N). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **interpolation techniques** (IDW and Kriging) used to convert point soil-test data into continuous fertility maps. Mention the role of semi-variograms in Kriging. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**STCR Concept (2 marks):**
Soil Test Crop Response (STCR) is a **targeted-yield** approach that integrates three factors: (i) indigenous nutrient supply from soil, (ii) crop nutrient uptake per unit yield, and (iii) fertilizer use efficiency. The general linear form is:

$$
F = a\,Y_T - b\,STV
$$

where $a$ = nutrient required per tonne of yield, $b$ = fraction of soil test value utilized by the crop.

**Calculation (5 marks):**

Given: $Y_T = 100\ t/ha$, $STV = 280\ kg/ha$, $F_N = 6.5\,Y_T - 0.45\,STV$.

$$
F_N = 6.5 \times 100 - 0.45 \times 280
$$

$$
F_N = 650 - 126 = 524\ kg\ N/ha
$$

Urea requirement (urea contains 46 % N):

$$
Urea = \frac{524}{0.46} = 1139\ kg/ha
$$

> **Final Answer:** Apply **524 kg N/ha** through **1139 kg urea/ha**, distributed across the fertigation schedule in 6 weekly splits.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Inverse Distance Weighting (IDW) — 3 marks:**
IDW estimates the value of an unsampled location $Z(x_0)$ as a weighted average of nearby sampled values, with weights inversely proportional to distance:

$$
\hat{Z}(x_0) = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{Z(x_i)}{d_i^p}}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{1}{d_i^p}}
$$

where $d_i$ = distance between sampled point $x_i$ and prediction point $x_0$, $p$ = power parameter (usually 2). IDW is simple, deterministic, and produces **"bullseye"** patterns around sample points.

**Kriging — 3 marks:**
Kriging is a **geostatistical** best-linear-unbiased-estimator (BLUE) that uses the spatial autocorrelation structure (the **semi-variogram**) to weight neighbouring samples. The semi-variogram is:

$$
\gamma(h) = \frac{1}{2N(h)} \sum_{i=1}^{N(h)} [Z(x_i) - Z(x_i + h)]^2
$$

The semi-variogram yields three key parameters: **nugget** (micro-variability), **sill** (total variance), and **range** (distance beyond which samples are uncorrelated). Kriging provides both an interpolated surface and an **estimation variance** map — the latter is its key advantage for precision agriculture.

**Comparison (1 mark):** Kriging is preferred for soil-fertility mapping because soil properties exhibit spatial autocorrelation; IDW is preferred only for quick, low-cost estimates.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in PEAGT751 / M3:**
> 1. **Trap 1 — Single composite sampling:** Many students treat the *entire* polyhouse as one unit. Examiner deducts 2 marks for ignoring spatial variability.
> 2. **Trap 2 — Wrong depth:** Sampling only 0–15 cm for a deep-rooted protected crop (capsicum, tomato) loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Trap 3 — Air-drying in sunlight:** UV degrades organic carbon and alters mineral-N. Examiner awards zero for this step.
> 4. **Trap 4 — Omitting the Kriging/IDW step:** Soil analysis without map generation is incomplete — at least 1 mark is reserved for the **map-interpolation** stage.
> 5. **Trap 5 — Confusing Olsen-P and Bray-P:** Olsen uses **NaHCO₃** (alkaline soils); Bray uses **NH₄F + HCl** (acidic soils). Mixing the two loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Trap 6 — Reporting ppm as kg/ha directly:** 1 ppm ≠ 1 kg/ha. Use the conversion factor **2.24** (for 0–15 cm, BD = 1.33 Mg/m³). Wrong unit = −1 mark.
> 7. **Trap 7 — Skipping DGPS:** A soil sample without GPS coordinates cannot enter a precision-farming prescription loop. Examiner explicitly checks for "**DGPS-tagged**" mention.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Soil Sampling and Analysis (PEAGT751 M3):**

- **Objective of soil sampling:** Generate a **spatial fertility map** to drive **variable-rate** input application — *not* a single farm-average report.
- **Standard sampling depth:** 0–15 cm (plough) and 15–30 cm (sub-plough); 0–30 + 30–60 cm for deep-rooted/perennial protected crops.
- **Sub-samples per composite:** **8 to 15 cores** in a **W-pattern** within the cell.
- **Composite area:** ≤ 2 ha (open), ≤ 0.4 ha (protected cultivation).
- **Quartying** reduces 1–2 kg bulk to **500 g lab aliquot**.
- **Air-dry in shade** (24–48 h) — never direct sun (degrades OC and mineral-N).
- **Sieve through 2 mm** mesh; store in labelled cloth/paper bags (not polythene — promotes fungal growth).
- **Geo-referencing is mandatory** — DGPS, accuracy ± 1 m preferred.
- **Lab tests** cover three domains: **Physical** (texture, BD, porosity, WHC), **Chemical** (pH, EC, OC, NPK, CEC, SAR, micronutrients), **Biological** (MBC, soil respiration).
- **STCR** equations are site-and-crop-specific; general form: $F = a Y_T - b\,STV$.
- **Fertilizer dose computation:** Use STCR or fixed-rate prescription; convert to **urea / DAP / MOP** using nutrient percentages.
- **Interpolation methods:** **IDW** (simple, bullseye artefact) and **Kriging** (semi-variogram-based, statistically optimal).
- **Software stack:** QGIS (open source) for map generation; ArcGIS (commercial) for enterprise deployments.
- **Kriging outputs:** Interpolated surface + **estimation-variance map** (uncertainty quantification).
- **STCR-rating chart (Muhr's):** N < 280, P < 11, K < 140 kg/ha = LOW; values above 560, 22, 280 kg/ha = HIGH respectively.
- **Muhr's chart critical:** pH 6.0–7.5 optimal, EC > 2.0 dS/m = saline, OC > 0.75 % = HIGH organic matter.
- **Bulk density threshold:** > 1.6 Mg/m³ indicates soil compaction — a frequent issue under greenhouse walkways.
- **Conversion sanity check:** 1 ppm in 0–15 cm depth (BD 1.33) ≈ **2.24 kg/ha**.
- **Sampling tool selection:** Screw auger (moist), soil probe/tube (dry), Dutch auger (general-purpose), post-hole auger (deep profiles).
- **Avoid sampling near:** Manure pits, irrigation outlets, field bunds, shade-lines, and trees (root-zone contamination).
- **Sampling frequency:** Once every **2–3 years** for open field; **annually** for high-value protected cultivation.
- **Quality control:** Include **blank samples** and **duplicate samples** (10 % of total) to check lab precision.
- **Closed loop:** Fertility map → VRT prescription → variable yield → next-year resampling (continuous feedback).
- **Protected-cultivation bonus:** Soil analysis also guides **substrate blending ratios** when shifting to soilless/hydroponic production.
- **Integration with PEAGT751 M2 (Sensors) and M4 (Variable Rate Tech):** Soil data feeds the **decision support system (DSS)** of the entire precision-farming loop.

> **End of Topic Notes — Soil Sampling and Analysis (PEAGT751 / M3)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Drip and Micro-Sprinkler Irrigation Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT751 - PRECISION FARMING TECHNIQUES FOR PROTECTED CULTIVATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design of Drip and Micro-Sprinkler Irrigation Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Drip Irrigation System Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Drip Irrigation System Design** is the systematic engineering process of determining the optimal spatial layout, hydraulic sizing, and component specifications of a micro-irrigation network to deliver precise quantities of water and nutrients directly to the root zone of each plant, with a target application efficiency exceeding **90%** and distribution uniformity of at least **85%–95%**.

In the context of **protected cultivation** (polyhouses, greenhouses, shade nets), drip and micro-sprinkler design becomes even more critical because:
- The enclosed environment has **no rainfall contribution**
- Evapotranspiration (ET) is the *only* water demand component
- High-value crops demand millimeter-level water precision

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "IV Drip for Plants"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of a drip irrigation system as an **"IV (Intravenous) drip for an entire field"**. Just as a hospital IV delivers a controlled, slow, precise flow of glucose/saline directly into a patient's vein, a drip system delivers water and dissolved fertilizers **drop-by-drop, directly to the root zone** of every plant. The "veins" are the **laterals**, the "mainline" is the artery from the heart (pump), and the "drippers" are the needles. Just as the IV flow rate (mL/hr) is calibrated to the patient's need, the emitter discharge (L/hr) is calibrated to the crop's ET demand.

## 1.3 Definition of Micro-Sprinkler Irrigation Design

**Micro-sprinkler (or mini-sprinkler) irrigation** is a low-pressure sprinkler system that delivers water in the form of a **fine spray or mist** over a wetted diameter typically ranging from **1.8 m to 10 m**, with operating pressures between **0.7 bar and 3.5 bar** and discharges from **30 L/h to 250 L/h**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction:** While drip systems wet only the *root zone* (point source), micro-sprinklers wet a *circular area* around the plant, making them ideal for closely spaced field crops, orchards with ground cover, and greenhouse humidity control.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value/Unit |
|---|---|
| Acceleration due to gravity, $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Density of water, $\rho$ | **1000 kg/m³** |
| Hazen-Williams coefficient (PVC) | **C = 140 to 150** |
| Hazen-Williams coefficient (smooth PE) | **C = 130 to 140** |
| Target Emission Uniformity (EU) | **≥ 90%** (drip), **≥ 85%** (micro-sprinkler) |
| Standard emitter discharge (drip) | **2, 4, 8 L/h** |
| Standard emitter spacing (drip) | **0.3 m, 0.5 m, 0.6 m, 1.0 m** |
| Design operating pressure (drip) | **1.0 kg/cm² (10 m head)** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Design Objectives — The Five Pillars

A properly engineered drip/micro-sprinkler system must simultaneously satisfy:

1. **Hydraulic Adequacy** — Deliver the design discharge at the design pressure to every emitter.
2. **Uniformity** — Maintain Emission Uniformity (EU) ≥ 90% across the wetted area.
3. **Efficiency** — Application Efficiency (Ea) ≥ 90% (i.e., minimize deep percolation and evaporation).
4. **Economic Viability** — Capital and operating cost within payback thresholds.
5. **Operational Practicality** — Easy to install, maintain, flush, and fertigate.

## 2.2 Design Input Parameters

The designer must collect the following data before any calculation:

- **Area** (length × width in metres)
- **Crop type** and **row-to-row** and **plant-to-plant spacing**
- **Soil type** (sand, loam, clay) — determines wetting pattern
- **Peak daily ET** (mm/day) or **monthly ET** for the cropping season
- **Water source** (well, pond, canal) — quantity and quality (pH, EC, TSS)
- **Topography** (slope %)
- **Power availability** (single/three phase, voltage)
- **Climatic data** (temperature, RH, wind speed for micro-sprinklers)
- **Emitter characteristics** (discharge exponent $x$, coefficient $k$)

## 2.3 The Emitter Discharge Equation — Foundation of Design

The relationship between emitter discharge and operating pressure is **non-linear** and given by:

$$q = k \cdot h^x$$

Where:
- $q$ = emitter discharge (L/h)
- $h$ = operating pressure head (m)
- $k$ = emitter discharge coefficient (a constant specific to emitter geometry)
- $x$ = emitter discharge exponent (a dimensionless constant)

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning of $x$:** The exponent $x$ quantifies how sensitive the emitter discharge is to pressure variations. **Lower $x$ values indicate better pressure-compensating (PC) emitters.**

| Emitter Type | Typical $x$ value | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| Pressure-compensating (PC) dripper | **0.05 – 0.1** | Excellent — discharge nearly constant over wide pressure range |
| Long-path dripper | **0.5 – 0.6** | Moderate sensitivity |
| Micro-sprinkler (rotating) | **0.5** | Standard square-root relation |
| Orifice / micro-sprinkler (nozzle) | **0.5** | Classical Torricelli discharge |

## 2.4 Wetted Diameter & Wetting Pattern

### For Drip Emitters:
The wetted diameter below a point-source dripper on a **loamy soil** is approximately:

$$D_w = 0.8 \cdot d_e + 0.11 \cdot q^{0.5}$$

Where:
- $D_w$ = wetted diameter (m)
- $d_e$ = depth of emitter (m, below surface if subsurface)
- $q$ = discharge (L/h)

### For Micro-Sprinklers:
$$D_w = f(q, P, \text{nozzle size, riser height})$$

Empirical correlations from **Keller & Bliesner (1990)**:
- For low-trajectory sprays: $D_w \approx 1.5$ to $4.0$ m
- For high-trajectory rotating mini-sprinklers: $D_w \approx 5$ to $10$ m

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-ready cheat sheet** for Module 4 numerical problems. Memorize these relations along with their units and boundary conditions.

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $q = k h^x$ | Emitter discharge vs. pressure |
| 2 | $T = \dfrac{d \cdot A}{q_e \cdot N_e}$ | System operation time (hr) |
| 3 | $Q_{req} = \dfrac{A \times d}{T}$ | Required system capacity (L/h or m³/h) |
| 4 | $Q_{pump} = \dfrac{Q_{req}}{E_p}$ | Pump discharge accounting for efficiency |
| 5 | $H_{pump} = H_f + H_{em} + \Delta z + H_{suction} + H_{fittings}$ | Total dynamic head (TDH) |
| 6 | $h_f = \dfrac{10.67 \cdot L \cdot Q^{1.852}}{C^{1.852} \cdot D^{4.87}}$ | Hazen-Williams head loss (SI: Q in m³/s, D in m) |
| 7 | $V = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$ | Flow velocity in pipe |
| 8 | $EU = \dfrac{q_{n,min}}{q_{avg}} \times 100$ | Emission Uniformity (lower quartile method) |
| 9 | $CU = \left(1 - \dfrac{\sigma_q}{\bar{q}}\right) \times 100$ | Christiansen's Coefficient of Uniformity |
| 10 | $q_{var} = q_{avg}\left(1 - 1.27\,CV_m\right)\sqrt{\dfrac{n_1}{n_e}}$ | Adjusted emitter discharge for field variation |
| 11 | $R = \dfrac{d \cdot S_l \cdot S_e}{q_e}$ | Application rate per emitter (mm/h) |
| 12 | $N_{em} = \dfrac{A}{S_l \times S_e}$ | Total emitters required |
| 13 | $L_{lat,max} = \dfrac{h_{allow} \cdot D^{4.87} \cdot C^{1.852}}{10.67 \cdot (q_{lateral})^{1.852}}$ | Maximum lateral length for allowable head loss |
| 14 | $V = C_d \cdot A_o \cdot \sqrt{2gh}$ | Orifice discharge (Torricelli) |
| 15 | $P_{hp} = \dfrac{Q \times TDH}{75 \times \eta}$ | Water horsepower (1 HP = 75 kg·m/s) |
| 16 | $P_{kW} = \dfrac{Q \times TDH}{102 \times \eta}$ | Pump power in kW |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Why Drip/Micro-Sprinkler Design Matters |
|---|---|
| **Protected Cultivation** (Polyhouse) | Eliminates leaf wetting → reduces fungal disease; precise fertigation |
| **Arid/Desert Agriculture** | Saves 40–60% water vs. flood irrigation |
| **Saline Soils** | Maintains high soil moisture → reduces osmotic stress |
| **Hilly Terrain** | Drip can be designed for slopes up to 30% with PC emitters |
| **High-Value Horticulture** | Grapes, banana, pomegranate, capsicum, tomato in greenhouse |
| **Landscape & Turf** | Micro-sprinklers provide aesthetic misting + irrigation |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Orifice Discharge for Drip Emitter

The fundamental physics of a drip emitter (a labyrinth or orifice-type flow path) is governed by **Torricelli's law**.

### Step 1: Energy Balance Across the Orifice

Apply Bernoulli's equation between the upstream point (1) inside the emitter and the downstream point (2) at atmospheric discharge:

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$$

**Conversion logic:** Since the emitter is small, the elevation difference $z_1 - z_2 \approx 0$ and the upstream velocity $V_1 \approx 0$ (water arrives slowly through the labyrinth). The downstream pressure $P_2 = P_{atm}$. All losses are lumped into $h_L$ and modeled using a discharge coefficient $C_d$.

### Step 2: Simplification

$$h = \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + h_L$$

For a sharp-edged orifice, $h_L$ is expressed as a fraction of the velocity head:

$$h = \frac{V^2}{2g}\left(1 + K_L\right) = \frac{V^2}{2g \cdot C_d^2}$$

where $C_d = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + K_L}}$.

### Step 3: Solve for Velocity

$$V = C_d \cdot \sqrt{2gh}$$

### Step 4: Multiply by Orifice Area to Get Discharge

$$Q = A_o \cdot V = C_d \cdot A_o \cdot \sqrt{2gh}$$

### Step 5: Express in L/h (volumetric flow)

Since emitter discharge is conventionally expressed in L/h:

$$q = 3600 \cdot C_d \cdot A_o \cdot \sqrt{2gh}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The square-root relationship ($x = 0.5$) is why orifice and micro-sprinkler nozzles are *pressure-sensitive*: doubling the pressure increases discharge by only $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$ times, not 2 times. This is the key insight behind **Pressure-Compensating (PC) emitters** which use flexible diaphragms to actively regulate flow.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Hazen-Williams Head Loss Equation (for Drip Lateral/Manifold Design)

The Hazen-Williams equation is **empirical** but universally adopted for pressurized irrigation water flow in smooth pipes (PVC, PE).

### Step 1: Start with the General Form

For SI units (Q in m³/s, D in m, hf in m per m of pipe):

$$h_f = \dfrac{10.67 \cdot L \cdot Q^{1.852}}{C^{1.852} \cdot D^{4.87}}$$

### Step 2: Velocity Form (eliminating Q)

Substitute $Q = V \cdot A = V \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$:

$$h_f = \dfrac{10.67 \cdot L}{C^{1.852} \cdot D^{4.87}} \left(V \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}\right)^{1.852}$$

### Step 3: Collect D Terms

$$h_f = \dfrac{10.67 \cdot L \cdot V^{1.852}}{C^{1.852} \cdot D^{4.87}} \cdot \left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)^{1.852} \cdot D^{3.704}$$

### Step 4: Final Velocity Form

$$h_f = \dfrac{13.6 \cdot L \cdot V^{1.852}}{C^{1.852} \cdot D^{1.17}}$$

This is the form used in **multiple-outlet pipe** calculations where flow decreases along the lateral.

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Worked Example — Drip Lateral Design

> [!NOTE]
> **Exhaustive numerical worked example** for full KTU-style 14-mark question practice.

### Given Data
- Crop: Tomato in polyhouse
- Lateral length: **L = 50 m**
- Lateral diameter: **D = 16 mm** (PE)
- Emitter spacing: **S_e = 0.5 m**
- Emitter discharge at design head: **q_e = 4 L/h**
- Number of emitters per lateral: **n = 100**
- Hazen-Williams coefficient: **C = 140**
- Design pressure head: **h = 10 m**
- Allowable head loss variation: **Δh_allow = 10% of h = 1.0 m**

### Step 1: Number of Outlets

$$n = \dfrac{L}{S_e} = \dfrac{50}{0.5} = 100 \text{ emitters}$$

### Step 2: Total Discharge at Lateral Inlet

$$Q_{lateral} = n \times q_e = 100 \times 4 = 400 \text{ L/h} = \dfrac{400}{3600} = 0.1111 \text{ L/s} = 1.111 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

### Step 3: Velocity in the Lateral

$$V = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2} = \dfrac{4 \times 1.111 \times 10^{-4}}{\pi \times (0.016)^2}$$

$$V = \dfrac{4.444 \times 10^{-4}}{8.042 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.553 \text{ m/s}$$

**Check:** 0.5 < V < 1.5 m/s → ✅ acceptable (avoids sediment deposition and water hammer).

### Step 4: Friction Loss for Multiple Outlets (Christiansen's Reduction Factor)

For **n outlets** spaced uniformly, the total head loss is reduced by a factor **F** (Christiansen's factor, also called $K_m$):

$$F = \dfrac{1}{1.85} + \dfrac{1}{n} - \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{(1.85 \cdot n)^3}}$$

For $n = 100$:

$$F = \dfrac{1}{1.85} + \dfrac{1}{100} - \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{(185)^3}}$$

$$F = 0.5405 + 0.0100 - \sqrt{2.42 \times 10^{-8}}$$

$$F = 0.5505 - 1.557 \times 10^{-4} = 0.5504$$

### Step 5: Friction Loss if Lateral were a Single Pipe (Full Flow)

$$h_{f,full} = \dfrac{10.67 \times 50 \times (1.111 \times 10^{-4})^{1.852}}{140^{1.852} \times (0.016)^{4.87}}$$

#### Sub-step 5a: Compute $Q^{1.852}$

$$(1.111 \times 10^{-4})^{1.852} = (1.111)^{1.852} \times 10^{-4 \times 1.852}$$

$$(1.111)^{1.852} \approx 1.213$$

$$10^{-7.408} = 3.91 \times 10^{-8}$$

$$Q^{1.852} = 1.213 \times 3.91 \times 10^{-8} = 4.74 \times 10^{-8}$$

#### Sub-step 5b: Compute $C^{1.852}$

$$140^{1.852} = 10^{1.852 \cdot \log_{10}(140)} = 10^{1.852 \times 2.1461} = 10^{3.974} = 9421$$

#### Sub-step 5c: Compute $D^{4.87}$

$$(0.016)^{4.87} = 10^{4.87 \times \log_{10}(0.016)} = 10^{4.87 \times (-1.7959)} = 10^{-8.745} = 1.799 \times 10^{-9}$$

#### Sub-step 5d: Plug into the Formula

$$h_{f,full} = \dfrac{10.67 \times 50 \times 4.74 \times 10^{-8}}{9421 \times 1.799 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$h_{f,full} = \dfrac{2.529 \times 10^{-5}}{1.695 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.492 \text{ m}$$

### Step 6: Apply Christiansen's Factor

$$h_{f,actual} = h_{f,full} \times F = 1.492 \times 0.5504 = 0.821 \text{ m}$$

### Step 7: Compare with Allowable Head Loss

$$h_{f,actual} = 0.821 \text{ m} \quad \text{vs.} \quad \Delta h_{allow} = 1.0 \text{ m}$$

✅ **0.821 m < 1.0 m** → The design is **hydraulically adequate**.

### Step 8: Pressure at the Last Emitter

$$h_{last} = h_{inlet} - h_{f,actual} - \Delta z$$

For level ground ($\Delta z = 0$):

$$h_{last} = 10.0 - 0.821 = 9.179 \text{ m}$$

### Step 9: Emission Uniformity Check

$$EU = \left(1 - 1.27 \cdot \dfrac{CV_m}{\sqrt{n_e}}\right) \times \dfrac{q_{min}}{q_{avg}} \times 100$$

Assuming manufacturer's $CV_m = 0.05$ and using $q_{min} = q_{avg}\left(1 - 0.65 \cdot CV_m\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{h_{min}}{h_{avg}}\right)^x$:

> For PC emitters with $x = 0.1$, EU remains > 90% across the full pressure variation range.

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Worked Example — Micro-Sprinkler Spacing Design

### Given Data
- Micro-sprinkler discharge: **q = 70 L/h**
- Wetted diameter: **D_w = 3.5 m**
- Micro-sprinklers on a 3 m × 3 m grid
- Operating pressure: **1.5 kg/cm² (15 m head)**
- Wind speed at design: 2.5 m/s
- Crop ET: 5 mm/day
- Operation time allowed: 4 hours/day

### Step 1: Application Rate per Micro-Sprinkler

$$R = \dfrac{q \times 1000}{A_w} = \dfrac{70 \times 1000}{\pi (D_w/2)^2}$$

$$R = \dfrac{70000}{\pi \times (1.75)^2} = \dfrac{70000}{9.621} = 7.27 \text{ mm/h}$$

### Step 2: Effective Application Rate (with wind/spacing factor)

Effective wetted area with square spacing S_l × S_e:

$$R_{eff} = \dfrac{q}{S_l \times S_e} = \dfrac{70}{3 \times 3} = 7.78 \text{ mm/h}$$

### Step 3: Net Water Applied in 4 Hours

$$d_{applied} = R_{eff} \times T = 7.78 \times 4 = 31.1 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 4: Compare with ET Demand

Since 31.1 mm ≫ 5 mm/day, the **irrigation interval** can be:

$$I = \dfrac{5}{7.78} = 0.643 \text{ h/day} = 38.6 \text{ min/day}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical interpretation:** The micro-sprinkler only needs to operate for **~39 minutes per day** to meet peak ET. This is typical — and demonstrates why micro-sprinklers are highly efficient for orchards and closely-spaced greenhouse crops.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Drip vs. Micro-Sprinkler Design Comparison

| Design Parameter | Drip System | Micro-Sprinkler System |
|---|---|---|
| Operating pressure | 0.5 – 1.5 bar | 1.0 – 3.5 bar |
| Wetted area | Point (0.3 – 1.0 m dia) | Area (1.8 – 10 m dia) |
| Emitter discharge | 1 – 8 L/h | 30 – 250 L/h |
| Typical spacing | 0.3 – 1.0 m on lateral | 2 – 6 m on lateral |
| Suitable crops | Row crops, vegetables, orchards | Orchards, nurseries, ground cover |
| Clogging sensitivity | **Very High** (filtration critical) | Moderate |
| Wind sensitivity | None (subsurface/point) | **High** (drift losses up to 15%) |
| Energy consumption | **Lowest** | Moderate |
| Application efficiency | 85 – 95% | 70 – 85% |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Complete Drip/Micro-Sprinkler System

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **component interactions** of a complete micro-irrigation system as installed in a protected cultivation unit.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Water Source\nWell or Pond] --> A2[Non-Return Valve\nNRV]
    A2 --> A3[Filter Station\nSand + Screen + Disc]
    A3 --> A4[Fertigation Tank\nVenturi Injector]
    A4 --> A5[Control Valve\nManual or Solenoid]
    A5 --> A6[Pressure Gauge\nat Pump Outlet]
    A6 --> A7[Centrifugal Pump\nor Submersible Pump]
    A7 --> A8[Main Pipe Line\nHDPE or PVC]
    A8 --> A9[Submain Manifold\nwith Air Vacuum Valve]
    A9 --> A10[Laterals\n16 mm PE Pipe]
    A10 --> A11[Emitters\nDripper or Microsprinkler]
    A11 --> A12[Root Zone\nof Crop]
    A8 --> A13[Flush Valve\nat End of Main]
    A9 --> A14[Pressure Regulator\nat Submain Inlet]
    A14 --> A10

    B1[Automation Sensor\nSoil Moisture or Tensiometer] -.-> A5
    B2[Timer Controller\nwith Solenoid Valves] -.-> A5

    style A1 fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,color:#000
    style A3 fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#000
    style A7 fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,color:#000
    style A11 fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d,color:#000
    style A12 fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#065f46,color:#000
    style B1 fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3,color:#000
    style B2 fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3,color:#000
```

## 4.2 System Architecture — Lateral, Manifold, Main Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P[Pump Unit] --> M[Main Pipe\nHDPE 63 mm]
    M --> V1[Isolation Valve]
    V1 --> MA[Submain Manifold\nHDPE 50 mm]
    MA --> V2[Pressure Regulator]
    V2 --> L1[Lateral 1\n16 mm PE]
    V2 --> L2[Lateral 2\n16 mm PE]
    V2 --> L3[Lateral 3\n16 mm PE]
    V2 --> L4[Lateral 4\n16 mm PE]
    V2 --> L5[Lateral 5\n16 mm PE]
    L1 --> E1[Emitter 1\n4 Lph]
    L1 --> E2[Emitter 2\n4 Lph]
    L1 --> E3[Emitter n\n4 Lph]
    L5 --> E4[Emitter 1\n4 Lph]
    L5 --> E5[Emitter 2\n4 Lph]
    L5 --> E6[Emitter n\n4 Lph]
    MA --> FV[End Flush Valve\nat Lateral End]

    style P fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,color:#000
    style M fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,color:#000
    style MA fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,color:#000
    style L1 fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#000
```
