# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q: Define the discharge exponent 'x' of an emitter and explain its significance in drip irrigation design. State typical values for pressure-compensating and non-pressure-compensating emitters.**

**Model Answer:**

The **discharge exponent $x$** appears in the fundamental emitter equation $q = k h^x$ and represents the **sensitivity of emitter discharge to changes in operating pressure head**.

- For a perfectly **pressure-compensating (PC) emitter**, $x \approx 0$ — discharge is independent of pressure.
- For a **non-pressure-compensating emitter** (orifice-type, micro-sprinkler nozzle), $x \approx 0.5$ — discharge varies with the square root of pressure.

**Significance:** A lower $x$ ensures **higher emission uniformity (EU)** across the lateral, even when the pressure head varies due to friction or slope. Hence, PC emitters (with $x = 0.05$–$0.1$) are preferred in undulating terrain or long laterals. The emitter discharge variation $\Delta q$ for a pressure variation $\Delta h$ is given by $\Delta q / q = x \cdot (\Delta h / h)$.

> Typical values:
> - **PC drippers:** $x = 0.05$ to $0.10$
> - **Long-path drippers:** $x = 0.5$ to $0.6$
> - **Micro-sprinkler nozzles:** $x = 0.5$

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Q: Differentiate between point-source and line-source drip laterals. Give one example crop where each is preferred.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Point-Source Lateral | Line-Source Lateral |
|---|---|---|
| Emitter spacing | **0.5 m or more** (widely spaced) | **0.15 to 0.3 m** (closely spaced) |
| Wetting pattern | Discrete wet bulbs | Continuous wetted strip |
| Soil type | Loam, clay (narrow wetted area OK) | Sandy (needs continuous wettin

topics.title: Fertigation of Greenhouse Crops
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAGT751 - PRECISION FARMING TECHNIQUES FOR PROTECTED CULTIVATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Fertigation of Greenhouse Crops ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Fertigation** is the precision-engineered agricultural practice of supplying dissolved water-soluble fertilizers directly to the root zone of crops through an integrated irrigation delivery system. The term is a portmanteau of **Fertilizer** and **Irrigation**. In the context of **Protected Cultivation (Greenhouse/Polyhouse)**, fertigation is not merely a convenience—it is the *primary mode* of nutrient delivery because the majority of greenhouse crops are grown under **soil-less culture** (hydroponics, coco-peat, perlite, rockwool, NFT) where the substrate has negligible native nutrient buffering capacity. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Syllabus Context):** > *"Fertigation is the application of fertilizers through an irrigation system, wherein nutrients are injected in precise quantities and concentrations into the irrigation water, ensuring uniform distribution directly to the active root zone of the crop."* The two fundamental Quality Control parameters governing every fertigation recipe in greenhouses are: | Parameter | Optimal Greenhouse Range | Instrument Used | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **EC** (Electrical Conductivity) | **1.5 – 3.0 mS/cm** (crop dependent) | EC Meter / Conductivity Probe | | **pH** (Potential of Hydrogen) | **5.5 – 6.5** | Calibrated pH Meter | ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hospital IV Drip" Imagine a patient in an ICU who cannot eat solid food. The doctor attaches an **IV drip** that delivers a precisely formulated cocktail of glucose, salts, vitamins, and water directly into the bloodstream—**24 hours a day, in exact quantities, with zero waste**. The patient receives *exactly* what the body needs, when it needs it. Greenhouse fertigation operates on the **identical principle**: - The **greenhouse crop** = the patient (high-value, sensitive, year-round production). - The **drip irrigation line** = the IV cannula. - The **nutrient stock solution** = the IV fluid bag. - The **fertilizer injector (dosing pump / Venturi)** = the IV flow regulator. - The **EC/pH controller** = the doctor's diagnostic monitor. Just as the IV nurse never feeds the patient a pile of raw rice, a greenhouse grower never "broadcasts" granular fertilizer onto a soilless bed. The nutrient delivery is **continuous, controlled, calibrated, and crop-specific**—this is the essence of fertigation. > [!NOTE] > **Why Fertigation is Mandatory in Greenhouses (Not Optional):** > - In open-field agriculture, soil acts as a *nutrient reservoir*. In greenhouses, the substrate (coco-peat, perlite) is essentially **inert**—it holds roots, not nutrients. > - Greenhouse crops like tomato, capsicum, and cucumber are **heavy feeders** (e.g., tomato removes ~250 kg K/ha). > - WUE (Water Use Efficiency) in greenhouses with fertigation reaches **>90%**, versus 30–40% in flood irrigation. ### 1.3 Key Terminology Students Must Memorize - **Stock Solution:** Concentrated fertilizer solution (typically 100× or 200×) held in **A-tank (Calcium Nitrate)** and **B-tank (Phosphates, Sulfates, Micronutrients)** to prevent precipitation. - **Head Unit / Head Control Unit (HCU):** The central assembly housing filters, injectors, valves, and controllers. - **Emitter:** Point of discharge delivering the nutrient solution to the plant. - **NPK Ratio:** Mass ratio of Nitrogen (N), Phosphorus (P₂O₅), and Potassium (K₂O). - **Drain Fraction (Leaching Fraction):** The % of nutrient solution that drains out of the substrate—**mandatory 20–30%** to prevent salt accumulation. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Principles Underlying Fertigation The scientific rationale for fertigation rests on four interlocking principles: 1. **Principle of Root-Zone Targeting (Spatial Efficiency):** Nutrients are placed within 20–30 cm of the active root zone, eliminating volatilization, runoff, and fixation losses seen in broadcast fertilization. 2. **Principle of Synchronous Supply (Temporal Efficiency):** Nutrients are supplied *in phase* with the crop's daily phenological demand (e.g., extra K during fruiting, extra N during vegetative flush). 3. **Principle of Solubility (Chemical Efficiency):** Only **100% water-soluble fertilizers** are used—examples: Urea (46% N), MAP (Mono-Ammonium Phosphate, 12% N, 61% P₂O₅), MOP (Muriate of Potash, 60% K₂O), Ca(NO₃)₂ (Calcium Nitrate, 15.5% N, 19% Ca). 4. **Principle of Compatibility (Chemical Stability):** Sulphates (SO₄²⁻) and Phosphates (PO₄³⁻) are **never mixed with Calcium (Ca²⁺)** in the same stock tank—this forms insoluble CaSO₄ or Ca₃(PO₄)₂ precipitates that clog drip emitters. ### 2.2 Classification of Fertigation Systems Fertigation systems are classified based on **how and when** fertilizer is introduced into the irrigation mainline: - **A. By Injection Mechanism:** - **Venturi Injector:** No moving parts, uses pressure differential, low cost, but inconsistent injection ratio (60–90% efficiency). - **Positive Displacement Pump (Piston/Diaphragm):** Highly accurate, motor-driven, industry standard. - **Differential Pressure Injector (Bypass Tank):** Fertilizer tank connected in parallel; flow diverted through fertilizer → mixed → returned to mainline. Simple but variable concentration. - **Proportional Injector (Hydraulic Motor-Driven):** Injects a *fixed ratio* (e.g., 1:100) regardless of mainline flow—best for constant-dose greenhouses. - **B. By Control Strategy:** - **Open-Loop (Time/Volume Based):** Injects a pre-set quantity per irrigation event. - **Closed-Loop (Sensor-Based / Proportional Control):** Uses **EC sensor and pH sensor** in the discharge line; the controller *automatically adjusts* injector output to maintain target EC (e.g., 2.4 mS/cm) and pH (e.g., 5.8). This is the **gold standard for high-value greenhouses**. ### 2.3 Standard Nutrient Solutions (Global Standards) Greenhouse agronomists worldwide rely on three reference formulae: | Solution | Developed By | Total Salt Concentration | Target EC | Typical Crop | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Hoagland & Arnon (1950)** | Univ. of California, USA | ~2.0 g/L | **2.0 mS/cm** | Tomato, Lettuce, Research | | **Steiner Universal Solution** | Steiner, 1968 | Variable | **2.0–2.5 mS/cm** | Tomato, Cucumber | | **Cooper's Solution (1979)** | UK | ~1.5 g/L | **1.6 mS/cm** | NFT Lettuce, Strawberry | ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAE — Master these for KTU ESE (End Semester Examination):** | # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Unit / Note | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Dilution Equation | $C_1 V_1 = C_2 V_2$ | $C$ = concentration, $V$ = volume | | 2 | EC ↔ TDS Conversion | $TDS \approx EC \times 640$ | $TDS$ in ppm, $EC$ in mS/cm | | 3 | Fertilizer Quantity (kg/ha) | $Q = \dfrac{R \times 100}{P}$ | $R$ = nutrient requirement (kg/ha), $P$ = % nutrient | | 4 | Irrigation Volume | $V_w = A \times d$ | $A$ = area (m²), $d$ = depth (mm), $V_w$ in Liters | | 5 | Total System Discharge | $Q_{tot} = q \times n$ | $q$ = emitter discharge, $n$ = number of emitters | | 6 | Injection Ratio | $IR = \dfrac{V_{fert}}{V_{water}}$ | e.g., 1:100, 1:200 | | 7 | Nutrient Dose (g/plant/day) | $D = \dfrac{C_{tgt} \times V_{daily}}{1000}$ | $C_{tgt}$ in mg/L, $V$ in L/day | | 8 | Leaching Requirement | $LR = \dfrac{EC_{iw}}{EC_{dw}}$ | $iw$ = irrigation water, $dw$ = drainage water | | 9 | Area Calculation | $A = L \times W$ | Greenhouse floor area in m² | | 10 | Molarity → g/L | $g/L = M \times MW$ | $M$ = molarity (mol/L), $MW$ = molecular weight | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Fertigation engineering directly powers the global **$350+ billion greenhouse vegetable industry**. The Netherlands—world's second-largest agricultural exporter—runs 100% of its 10,000+ ha greenhouses on **closed-loop sensor-controlled fertigation**, achieving tomato yields of **>70 kg/m²/year** (vs. 5 kg/m² in Indian open fields). In Kerala's polyhouse revolution, precision fertigation is the *single largest yield-determining factor* for capsicum (grossing ₹8–10 Lakh per 1000 m² annually) and Dutch roses. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > **Solved KTU-Style Numerical Problem (Full Working):** > *Greenhouse tomato variety 'Shivam' is grown in 1 hectare (10,000 m²) of polyhouse. The recommended fertilizer dose is:* > *N = 200 kg/ha, P₂O₅ = 80 kg/ha, K₂O = 250 kg/ha.* > *Available fertilizers: Urea [CO(NH₂)₂, 46% N], SSP [Ca(H₂PO₄)₂, 16% P₂O₅], MOP [KCl, 60% K₂O].* > *The drip system has emitters of 4 L/hr spaced at 50 cm × 50 cm. Each irrigation event runs for 1 hour.* > *Compute: (a) Quantity of each straight fertilizer in kg/ha, (b) Total water volume per irrigation, (c) Total nutrient load in solution.* ### 3.1 Derivation Part (a) — Straight Fertilizer Quantities We begin with the **fundamental fertilizer requirement formula**: $$Q = \dfrac{R \times 100}{P}$$ where $Q$ = quantity of fertilizer (kg/ha), $R$ = recommended nutrient (kg/ha), and $P$ = % nutrient in fertilizer. **Step 1: Compute Urea quantity (for N)** $$ Q_{\text{urea}} = \dfrac{R_N \times 100}{P_N} $$ Substituting the numerical values $R_N = 200$ kg/ha and $P_N = 46\%$: $$ Q_{\text{urea}} = \dfrac{200 \times 100}{46} $$ $$ Q_{\text{urea}} = \dfrac{20000}{46} = 434.78 \text{ kg/ha} $$ **Step 2: Compute SSP quantity (for P₂O₅)** $$ Q_{\text{SSP}} = \dfrac{R_{P_2O_5} \times 100}{P_{P_2O_5}} = \dfrac{80 \times 100}{16} = \dfrac{8000}{16} = 500 \text{ kg/ha} $$ **Step 3: Compute MOP quantity (for K₂O)** $$ Q_{\text{MOP}} = \dfrac{R_{K_2O} \times 100}{P_{K_2O}} = \dfrac{250 \times 100}{60} = \dfrac{25000}{60} = 416.67 \text{ kg/ha} $$ **Tabulated Result:** | Nutrient | Recommended (kg/ha) | Fertilizer | % Nutrient | Quantity Required (kg/ha) | | :--- | :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: | | N | 200 | Urea | 46 | **434.78** | | P₂O₅ | 80 | SSP | 16 | **500.00** | | K₂O | 250 | MOP | 60 | **416.67** | | **Total** | — | — | — | **1351.45** | ### 3.2 Derivation Part (b) — Total Water Volume per Irrigation **Step 1: Number of emitters per hectare** Area of 1 ha = 10,000 m². Spacing = 0.5 m × 0.5 m = 0.25 m² per emitter. $$ n = \dfrac{\text{Total Area}}{\text{Area per emitter}} = \dfrac{10000 \text{ m}^2}{0.25 \text{ m}^2} $$ $$ n = 40000 \text{ emitters} $$ **Step 2: Total system discharge per hour** $$ Q_{\text{tot}} = q \times n = 4 \text{ L/hr} \times 40000 $$ $$ Q_{\text{tot}} = 160000 \text{ L/hr} = 160 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr} $$ **Step 3: For a 1-hour irrigation event, total water = 160,000 L/ha = 160 m³/ha.** This is equivalent to a water depth of: $$ d = \dfrac{V}{A} = \dfrac{160 \text{ m}^3}{10000 \text{ m}^2} = 0.016 \text{ m} = 16 \text{ mm} $$ ### 3.3 Derivation Part (c) — Total Nutrient Load in Solution **Step 1: Convert fertilizer quantities to grams (per hectare):** $$ Q_{\text{urea}} = 434.78 \text{ kg} = 434780 \text{ g} $$ $$ Q_{\text{SSP}} = 500 \text{ kg} = 500000 \text{ g} $$ $$ Q_{\text{MOP}} = 416.67 \text{ kg} = 416670 \text{ g} $$ **Step 2: Sum total nutrient salts to be dissolved:** $$ M_{\text{total}} = 434780 + 500000 + 416670 $$ $$ M_{\text{total}} = 1351450 \text{ g} = 1351.45 \text{ kg} $$ **Step 3: Concentration of the irrigation solution (in g/L):** $$ C = \dfrac{M_{\text{total}}}{V_{\text{water}}} = \dfrac{1351450 \text{ g}}{160000 \text{ L}} $$ $$ C = 8.45 \text{ g/L} \quad (\text{or } 8450 \text{ ppm}) $$ **Step 4: Convert to EC (mS/cm) using the standard conversion $TDS \approx EC \times 640$:** $$ EC = \dfrac{TDS}{640} = \dfrac{8450}{640} = 13.20 \text{ mS/cm} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL INSIGHT:** A single-shot application of the entire 1351.45 kg in 160 m³ would produce a solution of EC **13.2 mS/cm**—this would be **toxic** to tomato (lethal EC > 5.0 mS/cm). This is why fertigation uses **split applications** (e.g., 20–30 split doses across the crop cycle), reducing per-event concentration to a safe **1.5–2.5 mS/cm**. This numerical insight is a **favorite KTU examiner question** to test *applied* understanding. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Fertilizer Compatibility Chart > [!NOTE] > **Compatibility Rule for Stock-Tank Preparation (KTU Viva Favorite):** | | Urea | MAP | MOP | Ca(NO₃)₂ | MgSO₄ | Fe-EDTA | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | **Urea** | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | | **MAP** | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | | **MOP** | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | | **Ca(NO₃)₂** | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | | **MgSO₄** | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | | **Fe-EDTA** | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | *✗ = Precipitates; do NOT mix in same tank.* This is why **Tank A** holds Ca(NO₃)₂ alone, and **Tank B** holds the rest. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete Greenhouse Fertigation System Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB WS1[Raw Water Source
Borewell or Open Well] PS1[Pressure Pump
1.5 to 3.0 HP Centrifugal] SF1[Sand Filter
20 to 60 Mesh] DC1[Disc Filter
120 Mesh Screen] AI1[Air Vessel
Pressure Stabilizer] FE1[Fertilizer Injector Assembly] VT1[Tank A
Calcium Nitrate Solution] VT2[Tank B
NPK and Micronutrients] VT3[Tank C
Acid or pH Down Solution] MX1[Inline Mixing Chamber
with Static Mixer] EC1[EC Sensor
Probe Type] PH1[pH Sensor
Probe Type] CL1[Automation Controller
PLC or Microcontroller] MN1[Mainline PVC Pipe
63 mm Class 2] SL1[Submain HDPE Pipe
40 mm] DL1[Drip Lateral LDPE
16 mm] EM1[Drip Emitters
Pressure Compensating 4 LPH] RH1[Plant Root Zone
in Coco Peat or Soil] DR1[Drainage Collection Tray
EC and pH Monitoring] WR1[Drainage Recycle Tank
Optional Closed Loop] WS1 --> PS1 PS1 --> SF1 SF1 --> DC1 DC1 --> AI1 AI1 --> FE1 FE1 --> MX1 VT1 --> FE1 VT2 --> FE1 VT3 --> FE1 MX1 --> EC1 EC1 --> PH1 PH1 --> CL1 CL1 -.Feedback Control.-> FE1 MX1 --> MN1 MN1 --> SL1 SL1 --> DL1 DL1 --> EM1 EM1 --> RH1 RH1 --> DR1 DR1 --> WR1 WR1 -.Optional Recycle.-> SF1 ``` ### 4.2 Process Logic Flow — Fertigation Sequence ```mermaid flowchart LR ST1[Step 1: Pre Irrigation
Pure Water 10 to 15 Minutes] ST2[Step 2: Inject Tank A
Calcium Nitrate Solution] ST3[Step 3: Inject Tank B
NPK and Sulfates] ST4[Step 4: Inject Tank C
Acid pH Correction] ST5[Step 5: EC and pH Verification
Target EC 2.4 pH 5.8] ST6[Step 6: Post Irrigation
Pure Water Flush 5 Minutes] ST7[End of Fertigation Cycle] ST8[Loop Restart
After Preset Interval] ST1 --> ST2 ST2 --> ST3 ST3 --> ST4 ST4 --> ST5 ST5 -->|EC OK and pH OK| ST6 ST5 -->|EC Mismatch| ST2 ST6 --> ST7 ST7 --> ST8 ST8 -.-> ST1 ``` ### 4.3 Component Functional Description Table | Block in Diagram | Engineering Function | Failure Consequence | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Sand Filter | Traps organic debris, silt | Emitter clogging | | Disc Filter | Removes fine particles (>120 mesh) | Blockage of labyrinth paths | | Tank A | Holds Ca(NO₃)₂ alone, prevents PO₄/SO₄ precipitation | Calcium phosphate scaling | | Tank B | Holds phosphates, sulfates, KNO₃, micronutrients | Prevents calcium precipitation | | EC Sensor | Real-time conductivity monitoring | Salt burn or deficiency | | pH Sensor | Real-time acidity monitoring | Iron/manganese lockout | | Drip Lateral | Delivers solution to root zone | Uniformity of growth | | Drain Tray | Captures leachate to monitor EC | Salt accumulation feedback | ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question Pattern]** **Q1. Define fertigation. List four advantages of fertigation in greenhouse cultivation. [3 Marks]** **Model Answer:** Fertigation is the precise application of water-soluble fertilizers through an irrigation system directly into the active root zone of crops. **Four advantages:** (i) Uniform nutrient distribution (Coefficient of Uniformity > 90%). (ii) Saving in fertilizer (30–50% less than broadcast). (iii) Reduced labour cost (no manual top-dressing). (iv) Synchronization of nutrient supply with crop phenology. *(Any 4 valid points accepted.)* --- **Q2. What is the importance of maintaining EC and pH in greenhouse fertigation? [3 Marks]** **Model Answer:** - **EC (Electrical Conductivity)** measures total dissolved salt concentration; an EC of **1.5–2.5 mS/cm** is optimal for tomato. EC > 4.0 mS/cm causes root burn; EC < 1.0 mS/cm causes nutrient deficiency. - **pH** controls nutrient availability; the ideal greenhouse pH is **5.5–6.5**. At pH > 7.0, iron (Fe) and manganese (Mn) become unavailable (lockout). At pH < 5.0, aluminium and manganese become toxic. - Together, EC and pH determine the **ion activity and uptake rate** by roots. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – End Semester Model Pattern]** > *Note: KTU ESE Part B carries 14 marks and offers internal choice. Model solutions below.* --- #### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** **(a) Explain in detail the different types of fertilizer injection systems used in greenhouse fertigation with their working principles. [7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** There are three principal types of fertilizer injection systems used in greenhouse fertigation: **1. Venturi Injector:** A constriction in the mainline creates a pressure drop. This negative pressure sucks fertilizer from a tank into the flowing water. - *Advantage:* No moving parts, low cost, simple. - *Disadvantage:* Inconsistent injection ratio (60–90% efficiency), requires 20–30% pressure loss in mainline. **2. Bypass Pressure Differential Tank (By-Pass Tank):** A closed fertilizer tank is connected between the mainline (inlet and outlet). A pressure differential of 0.5–1.5 kg/cm² forces part of the flow to enter the tank, mix with fertilizer, and return to the mainline. - *Advantage:* No external power needed. - *Disadvantage:* Concentration decreases as tank empties (non-uniform). **3. Positive Displacement Injection Pump (Piston/Diaphragm Pump):** Motor-driven pump injects fertilizer at a calibrated rate (e.g., 100 L/hr). Controlled by **PLC or microcontroller** based on EC/pH sensor feedback. - *Advantage:* Highly accurate, fully automatable, closed-loop control. - *Disadvantage:* High initial cost, requires electricity. **4. Proportional Injector (Hydraulic Motor-Driven):** The water flow itself drives a small hydraulic motor that operates a piston injecting fertilizer at a **fixed ratio** (e.g., 1:100), independent of flow rate. - *Advantage:* Self-powered, consistent ratio. - *Disadvantage:* Inconsistent if pressure varies. > *(Drawing a neat labelled diagram of any two systems fetches 2 extra marks.)* --- **(b) A greenhouse tomato crop requires N = 200 kg/ha, P₂O₅ = 80 kg/ha, and K₂O = 250 kg/ha. Calculate the quantity of Urea (46% N), SSP (16% P₂O₅), and MOP (60% K₂O) required. [7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** Using the formula: $$Q = \dfrac{R \times 100}{P}$$ **Step 1: Urea** $$ Q_{\text{urea}} = \dfrac{200 \times 100}{46} = 434.78 \text{ kg/ha} $$ **Step 2: SSP** $$ Q_{\text{SSP}} = \dfrac{80 \times 100}{16} = 500.00 \text{ kg/ha} $$ **Step 3: MOP** $$ Q_{\text{MOP}} = \dfrac{250 \times 100}{60} = 416.67 \text{ kg/ha} $$ **Result Table:** | Fertilizer | Quantity (kg/ha) | | :--- | :---: | | Urea | 434.78 | | SSP | 500.00 | | MOP | 416.67 | | **Total** | **1351.45** | > **Note (for full marks):** Mention that these are typically applied in **20–25 split doses** through fertigation, not as a single application, because a single application would result in EC ~13 mS/cm which is lethal to tomato. --- #### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]** **(a) Discuss the composition, principles of preparation, and application of Hoagland's Nutrient Solution for greenhouse crops. [7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **Hoagland's Solution** (Hoagland & Arnon, 1950) is the most widely used reference nutrient solution in greenhouses worldwide. **Composition (Full Strength, 1 Litre):** | Stock Salt | Quantity (g/L) | Macronutrient Supplied | | :--- | :---: | :--- | | KNO₃ (Potassium Nitrate) | 0.51 | K⁺, NO₃⁻ | | Ca(NO₃)₂ (Calcium Nitrate) | 0.82 | Ca²⁺, NO₃⁻ | | KH₂PO₄ (Mono Potassium Phosphate) | 0.14 | K⁺, H₂PO₄⁻ | | MgSO₄·7H₂O (Magnesium Sulphate) | 0.49 | Mg²⁺, SO₄²⁻ | | Micronutrient stock (Fe, Mn, B, Zn, Cu, Mo) | 1 mL | Trace elements | **Principle of Preparation:** - The solution is prepared in **deionized water** to avoid uncontrolled Ca²⁺ and HCO₃⁻. - **Ca(NO₃)₂ is always dissolved separately** (Tank A) to avoid precipitation with phosphates/sulphates. - Final pH is adjusted to **5.8–6.2** using HNO₃ or KOH. - Final EC target: **~2.0 mS/cm**. **Application:** Used in NFT (Nutrient Film Technique), DFT (Deep Flow Technique), and coco-peat substrate cultivation for tomato, lettuce, cucumber, capsicum, and strawberries. The solution is recycled through the root zone, and **drainage EC is monitored daily** to maintain a stable root environment. --- **(b) A greenhouse of dimensions 50 m × 40 m uses drip emitters of 4 L/hr spaced at 1 m × 1 m. The recommended fertilizer concentration in the irrigation water is 2 g/L. The injector used has a ratio of 1:100. Calculate: (i) total discharge per hour, (ii) amount of fertilizer to be mixed in the stock tank for 8 hours of operation, and (iii) volume of stock solution to be added per hour. [7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **Given:** Length = 50 m, Width = 40 m, Spacing = 1 m × 1 m, $q = 4$ L/hr, Conc. = 2 g/L, IR = 1:100, $t = 8$ hr. **Step (i): Total discharge per hour** Number of emitters $n = \dfrac{50 \times 40}{1 \times 1} = 2000$ emitters. $$ Q_{\text{tot}} = q \times n = 4 \times 2000 = 8000 \text{ L/hr} $$ **Step (ii): Total water for 8 hours** $$ V_{\text{water}} = 8000 \times 8 = 64000 \text{ L} $$ **Step (iii): Total fertilizer needed for 8 hours** $$ M_{\text{fert}} = C \times V = 2 \text{ g/L} \times 64000 \text{ L} = 128000 \text{ g} = 128 \text{ kg} $$ **Step (iv): Volume of stock solution per hour (using IR = 1:100)** Injection ratio of 1:100 means **1 L of stock solution per 100 L of water**. $$ V_{\text{stock/hr}} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{tot}}}{100} = \dfrac{8000}{100} = 80 \text{ L/hr} $$ **Step (v): Total stock for 8 hours** $$ V_{\text{stock,total}} = 80 \times 8 = 640 \text{ L} $$ **Step (vi): Stock concentration (g/L)** $$ C_{\text{stock}} = \dfrac{M_{\text{fert}}}{V_{\text{stock,total}}} = \dfrac{128000 \text{ g}}{640 \text{ L}} = 200 \text{ g/L} $$ **Final Result Table:** | Parameter | Value | | :--- | :--- | | Total discharge per hour | 8000 L/hr | | Water for 8 hours | 64000 L | | Total fertilizer needed | 128 kg | | Stock volume per hour | 80 L | | Stock concentration | **200 g/L** | --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > - **Mistake 1:** Many students compute the *number of emitters* using greenhouse area divided by the spacing area — but forget to verify units. (1 m × 1 m = 1 m² — so emitters per m² = 1.) Always double-check. > - **Mistake 2:** Confusing **NPK percentages** as % of *elemental* N, P, K vs. *oxide* form (P₂O₅, K₂O). P₂O₅ contains only **44% elemental P**; K₂O contains **83% elemental K**. KTU expects the **oxide form** as written on fertilizer bags. > - **Mistake 3:** Forgetting to mention **tank separation** (Tank A / Tank B) for Ca and PO₄/SO₄ — losing 2–3 marks on a 14-mark question. > - **Mistake 4:** Computing single-dose EC of ~13 mS/cm without explaining *split application* — examiner deducts for missing the **practical agronomic safety** dimension. ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (Boards-Ready):** - ✔ **Fertigation** = Fertilizer + Irrigation; mandatory in soil-less greenhouse cultivation. - ✔ **Optimal EC for tomato:** 1.5 – 2.5 mS/cm | **pH:** 5.5 – 6.5. - ✔ **Core formula:** $Q = \dfrac{R \times 100}{P}$ for straight fertilizer dose. - ✔ **Dilution rule:** $C_1 V_1 = C_2 V_2$ is the most-tested equation. - ✔ **EC ↔ TDS:** $TDS \approx EC \times 640$ (KCl scale). - ✔ **Tank rule:** Ca(NO₃)₂ in **Tank A**; all other salts in **Tank B**; never mix calcium with phosphates or sulphates in the same stock. - ✔ **Three injectors to memorize:** Venturi, Bypass Tank, Positive Displacement Pump. - ✔ **Three standard solutions:** Hoagland, Steiner, Cooper. - ✔ **Leaching requirement:** Always maintain **20–30% drainage** from the substrate to prevent salt accumulation. - ✔ **Emitter spacing** must be matched to plant spacing (commonly 50 cm × 50 cm for tomato). - ✔ **Drip system filter chain:** Sand filter → Disc filter → Screen filter at lateral. - ✔ **Closed-loop control** uses EC + pH sensors feeding back to a PLC controller that regulates the dosing pump — this is the *industry standard* for high-value greenhouses. - ✔ **Fertigation cycle:** Pre-irrigation (10 min) → Injection (15–30 min) → Post-flush (5 min). - ✔ **Greenhouse crops & preferred EC:** Lettuce 1.2–1.8, Strawberry 1.4–1.8, Tomato 2.0–2.5, Cucumber 1.7–2.0, Capsicum 2.0–2.5, Rose 1.5–2.0 (all in mS/cm). - ✔ **Golden KTU ESE Mantra:** Always *split-dose*; never broadcast-equivalent; always monitor drainage EC; always run a 20% leaching fraction.

topics.title: Water Quality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT751 - PRECISION FARMING TECHNIQUES FOR PROTECTED CULTIVATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Water Quality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Water Quality

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Water Quality** in the context of precision farming and protected cultivation refers to the chemical, physical, biological, and microbiological characteristics of irrigation water that collectively determine its suitability for crop production under controlled environment agriculture (CEA) systems. It is a composite parameter defined by measurable indices such as **pH**, **Electrical Conductivity (EC)**, **Total Dissolved Solids (TDS)**, **Sodium Adsorption Ratio (SAR)**, **Residual Sodium Carbonate (RSC)**, total hardness, and the concentration of specific ions (Na⁺, Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, Cl⁻, SO₄²⁻, HCO₃⁻, CO₃²⁻, B).

As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)** syllabus for Module 4 of PEAGT751, water quality assessment is positioned as a **pre-cultivation decision parameter**—it dictates the choice of irrigation system (drip vs. micro-sprinkler), the selection of fertilizer injectors, the leaching fraction, and the cropping pattern inside a polyhouse or greenhouse.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Examiner's Standard Phrasing):**
> *"Water quality is a measure of the dissolved and suspended constituents in irrigation water expressed through salinity, sodicity, toxicity, and alkalinity parameters that govern its long-term usability in protected cultivation systems."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are preparing **infant formula milk** for a newborn baby. You cannot use tap water straight from the borewell in many Indian cities because:

- It may contain **too much salt** (high EC → baby gets dehydrated)
- It may be **too hard** (excess Ca/Mg → digestion issues)
- It may have **wrong pH** (alkaline water → nutrient unavailability)

Plants inside a polyhouse are exactly like that newborn baby. They are:
- **Confined** to a limited root volume (small grow bags or trays)
- **Continuously fed** through drip irrigation (no leaching rain)
- **Sensitive to salt build-up** because there is no natural flushing

So, just as a pediatrician prescribes **tested, balanced water**, a precision farming engineer must test irrigation water for **EC, pH, SAR, and RSC** before designing the fertigation schedule. **A polyhouse fed with saline water will fail within 2–3 crop cycles**, regardless of how good the greenhouse structure is.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:** In open-field farming, rain acts as a natural "reset button" by leaching accumulated salts. In protected cultivation, this natural reset is absent. Therefore, **water quality becomes 3 to 5 times more critical** under polyhouse conditions compared to open-field cultivation.

## 1.3 Classification of Water Quality Parameters (Conceptual Map)

Water quality parameters are broadly grouped into **four diagnostic families**:

| Family | Parameter | What it Diagnoses |
|--------|-----------|-------------------|
| **Salinity Parameters** | EC, TDS | Salt load on roots |
| **Sodicity Parameters** | SAR, RSC | Sodium hazard / soil structure damage |
| **Toxicity Parameters** | Na⁺, Cl⁻, B | Specific ion toxicity to plants |
| **Operational Parameters** | pH, Hardness, Fe, Mn | Clogging of drippers & nutrient availability |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Units to Memorize:**
> - **EC** → dS/m (deciSiemens per metre) at **25 °C**
> - **TDS** → mg/L or ppm
> - **SAR** → dimensionless ratio
> - **pH** → negative log of H⁺ activity (log scale, dimensionless)
> - **Hardness** → mg/L as CaCO₃ equivalent
> - **Boron** → mg/L
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Explanation of Each Water Quality Parameter

### A. pH — Potential of Hydrogen

- **Definition:** A logarithmic measure of H⁺ ion concentration in water.
- **Optimal range for protected cultivation:** **5.5 to 6.5** (slightly acidic to neutral).
- **Why critical in polyhouses?**
  - At pH > 7.5, **iron (Fe), manganese (Mn), zinc (Zn), and phosphorus (P)** become unavailable to plants → deficiency symptoms even when fertilizers are supplied.
  - At pH < 5.0, **aluminum and manganese toxicity** can occur.
- **Standard:** measured using a calibrated pH meter with glass electrode.

### B. Electrical Conductivity (EC)

- **Definition:** The ability of water to conduct an electric current, directly proportional to the total concentration of dissolved ionized salts.
- **Unit:** **dS/m** at 25 °C (also written as mS/cm — numerically identical).
- **Interpretation:**

| EC (dS/m) | Salinity Class | Effect |
|-----------|----------------|--------|
| < 0.25 | Low | Suitable for all crops |
| 0.25 – 0.75 | Medium | Suitable with leaching |
| 0.75 – 2.25 | High | Restricts sensitive crops |
| > 2.25 | Very High | Unsuitable without treatment |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In polyhouses, irrigation water EC should ideally be below 0.75 dS/m** because salt accumulation in the root zone is 3–5× faster than open field due to absence of rainfall.

### C. Total Dissolved Solids (TDS)

- **Definition:** Total mass of all dissolved solids (organic + inorganic) in mg/L.
- **Derived from EC** using the empirical conversion factor.

### D. Sodium Adsorption Ratio (SAR)

- **Definition:** Measures the relative proportion of sodium to calcium and magnesium in irrigation water. It predicts the **sodium hazard**—i.e., whether irrigation water will deteriorate soil structure over time by displacing Ca²⁺ from clay particles.
- **Critical threshold:** SAR > 10 indicates sodic water.

### E. Residual Sodium Carbonate (RSC)

- **Definition:** Estimates the excess of carbonates and bicarbonates over calcium and magnesium.
- **Interpretation:** Positive RSC → water tends to precipitate Ca and Mg as carbonates → leaves Na in solution → increases SAR over time.

### F. Total Hardness

- **Definition:** Combined concentration of Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ expressed as CaCO₃ equivalent.
- **Critical for polyhouses:** Hard water (> 200 mg/L as CaCO₃) **clogs drip emitters** through CaCO₃ precipitation, especially when fertilizers containing sulfate are injected.

### G. Chloride and Sulfate Toxicity

- Cl⁻ > 4 me/L → toxicity to sensitive crops like tomato, capsicum.
- SO₄²⁻ > 20 me/L → can cause sulfur-induced acidity and Ca deficiency.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is the EXACT set of formulas KTU examiners test in ESE (End Semester Examination). Memorize the formulas, their variables, and their units.**

| S.No. | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variables & Units | Engineering Use |
|-------|--------------|------------------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | pH Definition | $\text{pH} = -\log_{10}[\text{H}^{+}]$ | [H⁺] = mol/L | Acidity / alkalinity of fertigation solution |
| 2 | EC to TDS Conversion | $\text{TDS (mg/L)} = \text{EC (dS/m)} \times 640$ | EC in dS/m, TDS in mg/L | Quick field estimation of dissolved salts |
| 3 | Sodium Adsorption Ratio (SAR) | $\text{SAR} = \dfrac{\text{Na}^{+}}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\text{Ca}^{2+} + \text{Mg}^{2+}}{2}}}$ | All cations in me/L | Predicts sodium-induced soil structural damage |
| 4 | Residual Sodium Carbonate (RSC) | $\text{RSC} = (\text{CO}_{3}^{2-} + \text{HCO}_{3}^{-}) - (\text{Ca}^{2+} + \text{Mg}^{2+})$ | All ions in me/L | Predicts long-term rise in SAR |
| 5 | Total Hardness (as CaCO₃) | $\text{TH} = 2.5 \times \text{Ca}^{2+} + 4.1 \times \text{Mg}^{2+}$ | Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺ in mg/L; TH in mg/L as CaCO₃ | Dripper clogging risk assessment |
| 6 | Adjusted SAR (for gypsum-amenable water) | $\text{adj SAR} = \dfrac{\text{Na}^{+}}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\text{Ca}^{2+} + \text{Mg}^{2+}}{2}}}$ (after gypsum amendment) | me/L | Used in reclamation planning |
| 7 | Leaching Requirement (LR) | $\text{LR} = \dfrac{\text{EC}_{iw}}{5 \times \text{EC}_{e}^{*} - \text{EC}_{iw}}$ | EC_iw = irrigation water EC; EC_e* = tolerable drainage water EC | Minimum leaching fraction to control root zone salinity |
| 8 | Conversion — mg/L to me/L | $\text{me/L} = \dfrac{\text{mg/L} \times \text{Valence}}{\text{Atomic Weight}}$ | — | Standardizing all ion concentrations to me/L |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EC_e* denotes the threshold EC of the soil saturated paste extract above which yield decline begins. For tomato in polyhouse, EC_e* ≈ 2.5 dS/m; for capsicum ≈ 1.5 dS/m; for cucumber ≈ 2.0 dS/m.**

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The formulas above are not just academic—they are used daily by:
- **Precision farming engineers** to decide whether to install an RO/demineralization unit before the fertigation tank.
- **Polyhouse consultants** in Kerala (where borewell water is often iron-rich and slightly acidic) to prescribe pH correction using nitric acid injection.
- **Greenhouse exporters** in Israel and Netherlands where SAR-based water blending is standard practice.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Why EC × 640 = TDS

**Starting Premise:** EC measures the electrical conductivity contributed by dissolved ions. TDS measures the mass of those same ions.

**Step 1 — Empirical Basis:** Across thousands of natural water samples, agricultural chemists (USDA Salinity Laboratory, Riverside) observed that the ratio TDS/EC clusters tightly around **640 mg/L per dS/m** for typical irrigation waters.

**Step 2 — Theoretical Justification:** The electrical conductivity of a solution is the sum of contributions from each ion weighted by its equivalent conductance and concentration.

**Step 3 — Mathematical Expression:**

$$\text{EC (dS/m)} \approx \dfrac{\text{TDS (mg/L)}}{640}$$

**Step 4 — Rearranging for the working formula:**

$$\text{TDS (mg/L)} = \text{EC (dS/m)} \times 640$$

**Example Numerical Evaluation:**

- Given: EC = 1.5 dS/m (typical Kerala borewell water in summer)
- Substitute:

$$\text{TDS} = 1.5 \times 640 = 960 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Conclusion:** EC of 1.5 dS/m implies TDS of 960 mg/L → water falls in the **"high salinity"** class and needs leaching management.

## 3.2 Worked Example — SAR Calculation (KTU Standard Type)

**Problem:** A water sample analysis report gives the following ionic concentrations in me/L:
- Na⁺ = 12.0 me/L
- Ca²⁺ = 3.0 me/L
- Mg²⁺ = 2.0 me/L

**Step 1 — Identify the SAR Formula:**

$$\text{SAR} = \dfrac{\text{Na}^{+}}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\text{Ca}^{2+} + \text{Mg}^{2+}}{2}}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

$$\text{SAR} = \dfrac{12.0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{3.0 + 2.0}{2}}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator and denominator separately:**

- Numerator: Na⁺ = 12.0
- Inner term: (Ca²⁺ + Mg²⁺)/2 = (3.0 + 2.0)/2 = 2.5

**Step 4 — Apply the square root:**

$$\sqrt{2.5} = 1.5811$$

**Step 5 — Final division:**

$$\text{SAR} = \dfrac{12.0}{1.5811} = 7.59$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:** SAR = 7.59 lies in the **S2 class (Medium Sodium Hazard)**. This water can be used for coarse-textured soils with good drainage, but NOT for fine-textured (clayey) soils in polyhouses. It is **NOT recommended for polyhouse cultivation without gypsum amendment**.

## 3.3 Worked Example — RSC Calculation

**Problem:** Water analysis shows:
- CO₃²⁻ = 1.5 me/L
- HCO₃⁻ = 4.5 me/L
- Ca²⁺ = 3.0 me/L
- Mg²⁺ = 2.0 me/L

**Step 1 — Apply the RSC formula:**

$$\text{RSC} = (\text{CO}_{3}^{2-} + \text{HCO}_{3}^{-}) - (\text{Ca}^{2+} + \text{Mg}^{2+})$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$\text{RSC} = (1.5 + 4.5) - (3.0 + 2.0)$$

**Step 3 — Compute each bracket:**

- (CO₃²⁻ + HCO₃⁻) = 6.0 me/L
- (Ca²⁺ + Mg²⁺) = 5.0 me/L

**Step 4 — Final subtraction:**

$$\text{RSC} = 6.0 - 5.0 = 1.0 \text{ me/L}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**

| RSC (me/L) | Water Class |
|------------|-------------|
| < 1.25 | Safe |
| 1.25 – 2.50 | Marginal |
| > 2.50 | Unsuitable |

Since RSC = 1.0 me/L, the water is **SAFE** but on the borderline.

## 3.4 Worked Example — Leaching Requirement (LR)

**Problem:** A greenhouse tomato crop has EC_e* = 2.5 dS/m. Irrigation water EC = 1.5 dS/m. Compute the leaching requirement.

**Step 1 — Apply the LR formula:**

$$\text{LR} = \dfrac{\text{EC}_{iw}}{5 \times \text{EC}_{e}^{*} - \text{EC}_{iw}}$$

> **Engineering Note:** The factor **5** comes from the assumption that the average EC of drainage water at the bottom of the root zone is approximately **5 times** the EC of irrigation water (due to 80% crop water uptake).

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$\text{LR} = \dfrac{1.5}{5 \times 2.5 - 1.5}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the denominator:**

$$5 \times 2.5 = 12.5$$
$$12.5 - 1.5 = 11.0$$

**Step 4 — Final division:**

$$\text{LR} = \dfrac{1.5}{11.0} = 0.1364 \text{ or } 13.64\%$$

**Step 5 — Practical Meaning:** For every **100 L of irrigation water** applied, the engineer must plan for **13.64 L of drainage** to flush accumulated salts below the root zone.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Water Quality vs. Suitability for Protected Cultivation

| Parameter | Excellent | Good | Marginal | Unsuitable for Polyhouse |
|-----------|-----------|------|----------|--------------------------|
| pH | 6.0 – 6.5 | 6.5 – 7.5 | 7.5 – 8.0 | > 8.0 or < 5.5 |
| EC (dS/m) | < 0.25 | 0.25 – 0.75 | 0.75 – 2.25 | > 2.25 |
| TDS (mg/L) | < 160 | 160 – 480 | 480 – 1440 | > 1440 |
| SAR | < 4 | 4 – 8 | 8 – 15 | > 15 |
| RSC (me/L) | < 1.25 | 1.25 – 2.0 | 2.0 – 2.5 | > 2.5 |
| Hardness (mg/L as CaCO₃) | < 100 | 100 – 200 | 200 – 300 | > 300 |
| Cl⁻ (me/L) | < 2 | 2 – 4 | 4 – 10 | > 10 |
| B (mg/L) | < 0.5 | 0.5 – 1.0 | 1.0 – 2.0 | > 2.0 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Water Quality Assessment Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Water Source: Borewell / Open Well / Pond] --> B[Sample Collection in Sterile Bottle]
    B --> C[In-situ Measurement: pH, EC, Temperature, Turbidity]
    C --> D[Laboratory Analysis: Na, Ca, Mg, CO3, HCO3, Cl, SO4, B, Fe, Mn]
    D --> E[Convert all ions to me/L]
    E --> F[Compute SAR and RSC]
    F --> G{Decision Matrix: Water Class?}
    G -->|Excellent / Good| H[Direct Use in Drip System]
    G -->|Marginal| I[Pre-treatment Required: Sand Filter + Acid Injection]
    G -->|Unsuitable| J[Reject or Install RO / Membrane Unit]
    H --> K[Design Fertigation Schedule]
    I --> K
    J --> L[Recycle Drainage through Constructed Wetland]
    L --> H
    K --> M[Continuous Monitoring every 30 days]
    M --> C
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** This is the standard workflow prescribed under **KTU Module 4**. Note that water quality assessment is **not a one-time activity**—it is a **closed-loop continuous monitoring process** with a 30-day recheck cycle inside a polyhouse.

## 4.2 Functional Hierarchy of Water Quality Parameters

```mermaid
graph TD
    WQ[Water Quality] --> SAL[Salinity Family]
    WQ --> SOD[Sodicity Family]
    WQ --> TOX[Toxicity Family]
    WQ --> OPR[Operational Family]
    SAL --> EC[EC]
    SAL --> TDS[TDS]
    SOD --> SAR[SAR]
    SOD --> RSC[RSC]
    TOX --> NA[Na Ion]
    TOX --> CL[Cl Ion]
    TOX --> B[Boron]
    OPR --> PH[pH]
    OPR --> HARD[Hardness]
    OPR --> FE[Iron]
```

## 4.3 Suitability Decision Tree for Polyhouse Irrigation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Water Sample] --> B{EC less than 0.75 dS/m?}
    B -->|Yes| C{SAR less than 8?}
    B -->|No| D{RO Treatment Available?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Blend with Treated Water]
    D -->|No| F[Reject Source]
    C -->|Yes| G{RSC less than 1.25 me/L?}
    C -->|No| H[Apply Gypsum Amendment]
    G -->|Yes| I[APPROVED for Polyhouse Use]
    G -->|No| J[Acid Injection Required]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Practice:** The above decision tree is the **standard checklist** adopted by the **Precision Farming Development Centres (PFDCs)** under the Ministry of Agriculture, Government of India.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define the term "Sodium Adsorption Ratio (SAR)" used in irrigation water quality assessment. State its significance in protected cultivation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** SAR is defined as the ratio of sodium concentration to the square root of the average of calcium and magnesium concentrations, all expressed in me/L:

$$\text{SAR} = \dfrac{\text{Na}^{+}}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\text{Ca}^{2+} + \text{Mg}^{2+}}{2}}}$$

- **(1 mark)** It is a **dimensionless index** that predicts the **sodium hazard** of irrigation water—i.e., the tendency of sodium to displace calcium and magnesium from soil clay particles, leading to soil structural degradation and reduced permeability.
- **(1 mark)** In protected cultivation where leaching by rainfall is absent, **high SAR water rapidly destroys the root zone structure inside polyhouse grow bags**, causing waterlogging and aeration stress. Hence SAR must be < 8 for safe use.

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supposed]**
**Explain the significance of irrigation water pH in a polyhouse cropping system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** pH is the negative logarithm of H⁺ ion activity, indicating acidic or alkaline nature of water on a **logarithmic scale from 0 to 14**.
- **(1 mark)** In polyhouse fertigation, ideal pH range is **5.5 to 6.5**. At pH > 7.5, micronutrients like Fe, Mn, Zn, and phosphate become **insoluble and unavailable**, causing deficiency even when supplied.
- **(1 mark)** At pH < 5.0, **aluminum and manganese toxicity** occurs, damaging fine root hairs. Thus pH correction using nitric acid or phosphoric acid injection is standard practice in polyhouses.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1): **[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) Describe in detail the various physical, chemical, and biological parameters used to assess irrigation water quality for protected cultivation. Mention the acceptable limits for each parameter. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Physical Parameters (2 marks):**
- **Temperature** — affects EC reading; must be standardized at 25 °C.
- **Turbidity** — caused by suspended clay/silt; high turbidity (> 50 NTU) clogs drippers.
- **Total Suspended Solids (TSS)** — should be < 50 mg/L for drip systems.

**(ii) Chemical Parameters (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Acceptable Limit for Polyhouse |
|-----------|-------------------------------|
| pH | 5.5 – 6.5 |
| EC | < 0.75 dS/m |
| TDS | < 480 mg/L |
| SAR | < 8 |
| RSC | < 1.25 me/L |
| Hardness | < 200 mg/L as CaCO₃ |
| Cl⁻ | < 4 me/L |
| B | < 0.5 mg/L |
| Fe | < 0.1 mg/L |
| Mn | < 0.05 mg/L |

**(iii) Biological Parameters (2 marks):**
- **Total Coliform Count** — must be < 1000 MPN/100 mL for vegetables.
- **Fecal Coliform** — must be **absent** in 100 mL sample.
- Pathogens like *E. coli*, *Salmonella* can cause post-harvest contamination of hydroponic produce.

---

**(b) The ionic composition of an irrigation water sample (in me/L) is given below: Na⁺ = 15, Ca²⁺ = 4, Mg²⁺ = 3, CO₃²⁻ = 2, HCO₃⁻ = 6, Cl⁻ = 5. Calculate SAR and RSC and comment on its suitability for polyhouse drip irrigation. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — SAR Calculation (3 marks):**

$$\text{SAR} = \dfrac{\text{Na}^{+}}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\text{Ca}^{2+} + \text{Mg}^{2+}}{2}}}$$

$$\text{SAR} = \dfrac{15}{\sqrt{\dfrac{4 + 3}{2}}} = \dfrac{15}{\sqrt{3.5}} = \dfrac{15}{1.8708} = 8.02$$

**Step 2 — RSC Calculation (3 marks):**

$$\text{RSC} = (\text{CO}_{3}^{2-} + \text{HCO}_{3}^{-}) - (\text{Ca}^{2+} + \text{Mg}^{2+})$$

$$\text{RSC} = (2 + 6) - (4 + 3) = 8 - 7 = 1.0 \text{ me/L}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation (1 mark):**
- SAR = 8.02 → falls in **S2 class (Medium Sodium Hazard)**.
- RSC = 1.0 me/L → falls in the **Safe** category (< 1.25).
- Overall verdict: Water is **marginally suitable** for polyhouse use on coarse-textured media (coco peat, perlite) but **NOT recommended** for soil-based polyhouse cultivation. **Gypsum amendment at 2 tonnes/hectare** is advised if used.

---

### Question B (Choice 2): **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Reframed]**

**(a) Explain the concept of Leaching Requirement (LR). A polyhouse tomato crop has a threshold EC of saturated paste extract (EC_e*) of 2.5 dS/m and the irrigation water has an EC of 2.0 dS/m. Compute the leaching requirement and interpret the result. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Concept of LR (3 marks):**
Leaching Requirement is the **minimum fraction of irrigation water that must drain below the root zone** to prevent the average root zone salinity from exceeding the crop tolerance threshold (EC_e*).

The mathematical expression is:

$$\text{LR} = \dfrac{\text{EC}_{iw}}{5 \times \text{EC}_{e}^{*} - \text{EC}_{iw}}$$

The factor 5 represents the average concentration factor due to crop water uptake (typically 80% of water is taken up by roots, leaving 20% as drainage concentrated with salts).

**Numerical Calculation (3 marks):**

Given:
- EC_iw = 2.0 dS/m
- EC_e* = 2.5 dS/m

$$\text{LR} = \dfrac{2.0}{5 \times 2.5 - 2.0} = \dfrac{2.0}{12.5 - 2.0} = \dfrac{2.0}{10.5} = 0.1905$$

$$\text{LR} = 19.05\%$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):**
The irrigation system must apply **at least 19.05% additional water** beyond the crop evapotranspiration demand, and this excess must drain freely out of the polyhouse grow bags. Practically, for every 100 L of irrigation, **19 L must leach out** to keep root zone salinity below the tomato threshold.

---

**(b) Discuss the various pre-treatment methods used to improve irrigation water quality for protected cultivation. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Physical Pre-treatment (2 marks):**
- **Media filters** (sand + gravel) — removes suspended particles.
- **Screen filters** — protects drippers from clogging.
- **Hydrocyclone separators** — removes sand from borewell water.

**2. Chemical Pre-treatment (3 marks):**
- **Acid injection** — HNO₃, H₂SO₄, or H₃PO₄ to lower pH of alkaline water.
- **Gypsum (CaSO₄·2H₂O) addition** — corrects high SAR and RSC water.
- **Chlorination** — kills algae and bacteria; typical dose 1–2 mg/L free chlorine.

**3. Membrane / Advanced Treatment (2 marks):**
- **Reverse Osmosis (RO)** — removes 95–99% salts; used when EC > 2.25 dS/m.
- **Ion exchange softening** — replaces Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ with Na⁺ (used for hardness removal only, not salinity).
- **Blending** — mixing poor-quality water with good-quality water to achieve target EC.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks (Verified from KTU Valuation Patterns):**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Error (–2 marks):** Students frequently forget to convert mg/L to me/L before applying SAR and RSC formulas. The conversion is:
> $$\text{me/L} = \dfrac{\text{mg/L} \times \text{Valence}}{\text{Atomic Weight}}$$
> For Na⁺ = mg/L ÷ 23, for Ca²⁺ = mg/L × 2 ÷ 40, etc.
>
> 2. **Mis-remembering the Leaching Factor (–1 mark):** The factor in the LR denominator is **5**, NOT 2 or 10. The factor 5 assumes 80% water uptake.
>
> 3. **Confusing EC and TDS (–1 mark):** They are not the same. TDS = EC × 640. If the question says "EC = 2 dS/m", do NOT directly call it "TDS = 2".
>
> 4. **Forgetting units in the final answer (–0.5 mark):** Always state EC in dS/m, SAR as dimensionless, RSC in me/L.
>
> 5. **Skipping the interpretation step (–1 to –2 marks):** After computing SAR/RSC, you MUST comment on suitability. KTU examiners allocate explicit marks for the **interpretation paragraph**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Water quality** is a composite of **4 families**: Salinity, Sodicity, Toxicity, and Operational parameters.
- [ ] **EC** measures total dissolved ionized salts; standard unit is **dS/m at 25 °C**.
- [ ] **Conversion rule:** TDS (mg/L) = **EC × 640**; memorize this factor.
- [ ] **Ideal polyhouse water:** EC < 0.75 dS/m, pH 5.5–6.5, SAR < 8, RSC < 1.25 me/L.
- [ ] **SAR** uses sodium, calcium, magnesium in **me/L**, NOT mg/L.
- [ ] **RSC** = (Carbonates + Bicarbonates) − (Calcium + Magnesium); all in me/L.
- [ ] **Leaching Requirement** formula uses a **factor of 5** in the denominator.
- [ ] **Hardness > 200 mg/L as CaCO₃** causes dripper clogging through CaCO₃ precipitation.
- [ ] **Protected cultivation** has 3–5× higher salt build-up than open field due to absence of rain leaching.
- [ ] **Pre-treatment options:** media filtration, acid injection, gypsum amendment, chlorination, RO.
- [ ] **Boron > 2 mg/L** is toxic; **Cl⁻ > 4 me/L** causes leaf burn in tomato and capsicum.
- [ ] **mg/L to me/L conversion** is mandatory before applying SAR and RSC formulas.
- [ ] **Iron > 0.1 mg/L** causes reddish-brown staining and dripper clogging via iron bacteria.
- [ ] **Continuous monitoring every 30 days** is mandatory inside an operational polyhouse.
- [ ] **KTU 2024 examiners** always award marks for the **interpretation** of numerical results—never skip the conclusion.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Greenhouse Use
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT751 - PRECISION FARMING TECHNIQUES FOR PROTECTED CULTIVATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Greenhouse Use

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Greenhouse Use** refers to the systematic application of a controlled-environment agricultural structure (greenhouse) for the year-round cultivation of high-value horticultural crops by manipulating the aerial and root-zone environment to achieve optimum productivity, quality, and resource-use efficiency.

A **greenhouse** (also called a *glasshouse* or *polyhouse*) is a framed structure covered with a transparent or translucent material (glass, polyethylene, polycarbonate, or acrylic) in which environmental parameters — **temperature, relative humidity, light intensity, CO₂ concentration, and soil moisture** — are partially or fully regulated to favour crop growth independent of external climatic constraints.

Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEAGT751)** curriculum, "Greenhouse Use" is studied as the **functional application domain** of the engineering, design, and automation principles taught in the preceding modules. It bridges the gap between structural design and agricultural productivity.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The "Climate Bubble" for Plants**
> Imagine giving your favourite indoor plant a personal, transparent "bubble" that traps sunlight, holds warmth, and blocks rain, wind, and pests — but still lets the gardener tweak the inside temperature, humidity, and light like adjusting the thermostat of an air-conditioned room. That bubble is a greenhouse.
>
> Now imagine running that bubble not for a single houseplant but for **tomatoes, capsicum, cucumbers, roses, gerberas, or even exotic herbs** on a commercial scale, with sensors auto-adjusting the climate every few seconds. That commercial bubble — with precision controls and data-driven decisions — is what the modern "greenhouse use" really means in Precision Farming.

In one line: **a greenhouse lets the farmer carry the *climate* of his/her choice into the field**, decoupling crop production from the mercy of weather.

## 1.3 Salient Features of Greenhouse Use (KTU Syllabus Highlights)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Six Pillars of Modern Greenhouse Use (must-know for KTU):**
> 1. **Off-season / round-the-year production** of vegetables, flowers, and nursery plants.
> 2. **Transplant production** — raising healthy, disease-free seedlings for open-field cultivation.
> 3. **Hardening of tissue-culture-raised plantlets** — acclimatization from lab to field conditions.
> 4. **Production of disease-free, high-quality planting material** (potato tubers, sugarcane sets, etc.).
> 5. **Hybrid seed production** under controlled pollination environments.
> 6. **Research & demonstration** — trials of new varieties, fertilizer response, and climate-change studies.

## 1.4 Typical Operating Conditions (Standard Metrics)

The commonly targeted interior environment for a **temperate-crop greenhouse in the tropics** (e.g., capsicum, tomato in Kerala) is:

| Parameter | Standard Target Range |
|---|---|
| Day temperature | **22 °C – 28 °C** |
| Night temperature | **16 °C – 20 °C** |
| Relative Humidity (RH) | **60 % – 80 %** |
| Light intensity (PAR) | **400 – 700 µmol m⁻² s⁻¹** |
| CO₂ concentration | **350 – 1000 ppm** |
| Soil pH | **6.0 – 6.8** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Greenhouses Based on Use

Greenhouse use is best understood by classifying the structures according to the **functional application** they are designed for. This classification is **frequently asked in KTU ESE questions**.

### A. Based on Function / End-Use

> [!NOTE]
> - **Production Greenhouse (Commercial):** Built for high-volume, profit-driven cultivation of vegetables, cut flowers, or fruits. Equipped with full climate control, hydroponics, drip irrigation, and CO₂ enrichment. Typical area: **0.4 ha – 4.0 ha**.
> - **Propagation / Nursery Greenhouse:** Used exclusively for raising seedlings, rootstocks, and cuttings. Shaded-net or low-cost polyhouses suffice. Area: **100 – 1000 m²**.
> - **Research / Demonstration Greenhouse:** Glass-clad, precision-controlled structures used by universities and ICAR institutes for varietal trials. Area: **50 – 500 m²**.
> - **Display / Retail Greenhouse:** Used in plant nurseries and botanical gardens for retail sale of ornamental plants. Public access is allowed.
> - **Conservatory:** Aesthetic structure for displaying exotic / non-commercial plants; not profit-oriented.

### B. Based on Environmental Control Level

| Class | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **High-tech** | Fully climate-controlled; automated vent, fog, shade, CO₂ | Cut flowers, exotic vegetables |
| **Medium-tech** | Partial climate control; manual vents, pad-fan cooling | Tomatoes, capsicum in tropics |
| **Low-tech** | Naturally ventilated; no active systems | Nursery raising in mild climates |

## 2.2 Crops Commonly Grown in Greenhouses

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield List (memorize for 3-mark questions):**

**Vegetables** — Tomato, Capsicum (Bell Pepper), Cucumber, Lettuce, Broccoli, Brinjal, Okra, Bush Beans, Asparagus.

**Flowers** — Rose, Carnation, Gerbera, Chrysanthemum, Lilium, Anthurium, Orchids, Gladiolus.

**Fruits** — Strawberry (most popular greenhouse fruit), Melons (Cantaloupe, Watermelon), Papaya (semi-protected).

**Others** — Herbs (Basil, Mint, Parsley), medicinal plants (Aloe vera, Stevia), Tobacco seedlings, Coffee seedlings.

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Operational Logic — How a Greenhouse is "Used"

### Step 1: Site Selection & Orientation
- **Flat, well-drained, elevated** site to avoid waterlogging.
- Orientation: **North–South ridge** in tropics (to obtain uniform sun on both slopes).
- Availability of **grid power and good-quality irrigation water** is verified.

### Step 2: Structure Selection
- Choose between **wooden, bamboo, GI pipe, or galvanized-steel-truss** structure based on investment.
- Cover material: **200-micron UV-stabilized polyethylene film** for low-cost; **double-wall polycarbonate** for premium houses.

### Step 3: Environmental Control Setup
- **Ventilation:** natural roof + side vents, augmented by **exhaust fans**.
- **Cooling:** **pad-and-fan system** (evaporative cooling) for hot dry regions; **fogging** for humid tropics.
- **Heating:** thermal screens, biomass heaters, or solar passive heating for cold nights.
- **Shading:** retractable shade nets (50 %, 75 %) or shade compounds.
- **CO₂ enrichment:** through CO₂ generators or pure liquid CO₂ dosing.

### Step 4: Growing System Installation
- **Open soil beds**, **raised beds**, or **soilless media** (coco peat, perlite, vermiculite, rockwool).
- **Drip irrigation** with fertigation tanks for precise nutrient delivery.

### Step 5: Crop Husbandry
- Variety selection, transplanting at 3–4 leaf stage, training/pruning, pollination (mechanical or bumble bees), integrated pest management (IPM), and harvest scheduling.

### Step 6: Monitoring & Automation
- Sensors for **temperature, RH, light, soil moisture, EC, pH, CO₂** feed data to a **Programmable Logic Controller (PLC)** or IoT gateway.
- Precision decisions on irrigation, fertigation, and climate are made in real time.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All high-yield formulas for "Greenhouse Use" problems:**

| Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Boundary / Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| Green-house area | $A = L \times W$ | $L$ = length (m), $W$ = width (m) | Used for sizing fans, pads |
| Greenhouse volume | $V = L \times W \times H$ | $H$ = gutter height (m) | Used for ventilation rate |
| Air-exchange rate (ACH) | $ACH = \dfrac{Q}{V} \times 60$ | $Q$ = fan flow (m³/s) | ACH between **20 – 60** for tropical greenhouses |
| Ventilation flow needed | $Q = \dfrac{V \times ACH}{3600}$ | $Q$ in m³/s | Sizing fans |
| Pad area requirement | $A_{pad} = \dfrac{Q}{v}$ | $v$ = face velocity (m/s), typically **1.0 – 1.5 m/s** | Clog-resistant range |
| Light Transmission Factor (LTF) | $LTF = \dfrac{I_{in}}{I_{out}} \times 100$ | $I$ = PAR intensity | Single PE film ≈ **70–85 %** |
| Vapour Pressure Deficit | $VPD = e_s - e_a$ | $e_s$, $e_a$ in kPa | Optimal range **0.8 – 1.2 kPa** |
| Saturation vapour pressure (Tetens) | $e_s = 0.6108 \cdot \exp\!\left(\dfrac{17.27 T}{T + 237.3}\right)$ | $T$ = °C | Used in humidity control |
| Cooling efficiency of pad | $\eta_{pad} = \dfrac{T_{out} - T_{in}}{T_{out} - T_{wb}} \times 100$ | $T$ in °C | Typically **70 – 85 %** |
| Heat load (solar) | $Q_s = A \cdot I \cdot \tau \cdot \alpha$ | $I$ = solar radiation (W/m²), $\tau$ = transmittance, $\alpha$ = absorptance | Used in summer cooling design |
| Productivity index | $Y = \dfrac{\text{Total yield (kg)}}{\text{Floor area (m²)}}$ | kg/m² | Tomato: **30 – 60 kg/m²/yr**; Capsicum: **15 – 25 kg/m²/yr** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always carry **units** in the final numerical answer. Marks are deducted for unit-less answers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example 1 — Sizing a Pad-and-Fan System

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU style):**
> A polyhouse of dimensions **50 m × 8 m × 4 m (L × W × H)** is to be equipped with a pad-and-fan evaporative cooling system. The required air-exchange rate (ACH) is **30 h⁻¹**. Compute: (i) greenhouse volume, (ii) required total ventilation flow in m³/s, (iii) required pad-face area if the face velocity of air through the pad is **1.2 m/s**.

### Step 1 — Greenhouse Volume

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= L \times W \times H \\
  &= 50 \times 8 \times 4 \\
  &= 1600 \; \text{m}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Volume is the product of all three internal dimensions; it gives the air mass that must be replaced for a single ACH.

### Step 2 — Air-Changes-per-Hour to Volumetric Flow

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{hr} &= V \times ACH \\
       &= 1600 \times 30 \\
       &= 48000 \; \text{m}^{3/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

Convert to SI units (m³/s):

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \dfrac{Q_{hr}}{3600} \\
  &= \dfrac{48000}{3600} \\
  &= 13.33 \; \text{m}^{3/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** One hour = 3600 seconds, so divide by 3600 to convert hourly flow to per-second flow.

### Step 3 — Pad-Face Area

The pad area is found by dividing the volumetric flow by the safe face velocity through the pad:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{pad} &= \dfrac{Q}{v} \\
        &= \dfrac{13.33}{1.2} \\
        &= 11.11 \; \text{m}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

If the pad height is **2 m**, then the pad length:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{pad} &= \dfrac{A_{pad}}{h_{pad}} \\
        &= \dfrac{11.11}{2.0} \\
        &= 5.56 \; \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Pad area = flow rate ÷ velocity. Standard practice limits face velocity to 1.0–1.5 m/s to avoid water carry-over and pad clogging.

### Final Answers
- Volume: **V = 1600 m³**
- Required ventilation flow: **Q = 13.33 m³/s**
- Pad area: **A_pad = 11.11 m²** (or **5.56 m × 2.0 m** pad).

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example 2 — Light Transmission Factor

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> Outside PAR is **1200 µmol m⁻² s⁻¹** and inside the greenhouse PAR is **900 µmol m⁻² s⁻¹** under a single-layer 200-micron polyethylene film. Calculate the Light Transmission Factor (LTF).

$$
\begin{aligned}
LTF &= \dfrac{I_{in}}{I_{out}} \times 100 \\
    &= \dfrac{900}{1200} \times 100 \\
    &= 75 \; \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** LTF is the ratio of inside-to-outside PAR expressed as a percentage. A 200-micron UV-stabilized PE film normally transmits 70–85 % of incident PAR; the calculated value of 75 % is within this range.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Analysis of Greenhouse Uses

| Use Case | Recommended Structure | Target Crop | Yield Benchmark | Key Engineering System | Typical Investment |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Off-season vegetable | Naturally ventilated polyhouse | Tomato, Capsicum | **20 – 40 kg/m²** | Drip + vent | Low – Medium |
| Cut-flower export | Hi-tech glasshouse | Rose, Carnation | **150 – 300 stems/m²** | Pad-fan + thermal screen + CO₂ | High |
| Nursery / seedling | Shade-net house (50 %) | Vegetable seedlings | **500 – 800 trays/cycle** | Mist irrigation | Very Low |
| Tissue-culture hardening | High-humidity polyhouse | Banana, Orchids | **Survival > 95 %** | Fogging + shade net | Medium |
| Hybrid seed production | Insect-proof net house | Tomato, Brinjal | **5 – 10 g seed/m²** | Trellis + isolation | Medium |
| Strawberry cultivation | Low tunnel / polyhouse | Strawberry | **2 – 4 kg/m²** | Drip + mulching | Low |
| Aquaponics integration | Polyhouse + fish tank | Lettuce + Tilapia | **40 heads/m²** + 30 kg fish/m³ | Recirculating system | Medium – High |

---

## 3.4 Numerical Problem on Cooling Efficiency of Pad

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> Outside dry-bulb temperature is **38 °C**, wet-bulb temperature is **24 °C**, and air leaving the pad enters the greenhouse at **26 °C**. Calculate the saturation efficiency of the pad.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{pad} &= \dfrac{T_{out} - T_{in}}{T_{out} - T_{wb}} \times 100 \\
           &= \dfrac{38 - 26}{38 - 24} \times 100 \\
           &= \dfrac{12}{14} \times 100 \\
           &= 85.71 \; \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Efficiency is measured against the thermodynamic limit (wet-bulb). A value of **85.71 %** indicates an excellently designed cellulose pad system.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Functional Flow of Greenhouse Use (Operational Architecture)

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below maps the **functional workflow of greenhouse use**, from site selection to harvest, including the climate-control loop and the precision-farming feedback layer.

```mermaid

    D --> D4[Shade Net 50 Percent Retractable]
    D --> D5[CO2 Enrichment System]
    D --> E[Growing System Installation]
    E --> E1[Soil Beds or Raised Beds]
    E --> E2[Soilless Media Coco Peat Rockwool]
    E --> E3[Drip Irrigation with Fertigation]
    E --> F[Crop Husbandry Transplanting Training Pruning IPM]
    F --> G[Sensor Network Deployment]
    G --> G1[Temperature RH Sensors]
    G --> G2[PAR Light Sensor]
    G --> G3[Soil Moisture EC pH Sensor]
    G --> G4[CO2 Sensor]
    G --> H[PLC and IoT Gateway Data Logger]

    I2 --> F
    I3 --> F

```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Classification of Greenhouse Uses

```mermaid

```

## 4.3 Schematic Block — Sensor-to-Actuator Feedback Loop (Precision Greenhouse)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ENV[Greenhouse Microclimate] -->|Measured by| SEN[Sensor Array T RH PAR CO2 EC pH]
    SEN --> DAQ[Data Acquisition and PLC]
    DAQ --> DEC[Decision Algorithm Threshold and PID]
    DEC --> ACT[Actuator Array]
    ACT --> V1[Vent Motor]
    ACT --> V2[Fan Motor]
    ACT --> V3[Drip Valve]
    ACT --> V4[CO2 Cylinder Solenoid]
    ACT --> V5[Shade Screen Motor]
    V1 --> ENV
    V2 --> ENV
    V3 --> ENV
    V4 --> ENV
    V5 --> ENV
    DAQ --> CLOUD[Cloud Dashboard Farmer Mobile App]
    CLOUD -->|Manual Override| DEC
```

## 4.4 Functional Description (Plain Text Block Diagram)

```

|                                                             |

|   | CANOPY  |      |  DRIP    |      | COCO-PEAT|           |

+-------------------------------------------------------------+

       |                |                |
   Sensors          Sensors          Sensors
       |                |                |
```
