<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`
**List any six applications of greenhouses in modern horticulture.**

**Model Answer:**
1. Off-season production of vegetables such as tomato, capsicum, and cucumber.
2. Year-round cultivation of cut flowers like rose, carnation, and gerbera.
3. Raising disease-free vegetable seedlings and rootstocks for open-field transplanting.
4. Hardening of tissue-culture-raised plantlets under controlled humidity.
5. Hybrid seed production under insect-proof net houses.
6. Research on climate-resilient varieties and fertilizer-response trials in university glasshouses.

*(Any 6 from the syllabus list fetch full 3 marks.)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`
**Differentiate between a high-tech and a low-tech greenhouse.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | High-Tech Greenhouse | Low-Tech Greenhouse |
|---|---|---|
| Climate control | Fully automated (PLC-based) | Manual or none |
| Cover | Double-wall polycarbonate / glass | Single-layer 200 µm PE film |
| Cooling | Pad-fan + fogging | Natural ventilation only |
| Investment | **> ₹ 8 – 10 million per 1000 m²** | **₹ 0.4 – 0.6 million per 1000 m²** |
| Crops | Cut flowers, exotic vegetables | Seedling raising, off-season veg |
| Yield | 3 – 5 × open field | 1.5 – 2 × open field |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of a precision greenhouse** with sensor–actuator feedback loop. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A polyhouse measures **40 m × 6 m × 3.5 m**. The required air-exchange rate is **25 ACH**. Design the **pad-and-fan cooling system**, calculating volume, required airflow in m³/s, and pad area if face velocity is **1.0 m/s**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

The precision greenhouse operates as a **closed-loop cyber-physical system**. The block diagram (refer to Section 4.3) has four layers:

1. **Sensing layer** — temperature, RH, PAR, CO₂, soil-moisture, EC, and pH sensors distributed across the canopy and root zone.
2. **Communication layer** — wired (RS-485, CAN bus) or wireless (LoRa, Zigbee, MQTT over Wi-Fi) transmission to the gateway.
3. **Decision layer** — a PLC or edge-AI controller runs **threshold logic** and **PID control** to compare sensor readings with setpoints.
4. **Actuation layer** — vent motors, exhaust fans, drip valves, foggers, shade-screen motors, CO₂ dosing valves.

The **feedback loop** ensures the inside microclimate is corrected every few seconds, decoupling crop growth from external weather.

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Step 1 — Volume:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= 40 \times 6 \times 3.5 = 840 \; \text{m}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Airflow:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{hr} &= 840 \times 25 = 21000 \; \text{m}^{3/h} \\
Q &= \dfrac{21000}{3600} = 5.83 \; \text{m}^{3/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Pad area:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{pad} &= \dfrac{5.83}{1.0} = 5.83 \; \text{m}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

If pad height = 1.5 m, then pad length = 5.83 / 1.5 = **3.89 m ≈ 4 m**.

**Final Answer:** V = 840 m³, Q = 5.83 m³/s, A_pad ≈ 5.83 m² (use 1.5 m × 4 m pad).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a)** Classify greenhouses based on **environmental-control level** and **functional use**. Mention suitable crops for each class. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **saturation efficiency of a cellulose cooling pad** given outside dry-bulb 36 °C, wet-bulb 25 °C, and pad-outlet temperature 27 °C. State whether the pad is operating efficiently. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

| Class | Description | Suitable Crops |
|---|---|---|
| High-tech | Fully climate-controlled with CO₂ enrichment | Rose, Carnation, Gerbera, Capsicum (export-grade) |
| Medium-tech | Pad-fan or fan-and-pad; partial automation | Tomato, Capsicum, Cucumber |
| Low-tech | Naturally ventilated polyhouse | Nursery seedlings, off-season leafy veg |
| Propagation | Shade-net house (50–75 %) | Vegetable seedlings, tissue-culture hardening |
| Display / Retail | Aesthetic structure with public access | Ornamental potted plants, flowering annuals |
| Conservatory | Aesthetic + educational | Exotic ornamentals, palms, ferns |

#### Model Solution to (b)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{pad} &= \dfrac{T_{out} - T_{in}}{T_{out} - T_{wb}} \times 100 \\
           &= \dfrac{36 - 27}{36 - 25} \times 100 \\
           &= \dfrac{9}{11} \times 100 \\
           &= 81.82 \; \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A cellulose cooling pad normally operates at **70 – 85 %** saturation efficiency. The computed value of **81.82 %** indicates that the pad is **operating efficiently and within acceptable engineering limits**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-losing mistakes in "Greenhouse Use" questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert ACH-based flow to m³/s** — always divide hourly flow by 3600.
> 2. **Mixing up T_in and T_out** in the pad-efficiency formula; remember: T_outside → T_pad-outlet → T_greenhouse.
> 3. **Writing LTF as a decimal instead of a percentage** — multiply by 100 at the end.
> 4. **Omitting units** in the final answer — every numerical answer MUST carry a unit (m, m³, m³/s, %, °C).
> 5. **Listing crops without relating them to the structure type** — examiners award marks only when the crop–structure pairing is justified.
> 6. **Skipping the feedback loop explanation** — for a 7-mark question on precision greenhouse, both sensing AND actuation layers must be explained.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Greenhouse Use:**
> - Greenhouse = transparent enclosed structure for **controlled-environment agriculture**.
> - **Six pillars of use:** off-season veg, cut flowers, nursery raising, TC hardening, hybrid seed production, research.
> - Crops to remember: **Tomato, Capsicum, Cucumber, Rose, Carnation, Gerbera, Strawberry**.
> - Three control classes: **high-tech, medium-tech, low-tech**.
> - Target interior: **T 22–28 °C, RH 60–80 %, CO₂ 350–1000 ppm**.
> - Ventilation formula: $Q = \dfrac{V \times ACH}{3600}$ m³/s.
> - Pad area: $A_{pad} = \dfrac{Q}{v}$ with $v$ = 1.0–1.5 m/s.
> - LTF = (PAR_in / PAR_out) × 100, normally **70–85 %**.
> - Pad efficiency target: **70–85 %**, formula $\eta = \dfrac{T_{out}-T_{in}}{T_{out}-T_{wb}} \times 100$.
> - Yield benchmarks: Tomato **30–60 kg/m²/yr**, Capsicum **15–25 kg/m²/yr**, Strawberry **2–4 kg/m²/cycle**.
> - VPD sweet spot for most greenhouse crops: **0.8 – 1.2 kPa**.
> - Always state **units** in the final answer.
> - In precision greenhouses, the loop is **Sensor → PLC → Actuator → Plant → Sensor**.
> - For KTU 14-mark questions, always draw the **block diagram** before writing theory.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Greenhouse
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT751 - PRECISION FARMING TECHNIQUES FOR PROTECTED CULTIVATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Greenhouse

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Greenhouse

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEAGT751 - Module 4)**
> A **Greenhouse** (also called a *glasshouse*, *polyhouse*, or *hothouse*) is a framed or inflated structure covered with a transparent or translucent glazing material in which crops are grown under controlled or partially controlled environmental conditions to obtain optimum growth, productivity, and quality. It is the most fundamental and widely adopted unit of **Protected Cultivation** engineering, designed to modify the natural environment for accelerated and year-round crop production.

According to the **Indian Council of Agricultural Research (ICAR)** and standard precision-farming literature, a greenhouse is essentially a **solar collector** that traps the short-wavelength solar radiation while blocking the outgoing long-wave terrestrial radiation, creating a favorable **greenhouse effect** that elevates internal air and soil temperature.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Simple Real-World Analogy — "The Parked Car on a Sunny Day"**
> Imagine parking a car with closed windows on a bright, sunny day. The sun's rays pass through the glass, heating the seats, dashboard, and air inside. The heat gets trapped because glass doesn't allow the warm infrared radiation to escape easily. Within an hour, the car interior becomes significantly hotter than the outside temperature. **A greenhouse works on the exact same principle** — but instead of parking for an hour, we park *plants* there for an entire season, with the added ability to control temperature, humidity, light, CO₂, water, and nutrients. It is essentially a "climate-controlled sunroom" for crops.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

The following engineering metrics and constants are universally used in greenhouse design:

- **Solar Constant (I_sc)**: **1,361 W/m²** (solar irradiance at the top of the atmosphere)
- **PAR (Photosynthetically Active Radiation) range**: **400 nm – 700 nm**
- **Earth's average albedo**: **0.30 (30%)**
- **Visible light transmission for plant growth**: **> 70%** (minimum recommended)
- **Standard CO₂ concentration (ambient)**: **~ 420 ppm** (enhanced greenhouse: 800–1500 ppm)
- **Optimum growing temperature**: **20 °C – 28 °C** for most vegetable crops
- **Relative Humidity (RH) optimum**: **60% – 80%**
- **Unit conversion factor**: **1 kcal/m²/hr = 1.163 W/m²**

## 1.4 Core Purpose of a Greenhouse

> [!NOTE]
> **Why do we need a Greenhouse? — The 5 Pillars of Protected Cultivation**
> 1. **Temperature Modification** — Protection from frost, cold waves, and heat stress.
> 2. **Humidity & Vapor Pressure Control** — Reduces fungal diseases and transpiration stress.
> 3. **Radiation Management** — Diffuses light, filters UV, extends photoperiod.
> 4. **Protection from Abiotic Stress** — Wind, hail, heavy rain, salinity, and pollutants.
> 5. **Biotic Stress Shielding** — Acts as a physical barrier against insects, birds, and larger pathogens when integrated with **net-house / insect-net** technology.

## 1.5 Scope of Greenhouse Technology in Kerala

Kerala, with its tropical humid climate, distinct **monsoon (June–September)**, and high pest pressure, has unique greenhouse requirements:

- Heavy rainfall → **Gable-roofed** or **rainwater-harvesting gutter-connected** designs are preferred.
- High humidity → Strong emphasis on **forced ventilation** and **anti-fungal cladding**.
- Year-round cultivation of **capsicum, tomato, cucumber, gerbera, orchid, anthurium**.
- Adoption of **naturally ventilated polyhouses** is common due to lower capital cost.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Greenhouses (KTU High-Yield Topic)

Greenhouse classification is a **favourite KTU exam question** (often asked for 7–14 marks). Master the following taxonomy:

### 2.1.1 Based on Shape / Roof Type

| Type | Description | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|
| **A-frame / Gable-roof** | Triangular cross-section, like a house | Maximum headroom; rainwater sheds easily |
| **Quonset / Arch-roof** | Semi-circular arch using GI pipes | Cheapest; low headroom at sides |
| **Gothic-arch** | Pointed Gothic arch roof | High light transmission; snow shedding |
| **Sawtooth** | Roof with one-sided ventilation slot | Excellent natural ventilation in tropics |
| **Venlo-type** | Multi-span with multiple gables + glass | Used in sophisticated Dutch greenhouses |
| **Dome / Geodesic** | Spherical structure | Aesthetic, high stability, experimental |
| **Slant-roof (even-span / uneven-span)** | Roof slants at fixed angle (usually equal to latitude) | Optimized for solar gain |

### 2.1.2 Based on Construction Material

> [!NOTE]
> **Cladding Material Hierarchy (in decreasing cost):**
> **Glass > Polycarbonate > Acrylic > Polyethylene (UV-stabilized) > Polyethylene (ordinary)**

- **Glass greenhouse**: Highest light transmission (~90%), long life (25+ yrs), high cost.
- **Polycarbonate multi-wall sheet**: Twin/triple-wall, light-weight, good insulation, life 10–15 yrs.
- **Polyethylene (PE) film**: 200 micron UV-stabilized; life 3–5 yrs; most common in India.
- **Acrylic / Plexiglass**: Light transmission ~85%, used in premium houses.
- **ETFE (Ethylene Tetrafluoroethylene)**: Modern fluoropolymer film; life 20+ yrs; used in advanced projects.

### 2.1.3 Based on Environmental Control

| Type | Control Level | Cost | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Low-tech / Naturally ventilated** | Temperature by ventilation only | Low | Kerala small farmers |
| **Medium-tech** | + Heating, cooling pad, shade net | Medium | Capsicum, gerbera |
| **High-tech / Fully controlled** | All parameters automated | High | Dutch roses, R&D |

### 2.1.4 Based on Cost & Government Subsidy Classification (India-specific)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NHM (National Horticulture Mission) Subsidy Categories — Frequently asked in KTU:**
> - **Small Greenhouse (≤ 500 m²)** — Subsidy ~50–75% for small farmers.
> - **Medium Greenhouse (500–1000 m²)** — Tubular structure, naturally ventilated.
> - **Large Greenhouse (> 1000 m²)** — High-tech fully controlled.

### 2.1.5 Other Special Classifications

- **Free-standing (independent)** vs **Gutter-connected (multi-span)**.
- **Permanent** vs **Temporary (walk-in tunnels / low tunnels)**.
- **Shade-net house** vs **Polyhouse** vs **Glasshouse** vs **Net-house**.
- **Hi-tech greenhouse** vs **Naturally ventilated greenhouse**.

## 2.2 Structural Components of a Greenhouse

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Always draw a **labelled cross-sectional diagram** of the greenhouse with the following components marked.

### 2.2.1 Foundation
- Made of **Reinforced Cement Concrete (RCC)** or pre-cast concrete blocks.
- Depth: typically **0.6 m – 1.0 m** below ground.
- Anchors the **column base plates** to resist wind uplift (critical in Kerala monsoon).

### 2.2.2 Structural Frame
- **Materials**: Galvanized Iron (GI) pipes, hollow steel sections (HSS), aluminium extrusions, or treated timber.
- **GI pipes**: Most common in India; diameter **25 mm – 50 mm** (25.4 mm / 1" NB standard).
- **Load-bearing**: Must withstand **dead load + live load + wind load (1.5 kN/m² typical)**.

### 2.2.3 Cladding / Glazing
- The transparent envelope — most critical component.
- Selected based on **transmittance, durability, IR/UV filtering, and cost**.

### 2.2.4 Ventilation System
- **Natural**: Side + roof vents; vent area = **15–25% of floor area**.
- **Mechanical (Forced)**: Exhaust fans + cooling pads (fan-pad system).
- **Rule of thumb**: Air exchange rate = **1–2 air changes per minute** in tropical conditions.

### 2.2.5 Heating System
- Used in cold regions or winter cultivation in North India.
- Methods: Hot water pipe heating, unit heaters, geothermal, solar.

### 2.2.6 Cooling System
- **Shade net (50% / 75% shading)**.
- **Evaporative cooling pads** (celdek / aspen pad) on one side + exhaust fans on the opposite side.
- **Foggers / Misters** for high-pressure humidity control.

### 2.2.7 Irrigation & Fertigation
- **Drip irrigation** is standard.
- Integrated with **fertigation unit** (Venturi, fertilizer tank, or injectors).
- Automated via **sensors + microcontroller / PLC**.

### 2.2.8 Other Accessories
- Shading screen (interior / exterior)
- Thermal / thermal screen for night insulation
- CO₂ enrichment system
- Benches / growing media (soil, coco-peat, perlite, NFT channels)
- Weather station (sensors for T, RH, PAR, wind)

## 2.3 Greenhouse Environment — Key Parameters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 6 Critical Control Parameters in Precision Greenhouse:**
> 1. **Air Temperature (T_air)** — optimum 20–28 °C
> 2. **Relative Humidity (RH)** — optimum 60–80%
> 3. **Photosynthetically Active Radiation (PAR)** — 400–700 nm
> 4. **CO₂ Concentration** — 350–1500 ppm
> 5. **Soil / Substrate Moisture** — controlled via tensiometer or volumetric sensors
> 6. **Electrical Conductivity (EC)** of nutrient solution — 1.5–3.0 mS/cm (hydroponics)

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All critical formulas tested in KTU 2024 PEAGT751 exams are consolidated below.**

### Table 2.4.A — Core Greenhouse Engineering Formulas

| # | Formula / Equation | Parameter Definitions | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\tau = \dfrac{I_{in}}{I_{out}}$ | $\tau$ = transmittance, $I_{in}$ = inside PAR, $I_{out}$ = outside PAR | dimensionless | Cladding material selection |
| 2 | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | $Q$ = heat transfer, $U$ = overall heat transfer coefficient, $A$ = area, $\Delta T$ = temp diff | W | Heat loss / gain calculation |
| 3 | $U = \dfrac{1}{R_t}$ | $R_t$ = total thermal resistance | W/m²·K | Insulation design |
| 4 | $V_{fan} = \dfrac{V_{green} \times N}{60}$ | $V_{fan}$ = fan capacity, $V_{green}$ = greenhouse volume, $N$ = air changes per minute | m³/min | Ventilation fan sizing |
| 5 | $A_{pad} = \dfrac{V_{fan}}{v_{pad}}$ | $A_{pad}$ = pad area, $v_{pad}$ = face velocity (typically 1.0–1.5 m/s) | m² | Cooling pad sizing |
| 6 | $E_{pan} = K_p \times (e_s - e_a)$ | $E_{pan}$ = pan evaporation, $K_p$ = pan coefficient, $e_s$ = saturation VP, $e_a$ = actual VP | mm/day | ET estimation |
| 7 | $ET_c = K_c \times ET_0$ | $ET_c$ = crop ET, $K_c$ = crop coefficient, $ET_0$ = reference ET | mm/day | Irrigation scheduling |
| 8 | $C_{CO_2} = \dfrac{M_{CO_2}}{V \times 10^6}$ | $C_{CO_2}$ = concentration, $M$ = mass, $V$ = volume | ppm | CO₂ enrichment |
| 9 | $\eta = \dfrac{PAR_{used}}{PAR_{incident}}$ | $\eta$ = PAR use efficiency | dimensionless | Light efficiency |
| 10 | $L = \dfrac{Q_{max}}{A \times \eta_{lum}}$ | $L$ = number of luminaires, $Q_{max}$ = max light demand, $\eta_{lum}$ = luminaire efficacy | count | Artificial lighting design |

### Table 2.4.B — Material Properties (Frequently asked)

| Material | Transmittance (%) | UV Block (%) | Service Life (years) | Cost (INR/m² approx.) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Float glass (4 mm) | 88–90 | 30 | 25+ | 800–1200 |
| Polycarbonate twin-wall (6 mm) | 78–82 | 99 | 10–15 | 700–1000 |
| UV-stabilized PE film (200 µm) | 85–88 | 40–60 | 3–5 | 60–100 |
| Acrylic sheet | 85–92 | 90 | 15–20 | 1500–2500 |
| 50% Shade net | 50 (block) | 30 | 5–7 | 30–50 |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in industry:**
> - A properly designed greenhouse in Kerala can yield **5–10 times** the open-field yield of capsicum.
> - **Dutch Venlo glasshouses** at 50°N latitude use computer-integrated climate control to produce **60 kg/m² of tomato/year** (open field: 3–4 kg/m²).
> - Greenhouse design integrates **mechanical (frame strength)**, **thermal (heat transfer)**, **optical (light transmission)**, and **biological (crop physiology)** engineering — making it a true **multi-disciplinary precision farming system**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient ($U$) for a Greenhouse Cover

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is a KTU favourite 14-mark question. Understand the derivation, do not memorize it blindly.**

The total heat loss through a greenhouse cladding is governed by conduction, convection, and radiation. We model the cover as a **series thermal resistance network**.

**Step 1: Identify the heat transfer modes**

Heat flows from inside air → through the cover material → to outside air. The thermal circuit involves:

- Inside air film resistance: $R_i = \dfrac{1}{h_i}$
- Cover material conduction resistance: $R_c = \dfrac{t}{k}$
- Outside air film resistance: $R_o = \dfrac{1}{h_o}$

**Step 2: Sum the resistances in series**

By Fourier's law of heat conduction and the surface heat transfer equation:

$$R_{total} = R_i + R_c + R_o$$

Substituting each:

$$R_{total} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{t}{k} + \frac{1}{h_o}$$

**Step 3: Derive the overall heat transfer coefficient**

By definition, the overall heat transfer coefficient is the reciprocal of total resistance:

$$U = \frac{1}{R_{total}}$$

Therefore:

$$U = \frac{1}{\dfrac{1}{h_i} + \dfrac{t}{k} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}}$$

**Step 4: Rearrange into a unified fraction**

Bringing to a common denominator (multiply by $h_i \cdot h_o / k$ appropriately):

$$U = \frac{h_i \cdot h_o \cdot k}{h_o \cdot k + h_i \cdot h_o \cdot t + h_i \cdot k}$$

**Step 5: Interpret each term**

- $h_i$ (inside convective coefficient) ≈ **4–8 W/m²·K** (free convection, still air)
- $h_o$ (outside convective coefficient) ≈ **10–30 W/m²·K** (forced convection from wind)
- $k$ (cover thermal conductivity): glass ≈ **1.05 W/m·K**, PE film ≈ **0.33 W/m·K**, polycarbonate twin-wall ≈ **0.21 W/m·K**
- $t$ = cover thickness (e.g., 0.004 m for glass, 0.0002 m for PE)

**Step 6: Numerical example (PE film, 200 µm)**

Given: $h_i = 6$ W/m²·K, $h_o = 20$ W/m²·K, $t = 0.0002$ m, $k = 0.33$ W/m·K.

Calculate each term:

$$h_o \cdot k = 20 \times 0.33 = 6.60 \text{ W/m·K}$$

$$h_i \cdot h_o \cdot t = 6 \times 20 \times 0.0002 = 0.024 \text{ W/m·K}$$

$$h_i \cdot k = 6 \times 0.33 = 1.98 \text{ W/m·K}$$

Sum the denominator:

$$\text{Denom} = 6.60 + 0.024 + 1.98 = 8.604 \text{ W/m·K}$$

Numerator:

$$h_i \cdot h_o \cdot k = 6 \times 20 \times 0.33 = 39.6 \text{ W²/m·K²}$$

Therefore:

$$U = \frac{39.6}{8.604} \approx 4.60 \text{ W/m²·K}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A single PE film has a higher $U$-value than expected because the **air film resistances dominate** (since the cover is so thin). Adding a **second inflated PE film layer** (double-layer inflation) creates an air gap that drastically reduces $U$ — this is why **double-poly houses** are common in cold climates.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Ventilation Fan Capacity (CFM / m³/min) Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Frequently asked in KTU 14-mark problems (PEAGT751 Module 4).**

**Step 1: Define air exchange rate**

The standard ventilation rate $N$ is the number of times the greenhouse air is completely replaced per minute (or per hour).

For tropical greenhouses, $N$ = **1 to 2 air changes per minute (ACM)** is recommended.

**Step 2: Compute the greenhouse internal volume**

For a **Quonset greenhouse** with floor length $L$, width $W$, and average height $H$:

$$V_{green} = L \times W \times H$$

For a **Gable-roof greenhouse**, include the roof volume:

$$V_{green} = L \times W \times H_{wall} + \frac{1}{2} \times L \times W \times H_{roof}$$

**Step 3: Fan capacity formula**

The total fan capacity $V_{fan}$ required:

$$V_{fan} = \frac{V_{green} \times N}{1} \quad \text{(in m}^3\text{/min)}$$

If expressed in m³/hr, multiply by 60:

$$V_{fan} = V_{green} \times N \times 60 \quad \text{(in m}^3\text{/hr)}$$

**Step 4: Cooling pad area**

The required cooling pad area $A_{pad}$ given face velocity $v_{pad}$ (typically 0.75–1.5 m/s):

$$A_{pad} = \frac{V_{fan}}{v_{pad}}$$

**Step 5: Worked Numerical Example**

> **Problem:** A naturally ventilated polyhouse is upgraded to a fan-pad cooled greenhouse. Dimensions: length = 30 m, width = 8 m, average height = 4 m. Required air exchange = 1.5 ACM. Cooling pad face velocity = 1.2 m/s. Calculate (a) fan capacity, (b) pad area.

**Step 5.1: Volume**

$$V_{green} = 30 \times 8 \times 4 = 960 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 5.2: Fan capacity**

$$V_{fan} = 960 \times 1.5 = 1440 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}$$

In m³/hr:

$$V_{fan} = 1440 \times 60 = 86{,}400 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}$$

**Step 5.3: Pad area**

$$A_{pad} = \frac{1440}{1.2 \times 60} = \frac{1440}{72} = 20 \text{ m}^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Conclusion:** A 1440 m³/min fan (≈ 1.5 HP per fan × 4 fans typically) and a 20 m² celdek pad are required. The pad is fitted to one gable end; opposite gable has the exhaust fans — this is the classic **fan-pad system**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Greenhouse PAR Transmittance & Light Use Efficiency

**Step 1: Definition of transmittance**

Transmittance is the ratio of inside PAR to outside PAR:

$$\tau = \frac{PAR_{inside}}{PAR_{outside}}$$

**Step 2: Combined transmittance of a multi-layer system**

If a greenhouse uses two covers in series (e.g., glass + inner thermal screen, or double PE film), the **combined transmittance is multiplicative**, not additive:

$$\tau_{combined} = \tau_1 \times \tau_2 \times \tau_3 \cdots$$

**Step 3: Light Use Efficiency (LUE)**

$$\text{LUE} = \frac{\text{Dry matter produced (g/m}^2\text{)}}{\text{PAR intercepted (MJ/m}^2\text{)}}$$

**Step 4: Numerical Example**

Given: $\tau_1$ (glass) = 0.88, $\tau_2$ (whitewash shading) = 0.75, $\tau_3$ (thermal screen, partially open) = 0.65.

$$\tau_{combined} = 0.88 \times 0.75 \times 0.65 = 0.429$$

So only **42.9%** of outside PAR enters the canopy — illustrates why each layer must be optimized.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — KTU 2024 Module 4 Mapping

| KTU Concept | Engineering Domain | Typical KTU Marks | Bloom's Level |
|---|---|---|---|
| Definition & Classification of Greenhouse | Agricultural Engineering | 3–7 | Remember / Understand |
| Cladding materials & properties | Materials Science | 7 | Understand / Apply |
| Structural components (frame, foundation, vent) | Civil / Mechanical | 7 | Apply |
| Heat transfer & U-value calculation | Thermal Engineering | 14 | Apply / Analyze |
| Ventilation & cooling pad sizing | HVAC | 7–14 | Apply / Analyze |
| Light transmission & PAR | Optics / Plant Physiology | 7 | Understand / Apply |
| Comparison of polyhouse vs net-house vs glasshouse | Comparative Analysis | 14 | Analyze / Evaluate |
| Site selection & orientation | Civil Engineering | 3–7 | Apply |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Precision Greenhouse System

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `n1`, `n2`, `n3`); labels contain only raw uppercase alphanumeric text with no markdown formatting.


# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern: 4 questions × 3 marks = 12 marks; answer ALL. Each answer should be 0.5–1 page with diagrams where required.**

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Define a greenhouse. List any four advantages of protected cultivation using greenhouses.**

**Model Answer:**

> A **greenhouse** is a framed or inflated structure, covered with transparent or translucent cladding material, in which crops are grown under **partially or fully controlled** environmental conditions to achieve optimum growth, productivity, and quality. It functions as a **solar collector**, exploiting the greenhouse effect to elevate internal temperature while protecting crops from adverse weather, pests, and diseases.

**Four advantages of greenhouse cultivation:**

1. **Off-season / year-round production** — Crops can be grown in any month, irrespective of the open-field season, fetching premium market prices.
2. **Higher yield per unit area** — Yield increases of **3–10 times** over open-field are commonly reported (e.g., capsicum: 80–100 t/ha vs 10–12 t/ha).
3. **Better quality produce** — Uniform size, colour, and shape; lower pesticide residue due to controlled environment.
4. **Efficient resource use** — Water savings up to **40–60%** through drip fertigation; precise nutrient delivery reduces fertilizer wastage.
5. *(Bonus)* Protection from heavy rain, wind, hail, frost, and vector-borne diseases.

**Diagrams expected:** Cross-section of a basic polyhouse.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Differentiate between polyhouse, net-house, and glasshouse. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison Table — Always draw a 3-column table in the exam:**

| Parameter | Polyhouse | Net-house | Glasshouse |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cladding | UV-stabilized polyethylene film (200 µm) | HDPE/HDPE + UV shade net (40–75%) | Float glass (4 mm) |
| Light transmission | 80–88% | 25–60% (intentionally reduced) | 88–92% |
| Primary purpose | Temperature + humidity control | Shade, pest exclusion, wind break | Year-round precision climate control |
| Service life | 3–5 years | 5–7 years | 25+ years |
| Cost (INR/m²) | 600–1000 | 250–450 | 2000–4000 |
| Typical crops | Capsicum, tomato, cucumber | Nursery, leafy greens, orchids | Cut flowers, R&D, Dutch roses |
| Best suited for | Indian tropical climate | Kerala humid climate | Cold-climate high-tech farms |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Module 4 carries approximately 14–21 marks. The exam provides an **internal choice** between two questions. Model solutions below for BOTH choices.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **classification of greenhouses** based on (i) shape, (ii) cladding material, and (iii) environmental control level. Add a note on the suitability of each type for Indian conditions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A polyhouse of length 40 m, width 9 m, and average height 4.5 m is to be fitted with a fan-pad cooling system. The required air change rate is **1.8 air changes per minute (ACM)**, and the recommended face velocity through the celdek pad is **1.25 m/s**. Calculate:
- (i) Total ventilation fan capacity in m³/min and m³/hr.
- (ii) Required cooling pad area.
- (iii) The transmittance of the cladding if the outside PAR is 850 W/m² and the inside PAR measured is 680 W/m². **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagrams expected:** Labelled sketches of at least 4 different greenhouse shapes.

**(i) Classification by Shape (3 marks):**

| Shape | Description | Suitability for India |
|---|---|---|
| **A-frame / Gable** | Triangular roof; high headroom; easy rain shedding | Excellent for high-rainfall Kerala |
| **Quonset** | Semi-circular arch; low cost; low side headroom | Suitable for low-cost production in plains |
| **Sawtooth** | Asymmetric roof with one-sided vent | Best for tropical India (passive ventilation) |
| **Gothic** | Pointed arch; sheds snow; high light | Suitable for North Indian hills |
| **Venlo (multi-span)** | Multi-bay glass; modular; maximum land efficiency | Used in Karnataka & Maharashtra export hubs |

**(ii) Classification by Cladding Material (2 marks):**

- **Glass (4 mm float)**: 90% transmission, 25-year life, heavy & expensive.
- **Polycarbonate (multi-wall)**: 80% transmission, 10-year life, good insulation, premium cost.
- **UV-stabilized PE film (200 µm)**: 85% transmission, 3–5 year life, cheapest, most common in India.
- **Acrylic / Plexiglass**: 92% transmission, 15-year life, premium.

**(iii) Classification by Environmental Control (2 marks):**

- **Naturally ventilated (low-tech)**: Vent area 15–25% floor area; no mechanical cooling. Cost: ₹ 600–1000/m².
- **Medium-tech (fan-pad cooled)**: Includes exhaust fans, cooling pads, drip system. Cost: ₹ 1500–2500/m².
- **High-tech (fully automated)**: Computerized control of T, RH, CO₂, fertigation. Cost: ₹ 4000–8000/m². Used in export floriculture and R&D.

> **Indian Context:** Small farmers prefer **naturally ventilated polyhouses** under NHM subsidy. High-tech houses are concentrated in **Maharashtra (Pune, Nasik)**, **Karnataka (Bengaluru, Kolar)**, and **Gujarat**.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given data:**
- Length $L = 40$ m
- Width $W = 9$ m
- Average height $H = 4.5$ m
- Air change rate $N = 1.8$ ACM
- Face velocity $v_{pad} = 1.25$ m/s
- Outside PAR = 850 W/m²
- Inside PAR = 680 W/m²

**Step 1: Compute greenhouse volume**

$$V_{green} = L \times W \times H = 40 \times 9 \times 4.5 = 1620 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2: Fan capacity in m³/min**

$$V_{fan} = V_{green} \times N = 1620 \times 1.8 = 2916 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}$$

**Step 3: Fan capacity in m³/hr**

$$V_{fan} = 2916 \times 60 = 1{,}74{,}960 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}$$

**Step 4: Cooling pad area**

Convert $v_{pad}$ from m/s to m/min: $1.25 \text{ m/s} = 1.25 \times 60 = 75$ m/min.

$$A_{pad} = \frac{V_{fan}}{v_{pad}} = \frac{2916}{75} = 38.88 \text{ m}^2 \approx 38.9 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 5: Transmittance of cladding**

$$\tau = \frac{PAR_{in}}{PAR_{out}} = \frac{680}{850} = 0.80 = 80\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Many students **forget to convert m/s to m/min** when computing $A_{pad}$, leading to a 60× error.
> 2. Some students **omit the unit** of fan capacity (must clearly state m³/min or m³/hr).
> 3. Transmittance should always be given as a **dimensionless ratio or percentage**, not in W/m².

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024]

**(a)** Describe the **major structural components** of a greenhouse with a neat labelled diagram. Explain the role of the cladding material and the criteria for its selection. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the formula for the **overall heat transfer coefficient (U)** of a greenhouse cover. A single-layer PE film cover (thickness = 0.0002 m, $k = 0.33$ W/m·K) has $h_i = 5.5$ W/m²·K and $h_o = 22$ W/m²·K. Calculate $U$ and the heat loss per m² if the inside–outside temperature difference is 18 °C. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

> **Labelled diagram (4 marks):** A neat Gable-roof polyhouse showing — Foundation, Column, Rafter, Purlin, Gutter, Cladding, Side vent, Roof vent, Door, Drip line, Fan, Pad, Sensors.

**Structural Components (2 marks):**

1. **Foundation**: RCC or pre-cast blocks, depth 60–100 cm; resists wind uplift.
2. **Structural Frame**: GI pipes (25–50 mm dia), aluminium, or treated timber; carries dead + live + wind load.
3. **Cladding / Covering**: Transparent film (PE, polycarbonate, glass); determines light, heat, and durability.
4. **Ventilation System**: Side roll-up + roof vents (natural) or exhaust fans (mechanical).
5. **Irrigation / Fertigation**: Drip lines, venturi injectors, sand filters, automation.
6. **Heating / Cooling**: Pad-fan, heaters, shade net, foggers.
7. **Control & Monitoring**: Sensors (T, RH, PAR, CO₂), PLC / microcontroller.

**Cladding Selection Criteria (1 mark):**

- **Light transmission** ≥ 80% (for most crops).
- **Diffusion** — high diffusion reduces scorching.
- **UV stability** — must block harmful UV-B but transmit PAR.
- **IR retention** — high IR retention saves heating energy.
- **Mechanical strength** — wind, hail resistance.
- **Service life** vs **cost** trade-off.
- **Anti-drip / anti-fog** properties (for high humidity like Kerala).

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Derivation of $U$-value (4 marks):**

The total heat transfer from inside to outside the greenhouse cover passes through three series resistances:

- Inside air film: $R_i = 1/h_i$
- Cover material (conduction): $R_c = t/k$
- Outside air film: $R_o = 1/h_o$

Total resistance:

$$R_{total} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{t}{k} + \frac{1}{h_o}$$

Overall heat transfer coefficient:

$$U = \frac{1}{R_{total}} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{1}{h_i} + \dfrac{t}{k} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}}$$

Or, expressed in expanded form:

$$U = \frac{h_i \cdot h_o \cdot k}{h_o \cdot k + h_i \cdot h_o \cdot t + h_i \cdot k}$$

**Numerical calculation (3 marks):**

Given: $h_i = 5.5$ W/m²·K, $h_o = 22$ W/m²·K, $t = 0.0002$ m, $k = 0.33$ W/m·K.

Step 1 — Compute each denominator term:

$$h_o \cdot k = 22 \times 0.33 = 7.26 \text{ W/m·K}$$

$$h_i \cdot h_o \cdot t = 5.5 \times 22 \times 0.0002 = 0.0242 \text{ W/m·K}$$

$$h_i \cdot k = 5.5 \times 0.33 = 1.815 \text{ W/m·K}$$

Sum: $7.26 + 0.0242 + 1.815 = 9.0992$ W/m·K

Step 2 — Numerator:

$$h_i \cdot h_o \cdot k = 5.5 \times 22 \times 0.33 = 39.93 \text{ W}^2/\text{m}^2\text{·K}^2$$

Step 3 — $U$:

$$U = \frac{39.93}{9.0992} = 4.388 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$$

Step 4 — Heat loss per m² (Newton's law of cooling, $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$):

With $A = 1$ m², $\Delta T = 18$ K:

$$Q = 4.388 \times 1 \times 18 = 78.98 \text{ W/m}^2$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Students often **swap $h_i$ and $h_o$** values — always verify which is inside and which is outside.
> 2. Forgetting the **unit conversion of $t$** (must be in metres) — common 14-mark killer.
> 3. The conduction term $t/k$ is **often very small** compared to the convective film terms; do NOT skip it (examiners expect the full expression).
> 4. Final answer must carry **units** — students lose 0.5–1 mark for missing units.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Save this for the night before the exam)**

### ✅ Core Definition
- A **greenhouse** is a **solar collector** exploiting the **greenhouse effect** to modify the microclimate for crop production.

### ✅ Classification (Must-know 3 axes)
- **By shape**: A-frame, Quonset, Gothic, Sawtooth, Venlo, Dome.
- **By cladding**: Glass > Polycarbonate > Acrylic > PE film.
- **By control**: Low-tech natural, Medium-tech pad-fan, High-tech fully automated.

### ✅ Components
- Foundation → Frame → Cladding → Ventilation → Heating/Cooling → Irrigation → Sensors → Control.

### ✅ Key Cladding Metrics
- Light transmission: **> 80%**; UV block: **30–99%**; service life: **3–25 years**.

### ✅ Formula You MUST Memorize
- $U = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{1}{h_i} + \dfrac{t}{k} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}}$
- $V_{fan} = V_{green} \times N$ (where $N$ = air changes/min)
- $A_{pad} = \dfrac{V_{fan}}{v_{pad}}$
- $\tau = \dfrac{PAR_{in}}{PAR_{out}}$

### ✅ Kerala-Specific Facts
- Heavy monsoon → use **gable-roof** with **strong gutter systems**.
- High RH → use **forced ventilation** + **anti-condensate film**.
- Popular crops: **capsicum, tomato, cucumber, gerbera, anthurium, orchid**.
- Subsidy available under **MIDH / NHM** schemes.

### ✅ NHM Size Categories (India)
- Small: ≤ 500 m²
- Medium: 500–1000 m²
- Large: > 1000 m²

### ✅ Numerical Conversions to Remember
- $1 \text{ kcal/hr} = 1.163 \text{ W}$
- $1 \text{ ACM} = 60 \text{ air changes/hr}$
- $1 \text{ m/s} = 60 \text{ m/min}$

### ✅ Common Exam Mistakes to Avoid
- ❌ Drawing only a *box* labelled "greenhouse" without labelled components.
- ❌ Confusing **polyhouse** with **net-house** in definitions.
- ❌ Omitting the **transmittance equation** derivation step.
- ❌ Not stating **units** in numerical answers.
- ❌ Missing **site orientation** (N–S long axis in N. India, E–W in S. India) in design questions.

### ✅ Past Paper Hot Topics
- Classification — appears almost every year.
- $U$-value derivation + numerical.
- Pad-fan sizing numerical.
- Comparison of polyhouse / net-house / glasshouse (3-mark or 14-mark).
- Cladding material selection criteria.

> **End of Notes — Module 4: Greenhouse | PEAGT751 | KTU 2024 Scheme**

topics.title: Insect Pests and Disease Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT751 - PRECISION FARMING TECHNIQUES FOR PROTECTED CULTIVATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Insect Pests and Disease Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 : Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Protected Cultivation Pest & Disease Management** is the science and practice of preventing, monitoring, and controlling biotic stresses (insect pests, mites, pathogens, and weeds) within an enclosed or semi-enclosed growing environment (polyhouse, greenhouse, net house, or shade net) using a combination of cultural, mechanical, biological, chemical, and **precision-monitoring (IoT/sensor-based)** techniques to sustain the **economic threshold level (ETL)** and ensure a healthy crop yield.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, this topic falls under the **Integrated Pest Management (IPM)** paradigm, which the Indian Council of Agricultural Research (ICAR) defines as:

> A sustainable approach combining biological, cultural, physical, and chemical tools in a way that minimizes economic, health, and environmental risks.

For protected structures, the management strategy is **fundamentally different** from open-field cultivation because:

- The enclosed micro-climate raises **relative humidity (RH)** to **>85%**, accelerating fungal pathogens.
- The **monoculture / continuous cropping** pattern of high-value vegetables (capsicum, tomato, cucumber, gerbera) creates an ideal breeding ground for sucking pests like *Bemisia tabaci* and *Tetranychus urticae*.
- **Natural enemies** (ladybird beetles, lacewings) are often excluded by the screen/film.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The syllabus explicitly demands knowledge of *biological control agents* (parasitoids, predators, and microbial antagonists) and *precision tools* (yellow/blue sticky traps, pheromone traps, spore traps, and AI-based image diagnostics) — these are **high-yield topics** for 14-mark questions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Greenhouse as a Hospital ICU"

Imagine a polyhouse as a **hospital Intensive Care Unit (ICU)** for plants.

- Just as an ICU keeps patients in a controlled environment to recover from disease, the polyhouse keeps crops in a controlled environment to maximize yield.
- But an ICU is also **vulnerable to outbreaks** — a single bacterium (MRSA) can spread rapidly among immunocompromised patients. Similarly, a single whitefly entering a polyhouse can multiply **10–15× in 7–10 days**, infecting the entire crop.
- The pest manager acts like the **doctor**: he must *diagnose* (scouting + sensors), *prescribe* (biocontrol/chemical), and *prevent reinfection* (sanitation + screening).
- The **"immune system"** of the polyhouse is the population of **natural enemies** (biocontrol agents) plus the **cultural practices** (crop rotation, clean planting material).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exam:** Protected cultivation favors *sucking pests* and *fungal pathogens* far more than *chewing pests* and *bacterial diseases*, because the high humidity and absence of rain wash-off create a "soft, humid incubator" environment.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics in Protected Cultivation Pest Management

- **ETL (Economic Threshold Level)** = pest density at which control measures should be applied to prevent the pest population from reaching the **EIL (Economic Injury Level)**.
- Standard scouting units: **1 yellow sticky trap per 100 m²** area.
- Standard IPM scouting frequency: **once every 7 days** (weekly).
- Recommended greenhouse screening mesh size to exclude whiteflies: **50-mesh (≈ 0.3 mm aperture)**.
- Optimal greenhouse temperature to suppress *Botrytis cinerea*: **< 22 °C with RH < 85%**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 : Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Pests & Diseases in Protected Cultivation

#### A. Major Insect & Mite Pests (with scientific names)

| Category | Common Pest | Scientific Name | Typical Crop in Polyhouse | Damage Symptom |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Sucking | Whitefly | *Bemisia tabaci* | Tomato, Capsicum | Yellowing, sooty mold, transmits **ToLCV** |
| Sucking | Aphid | *Myzus persicae* | Capsicum, Cucumber | Leaf curling, honeydew |
| Sucking | Thrips | *Thrips palmi* | Capsicum, Gerbera | Silvery streaks, transmits **TSWV** |
| Mite | Two-spotted spider mite | *Tetranychus urticae* | Cucumber, Rose | Stippling, webbing |
| Borer | Tomato fruit borer | *Helicoverpa armigera* | Tomato | Bored fruits |
| Leaf miner | Serpentine leaf miner | *Liriomyza trifolii* | Gerbera, Tomato | White zig-zag mines on leaf |

#### B. Major Diseases (with pathogens)

| Disease Type | Disease | Pathogen | Favourable Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fungal | Powdery Mildew | *Podosphaera xanthii* | High RH at night, dry day (60–80% RH) |
| Fungal | Downy Mildew | *Pseudoperonospora cubensis* | RH > 85%, leaf wetness 6 h |
| Fungal | Grey Mold | *Botrytis cinerea* | Temp 15–22 °C, RH > 90% |
| Fungal | Damping Off | *Pythium* spp., *Rhizoctonia solani* | Wet soil, poor drainage |
| Fungal | Fusarium Wilt | *Fusarium oxysporum* f. sp. *lycopersici* | Soil temp 28 °C |
| Bacterial | Bacterial Wilt | *Ralstonia solanacearum* | High soil moisture, temp 30–35 °C |
| Viral | Tomato Leaf Curl | *Begomovirus* (ToLCV) | Transmitted by whitefly |

### 2.2 The Disease Triangle — "Why Diseases Occur"

A disease in a polyhouse occurs only when **three factors intersect simultaneously**:

1. **Susceptible Host** (e.g., capsicum variety).
2. **Virulent Pathogen** (e.g., *Phytophthora capsici* sporangia).
3. **Favourable Environment** (e.g., RH > 90%, temp 25 °C, free water on leaf for 4 h).

> The polyhouse artificially controls #3, so the manager's role is to **break at least one corner of the triangle** to prevent disease.

### 2.3 The IPM Pyramid (Hierarchy of Control in KTU Protected Cultivation)

The IPM strategy follows a **bottom-up hierarchy**:

- **Level 1 — Cultural Practices (Foundation):** Resistant varieties, certified disease-free seeds, sanitation, crop rotation, spacing, sterilization of substrate.
- **Level 2 — Mechanical / Physical Methods:** Yellow & blue sticky traps, pheromone traps, insect-proof nets (50-mesh), light traps, hot water treatment of seed (52 °C for 30 min).
- **Level 3 — Biological Control:** Release of parasitoids (*Encarsia formosa*), predators (*Phytoseiulus persimilis*), and microbial antagonists (*Trichoderma viride*).
- **Level 4 — Botanical / Biorational Pesticides:** Neem oil (Azadirachtin 0.03%), *Beauveria bassiana* sprays.
- **Level 5 — Chemical Pesticides (Last Resort):** Used only when pest population crosses ETL. Rotate insecticide groups to prevent resistance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this hierarchy?** In KTU valuation, mentioning the *order* of IPM components (Cultural → Mechanical → Biological → Chemical) carries marks. The examiner looks for the student's understanding that **chemicals are the LAST resort**, not the first option.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE FOR MARKDOWN:** All absolute values and vertical bars below are rendered using `\vert` instead of `|` to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax. The same convention applies to set-builder notation.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Expression | Application in Polyhouse |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Pesticide Dose Calculation** (for any concentration-based spray) | $D = \dfrac{C_r \times V_s}{F_s \times 100}$ | Where $D$ = dose of commercial formulation (mL or g), $C_r$ = recommended concentration (%), $V_s$ = spray volume (L), $F_s$ = formulation strength (%) |
| 2 | **Spray Volume per Unit Area** (for canopy-based spraying) | $V_s = A_c \times d$ | $A_c$ = canopy area, $d$ = depth of spray deposit (≈ 0.0005 L/m²) |
| 3 | **Economic Threshold Level (ETL)** | $ETL = \dfrac{C_c}{V \times D_u \times K}$ | $C_c$ = cost of control, $V$ = market value (₹/kg), $D_u$ = damage per unit pest, $K$ = control efficacy |
| 4 | **Economic Injury Level (EIL)** | $EIL = \dfrac{C_c}{V \times D_u}$ | The pest density that causes economic loss exactly equal to cost of control |
| 5 | **Van der Plank Disease Progress** (logistic model) | $\dfrac{dx}{dt} = r \cdot x \cdot (1 - x)$ | $x$ = proportion of diseased tissue, $r$ = infection rate, $t$ = time in days |
| 6 | **Logistic Solution (Apparent Infection Rate)** | $r = \dfrac{1}{t_2 - t_1} \ln\!\left[\dfrac{x_2 (1 - x_1)}{x_1 (1 - x_2)}\right]$ | Used to compare pathogen aggressiveness across seasons |
| 7 | **Area Under Disease Progress Curve (AUDPC)** | $AUDPC = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \dfrac{(Y_i + Y_{i+1})}{2} (t_{i+1} - t_i)$ | $Y_i$ = disease severity (%) at time $t_i$, $n$ = number of observations |
| 8 | **Disease Severity (%)** | $DS = \dfrac{\sum (n_i \times s_i)}{N \times S_{max}} \times 100$ | $n_i$ = number of plants in grade $i$, $s_i$ = score of grade $i$, $N$ = total plants, $S_{max}$ = max grade |
| 9 | **Trap Density (Sticky Traps)** | $N_t = \dfrac{A_{poly}}{100}$ | $A_{poly}$ = polyhouse area in m², $N_t$ = number of yellow sticky traps |
| 10 | **Biocontrol Release Ratio** (e.g., *Encarsia*) | $R_r = \dfrac{P_{released}}{P_{observed}}$ | Standard release: 1 parasitoid per 10 whitefly adults on sticky trap |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas and concepts are not just academic — they are used by:

- **Indo-Israel Greenhouse Projects** in Karnataka, Maharashtra, and Haryana where IPM is mandatory for export-grade produce.
- **Precision AgTech startups** (CropIn, Fasal) deploying IoT sensors + AI cameras for early pest detection.
- **NABL-accredited plant pathology labs** in Kerala (KAU, IIHR) for disease diagnosis using ELISA and PCR techniques.

## SECTION 3 : Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Logistic Disease Progress Equation (Van der Plank)

**Starting Concept:** Disease does not grow infinitely — it saturates when nearly 100% of the host tissue is infected. We want an equation that captures this "S-shaped" growth.

**Step 1 — Set up the basic assumption.**
Let $x$ = fraction of host tissue that is currently diseased.
Let $(1 - x)$ = fraction that is still healthy and therefore **susceptible** to new infection.
The number of new infections per unit time must be proportional to **both** the amount of healthy tissue AND the amount of infectious (diseased) tissue already present (since that tissue produces inoculum).

$$
\dfrac{dx}{dt} = r \cdot x \cdot (1 - x)
$$

Where $r$ = apparent infection rate constant (per day).

**Step 2 — Separate the variables.**

$$
\dfrac{dx}{x(1 - x)} = r \cdot dt
$$

**Step 3 — Apply partial fraction decomposition.**

$$
\dfrac{1}{x(1 - x)} = \dfrac{1}{x} + \dfrac{1}{1 - x}
$$

So the equation becomes:

$$
\int \left( \dfrac{1}{x} + \dfrac{1}{1 - x} \right) dx = \int r \, dt
$$

**Step 4 — Integrate both sides.**

$$
\ln \vert x \vert - \ln \vert 1 - x \vert = r \cdot t + C
$$

$$
\ln\!\left( \dfrac{x}{1 - x} \right) = r \cdot t + C
$$

**Step 5 — Apply initial condition** $x = x_1$ at $t = t_1$ to find $C$.

$$
C = \ln\!\left( \dfrac{x_1}{1 - x_1} \right) - r \cdot t_1
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $r$ between two observation points** $(x_1, t_1)$ and $(x_2, t_2)$.

Subtracting the two logit equations:

$$
\ln\!\left( \dfrac{x_2}{1 - x_2} \right) - \ln\!\left( \dfrac{x_1}{1 - x_1} \right) = r (t_2 - t_1)
$$

$$
\ln\!\left[ \dfrac{x_2 (1 - x_1)}{x_1 (1 - x_2)} \right] = r (t_2 - t_1)
$$

$$
\boxed{\, r = \dfrac{1}{t_2 - t_1} \ln\!\left[ \dfrac{x_2 (1 - x_1)}{x_1 (1 - x_2)} \right] \,}
$$

This is the **Apparent Infection Rate** used to compare the aggressiveness of pathogens.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — AUDPC & Disease Severity

**Problem:** A capsicum polyhouse is monitored for *powdery mildew* for 4 weeks. The disease severity (%) recorded at 7-day intervals is: 5, 18, 42, 75. Calculate (a) the AUDPC and (b) the Apparent Infection Rate between Day 7 and Day 21.

#### Part (a) — AUDPC

Using:

$$
AUDPC = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \dfrac{(Y_i + Y_{i+1})}{2} (t_{i+1} - t_i)
$$

Segment 1 (Day 0 → Day 7): $Y_0 = 0$ (assumed), $Y_1 = 5$

$$
A_1 = \dfrac{(0 + 5)}{2} \times 7 = 17.5
$$

Segment 2 (Day 7 → Day 14): $Y_1 = 5$, $Y_2 = 18$

$$
A_2 = \dfrac{(5 + 18)}{2} \times 7 = 80.5
$$

Segment 3 (Day 14 → Day 21): $Y_2 = 18$, $Y_3 = 42$

$$
A_3 = \dfrac{(18 + 42)}{2} \times 7 = 210
$$

Segment 4 (Day 21 → Day 28): $Y_3 = 42$, $Y_4 = 75$

$$
A_4 = \dfrac{(42 + 75)}{2} \times 7 = 409.5
$$

Total AUDPC:

$$
AUDPC = 17.5 + 80.5 + 210 + 409.5 = 717.5 \; \% \cdot \text{day}
$$

#### Part (b) — Apparent Infection Rate

Using $t_1 = 7$ days, $x_1 = 0.05$; $t_2 = 21$ days, $x_2 = 0.42$:

$$
r = \dfrac{1}{21 - 7} \ln\!\left[ \dfrac{0.42 \times (1 - 0.05)}{0.05 \times (1 - 0.42)} \right]
$$

$$
r = \dfrac{1}{14} \ln\!\left[ \dfrac{0.42 \times 0.95}{0.05 \times 0.58} \right]
$$

$$
r = \dfrac{1}{14} \ln\!\left[ \dfrac{0.3990}{0.0290} \right]
$$

$$
r = \dfrac{1}{14} \ln(13.7586)
$$

$$
r = \dfrac{1}{14} \times 2.6214
$$

$$
\boxed{\, r \approx 0.187 \; \text{per day} \,}
$$

**Interpretation:** Each day, the disease multiplies at ≈ 18.7% — a rapid epidemic that demands **immediate fungicide rotation + humidity reduction below 80% RH**.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Pesticide Dose Calculation

**Problem:** A 400 m² capsicum polyhouse requires *Imidacloprid 17.8% SL* spray against whitefly at a recommended concentration of 0.3 mL/L. Recommended spray volume = 1 L per 50 m² area. Commercial formulation strength = 17.8% SL. Calculate the total pesticide required.

**Step 1 — Calculate total spray volume.**

$$
V_s = \dfrac{400}{50} \times 1 = 8 \; \text{L}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the dose formula.**

$$
D = \dfrac{C_r \times V_s}{F_s} = \dfrac{0.3 \times 8}{17.8} \times 100
$$

$$
D = \dfrac{2.4}{17.8} \times 100 = 13.48 \; \text{mL}
$$

**Step 3 — Sanity check.** For a 400 m² polyhouse, 13.48 mL of imidacloprid is realistic (typical commercial dose = 10–15 mL per 400 m²).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right IPM Tool

| Pest / Disease | Threshold Trigger | 1st Choice Tool | 2nd Choice Tool | Avoid |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Whitefly (Bemisia tabaci) | > 5 adults/trap/week | Yellow sticky trap + *Encarsia formosa* release @ 1 per 10 adults | Neem oil 0.5% | Broad-spectrum pyrethroids (kill biocontrol agents) |
| Spider Mite (*T. urticae*) | > 10 mites/leaf | *Phytoseiulus persimilis* release (2/m²) | Abamectin 0.5 mL/L | Sulphur at > 30 °C (phytotoxic) |
| Thrips | > 3 thrips/flower | Blue sticky trap + *Neoseiulus cucumeris* | Spinosad 0.3 mL/L | — |
| Powdery Mildew | First symptom | Sulphur dust 0.2% | *Ampelomyces quisqualis* (biocontrol) | Overhead irrigation (raises RH) |
| Botrytis (Grey Mold) | RH > 90% for 6 h | Ventilation + heating to drop RH < 85% | *Bacillus subtilis* spray | Late evening sprays (prolong leaf wetness) |
| Damping Off | Pre-emergence | *Trichoderma viride* seed treatment @ 4 g/kg | Captan 50% WP @ 2 g/L | Overwatering |

## SECTION 5 : KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Q1.** Define **Integrated Pest Management (IPM)**. List any four components of IPM in a protected cultivation system.

**Model Answer:**
IPM is a sustainable pest management strategy that combines cultural, mechanical/physical, biological, and chemical methods in a compatible manner to keep pest populations below the economic threshold level (ETL) while minimizing environmental and health hazards.

**Four components of IPM in protected cultivation:**
1. **Cultural practices** — resistant cultivars, crop rotation, sanitation, proper spacing, clean planting material.
2. **Mechanical/Physical methods** — yellow & blue sticky traps (1 per 100 m²), pheromone traps, insect-proof nets (50-mesh), light traps.
3. **Biological control** — release of natural enemies such as *Encarsia formosa* (whitefly parasitoid), *Phytoseiulus persimilis* (predatory mite), and microbial antagonists like *Trichoderma viride*.
4. **Chemical control** — used as last resort with selective, rotation-based insecticides/fungicides only when pest crosses ETL.

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** What is the **Economic Threshold Level (ETL)**? Write the formula for ETL.

**Model Answer:**
ETL is the pest population density at which control measures must be applied to prevent the rising pest population from reaching the **Economic Injury Level (EIL)** — the density at which the cost of crop damage equals the cost of control.

$$
ETL = \dfrac{C_c}{V \times D_u \times K}
$$

Where $C_c$ = cost of control (₹/ha), $V$ = market value of produce (₹/kg), $D_u$ = damage per unit pest (kg/pest), $K$ = efficacy of control measure (0 to 1).

For example, in capsicum, the ETL for whitefly is **5 adults per yellow sticky trap per week** — beyond this, *Encarsia formosa* release or neem spray is recommended.

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *Focus: Biological Control & ETL Calculation*

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the **biological control** of **whitefly (*Bemisia tabaci*)** in a polyhouse using parasitoids, predators, and entomopathogenic fungi. Mention the release schedule and dosage.

**Model Solution:**

**Whitefly — *Bemisia tabaci* — a key pest of tomato, capsicum, and cucumber in polyhouses.**

**Step 1 — Why whitefly is the most damaging pest in polyhouses:**
- It transmits the devastating **Tomato Leaf Curl Virus (ToLCV)**.
- Produces honeydew → sooty mold → reduces photosynthesis by up to **40%**.
- Multiplies rapidly — a female lays **100–200 eggs**, generation time = **18–25 days**.
- Develops resistance to insecticides quickly (especially pyrethroids and organophosphates).

**Step 2 — Biological control agents (the "Three Pillars"):**

| Agent | Type | Target Stage | Dose per m² | Release Schedule |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| *Encarsia formosa* | Parasitoid wasp | 2nd instar nymph | 5–10 wasps/m² | Weekly for 4–6 weeks |
| *Delphastus catalinae* | Predatory beetle | Eggs & adults | 2–3 beetles/m² | Bi-weekly, 2 releases |
| *Beauveria bassiana* | Entomopathogenic fungus | All stages | 1×10¹³ spores/ha (5 g/L) | Spray at 7-day interval |
| *Macrolophus pygmaeus* | Predatory bug | Eggs, larvae | 5 bugs/m² | Once at transplanting + 2 weeks after |

**Step 3 — Release Protocol:**
- Begin release **at first sign of whitefly** (≥ 1 adult per yellow sticky trap per week).
- Release *Encarsia formosa* in the **morning** when temperature = 20–25 °C and RH > 60%.
- Avoid pesticide sprays **at least 7 days before and 14 days after** release.
- Maintain RH between **70–85%** to favor parasitoid survival.

**Step 4 — Monitoring & Re-release:**
- Count parasitized scales (turn **black**) on leaves every 7 days.
- If parasitism > 70% → no further release needed.
- If parasitism < 50% → re-introduce at higher dose.

**Conclusion:** Biological control of whitefly in polyhouses is a **proven, sustainable** method that achieves > 90% pest suppression in 4–6 weeks without chemical residues, making it ideal for export-grade produce.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** In a tomato polyhouse of 1000 m² area, the cost of one insecticidal spray is ₹ 2,500. The market price of tomato is ₹ 20 per kg, and one whitefly adult causes a damage of 0.02 kg of fruit per week. The control measure has an efficacy of 0.8. Calculate the **EIL and ETL**. If a yellow sticky trap counts 8 whitefly adults per week, should the farmer spray?

**Model Solution:**

**Given data:**
- $C_c$ = ₹ 2,500
- $V$ = ₹ 20/kg
- $D_u$ = 0.02 kg/whitefly/week
- $K$ = 0.8 (efficacy)
- Observed pest = 8 adults/week

**Step 1 — Calculate EIL.**

$$
EIL = \dfrac{C_c}{V \times D_u} = \dfrac{2500}{20 \times 0.02} = \dfrac{2500}{0.4} = 6250 \; \text{whiteflies}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate ETL.**

$$
ETL = \dfrac{C_c}{V \times D_u \times K} = \dfrac{2500}{20 \times 0.02 \times 0.8} = \dfrac{2500}{0.32} = 7812.5 \; \text{whiteflies}
$$

**Wait — re-check the logic.** A 1000 m² polyhouse is too small to host 7,800 whiteflies weekly. The values seem high.

**Correction:** In standard textbook problems, $D_u$ is interpreted as **damage per whitefly per unit area**, and the formula yields a *density per m²* or *per leaf*, not total count. The correct interpretation is:

- ETL ≈ 5 whiteflies per **yellow sticky trap per week** (industry standard for *Bemisia tabaci* in polyhouses).

**Step 3 — Compare observed value with ETL.**
- Observed = 8 whiteflies/trap/week.
- Standard ETL = 5 whiteflies/trap/week.
- Since 8 > 5, the farmer **should spray** (or release biocontrol agents).

**Conclusion:** When pest density **exceeds** ETL, immediate control action is justified because the projected damage will exceed the cost of control. Below ETL, natural control / monitoring is sufficient.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *Focus: Disease Cycle, Calculation, and Precision Tools*

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **disease cycle of *Botrytis cinerea* (grey mold)** in a polyhouse with a neat diagram. Discuss the cultural, biological, and chemical management strategies.

**Model Solution:**

**Pathogen:** *Botrytis cinerea* (imperfect fungus, order Moniliales).
**Crops affected:** Tomato, capsicum, cucumber, gerbera, rose.

**Step 1 — Disease Cycle (six stages):**

1. **Survival:** Pathogen survives as **sclerotia** (black resting bodies) in dead plant debris and as mycelium in infected tissue.
2. **Sporulation:** Under high RH (> 90%) and cool temp (15–22 °C), sclerotia germinate to produce **conidiophores** bearing **conidia** (grey, grape-cluster-like — hence "grey mold").
3. **Dispersal:** Conidia are spread by **air currents, splashing water, and worker clothing/tools** inside the polyhouse.
4. **Infection:** Conidia land on **wounded, senescent, or flower petals** and germinate in the presence of free water (> 4 hours).
5. **Colonization:** Mycelium spreads intercellularly, secreting **cell wall-degrading enzymes** (pectinases, cellulases) and the toxin **botrydial**.
6. **New sclerotia:** After 5–7 days, new sclerotia form on dead tissue, completing the cycle.

**Step 2 — Cultural Management:**
- Remove infected plant debris **immediately** (sanitation is the #1 control).
- Maintain RH **below 85%** by ventilation and heating.
- Avoid overhead irrigation; use **drip irrigation**.
- Proper plant spacing (≥ 60 cm for capsicum) to improve air circulation.
- Avoid wounding during pruning; disinfect tools with **1% sodium hypochlorite**.

**Step 3 — Biological Management:**
- Spray *Bacillus subtilis* (e.g., Serenade) @ 5 g/L at 10-day intervals.
- Apply *Trichoderma harzianum* @ 10 g/L as a preventive soil drench.
- Use *Ulocladium atrum* (mycoparasite) on pruned wounds.

**Step 4 — Chemical Management:**
- **Cyprodinil + Fludioxonil** (Switch 62.5 WG) @ 1 g/L — most effective.
- Rotate with **Fenhexamid** (Elevate 50 WG) @ 1.5 g/L to prevent resistance.
- Always spray in the **morning** so leaves dry before nightfall.

**Step 5 — Precision Tool Integration:**
- Install **leaf wetness sensors** to alert when free water persists > 4 hours.
- Use **spore traps + PCR** for early detection of airborne *Botrytis* conidia.
- Deploy **auto-ventilation systems** triggered when RH > 85%.

**Conclusion:** *Botrytis cinerea* is best managed by **environmental control (RH, temperature)** combined with sanitation and **preventive biological sprays** — chemicals are a backup, not the primary line of defense.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** The following data shows the severity of powdery mildew on capsicum in a polyhouse over 5 weeks: 2, 8, 25, 55, 80 percent. Calculate the **AUDPC** and the **apparent infection rate (r)** between week 2 and week 4. Comment on the epidemic.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — AUDPC Calculation.**

$$
AUDPC = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \dfrac{(Y_i + Y_{i+1})}{2} (t_{i+1} - t_i)
$$

Assuming $Y_0 = 0$ at $t_0 = 0$:

Segment 1 (Week 0 → Week 1): $Y_0 = 0$, $Y_1 = 2$

$$
A_1 = \dfrac{(0 + 2)}{2} \times 7 = 7.0
$$

Segment 2 (Week 1 → Week 2): $Y_1 = 2$, $Y_2 = 8$

$$
A_2 = \dfrac{(2 + 8)}{2} \times 7 = 35.0
$$

Segment 3 (Week 2 → Week 3): $Y_2 = 8$, $Y_3 = 25$

$$
A_3 = \dfrac{(8 + 25)}{2} \times 7 = 115.5
$$

Segment 4 (Week 3 → Week 4): $Y_3 = 25$, $Y_4 = 55$

$$
A_4 = \dfrac{(25 + 55)}{2} \times 7 = 280.0
$$

Segment 5 (Week 4 → Week 5): $Y_4 = 55$, $Y_5 = 80$

$$
A_5 = \dfrac{(55 + 80)}{2} \times 7 = 472.5
$$

Total AUDPC:

$$
\boxed{\, AUDPC = 7.0 + 35.0 + 115.5 + 280.0 + 472.5 = 910 \; \% \cdot \text{day} \,}
$$

**Step 2 — Apparent Infection Rate (r) between Week 2 and Week 4.**

Using $t_1 = 14$ days, $x_1 = 0.08$; $t_2 = 28$ days, $x_2 = 0.55$:

$$
r = \dfrac{1}{t_2 - t_1} \ln\!\left[ \dfrac{x_2 (1 - x_1)}{x_1 (1 - x_2)} \right]
$$

$$
r = \dfrac{1}{28 - 14} \ln\!\left[ \dfrac{0.55 \times 0.92}{0.08 \times 0.45} \right]
$$

$$
r = \dfrac{1}{14} \ln\!\left[ \dfrac{0.5060}{0.0360} \right]
$$

$$
r = \dfrac{1}{14} \ln(14.0556)
$$

$$
r = \dfrac{1}{14} \times 2.6427
$$

$$
\boxed{\, r \approx 0.189 \; \text{per day} \,}
$$

**Step 3 — Comment on the Epidemic.**

- AUDPC = **910 %·day** — a **very high value**, indicating a severe epidemic.
- $r$ ≈ 0.189 per day — this is **fast** (typical $r$ for *Podosphaera xanthii* ranges 0.10 to 0.20).
- Powdery mildew thrives at **moderate RH (60–80%)** and **warm days / cool nights** — a common polyhouse micro-climate.
- **Recommendation:** Apply **sulphur dust 0.2%** or *Ampelomyces quisqualis* immediately. Reduce nitrogen fertilization (excess N favours mildew). Improve ventilation to reduce RH spikes at night.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — How Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch in AUDPC:** Writing AUDPC = 910 (without units %·day) loses 1 mark. Always mention **%·day** or **percent-days**.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention K:** In the ETL formula, omitting the control efficacy **K** gives the EIL, not the ETL. Examiners deduct 2 marks for this confusion.
> 3. **Confusing ETL with EIL:** EIL is the damage-threshold; ETL is the action-threshold. ETL < EIL always.
> 4. **Van der Plank equation domain:** The logistic equation is valid for $0 < x < 1$. Using it when $x = 0$ or $x = 1$ gives $\ln(0) = -\infty$, which is meaningless.
> 5. **Mermaid syntax errors:** Using reserved words like `end`, `subgraph`, or `graph` as node IDs will crash the diagram. Always use alphanumeric IDs like `nodeA`, `stepB`.
> 6. **Miscalculating AUC for the FIRST segment:** Students often forget to include $Y_0 = 0$ at $t_0 = 0$, which underestimates AUDPC.

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Bullet Summary — Revise within 5 minutes before the KTU exam:**

- **Polyhouse pests** are dominated by **sucking pests** (whitefly, aphid, thrips, mites) due to enclosed, humid, no-rain-wash environment.
- **Major diseases:** Powdery mildew, Downy mildew, Grey mold (*Botrytis*), Damping off (*Pythium*), Fusarium wilt, Bacterial wilt, ToLCV.
- **IPM hierarchy (in order):** Cultural → Mechanical/Physical → Biological → Biorational → Chemical (last resort).
- **Biocontrol agents to remember (most important for KTU):**
   - Whitefly → *Encarsia formosa* (parasitoid), *Beauveria bassiana* (fungus).
   - Spider mite → *Phytoseiulus persimilis* (predator).
   - Thrips → *Neoseiulus cucumeris* (predator).
   - Soil pathogens → *Trichoderma viride* (fungus antagonist).
- **Trap standards:** 1 yellow sticky trap per 100 m²; blue traps for thrips; pheromone traps for borers.
- **ETL formula:** $ETL = C_c / (V \times D_u \times K)$ — distinguish from EIL = $C_c / (V \times D_u)$.
- **Pesticide dose formula:** $D = (C_r \times V_s) / F_s$ (output in mL or g of commercial product).
- **AUDPC formula:** $\sum [(Y_i + Y_{i+1})/2] \times (t_{i+1} - t_i)$ — unit is **%·day**.
- **Van der Plank apparent infection rate:** $r = [1/(t_2 - t_1)] \ln[x_2(1-x_1) / \{x_1(1-x_2)\}]$.
- **Disease triangle:** Host + Pathogen + Environment → Disease. Break **any one corner** to prevent disease.
- **Polyhouse screening:** 50-mesh net (≈ 0.3 mm) excludes whitefly; 40-mesh excludes thrips; 60-mesh excludes aphids.
- **Precision tools for IPM:** IoT sensors (T, RH, leaf wetness), AI cameras, smart sticky traps, spore traps, drone-based NDVI imaging.
- **Sanitation is #1** — remove infected debris immediately; disinfect tools with 1% NaOCl.
- **Maintain RH < 85%** and **temperature 22–28 °C** to suppress most polyhouse pathogens.
- **Rotate insecticides/fungicides** to prevent resistance (alternate FRAC / IRAC groups).
- **Export-grade produce** demands **zero pesticide residue** → biological + cultural methods preferred.
